_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-03T15:03:14-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FJV29.10303$tY5.2279087@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3D48304A.5BBC408F@shaw.ca> <1028203538.201781@newsmaster-03.atnet.at> <bfdfc3e8.0208011712.19d16180@posting.google.com> <OaU29.99811$ND5.8994381@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D4C1A3B.F2A45399@shaw.ca>`

```
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> > Please carefully read this sentence, extracted from the Net Express 3.1
EULA, Section 11:

>"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in response
 news:3D4C1A3B.F2A45399@shaw.ca...
> Agree with your sentiments. But more importantly, glad to see you back. I
nearly quoted your name to Donald about a week ago with a, "Go
> read Judson in cobolreport.com - he *actually* writes there *might* be
circumstances under which OO COBOL is viable".
>
> There's a turn-up for the book - just so long as you weren't thinking of
Rick's 'pure OO' <G>
>
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
>

Thinking of which may work ...

Picture a cobol parser that reads source code, and builds "Cobol Objects"
that are the functional equivalent of the old railroad track specifications
for Cobol code.  That is, programs are objects within systems and run units,
and contain four sub-objects, labeled "IDENTIFICATION DIVISION", thru
PROCEDURE DIVISION. That proceeds right down to byte level character
objects.  A Cobol program parsed in such a manner resembles Ricks model
exactly.

Such objects could not only emulate Cobol Code thus providing a debug mode,
but write, compile and link their own code, with whatever Cobol compiler is
available. By doing that, AND doing so with the original compiler they were
written for, they should be able to rewrite themselves into any Cobol
dialect, then test themselves.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Licensing

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T08:03:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%Eu39.127203$ND5.10660295@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3D48304A.5BBC408F@shaw.ca> <1028203538.201781@newsmaster-03.atnet.at> <bfdfc3e8.0208011712.19d16180@posting.google.com> <OaU29.99811$ND5.8994381@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D4C1A3B.F2A45399@shaw.ca>`

```
Hello Jimmy,

Glad to be baaaaaaaaaaaack. ;-)

Micro Focus makes it plain that after installing Net Express 3.1 SP 1,
*anything* compiled using NE requires a runtime fee.  Now, consider the
ramifications.  If every object compiled with compilers X, Y and Z require
runtime licensing.  Now consider a project developed using compiler X, also
using object modules compiled with compilers Y and Z.  What does THAT do for
the OO reusability model (or non-OO for that matter)?  Uck!

I'm a very big believer in 'principles'.  At least partly because, but perhaps
entirely because, we live in a causative universe, everything, and I mean
everything, works because of 'laws' or 'principles' that are objective and
universally effective, though obviously not always universally known,
recognized and understood.  This applies not only to the base physical
universe (e.g. F=MA),  but the vastly more complex and subtle (therefore much
less obvious) laws of human affairs and endeavors (two obvious examples: you
can't spend more money than you make indefinitely, and abusive acts engender
hate).  Most of us in technical careers are more familiar with physical laws.
But those who work in politics or psychology, for example, are well aware that
there are laws governing human relations.

Naturally, there are business principles as well.  People who understand these
principles and apply them effectively will make lots of money.  Those who
don't, won't.  Sometimes these principles work rapidly, sometimes very slowly,
but always inexorably.  Let me give you an example.  Circa 1979, Apple was
arguably the most successful microcomputer vendor in the world.  They were
doing lots of things right, and were reaping the benefits, both in money and a
fanatically loyal customer base.  So successful, in fact, that IBM, the then
largest computer manufacturer in the world, broke an inviolable rule of theirs
when they emulated Apple's open architecture policy when introducing the IBM
PC in 1981.  But in 1979 Apple broke a fundamental business rule; they started
playing hardball with both dealers and customers.  When this happened, I knew
without doubt, and said so many times, that Apple would pay dearly for this.
It took 20 years, but eventually had to be bailed out by arch-enemy Bill Gates
to the tune of $350 million.  No one not familiar with the Apple community can
understand what a humiliating circumstance this must have been.  Playing
hardball with customers, therefore alienating them, will eventually kill a
business, though it may take time.

I am personally convinced that when we break any of these principles it will
*always* cost us in some way eventually.  I am also convinced that charging
for runtime fees breaks both business and ethical principles.  First, because
if you examine the costs of using tools, across all human experience, you will
find that charging the end user of a product created using a tool just isn't
done, except in this instance, and possibly a few other very, very rare
instances, though I can't think of any.  Secondly, it simply isn't fair,
especially when it is applied after a tool has been in use for some time,
which is certainly the case of Micro Focus.  In such cases, it is simply
another form of extortion.  Both the developer and the end user have made
business decisions based on certain circumstances, then the tool vendor
changes the rules of the game *after* both developer and end users are
committed.  I will agree that a runtime licensing fee *can* be "fair", but
only if it is applied in such a way that, to the end user of the product
created to use the runtime, it is a strictly one-time charge that can be
absorbed as part of the purchase price,  and when they require an upgrade, say
for running on another platform.

The interesting thing about these principles is that they are completely
objective, and they work whether we understand them or not, or even know about
them.  If a compiler vendor want to charge an exorbitant price for their
compiler, fine, free market forces will control that.  If they want to charge
for support and upgrades, fine, R&D needs to be funded, and you get benefit
for your money.

Yes, I have heard the arguments "We need the runtime revenues or we will go
out of business" until I am sick of it.  Absolute rubbish!  The true statement
would be "We need the runtime revenues or we will go out of business *using
our current business model, which we created by figuring in runtime fees*."
Does anybody think that the market would (or even could) let absolutely
*essential* items such as compilers, go away, simply because no runtime fees
are charged?  Duh!  The market will pay what is necessary, until the cost
becomes so high that other alternatives make more sense.  Does anybody think
that COBOL is any different in this respect from any other computer language?
Duh!  COBOL is the most stable computer language ever designed, and has been
so for longer than most other languages have existed.  In the last decade or
so, where has most of the cost of maintaining COBOL compilers been?  It *has*
to have been in two areas: updating runtimes for new platforms and adding new
features ala the new COBOL spec.  In the first case, I certainly don't object
to paying for the ability to now compile to a 32 bit Windows platform instead
of 16 bit DOS or 16 bit Windows platforms, for example.  In the second case,
why should end users of developed software have to pay for elaborate and
(obviously) costly features such as OO COBOL (sorry Jimmy!  ;-), that may
never be used by more than a tiny fraction of COBOL developers anyway?  Even
you real OO advocates have to admit that OO COBOL will almost certainly never
be more than a small percentage of all COBOL, regardless of what it's merits
may be (See, I'm trying to be nice :-).

Bottom line: Vendors, charge us whatever is necessary for the compiler,
support and upgrades for you to stay in business.  We can plan for that and
pass the costs along to our customers in the price of our products.  Break up
the compiler into levels (e.g. VB, Visual Studio, MS Office), costing more for
more features.  The market will then determine which COBOL vendors survive,
based on merit and value.  But please, don't make *our* customers subject to
your whims, and please stop telling us that runtime fees are necessary.  It is
at the very best an ignorant statement, or disingenuous.  If you doubt it,
there are many compiler vendors (e.g. PowerBASIC, smaller market than COBOL,
few 'deep pocket' developers) who produce excellent compilers of very high
quality, have been profitable for years, and never charge runtime fees.
Q.E.D.
```

```
a real education for me when I when shopping for my first COBOL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Licensing

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T19:10:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aimii5$hug$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3D48304A.5BBC408F@shaw.ca> <1028203538.201781@newsmaster-03.atnet.at> <bfdfc3e8.0208011712.19d16180@posting.google.com> <OaU29.99811$ND5.8994381@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D4C1A3B.F2A45399@shaw.ca> <%Eu39.127203$ND5.10660295@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <aimbs50250q@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
This may be one reason CoBOL is declining.

Library based languages are relatively cheap to produce, and they can be
made available to beginners inexpensively.   Compilers are more expensive to
produce, so the compiler makers have to figure out how to get their money
back.   It is expensive enough that even today we don't have a MacIntosh
CoBOL compiler.

What we end up with is a compromise - compilers are more expensive than
students want to buy, and more expensive than developers want to use.

Some languages can be even produced at a loss, if their use is in the
interest of rich corporations such as MicroSoft or Sun.  (I suppose IBM
doesn't need to make a profit on its Mainframe CoBOL compiler).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Licensing

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T15:03:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GGA39.144694$Og3.37460345@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3D48304A.5BBC408F@shaw.ca> <1028203538.201781@newsmaster-03.atnet.at> <bfdfc3e8.0208011712.19d16180@posting.google.com> <OaU29.99811$ND5.8994381@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D4C1A3B.F2A45399@shaw.ca> <%Eu39.127203$ND5.10660295@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <aimbs50250q@enews4.newsguy.com> <aimii5$hug$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
> (...)
> Library based languages are relatively cheap to produce, and they can be
> made available to beginners inexpensively.   Compilers are more expensive to
> produce, so the compiler makers have to figure out how to get their money
> back. (...)

Howard, I think I have an answer to the "cheap compiler for beginners"
problem.
Create a version of the compiler that is limited to some fairly small number
of
source lines and/or limited in the size executable it will create, with no
free
support, but *not* limited in functions, and *certainly able* to create
executables
(which is the problem with MF Personal COBOL).  Then sell it *cheap* (not
over $50!), or even give it away, CD only, docs on the CD.  No serious
developer
(certainly not one who is willing to pay $2k-$4k for a production compiler)
would
use such a limited product, but hobbyists and students would love it.  Wow!  A
real,  full-blown, quality COBOL compiler they can afford without stealing it!
There would be virtually no loss of sales of the full version, yet would still
be
attractive to hobbyists and students, if the limiting is done wisely.  Once
the
compiler's own build setup was configured to generate the limited model, the
creation of the cheap/freebie version would be cheap and easy.  The cost of
producing CD's in quantity is so low that AOL has blanketed the earth with
them.
Sure, charge the buyer for shipping charges, say $5-$10.  That, in my opinion,
would do more to bolster the COBOL market than anything else, more than
everything else now being done all together.

This, my friends, is what I mean by "planting COBOL seed."  You *have* to get
compilers into the hands of students and hobbyists to make COBOL viable in
the long term.  They won't pay a lot of money, and they won't pay at all for
compilers that won't create EXE files, or that time out after a trial period.
Any
time you can build a large base of student/hobbyist users, you will have a
market
for the product.  How else did radio, audio, automobiles, and PCs themselves
become popular?
```

```
(*sigh*)
IBM does not do *anything* without making a profit on it. The B.S. running around about IBM
'subsidizing' their COBOL compiler by mainframe costs is just pure rubbish.  The $3K per seat they
charge for VA COBOL is that high because they don't sell a lot of seats, and the no runtime licensing
pays off in vastly reduced costs for maintaining all those licenses.

You want to upgrade your customers? Upgrade your compiler(s). It's that simple.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Run-times was Re: MF Licensing

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-03T15:43:24-03:00

```
On the IBM mainframe, normally the end customer is responsible for
obtaining the run-time and interacts directly with IBM in that regard. 
This brings up the interesting question.  Do I as customer X of both
vendors A and B both of which develop using the same COBOL and run-time
set (AccuCOBOL, Net Express, etc.) have to get the run-time from both A
and B.  Do I have even worse problems if I now get a package from vendor
C which requires a run-time from a different compiler manufacturer?  How
does this work for languages such as C++ and Pascal? 

Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Please carefully read this sentence, extracted from the Net Express 3.1 EULA, Section 11:
…[97 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > Wolf
```

```
I will add that the c runtimes are included with every UNIX I can think of because the UNIX itself won't run without it. Except for
very small parts of the kernel, UNIX has been written mostly in C from the beginning.

MMM- maybe what we need to do is similar what I suggest to Liant a while back - cook up and Operating System and built in COBOL
compiler to handle all this stuff, rather than just thinking about putting a compiler together. MMmmm - that actually might be
easier than trying to match a compiler into Windows,
and a heck of alot more reliable. Besides which, the file system could natively support record based processing...

Wonder what O/S's are out there that could serve as a good base for that? Linux / FreeBSD comes to mind as a starting point. OS/390
sounds like a good model to go forward on too.

-Paul
```

```
"Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote in
message news:0im39.39517$Yd.1859101@twister.austin.rr.com...
> Good question - better answer. People pay me to make their
windows stuff work.
> Also, it would be stupid to ignore a potential market of
several hundred million.

Do you use any other PC platform on any of your machines --
home or office -- more than 1% of the time?

______________

For my personal use, I would go with something else if there
was a viable desktop alternative.

I develop what I can on MacOS, but that's largely residual
nostalgia -- it's too bad Apple has done so little in the last
7 years.  I brought Linux (Red Hat 7.2) in to bask in the
stability, but it's not panned out.  The desktops (KDE/Gnome)
are just too flakey.  I was surprised by this, but soon learned
that many Linux users have accepted this as the price of
freedom.

Windows 9x/ME has an admittedly weak memory model, but after 5
years, Windows has matured into a stabile and highly useable
platform with Win 2000/XP.  I've had this Win 2000 machine for
a year now, and I've only had 1 crash.  Mean time between
failures is now 10 months and counting.  (Though 1 data point
doesn't give a lot of certainty on that calculation.)

Given the size of the iron, and the aggressiveness of their
development, I think microsoft has done a pretty good job with
windows.  Which is not to say, however, that they're not an
evil empire that must be destroyed.
```

```
Grinder wrote:
> 
> Do you use any other PC platform on any of your machines --
> home or office -- more than 1% of the time?

I use IMS Real/32 for 90% of my development work.  Well actually I use
XTree Gold and TSE editor plus a few others 90% of the time and these
happen to run very nicely on Real/32 (which is a multiuser/multitasking
DOS system).  Tested code is then copied to Windows/Unix/Linux for
recompiling.

I have Windows, Linux, Unix and OS/2 systems for use as required.

> Given the size of the iron, 

I do have old 486SX boxes with no HD doing useful tasks running Linux:
router, firewall, telnet terminal.  I was running Real/32 on a 486DX100
until it broke, its now on a P133.

> and the aggressiveness of their development, 

Aggressiveness, yes.  They used to be agressive to the programming
(Windows 3.x on a 386) then they became agressive to their conpetitors
(WordPerfect, Lotus), then they became agressive to their OEMs (IBM, HP,
Sony), now they are agressive to their customers (Licence 6, BSA, XP
SP3).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Run-times was Re: MF Licensing

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T08:36:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<X7v39.127865$ND5.10680441@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3D48304A.5BBC408F@shaw.ca> <1028203538.201781@newsmaster-03.atnet.at> <bfdfc3e8.0208011712.19d16180@posting.google.com> <OaU29.99811$ND5.8994381@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D4C244C.B09D179A@istar.ca> <1028461835.767473@newsmaster-03.atnet.at> <7Lb39.34348$Yd.1734128@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
"Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:
>
> MMM- maybe what we need to do is similar what I suggest to Liant a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> based processing...

Back in the 1960's, Burroughs (later merged with Univac to form Unisys) went
even further: they designed a computer system, hardware and everything else,
from the ground up, specifically to be a good COBOL machine.  All the hardware
math, including machine code addresses, was in decimal, even the hardware op
codes were all decimal.  Made reading a memory dump a piece of cake.  Fixed or
floating point precision to 100 digits.  Two machine instructions to test a
field for alpha or numeric, or scan for a string.  Table search using one
instruction, table sort using (IIRC) four or five instructions.  One
instruction translate (e.g. to all uppercase, to all lowercase, or ASCII to
EBCDIC,).  This was the very successful B2500/B3500 series, later called their
"Medium Systems".  The easiest and friendliest computer system I ever worked
with.
```

- **References:** `<3D48304A.5BBC408F@shaw.ca> <1028203538.201781@newsmaster-03.atnet.at> <bfdfc3e8.0208011712.19d16180@posting.google.com> <OaU29.99811$ND5.8994381@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D4C244C.B09D179A@istar.ca>`

```
"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:
>
> On the IBM mainframe, normally the end customer is responsible for
> obtaining the run-time and interacts directly with IBM in that regard.

It is customary for mainframe users to buy runtimes for whatever they run on
their mainframes, partly because, historically, mainframe clients almost
always developed in-house software too.  Also, the mainframe vendor was
usually the compiler vendor too, as in your example.  All very different from
a mom & pop store buying a business package for their PC.
```

```
the Software and that such application software product
> adds significant and primary functionality to the Generated Code.  Your
license to reproduce and distribute any Generated Code
> generated by you using the Software prior to the termination of this
Agreement shall survive any termination of this Agreement.
> Copyright (C) 2000 Micro Focus International Ltd. All rights reserved."
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> encoded software: the binary version of a computer program that is used by
the computer to run the program.
> See also source code
> Microsoftï¿½ Encartaï¿½ Reference Library 2002. ï¿½ 1993-2001 Microsoft
Corporation. All rights reserved."
>
> In my opinion, the situation is *very clear*.  When I compile a free
standing, statically linked EXE program, then the WHOLE EXE is
> "Generated Code" (object code), is it not?  What other functions are in
there, except those necessary to translate my COBOL language
> program into binary executable?  If Micro Focus chooses to copy some of
those routines from a runtime library instead of doing
> direct translation, that is merely a code generation technique, not a
difference in substance.  Even assemblers can have *some*
> runtime library code.  Often file I/O code, and if nothing else,
initialization and program exit code.
>
> This is the 'Bottom Line' as I see it:
>
> a) In what other industry do you pay fees to a tool vendor in order to
distribute a thing created using those tools?  Can you
> imagine every user of a piece of furniture paying royalties to the
manufacturers of the saws, hammers, staplers and other tools used
> to create the furniture?  What about the tools used to create the fabric?
Imagine an automobile!  Such a concept is absurd on the
> face of it, and should be rejected by any user of any "tool" product.  No
other industry could enforce such a rediculous thing, and
> just because enforcement is possible in software, why should we put up
with it?  The end user of a compiler is the developer, *not*
> the end user of applications written using the compiler.
>
> b) My own Net Express compiler license was just trashed three times in 12
days, while both installing (one machine) and uninstalling
> (two machines) Net Express SP 1.  In addition, uninstalling SP 1 removed
the icons from the Net Express Start Menu directory from
> both machines!  Now, Micro Focus is insisting that not only I, but my
customers as well, suffer this outrageous, buggy, greed
> motivated practice of theirs, applied to the software I create using Net
Express.  Even if the runtime licenses were free, this
> problem would still exist.  This directly attacks my reputation as a
software developer, without *any* benefit to me, or any
> recourse on my part, other than moving to a different COBOL compiler, or
abandoning COBOL altogether.  Either course of action would
> have a significant impact on my company and my clients.
>
> c) I have invested something like 20 years of my career into developing
applications using Micro Focus COBOL.  One of my currently
> running systems is over 17 years old, another is 15 years old, both
originally written under MS-DOS.  Now Micro Focus are changing
> the rules on me, shortly *after* I paid them two years maintenance with
the understanding that I could distribute statically linked
> EXE files without run time licensing.  This is unconsionable at best.
They have changed my business model significantly without my
> consent or approval, and certainly not to my advantage.  My feelings are
very clear: I have been BETRAYED and SCREWED.
>
> d) We've talked a lot in these newsgroup about the demise of COBOL.
Personally, I do not see how COBOL can survive long term,
> unless some drastic changes are made.  Most of the industry as a whole
doesn't have a clue *why* COBOL is a good language for
> business applications, and why others aren't.  Virtually nobody is
promoting COBOL, and the compiler vendors are doing their best to
> eliminate small independent developers like myself.  Yes, no doubt COBOL
will continue running on big iron for a long time, through
> inertia.  But most people don't realize that, at least in the U.S., more
than 75% of people work in companies with fewer than 20
> employees.  We small companies, collectively, hire more than *three times*
the number of people as companies with 21 employees or
> more.  We little guys *are* the larger market.  Not many people have
invested more time in COBOL than I have, or would grieve its
> passing more than I.  But I am seriously considering moving to a different
development platform, as a matter of self defense.
> Unfortunately, there *is* no language that I can use and have confidence
the programs will last 15 years without significant
> modifications or even rewrites.  For example, there is no Microsoft
product in existence that one could have used to write a major
> application even 10 years ago, and it still be running on their latest OS
today, with little more than recompiling to get here.
> Looks like my clients will have to pay the price, right along with me.  I
am not a happy camper.
> --
> Judson McClendon      jud@sunvaley.com
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> > "Wolf Grossi" <Go2devnull@magro-soft.com> wrote in message
news:<1028203538.201781@newsmaster-03.atnet.at>...
> > > This agressive licensing policy of MF encourages several of my
> > > customers running huge cobol apps (1200 progs/150 users) to consider a
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Licensing

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T09:20:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aNv39.128793$ND5.10707043@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3D48304A.5BBC408F@shaw.ca> <1028203538.201781@newsmaster-03.atnet.at> <bfdfc3e8.0208011712.19d16180@posting.google.com> <OaU29.99811$ND5.8994381@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <EOX29.77172$nm.2924118@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
>
> "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
> > Please carefully read this sentence, extracted from the Net Express 3.1
EULA, Section 11:
>
> [...]
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Do you have an opinion on Fujitsu's licensing scheme?


I bought Fujitsu Enterprise COBOL and paid maintenance for several years, as a
hedge against Micro Focus making my life harder, or going belly-up.  When
Fujitsu made it impossible for me to even install Fujitsu COBOL on even one
extra machine as a backup, I wrote them off.  At least Micro Focus was kind
enough to grant me license to install  my compiler on my desktop and laptop
both, which I appreciate.

Let me clarify my position about Micro Focus.  I like their COBOL compiler a
lot.  I have been able to migrate from MS-DOS to Windows 32 bit with virtually
no source code changes.  They are generally very helpful when I have a
problem, and very quick to supply me with a response key to get a my compiler
up and running when I move the compiler to a new computer or when the
licensing system crashes, which happens a lot.  Their compiler seems somewhat
expensive to me, but it is worth it.  I have only two real complaints, both of
which go far beyond annoyance with me:

1) The restrictive license protection system.  No problem with the licensing
legal requirements, just the software enforcement.  This is for two reasons,
first because it prohibits me easily moving the compiler from one machine to
another, or installing more than one backup, and secondly because it trashes
the license from time to time, costing me time and money with no benefit to
me.

2) Runtime licensing makes my customers subject to the same problems as I have
in 1) above, plus the costs, plus the hassle of making the installation of my
software more complicated and error-prone for me and my clients.

I must add that Micro Focus has told me they will offer me an amnesty
agreement, at least for the present, so that my current clients won't have to
pay for their runtimes.  The technical people have supplied me with
instructions on how to implement it, but I still haven't received the
appropriate licensing information from sales.  I appreciate this, I really do.
But the cost of the runtime licenses is secondary for most of my paying work,
it is the complexity and reliability issues for my clients that worry me.  I
am still in business because my software is highly reliable.  Micro Focus'
licensing enforcement software is not even in the same league as mine,
reliability wise, by several orders of magnitude.  When it breaks, it will
cost me time and money to fix, and how do I charge my clients for that?  Will
Micro Focus come fix the runtime on my clients machines when it breaks?  Will
they reimburse me for fixing a crash caused by their buggy licensing software?
Will they fix the damage to my professional reputation when their buggy
license protection software breaks?

I write a lot of stuff for giveaway, much of it just for fun.  I have written
software for my church on occasion, and recently wrote a COBOL loan
amortization program I intended to give to members of my Sunday School class
and other friends.  Those people aren't going to pay hundreds of dollars for
runtimes.  Runtime licensing simply removes Micro Focus COBOL out of the
market for that stuff.  I will throw away the COBOL amortization program and
write it in PowerBASIC.  I have a COBOL compiler that costs me thousands of
dollars to obtain and maintain, and I can't even use it to write a checkbook
balancing program for my kids, without them (probably eventually) having to
pay runtime fees.  Sheesh!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Licensing

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-04T01:17:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sg%29.176696$88.3641479@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
The problem is that MicroFocus does not see it as selling the compiler, they see it as selling the runtimes. You  buy the compiler
for the sole purpose of running things on their runtime. Damn the actual text of the license.

I just spent Friday night and ALL DAY today moving a customer off of Microfocus for this very
same reason.  In fact, they are very happy.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-08-03T10:41:58+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D4BA566.50F99141@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <3D43AAC8.4E6BFC80@Azonic.co.nz> <ai6dt9$aji$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> On 28-Jul-2002, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Depends on how small the table is, and how efficient memory management is.

If it is a 'small' table then 'memory management' would not be an issue
at all.  If it is a 'large table' such that memory management would be
an issue then it would affect both a SEARCH and a SEARCH ALL.

However, given a table in key order the average number of comparisons
required to find random keys or to determine if the key doesn't exist
might be:

   Table size     SEARCH     SEARCH ALL
       10           5            4
       50          25            6
      100          50            7
     1000         500           10

Which of these would you call 'small' ?
```

```
>From: Richard Plinston riplin@Azonic.co.nz 
>Date: 2002-08-03 05:41 Eastern Daylight Time
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>Which of these would you call 'small' ?

It looks to me as though the SEARCH ALL table is showing the
maximum number of comparisons, not the average.  If the minimum
is 1 and the maximum is 10, then I guess the average would be 5 1/2. 


Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-03T11:40:57+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.h09y890.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`
- **References:** `<3D4BA566.50F99141@Azonic.co.nz> <20020802220429.08109.00001239@mb-ce.aol.com>`

```
On 03 Aug 2002 02:04:29 GMT, Ross Klatte wrote:

>>From: Richard Plinston riplin@Azonic.co.nz 
>>Date: 2002-08-03 05:41 Eastern Daylight Time
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Ross,

Well not exactly.

Assumingf that SEARCH ALL uses a binary search

In a table of 1000 elements, only one will be found with one comparison, two
with two comparisons, four with three, eight with four and so on.
That means that 511 out of 100 will be found with maximum 9 comparisons,
leaving 489 numbers needing ten.

Total number of comparisons

1	x 	1	1
2	x	2	4
3	x	4	12
4	x	8	32
5	x	16	80
6	x	32	192
7 	x	64	448
8	x	128	1024
9	x	256	2304
10	x	489	4890

This total 8987, meaning that the average number of comparisons is 8987 /
1000 = 8.987

Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020803104336.23276.00001195@mb-mp.aol.com>`

```
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…

Thanks, I knew it couldn't be that simple.  
If I understand you correctly, which I think I do, and if my arithmetic 
is correct, which I doubt, the table should read: 
Table size       SEARCH     SEARCH ALL
    10              5               2.9
    50            25               4.9
   100            50               5.8
 1,000           500               9.0
200M          100M              17.4

meaning that a binary search of 200,000,000 items makes 
fewer comparisons than a sequential search of 36 items. 


Ross
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-08-04T08:10:47+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <3D43AAC8.4E6BFC80@Azonic.co.nz> <ai6dt9$aji$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3D4BA566.50F99141@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Ross Klatte wrote:

> >However, given a table in key order the average number of comparisons
> >required to find random keys or to determine if the key doesn't exist
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >     1000         500           10

> It looks to me as though the SEARCH ALL table is showing the
> maximum number of comparisons, not the average.  If the minimum
> is 1 and the maximum is 10, then I guess the average would be 5 1/2. 

No. Bianry searches don't 'average' that way. I had also added 'or if
the key doesn't exist'. 

With a serial search each comparison has an equal chance of matching,
thus average is of:

     1/1000 + 1/1000 + 1/1000 + .... 1000 times.

If the search fails to find a match (ie an item is found that is
greater) then the average is still the same.

Note that with an unordered table the average for a hit is half the
table size and the average for a miss is the whole table size because
every item must be tested.

Because it is a binry search then each step has an increasing chance of
being a hit. 

     1/1000 + 1/500 + 1/250 + 1/125 + 1/64 + 1/32 + 1/16 * 1/8 + 1/2 + 1

This means that the 'average' of finding a match is around 9 while to
determine non-match still takes 10 comparisons.  The actual average
depends on the proportion of hits and misses being made (which is why I
said 'might be').

While the minimum is 1, it only occurs 1 in a 1000 times that results in
a match.  The maximum is 10 but it occurs every time there is no match
and more often than not to find a match.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T14:17:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aim1ch$at5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <3D43AAC8.4E6BFC80@Azonic.co.nz> <ai6dt9$aji$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3D4BA566.50F99141@Azonic.co.nz> <3D4CD377.31B10662@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

On  4-Aug-2002, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> Because it is a binry search then each step has an increasing chance of
> being a hit.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> said 'might be').

But the number of computer commands for each comparison is not the same for
binary and linear searches.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T14:15:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aim19c$asu$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <3D43AAC8.4E6BFC80@Azonic.co.nz> <ai6dt9$aji$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3D4BA566.50F99141@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

On  3-Aug-2002, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> However, given a table in key order the average number of comparisons
> required to find random keys or to determine if the key doesn't exist
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Which of these would you call 'small' ?

I would have to look at the assembly code to determine the break-even point.
 The Binary search takes more commands to prepare for each comparison than a
linear search does.   There can be a further cost if the table was sorted or
tested for this search.  Using the KISS principle, I would use a linear
search until the size is such that the advantage of a binary search is
significant.

Or explore some other possibilities - a hash table or direct look up table
can sometimes be the most efficient - if they apply.

The point is to not assume that one method is always the best one.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** Brian Walker <walkerbj@optus(remove)net.com.au>
- **Date:** 2002-08-01T12:47:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at>`

```
"News" <Martina.Moritz@chello.at> wrote in
news:6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at: 

> I just started to do some research abt. performance and try to use it
> for my work.
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Hi
I did a lot of work at my site on COBOL CPU optimisations and after
testing various options decided to go with OPT(FULL) as the only really
significant impator. The NORENT option made no measurable difference. To
activate NORENT all programs in the load module must be NORENT and you
need to link in the LE NORENT directive, else the load module is RENT.
Same for REUSE. We already had NOSSRANGE as default. The SSRANGE option
causes the compiler to generate heaps of extra assembler to bounds check
all arrays, that's why it causes such a performance hit. A word of
warning on the TRUNC option, if you use DB2 or anything else that uses
INTEGER as a datatype then the TRUNC(OPT) option is a risk. The option
allows the compiler to manipulate the data item EITHER on the byte
boundary or as defined by the number of digits in the picture string.
The only way to determine which method was generated for a particular
COBOL statement is to read the assembler listing. The FASTSRT option
only affects sorts that don't manipulate the data via input / output
procedures. (If you code input or output procedures on the sort
statement then FASTSRT has no affect). In conclusion, using OPT(FULL),
For programs with loads of DB2 and / or IMS access, we achieved on
average 4 percent improvement. For programs with little DB2 or IMS we
achieved on average 25 percent improvement. I benchmarked approximately
300 programs for 2 weeks. There were other changes that were well worth
while, for example I was able to changed a few utility programs to
procedure division copybooks, though not as many as I wanted to. 
Another big thing was to ensure that programs called frequently (more
than a million times per main program execution) didn't perform the
initialise type code for every call. Obviously this depends on what the
sub-program does. Another thing to watch for is that MVS time calls are
very expensive and it is almost certainly worth writing an assembler
program to return the current date and time rather that allowing COBOL
to do it (accept date, function current date, all bad) for any program
that does it many times. Using Strobe I saw several programs that
executed for 10 CPU minutes and the date call used more than 10 percent
of that CPU. Hope some of this helps. I have to go now, best of luck. 
Cheers.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-01T13:29:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254>`

```
A couple of comments on the TRUNC discussion:

A) With the latest compiler (Enterprise COBOL) it is possible to compile
with TRUNC(OPT) and use "USAGE COMP-5" within your source code to "force"
individual fields into proper "INTEGER" handling. See:
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/1.3.4.4


B) If you *must* use TRUNC(BIN), make certain that you are AT LEAST on IBM
COBOL for OS/390 & VM, V2R2.  There were "significant" performance
enhancements (still not as good as TRUNC(OPT)) in this release. See:
   http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igypg205/PREFACE.6.1
```

```
Hello there,
 
I have some off-topic note how to reduce CPU load caused by DB2/IMS on
MVS and therefore
get more CPU for other applications
 
You possibly are not aware that DB2 ,IMS, VSAM data can be made
accessible from UNIX/Windows
PC by just PLUG IN scsi/fiber line to Mainframe disk controller. (HDS
or EMC)
 
Combined with  newframe NFDSS server (see www.newframe.com) which is
capable to directly access  DB2/IMS shared disk from UNIX/Win ( it is
like scsi disk) , the solution allows you to reduce CPU burden and
even access the data  when MVS is not in use.
 
Thanks for your time,
Lev
```

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

#### ↳ Re: About AcuCOBOL 5.2???

- **From:** vinsnash <>
- **Date:** 2002-08-01T13:40:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1622526.1028208728@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3tp19.1341$672.693900@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

Jin Zhang,

Any code you write using Acucobol can be used in a Client/Server mode.

The client/server setup is transparent to your cobol code and there are
no unique coding requirements necessary if you are using the Acucobol
Vision file system.

Whether or not you need bridging software like ACU4GL or ACUServer
depends how your system is configured and really is not a concern for
the cobol programming techniques.

Regards

Vins Nash
```

```
If your shop doesn't have Xpediter, you might have the IBM Debug Tool (which
supports COBOL for OS/390 & VM, PL/1 FORTRAN, and C/C++).  This tool is fully
capable of debugging a COBOL program, even one running under CICS or IMS, or
using DB2.

If you have BookManager READ on the mainframe (and it's a standard part of
OS/390 and z/OS), and have a COBOL for OS/390 bookshelf, there should be a book
there telling about Debug Tool.
```

## Thread: global vs external — 2 msgs

### global vs external

- **From:** tailchaser@pandora.be (mike)
- **Date:** 2002-08-02T03:09:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f85ddca6.0208020209.3e78ab6c@posting.google.com>`

```
HI there...need some help with this.

What is the difference between the external and the global?

01 a pic 99.
01 b pic 99 global.
01 c pic 99 external.

thx
```

#### ↳ Re: global vs external

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-08-03T04:43:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0208030343.4f2732b8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<f85ddca6.0208020209.3e78ab6c@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard is right with respect to EXTERNAL, but didn't explain GLOBAL.

GLOBAL means that the dataname can be used without needing to be
passed as a linkage parameter by any nested programs directly or
indirectly contained within the program that declares that dataname. 
Nested programs are those that are contained within other programs and
not those that are separately compiled and connected with the calling
program at link edit time.

Robert

```

<a id="t-9884765994"></a>
## Thread: global vs external — 2 msgs

### Re: global vs external

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-08-03T08:11:08+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D4B820C.94E8A3BC@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<f85ddca6.0208020209.3e78ab6c@posting.google.com> <0t0lku8srv1ooonk8hcg5bk1vlb54v3ukj@4ax.com>`

```
Michael Todd wrote:

> If both program A and
> program B defined an 01 level with the same name, same length
> and both specify EXTERNAL, then they both access the same
> memory at run time.

As long as they are in the same run-unit.

I have seen some try to use this as if it were some form of shared
memory that any process could access so it is worth pointing out that it
is only the same memory within a group of programs calling each other.
```

#### ↳ Re: global vs external

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-02T15:49:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aier8l$eeo$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<f85ddca6.0208020209.3e78ab6c@posting.google.com> <0t0lku8srv1ooonk8hcg5bk1vlb54v3ukj@4ax.com> <3D4B820C.94E8A3BC@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard is absolutely correct.

As a follow-up, the current ANSI/ISO Standard *assumes* that the entire
world is a single run-unit.  It really has NO rules about what happens in an
environment where multiple run-untis may co-exist (i.e. - depending on OS -
multi-threading, multi-tasking, whatever).

Many COBOL implemenations that DO work in this (now common) environment, do
provide rules (and/or extensions) to handle this, but such "rules" are NOT
necessarily portable.

CAVEAT coder!!!

The draft Standard *begins* to discuss this, but certainly not in depth. In
fact, in its definition of its scope, it explicitly states,

"This International Standard does not specify: ...
    - The sharing of resources other than files among run units."
```

### Micro Focus' "Ask the Executives Forum"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-02T15:59:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aierrm$4s1$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I have been requested (by a usually reliable source) to make the following
clear to those in this (or any other) forum who are concerned about THEIR
posts being deleted from the Micro Focus' "Ask the Executives Forum".
Please pass this on to anyone else who might be interested.

A) It is true that this is a "moderated forum".

B) It is *not* true that anyone's recent posts have been deleted.

C) *UNFORTUNATELY* the person who is (internally) designated as the
moderator (and therefore able to "promote" posts to general visibility) is
currently out-of-the office. Furthermore, this person didn't (forgot?) to
designate someone to do this in his/her absence.

D) This is a RECOGNIZED "bug" in the new system and Micro Focus intends to
remedy it as soon as possible (with no guaranteed date for delivery)

    ***

Bottom-Line:
  If you tried to post a note to that forum recently (last couple of weeks
or so) and haven't seen it "generally visible" - this does NOT mean that
Micro Focus is censoring you; it simply means that it has yet to be
processed (reviewed) and released.  My understanding (again no guarantee) is
that "review" is intended for "relevance" and "language" and NOT for
content.  However, I do *not* speak for Micro Focus on this issue.
```

```
In article <aierrm$4s1$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

[snippage]

>C) *UNFORTUNATELY* the person who is (internally) designated as the
>moderator (and therefore able to "promote" posts to general visibility) is
>currently out-of-the office. Furthermore, this person didn't (forgot?) to
>designate someone to do this in his/her absence.

I am reminded of a conversation a physician I know had with an insurance 
company that was a bit... tardy in processing the claims due to him.

Phys: 'I don't understand... this has been going on far too long; why 
can't you cut me a check?'

Clerk: 'Oh, I'm sorry but the girl (ed note - according to the physician 
this was the term she used; the situation occurred in the late 1960s so it 
might well be accurate reportage) who processes the check has gone on 
vacation; she'll be out for another week.'

Phys: 'I see... so tell me, when does the girl who processes the premium 
checks go on vacation?'

Clerk: 'Oh, that *never* happens... oh... tee hee hee, I see what you're 
saying... let me see what I can do.'

It still took another six weeks for the check to reach him... and there 
was no change in processing-time subsequent thereunto that he noticed.

>
>D) This is a RECOGNIZED "bug" in the new system and Micro Focus intends to
>remedy it as soon as possible (with no guaranteed date for delivery)

Reminds me of some Tech Bulletins that WANG computers used to issue...

Issue: Something Blows Up Unpredictably and Without Apparent Cause.

Recommendation: Be aware of this situation.

DD
```

## Thread: Report files on unix and head -n — 1 msgs

### Report files on unix and head -n

- **From:** "Dan" <deacondan25@NOSPAMhotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-02T22:11:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ukmidrkmf2rpb4@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi all,

Is there a way to display all print lines the way the will be printed? My
files are wrapping around. If I use toggle-truncate-lines in emacs, I will
get the lines to display the way I want, except for page advances. The ^L is
tacked on after the previous ^M instead of displaying as a new line.

If I use: head -n15 script.ksh > test.txt
I get the first 15 lines like I am supposed to.

If I use: head -n15 report.lis > test.txt
all I get is a ^L in the file.

I can get the results I want by using: head -c 2295 report.lis > test.txt
(taking the lrecl +2)

Can any one explain why this is happening?

Thanks,
Dan
```

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-08-03T09:30:15+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1028366972@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file — 13 this month / 25 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

_Thread started [2002-07](2002-07.md#t-e1284e218b)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

- **From:** foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth)
- **Date:** 2002-08-04T06:13:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93dfc7c7.0208040513.2bab448f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com>`

```
robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones) wrote in message news:<fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com>...
> Hello
> 
> I think you should use the file status with the file that you are
> reading and test it for success after every I/O action, i.e. OPEN,
> READ and CLOSE. 

Hi:

I am slightly surprised that there is at least one other person in CLC
who believes that the File Status should be checked.  

It is categorically impossible to write a program which is 100% 
dependable and fully informative to the user without testing 
the File Status. Period.

I think that there are four main reasons why programmers don't use the
File Status:  

A. They either don't know about it or don't understand the importance of it.

B. It cannot be conveniently coded when using structured style. 

C. It is quite a bit of extra work and therefore people don't bother.
 
D. They just assume that everything will always be the way they 
   want it to be, so why bother.
   
This is the original code.

> PERFORM 500-READ-RECORDS.
           PERFORM VARYING EMP-TABLE-ROW-CTR FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL EMP-TABLE-ROW-CTR>10
             MOVE EMP-RECORD TO WS-EMP-TABLE(EMP-TABLE-ROW-CTR)
             PERFORM 500-READ-RECORDS
           END-PERFORM.

500-READ-RECORDS.
           READ EMP-FILE RECORD
               AT END MOVE "YES" TO END-FILE.

Including File Status checks in the above would change it considerably.

Will some kind-hearted soul who is an advocate of structured programming
(Mr. Dashwood?) kindly recode the above (so as to educate the original 
poster) to include an OPEN and to check the File Status afterwards, 
to check it after the read, and, to provide an informative message 
to the user giving them the option to do something about ALL errors.
For the benefit of the poster: consider the following situations;
file not there, file empty, file in wrong format, file damaged, etc.
(Also consider file out of sequence.)

Whether the poster is writing this program for fun or is being paid
to do it by his employer, I don't know, but they use notepad to create
the data. There may be an occasion when notepad could be used for
convenience in a one-time emergency situation.  To use notepad
for data entry in any system which sees regular use is like, hopeless.

For the benefit of the original poster:  Instead of using notepad
write a file-maintenance program which allows the user to add records,
change records, delete them, and, MOST IMPORTANTLY, to EDIT THE DATA
and which will put the data in an ISAM or DBMS format with keys 
appropriate to the application.

I wonder why the experienced programmers in CLC seldom point out
obvious flaws in programs posted and devote their responses solely to
the question raised. In effect, the CLC programmer does the neophyte
programmer a disservice by not mentioning other stuff.

Finally, for the benefit of the poster, report programs are
the most basic of commercial-application programs.  To fully
understand ALL of the considerations in such programs, you
could visit foodman123.com/flow2.htm to learn more.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

```
100-INITIALIZATION.

    OPEN INPUT EMP-FILE
    PERFORM 900-CHECK-FILE-STATUS

    READ EMP-FILE RECORD
        AT END
            MOVE 'YES' TO END-FILE
        NOT AT END

    END-READ

900-CHECK-FILE-STATUS.

        etc., etc
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-04T13:43:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208041243.7b327fbf@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com> <93dfc7c7.0208040513.2bab448f@posting.google.com>`

```
foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth) wrote 

> I am slightly surprised that there is at least one other person in CLC
> who believes that the File Status should be checked.  

Your surprise is most likely because you don't pay much attention to
what others write.  There have been several threads that have
discussed file status.

> I think that there are four main reasons why programmers don't use the
> File Status:  

In a student environment, simple programs will fail with an error
message if the file status clause is omitted.  This saves having much
code which merely duplicates what the default run-time system does.

There are several ways of learning: one is 'I do this because I was
told to'; another is 'I do this because I found out what happens when
I don't'.

> A. They either don't know about it or don't understand the importance of it.

Not yet, no.  There are many things that students don't understand the
importance of yet.  There are many things that 30 year programmers
don't understand the importance of, and maybe never will.

> B. It cannot be conveniently coded when using structured style. 

What is your basis for this ?
 
> Will some kind-hearted soul who is an advocate of structured programming
> (Mr. Dashwood?) kindly recode the above (so as to educate the original 
> poster) to include an OPEN and to check the File Status afterwards, 
> to check it after the read, and, to provide an informative message 

Informative messages are already provided by the run-time in the
environment the student is working in.

> to the user giving them the option to do something about ALL errors.

For many errors, especially at the level of the original poster, there
is usually _nothing_ that the 'user' can do.

> Whether the poster is writing this program for fun or is being paid
> to do it by his employer, I don't know, 

No. He's a student, pay attention.

> For the benefit of the original poster:  Instead of using notepad
> write a file-maintenance program which allows the user to add records,
> change records, delete them, and, MOST IMPORTANTLY, to EDIT THE DATA
> and which will put the data in an ISAM or DBMS format with keys 
> appropriate to the application.

Poor advise, he should follow the course and learn in a structured
way.  Trying to learn _everything_ all at once is a poor strategy and
leads to students simply not coping and becoming very confused.

> I wonder why the experienced programmers in CLC seldom point out
> obvious flaws in programs posted 

I thought that your experience would be quite the reverse.  Post some 
more of your code and see what happens.  Or do you simply ignore this.

> and devote their responses solely to
> the question raised. In effect, the CLC programmer does the neophyte
> programmer a disservice by not mentioning other stuff.

No. Wrong.  'Other stuff' often is mentioned.  Overloading answers
with issues unrelated to what the student is learning will distract
the student and may cause them to argue with the instructor.
 
> To fully
> understand ALL of the considerations in such programs, you
> could visit foodman123.com/flow2.htm to learn more.

How entirely arrogant of you to claim that you have the answers to
_ALL_ the considerations in such programs and that you _fully_ cover
_all_ understanding.

> "Every COBOL programmer is going to end up writing tons of
programs of this type "..." 

Nonsense.  20 years ago I decided that rewriting the same report
program with variations (file, layout, controls, selection) was a
complete waste of time and so I wrote my own report scripting
sub-system that does >80% of all reports and provide user specificed
ad-hoc enquiries.  There are also many commercial reporting programs,
such as Chrystal Reports.

You are a quarter of a century out of date.

> "...and the Flow Chart serves as a model for all of them."

Once again you show that you are out of date - flowcharts are from the
60s and 70s, and arrogant in thinking that this is for _all_ of them.

There are more things in this world than you can imagine.
```

```
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

Huh?  Give me a break.  Insert:

        IF EMP-FILE-STATUS IS NOT EQUAL TO "00"
            PERFORM GIVE-TONY-A-PROGRAMMING-LESSON.

at the end, create the paragraph at the end of your existing code, and write
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T14:40:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aim2nh$bbr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com> <93dfc7c7.0208040513.2bab448f@posting.google.com> <shh39.1255$Xf7.173100@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

On  4-Aug-2002, "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

> Huh?  Give me a break.  Insert:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> writing any other type of code?

I sometimes have code such as this:
 01  SPREAD-FILE-IN-STATUS   PIC X(02)   VALUE '00'.
     88 SPREAD-IN-STATUS-OK                  VALUE '00' '97' '10'.
     88 SPREAD-IN-AT-END                         VALUE '10'.

 01  SPREAD-FILE-OUT-STATUS  PIC X(02)   VALUE '00'.
     88 SPREAD-OUT-STATUS-OK                  VALUE '00' '97'.

Using English with more status codes than "00" can help.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T14:35:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aim2dp$bb9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com> <93dfc7c7.0208040513.2bab448f@posting.google.com>`

```

On  4-Aug-2002, foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth) wrote:

> I think that there are four main reasons why programmers don't use the
> File Status:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>    want it to be, so why bother.

And a fifth reason why programmers don't ALWAYS use the File Status:

E.  In some cases knowing the file status won't be useful to the program.

I have even seen it harmful (the code checked for file status = '00' and
aborted when it was '97'.   That program was also worthless in that it
translated ALL non '00' VSAM return return codes to a program return code of
'7', as a signal to tell the calling program to abort.  Meanwhile the abort
doesn't abort with the VSAM return code visible, making it hard to determine
what the problem was.

If they hadn't defined a VSAM return code at all, the program would have
aborted, and the abort message would have told us line number and VSAM
return code, making it easy to determine what was wrong.

If the program can make informed decisions based upon the return code (that
it wouldn't make just as well with at-end type English CoBOL), by all means
use the return code.   But if it just obfuscates the system default abends
that would have told us the same thing, why use it?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T03:35:42-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D54C24E.7030705@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com> <93dfc7c7.0208040513.2bab448f@posting.google.com> <aim2dp$bb9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  4-Aug-2002, foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth) wrote:
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> aborted when it was '97'.  

This is sloppy coding, i.e., ignoring a RC (97) which indicates another 
valid OPEN, not a problem with using the file-status field. If one is 
going to code using only some of the valid RC's, the program's bound to 
be a mess.

> That program was also worthless in that it
> translated ALL non '00' VSAM return return codes to a program return code of
> '7', as a signal to tell the calling program to abort.  Meanwhile the abort
> doesn't abort with the VSAM return code visible, making it hard to determine
> what the problem was.

Again, *very* sloppy coding. Was this written by a trainee, or by an old 
timer who hadn't learned anything new since he finished his COBOL class 
15 years ago?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T03:11:15-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D54BC93.1020909@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com> <93dfc7c7.0208040513.2bab448f@posting.google.com>`

```
Anthony Dilworth wrote:
> robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones) wrote in message news:<fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com>...
> 
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>                AT END MOVE "YES" TO END-FILE.


Foodman Tony is something of a troll with respect to structured 
programming, but I would approach it thusly:

blah
blah
blah

OPEN INPUT EMP-FILE ...

IF not VALID-EMP-OPEN
     deal_with_it
END-IF
<we know now that EMP-FILE opened successfully>


<in the mainline code>
PERFORM  VARYING EMP-TABLE-ROW-CTR FROM +1 BY +1
            UNTIL EOF-EMP-FILE
               or EMP-TABLE-ROW-CTR > +10
		/* is the cutoff *really* 10 records? */
		/* terminating the READ loop by ">10" looks very odd
		   to me */

	READ  EMP-FILE

		EVALUATE		TRUE
                	WHEN  			VALID-EMP-FILE-READ
		    <I usually count input records>
		    MOVE EMP-RECORD TO										WS-EMP-TABLE(EMP-TABLE-ROW-CTR)

                	WHEN  			EOF-EMP-FILE
		    continue
	        WHEN			OTHER
		    <deal with the read failure>
		END-EVALUATE

	End-READ

END-PERFORM

blah
blah
blah

   -----------------------------------------------

Of course, the 88-levels for VALID-EMP-FILE-READ, EOF-EMP-FILE, etc., 
must be defined correctly, e.g., EOF-EMP-FILE must be assigned to the 
EOF value in the EMP-FILE file status.

No priming READ required.
```

- **Date:** 2002-08-10T14:04:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208101304.78c74054@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com> <93dfc7c7.0208040513.2bab448f@posting.google.com> <3D54BC93.1020909@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`

```
Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote 

> Foodman Tony is something of a troll with respect to structured 
> programming, 

Or indeed anything that has happened in the last quarter century   ;-)

> but I would approach it thusly:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> <we know now that EMP-FILE opened successfully>

Hmmm, I don't know what magic your 'deal_with_it' is capable of but my
code can't guarantee any such 'opened successfully'.  In cases where
it is not possible, though, it may only be necessary (in this example
code) to set EOF-Emp-File so that it doesn't attempt any reads.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-12T18:36:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ULW59.145396$D8.5150176@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com> <93dfc7c7.0208040513.2bab448f@posting.google.com> <3D54BC93.1020909@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <217e491a.0208101304.78c74054@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote 
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> code) to set EOF-Emp-File so that it doesn't attempt any reads.

If you're unable to open your input file, how can you continue 
processing. In my world, IBM mainframes, dealing with the inability to 
open a file leads to program termination, so there's no point in setting 
EOF on.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-12T21:45:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208122045.5dc7df92@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com> <93dfc7c7.0208040513.2bab448f@posting.google.com> <3D54BC93.1020909@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <217e491a.0208101304.78c74054@posting.google.com> <ULW59.145396$D8.5150176@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```
Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote 

> > Hmmm, I don't know what magic your 'deal_with_it' is capable of but my
> > code can't guarantee any such 'opened successfully'.  In cases where
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> EOF on.

On mainframes you probably could get away with a GOBACK, unless it was
CICS.  As you say it has to go back through some administrator to sort
out who did what wrong.

With interactive systems you can't just 'bang out' of a program.  In
particular the structure you coded could need a few minor changes to
work acceptably by cycling through the open process to give the user
the opportunity to correct the error: misspecified file name, file
locked to another process, etc. Or it may be that a missing file is
acceptable (as long as the program doesn't try a read on it).
```

```
Richard wrote:
> Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> CICS.

If it were CICS I'd expect to be using CICS commands and not trying to 
OPEN or CLOSE the file, or issue READs, either. As you said, things are 
different online.
```

## Thread: C Header file from COBOL copybook — 2 this month / 4 total

### C Header file from COBOL copybook

- **From:** "Kiran Ingale" <kingale@socal.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-04T18:58:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3Pe39.37706$Jz.7687329@twister.socal.rr.com>`

```
Is there any tool available in the market to generate C header file from
COBOL copybook?
```

#### ↳ Re: C Header file from COBOL copybook

- **From:** "Ira Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T12:16:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d4eb09a$1@giga.realtime.net>`
- **References:** `<3Pe39.37706$Jz.7687329@twister.socal.rr.com>`

```

"Kiran Ingale" <kingale@socal.rr.com> wrote in message
news:3Pe39.37706$Jz.7687329@twister.socal.rr.com...
> Is there any tool available in the market to generate C header file from
> COBOL copybook?

The DMS Software Reengineering Toolkit is generalized compiler
technology for parsing, analyzing, transforming and prettyprinting
sources in many computer langauges.  DMS is available with
C and COBOL front ends, including name and type resolution.
See http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMS/DMSToolkit.html.

DMS could be configured to parse a COBOL copybook,
carry out name/type resoluiton, and generate the C header
file you desired.
```

## Thread: COBOL vendors! Me so angry! — 8 msgs

### COBOL vendors! Me so angry!

- **From:** maross@texoma.net (Mike)
- **Date:** 2002-08-04T14:36:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2763b81a.0208041336.76d5c338@posting.google.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote
>Do you have an opinion on Fujitsu's licensing scheme?

  Well Jerry, Fujitsu's may be better than the rest; but it also has
flaws mostly due to their lack of support of WWW user forums for user
feedback. Even M$ knows educating the world with allowed pirated
M$-OS-developmental tools (or donated as in Peru per US payees) will
lead to an educated user group that can be forced to eventually pay
for their thievery verses their lazy human nature resistance to
reeducate themselves to a new OS or programming skill. Fujitsu needs
to allow everyone to see user problems in WWW-forum verses their
closed door Service Wide method. They should write one using their
COBOL-CGI method if it is so good, but that would take a year and
ready-made PHP-Phorum can be downloaded and set up in a day.  This to
me is the same COBOL-Iron-mainframe-fortress separate the
PC-WWW-programmers mentality that will prevail till the baby boomers
are retired and COBOL dies.
      1.Their charge for more than 1 processors($1,500 >)  is stupid?
If your programs are downloadable from a web page do you inquire your
users PC-configurations incase they have a dual-pent3 so you can pay
Fujitsu? I use Lightwave-3d for rendering scenes for Web graphics and
neither Newtek (or Maya) charge for a second processor and 3d work is
the kind of area that as developers grow so does render farms and
multi-processor needs.
      2. Users that wish to use only CGI(standard version) do not get
multi-thread-multiprocess record locking but must upgrade to
Enterprise. That puts a nail into any intranet/internet developers
hope using their Standard version with a non RDBMS! They only provide
certification for MS-SQL/Oracle. Their Linux versions seems to tell
the  developer to test other RDBMS them self. Well I can get ms-access
to work right, but my MySql is useless compared to my PHP 4.2 which
works perfect.
      3.What are the alternatives: CA-COBOL CGI requires a doggle(
just tell your ISP-host to place a doggle on their computer). Don't
dream that MF new investors (600+mil) will change their licensing
revenue dreams. The other next to nothing-cost compilers have poor
documentation, untested reliability and just no followers for a
serious developer to use compared to the C type languages
(C++,PHP,JAVA,). Eventually these C type language cost-effective
created programs will Kill your COBOL programs +runtime ability to
compete as well as COBOL's poor UI.
    Also Fujitsu  has  wasted time with M$.NET and have developed what
I call Fo-OLCOB (or just Fool's COBOL). If you can find any examples
of a program please provide a link. However the rest of the world is
trying to figure .NET also http://news.com.com/2010-1071-948117.html
.NET is nothing more than a slimmed down Web-server to CGI scheme on a
PC. Microsoft let the cat out of the bag with PWS-IIS on a PC, which
allowed PC users to develop WWW-CGI-RDBMS like mine that uses
ICS(internet connection sharing)  with 3 Redhat-M$(for the kids and
rendering) dual boots. With Redhat 8 there s/b a usable desktop for
the non-geek and Maya (soon Lightwave) rendering hardware nodes that
cost as much as a M$ license. Sorry for my seemingly COBOL-troll post
but being a non-professional programmer it irks me to learn other
languages to develop an Internet site or use Lscript(for 3d, Cool!)
when my minor was DP-COBOL. Mike.
```

```
> You should be aware that most of the computer-literate community views Linux

Do you think that Fujitsu views Linux as a cult ?  They adopted Linux
as a platform a couple of years ago and now have announced NetCobol
for Linux.

> Some governments (U.S., China, France, to name three) deal
> harshly with cults.

not, in fact, 'deal harshly' with Linux, thus it is not a cult.  In
actuality the Chinese Government supports 'Red Flag Linux' and other
GNU/Linux projects and software houses.

> For your own safety, avoid Linux.

On the other hand Microsoft, in its most recent EULAs, has been taking
the right to explore, update and disable any software on your computer
without any further need for your permission.

For your own safety, avoid Microsoft.
```

```
> hope using their Standard version with a non RDBMS! 



> They only provide
> certification for MS-SQL/Oracle. Their Linux versions seems to tell

> to work right, but my MySql is useless compared to my PHP 4.2 which
```

```
JerryMouse wrote 
>Fujitsu's licensing scheme has flaws because they don't have a user

   Sorry poor Memo logic flow, I stated my etiology-solution before
the numbered problems.
Example: http://www.newtek.com/community/discus/nest.html   My
3dsoftware(Lightwave3d) sponsors a open user forum that has company
paid moderators(including the CEO) screen all messages and response to
users of their products. This improves next version product
development, error fixes, user requested enhancements, visitors can
evaluate user satisfaction-loyalty and product usefulness for them.
Most COBOL vendors like Fujitsu just point you to general COBOL forums
of  they neither moderate or respond to problems. I understand MF just
restarted the forums after a long suspension, a great error on their
part that probably resulted in decrease user satisfaction. They may
have gotten tired of hearing disgruntled users with their 3.1
licensing scheme that was a thorn to recruit new users, this was when
I went to Fujitsu without knowing and I almost switched back to MF
without that licensing knowledge thanks for the heads up here.

>Uh, who certifies Linux applications?
Redhat and others. Numerous benchmark articles sponsored by most large
companies (IBM,MS,oracle) and user forum satisfaction.

>Yeah, I know. I've been hearing about the demise of COBOL 
Human Ideologies, religions, major stock-market cycles and methods
usually have the same lifespan of a given population (i.e.
baby-boomers) and the next generation usually enhances these or
totally replaces them with something more efficient. COBOL is middle
aged and signs of its declined in use is everywhere. Whether it
exceeds the medium lifespan (74y/o) only time will tell.
    
>Some governments (U.S., China, France, to name three) deal
>harshly with cults. For your own safety, avoid Linux
Jerry your hand holding with M$-Gates looks unnatural. You are either
a MS employee or your products are too dependent on M$. You reverend
Communist China persecutes Christians too so do you go along with that
too?

Richard_from_azonic_nz wrote:
>The multi-thread is only required for ISAPI and ASP. CGI does not
>appear to require this
Fujitsu clarified via email support : That multithread-record-locking
works only on Enterprise Version. So even on a 2-computer intranet a
request to read a ISAM file can corrupt file structure or at least
leave erroneous updated records! So if someone wanted only CGI the
standard version you must buy PowerCOBOL-etc Which   uses Windows VB6
objects and M$ wants everyone to upgrade to VB.NET and have stated
plans to drop support for VB6 in the future, making it useless in the
next M$ version.

>Not unreasonable.  They have no control over the quality of the ODBC
>drivers or the RDBMS that you may choose to use.
M$,hardware vendors, and everyone else certifies drivers to work with
their products. I believe Fujitsu is using a M$ supplied module to
communicate to the ODBC's and M$ just can't get by with no Oracle
support. If your in-flight pilot changes to Mohammad-something and he
says "leave the flying to us" do you believe him and go to sleep?
 
>Is PHP using ODBC or direct to MySQL?
Yes! A MySql-RDBMS module is written directly into PHP and referenced 
just like native COBOL ISAM/Sequential files in any HTML-editor as and
IDE (if you have you Web-server installed correct. Examples
http://wdvl.internet.com/Authoring/DB/SQL/Build/build1-3.html 
http://hotwired.lycos.com/webmonkey/programming/php/tutorials/tutorial4.html
  
>Have you tried CobolScript ?  Not entirely Cobol, but based on.
Yes I have and I like it. However it has security problems as in below
article, poor documentation, modules must be setup by ISP-provider and
It's rare to fine problem-solution threads on the Internet, meaning
lack of users. Perhaps in next version but as a scripting interpreter
PHP seems superior and can be optimized  with http://zend.com tools?
http://www.linuxworld.com/linuxworld/lw-1999-11/lw-11-cobol.html 
Thanks Mike.
```
