<a id="t-fac9d31281"></a>
## Thread: Dynamic deletion of files in os/390 COBOL — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Dynamic deletion of files in os/390 COBOL

- **From:** ssmith@adelaidebank.com.au (Simon Smith)
- **Date:** 2002-08-04T19:11:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<64826f50.0208041811.3a3b5ef6@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello

We currently use module BPXWDYN to dynamically allocate datasets. 
Basically the process involves processing a sequential file that
contains data for multiple destinations.  We detect change of
destination and close/de-alloc then alloc/open.

What I'm looking for is some way of either:

In a preceding job step, deleting all datasets containing a given mask
( e.g sys.env.myprog.daily.* , or

in my cobol program, changing the sequence to close/de-alloc then
delete/alloc/open, or

some other suggested means of ensuring that I clean-up a file that I
subsequently want to allocate.

Thanks

Simon
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic deletion of files in os/390 COBOL

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T12:40:27+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<othskug97gafo2pqdifp2d24r5env0pbd3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0208041811.3a3b5ef6@posting.google.com>`

```
On 4 Aug 2002 19:11:18 -0700 ssmith@adelaidebank.com.au (Simon Smith) wrote:

:>We currently use module BPXWDYN to dynamically allocate datasets. 
:>Basically the process involves processing a sequential file that
:>contains data for multiple destinations.  We detect change of
:>destination and close/de-alloc then alloc/open.

:>What I'm looking for is some way of either:

:>In a preceding job step, deleting all datasets containing a given mask
:>( e.g sys.env.myprog.daily.* , or

TSO DELETE will do that.

:>in my cobol program, changing the sequence to close/de-alloc then
:>delete/alloc/open, or

If you will always be creating all files, why not simply OPEN OUTPUT and
overwrite the data?

:>some other suggested means of ensuring that I clean-up a file that I
:>subsequently want to allocate.

Why do you need to do a "clean-up"?
```

<a id="t-107d555d65"></a>
## Thread: DB2 joins w/"standard" working-storage — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### DB2 joins w/"standard" working-storage

- **From:** gnuoytr@rcn.com (robert)
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T06:12:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<da3c2186.0208050512.8270455@posting.google.com>`

```
folks:

background:  my colleagues "converted" from VSAM to DB2 by
simply changing meta-names; file->table, record->row, field->
column.  and moving the data, of course (well, sort of; it's
390, and i'm told that storage is really still VSAM).

the issue:  they use "copybooks" which still contain things like
widget001...widget025 in working-storage.  the referenced tables
still have the repeating columns; a referential no-no.  i have
been rebuffed in my attempts to split these situations into
multiple tables by the assertion that there is no way to handle
the resulting variable number of widgets (which, it seems to 
me from my relational perspective is a Good Thing) in a working-
storage declaration.

i find it hard to believe that this situation still persists after
20 years of DB2/COBOL.  i just think i work with a bunch on numbskulls.

is there a reference that describes how to handle this?  rooting
around in the COBOL books at my neighborhood B&N wasn't much help.
the current VSAM book is 1986!!!!!!!!!  the COBOL books only dealt
with DB2 as a source of flat files.  

please, it can't be this stupid.

toodles,
robert
```

#### ↳ Re: DB2 joins w/"standard" working-storage

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T13:47:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020805094706.00688.00001072@mb-df.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<da3c2186.0208050512.8270455@posting.google.com>`

```
Robert writes...

>folks:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't understand what you're saying here. 1) what do you mean by "changing
meta-names"? where is this done? not in a COBOL program, I have never heard of
meta-names in a COBOL program. 2) what do you mean by "moving the data"? from
your next sentence it doesn't sound like any DB2 work was done (no storage
groups, table spaces, data spaces, tables, indexes created / loaded) if you are
"really still VSAM". So what do you mean they "converted" to DB2? Where's the
DB2 aspect reflected.



>the issue:  they use "copybooks" which still contain things like
>widget001...widget025 in working-storage.  the referenced tables
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>storage declaration.

If the programs are not using DB2, there is no real issue. You know, COBOL
tables have _nothing_ to do with DB2 tables. An application may certainly have
tables-in-the-COBOL-sense, and perhaps populate them with data read from a
relational database. Tables in this sense have nothing to do with relational
databases, referential integrity, SQL, and so on. Either you don't understand
the application or you are not explaining it to us very clearly.


>
>i find it hard to believe that this situation still persists after
>20 years of DB2/COBOL.  i just think i work with a bunch on numbskulls.

I find it hard to believe that an adult in the business won't capitalize
sentences and proper nouns and more clearly explain what they are trying to
say.


>
>is there a reference that describes how to handle this? 

How to handle what? What is it you're talking about?


> rooting
>around in the COBOL books at my neighborhood B&N wasn't much help.
>the current VSAM book is 1986!!!!!!!!!  the COBOL books only dealt
>with DB2 as a source of flat files.  
>

Really? What COBOL book treats DB2 as a source of flat files? 

(BTW, from an application programmer's perspective, VSAM hasn't changed much
since the mid-1980's, so it's probably OK. But if you want, you can download
the latest IBM VSAM pubs for free in Adobe Acrobat format.)


>please, it can't be this stupid.
>

Hmmm. How stupid can't it be?


Anxiously awaiting clarification.

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

<a id="t-1d63b596a8"></a>
## Thread: Programming Language vendors, run-times, and "business models" — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Programming Language vendors, run-times, and "business models"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T13:34:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aimgg9$3ov$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
This is a serious question.

Does anyone know of any vendor (or vendors):

A) Whose product(s) are related to either a single or multiple programming
language (but NOTHING else, no "games", "operating systems", hardware,
whatever)

    and

B) Do *not* have any run-time license fees (and/or EXORBITANT maintenance
fees)?

    ***

Certainly, this doesn't apply to Fujitsu or CA (for whom COBOL and other
compilers are a MINISCULE part of their business) or AcuCORP, Micro Focus,
or Liant (who have run-time license fees).  Equally true, it doesn't apply
to IBM, Unisys, HP, etc for whom the OS is their "major" product and
compilers "come along for the ride".

But what I don't know is whether any of the C++, Pascal, Ada, etc vendors
meet both of these criteria. I looked at the Borland site, and couldn't
figure out if they did or didn't meet them.  It seems to me that (at least
historically) most of the "C" vendors did *not* have a C product as their
"primary focus".  (Obviously, SUN and Java, does NOT meet both of these).
Similarly, any Microsoft compiler is eliminated as meeting BOTH of these.

Unless I am missing something, it seems that the compiler, and even
compiler-plus-tools, "business" does NOT make a profit *unless* some sort of
"run-time" profit is made by the original vendor.  On the other hand, it is
certainly true, that unless the "developer" (using the compiler) can still
make a profit, they won't buy (continue to use) that compiler.
```

#### ↳ Re: Programming Language vendors, run-times, and "business models"

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T16:31:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1ZB39.145882$Og3.37856616@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<aimgg9$3ov$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,

I have been programming PCs since 1977, and this runtime fee requirement
from Micro Focus is the *first time* I have ever been personally presented
with this.  I have used Microsoft programming languages since they have
been selling them, and I have never personally encountered it with them.
Last time I checked, you could download every VB runtime since 1.0 from
MS's web site.  I know for a fact that there are some excellent, top quality
compiler vendors that have never charged runtime fees and have always been
profitable; PowerBASIC, for example.  They have excellent IDE's, generate
tight, fast code, and compile like blazes!  The compilers are so clean, they
don't even release patches between releases, as far as I know.  Understand,
these compilers have a very large number of built-in commands, as complex
as any COBOL commands (e.g. email) except perhaps indexed files.  They
do offer an inexpensive (no runtime fee) database add-on, and you can use
Btrieve, SQL drivers, etc.  These compilers cost under $200, and the market
for BASIC compilers can't be as large as COBOL, plus, if there is any
language that has prompted more interpreters and compilers to be produced
(competition) than BASIC, I don't know what it is.  I'm not trying to promote
PowerBASIC (though I do use their compilers and like them) but if anyone
wants to check out what I said, their URL is www.powerbasic.com.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Language vendors, run-times, and "business models"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T17:13:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aimtat$1dg$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<aimgg9$3ov$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <1ZB39.145882$Og3.37856616@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```
Judson,
  PowerBASIC *does* seem to meet both of my criteria.  On the other hand,
when you are talking about Microsoft compilers, (compilers directly SOLD by
MS - not those that run under their OS), then you have missed the point.
There is no doubt that MS (or as some call them "M$") does NOT get the
majority (or even any large "chunk") of their profit from their compilers.

I am WELL aware (from internal knowledge) of how Micro Focus made its
profits between the mid-80's and mid-90's.  I won't get into the MERANT
"fiasco" - but I do know that MF *never* made its profit from sales of
"individual" compilers - or even compiler-and-tool packages.

I am little surprised, however, by your statement

 "and the market  for BASIC compilers can't be as large as COBOL"

when we are talking about "developing for workstations", this doesn't sound
reasonable to me, but I could be mistaken.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Language vendors, run-times, and "business models"

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-06T00:38:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mUE39.189068$88.4020809@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<aimgg9$3ov$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <1ZB39.145882$Og3.37856616@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <aimtat$1dg$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Well, the argument regarding Microsoft is kinda half true and half not true. For example, Microsoft sold compilers well before any
operating system became their prime source of income. The classic Gates rants about people pirating BASIC are probably still around
on the web somewhere. I, in fact, still have Microsoft Basic compilers for CP/M, Pascal Compilers for DOS and XENIX, and so forth.

Historically, Turbo-Pascal was the first compiler I can think of that became wildly profitable. $49.

Of course, both Microsoft and Borland mutated, but they both definitely got their start from Compiler sales not related to a single
O/S.

-Paul

…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:1ZB39.145882$Og3.37856616@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com...
> Bill,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> MS's web site.  I know for a fact that there are some excellent, top
quality
> compiler vendors that have never charged runtime fees and have always been
> profitable; PowerBASIC, for example.  They have excellent IDE's, generate
> tight, fast code, and compile like blazes!  The compilers are so clean,
they
> don't even release patches between releases, as far as I know.
Understand,
> these compilers have a very large number of built-in commands, as complex
> as any COBOL commands (e.g. email) except perhaps indexed files.  They
> do offer an inexpensive (no runtime fee) database add-on, and you can use
> Btrieve, SQL drivers, etc.  These compilers cost under $200, and the
market
> for BASIC compilers can't be as large as COBOL, plus, if there is any
> language that has prompted more interpreters and compilers to be produced
> (competition) than BASIC, I don't know what it is.  I'm not trying to
promote
> PowerBASIC (though I do use their compilers and like them) but if anyone
> wants to check out what I said, their URL is www.powerbasic.com.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I will second that.  I have been writing software for micro's since well
before the PC, and still have a copy of DOS 1.0.   I cannot think of a
single piece of software that charges both for the actual software package,
then everytime you use it for its prime purpose ... in this case to write
software for other computers.

In fact, the output of the program is completely, 100% useless without a
second software package snuck in after the first one has been paid for.
What would you think of a financial package, sold to accountants, that
required a percentage of all accounting fees? Think it would be worth 2-3000
per accountant?  How about a word processor that required a percentage of
all authors royalties for anything written using it?   How long do you thing
the banks would use Cobol, if the language vendor required a percentage of
every deposit?

When the only way to make a profit is to charge your customers, then require
them to charge their customers for you, then require them to charge THEIR
customers in addition, you are getting ridiculous.  I sell my software
through scale companies and cash register companies.  How the hell do I
explain that I am selling something I do not own, and after they buy it,
they will be required to pay some weird company somewhere extra money, and
sign a contract with them, before they can use what I am selling (and claim
to own).

Then after they have done that, they have to rent a telephone line for every
piece of equipment, hook it up to the internet, and call Britain to get
permission to start their cash register.  Pardon the language, but the
response I get is "You have to be out of your fucking mind".  Small vars
like myself cannot make a living using a language crippled like that.

Donald


Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: Programming Language vendors, run-times, and "business models"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-05T23:47:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D4F0F3D.BB35B04@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<aimgg9$3ov$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> This is a serious question.
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> make a profit, they won't buy (continue to use) that compiler.

Bill,

You've answered your own questions in your last statement. I feel concern for
the vendors just making money from software alone - but that doesn't extend to
my being charitable enough to stick with their money generating schemes.

Reality check - February 1981, prices are Canadian :-

64K Radio Shack (Model II)                                   $5,645
1-Drive expansion (gave total of  4 x 8 " floppies)     $1,699

-------

$7,344

Radio Shack - RM/COBOL compiler                       $  399

Having produced the compiler, and presumably the LRM, which came shrink-wrapped
in a Radio Shack cover, no more involvement by RM(Liant). There was no Tech
support, nor upgrades, plus no examples. (Well, Tandy did introduce *some* tech
support, including COBOL - first time I 'phoned asking for 'COBOL Support' -
"Yew all, we don't support other folks software !".)

My experience of tech support generally, Radio Shack, MS COBOL and to a lesser
extent MF COBOL, not worth the money you are being asked to shell out for
perhaps a once a year query. The plus side is the maintenance cost gives you
access to latest Fixpacks/upgrades - assuming you currently need them

In an effort to generate income over and above basic product price, our software
only vendors have turned to other approaches. (Who sez it was Arthur Andersen
that introduced the concept of  'inventive accounting' ?).

Prices are theoretical, because I don't know current status - Would you buy a
new compiler every four years ? Base price $3,600, now add 4 years maintenance
at $900 ???.  Add to that runtime fees.

There has to be a compromise between what vendors need to survive and what the
small guy is prepared to pay to stay in business.

COBOL is shooting itself in the foot, and it's a combination of following, in no
particular order :-

- Just like the larger populace doesn't bother to vote, most developers just sit
looking in and puzzled. The mainframe crowd, "Not our problem".

- COBOL Standards - too damn long a process. Plus the intended Standard
includes whole chunks that wont be implemented by some vendors, plus even if
implemented, probably wont be used by the majority of developers.

- Tools are available for SQL/DB, GUIs and  Printing that diminish COBOL's role
-
( viz. COBOL Files, Screen Section, Report Writer).

- Vendor pricing structures

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Programming Language vendors, run-times, and "business models"

- **From:** hebisch@math.uni.wroc.pl (Waldek Hebisch)
- **Date:** 2002-08-14T16:00:49+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<ajdurh$cue$1@panorama.wcss.wroc.pl>`

```
<snip>

I guess you disqualified GNU C. However Ada Core Technologies may qualify.
They seem to get most profit from development tools and maintainence

you get full compiler and runtime for free. So you may distribute Ada

back-end the Ada specific part is quite big.
```

```
I do NOT believe (but could be mistaken) that *all* of Micro Focus'
SupportLine "forums" (fora?) are moderated. I think that this applies to
their "Ask the Executive" forum.  I do *not* expect that type of
"give-and-take" *ever* to be handled via this (or any other PUBLIC) forum.

As to WHY use a NON-moderated vendor forum, the answer is that you MAY be
able to get better/faster replies from "knowledgeable" (possibly even
internal) people.

On the other hand, OBVIOUSLY, if you are interested in "real users, real
world" or other similar input, I suspect that I would recommend this NG. It
is also the only type of place for discussing cross-vendor issues.
```

<a id="t-8c1481c20e"></a>
## Thread: Still having trouble — 2 this month / 4 total

_Thread started [2002-07](2002-07.md#t-8c1481c20e)._

#### ↳ Re: Still having trouble

- **From:** "Topcoder" <No@Spam.Com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T06:26:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Rm259.5616$Ke2.461370@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<adb84bbf.0207282123.58c8012b@posting.google.com>`

```

Lost <guglert80@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:adb84bbf.0207282123.58c8012b@posting.google.com...
> I made some changes to the file its still doing the same thing, I
> think we are getting closer though.  I made some changes.

You need to define EMP-RECORD as 24 bytes only - you have 48.
You need to read the company number record into WS-COMPANY-NAME

I would also recode WS-EMP-TABLE as

01 WS-EMP-TABLE.
      05 WS-EMP-RECORD OCCURS 10 TIMES.
            10  WS-EMP-DEPT-NUM PIC 999.
etc

Then read the remaining records into the subscripted WS-EMP-RECORD area.

When you are having trouble with a program, you need to learn to patiently
desk-check it.
That means going through a couple of passes of the program line-by-line,
asking yourself "what does this do" as you analyze each statement.
Write down each field content change as it occurs.  Analyze that closely.

Bugs will rear their ugly little heads much faster if you adopt this
training/testing aid.
```

#### ↳ Re: Still having trouble

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T11:35:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0208101035.571bf2ec@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<adb84bbf.0207282123.58c8012b@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello again

In addition to the changes proposed by Richard and Topcoder, you also
need to have separate 01 levels in the FD for the EMP-FILE to separate
the header from the details as follows:

01  EMP-HEADER.
    05  COMPANY-NAME            PIC X(24).

01  EMP-RECORD.
    05  EMP-CODE.
    etc as before

These two 01 levels being subordinate to the same FD statement are
implicit redefinitions, so you do not have to use the REDEFINES clause
to get the desired effect.  You subsequently need to ensure that the
first READ of EMP-FILE is done outside of the table building
procedure, e.g. in 200-START-UP or 300-INIT-ITEMS, to save the
company-name somewhere else.

Your PERFORM VARYING in 210-PROCESS-DETAIL-RECORD should also test for
end of file so should take the following form

PERFORM VARYING EMP-TABLE-ROW-CTR FROM 1 BY 1
          UNTIL END-FILE = "YES"
             OR EMP-TABLE-ROW-CTR>10
 etc as before
END-PERFORM.

This latter, while not strictly necessary in this case, is generally
good practice as it ensures that you catch the cases when someone
tries to use your program with some missing data.

Making progress, I hope

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic file allocation on IBM mainframe

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** chancomm@aol.com (Chancomm)
- **Date:** 2002-08-13T23:26:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020813192626.16657.00000250@mb-ch.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<01c240ec$6a440f60$81c2f943@chottel>`

```
 Let me elaborate, if the system tries to allocate the file on the same pack,
you get back an error , duplicate dataset on direct access device. If the
system tries to allocate the dataset  on a different pack, no error message is
generated and the dataset is created (in an  uncatlgd  state on that pack). 

   The problem many rookies run into is seeing no obvious error messages, they
assume their job worked. BUT the dataset your next job reads is not the one you
just created .  
    
     I have seen programmers spread 10 files accross packs one for each bum
test.
Then when they think  they have it right, the file fed into their payoff step
is the one from their morning disaster. 

  Why don't you just use an iefbr14 before running your test??
```

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

_Thread started [2002-07](2002-07.md#t-5eb5f873b2)._

#### ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

```

Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com...
> Much of the data contained in business systems is representational;
> that is, it represents an event -- it has no behavior. One of the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> original sale 'object' has no change in its state.

Incorrect.  The original Sales Order will be updated
with the RMA reference key, at a minimum.

> The behavioral abstractions that are discussed as part of
> object-orientation do not occur as often in business applications
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> of business data processing.

Inaccurate.  These are 'the design patterns", to use your terminology,
of batch processing.  Online transaction processing differes substantially.
>
> Example: The summarize pattern might be seen to consist of:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> value zero. I think doing so would be a mistake

I think your last 2 paragraphs were a mistake.  No offense.

> Data processing (DP) differs from 'good' OO applications in
> at least two ways.
>
> First, DP has a lot of representational data, as discussed
> above; OO applications have data in which the state changes.

Gobbledygook.  The state changes to represent an event
that has just occurred, does it not? (the answer is yes).
>
> Second, the nature of 'good' OO applications is that they have
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> asynchronous with propogation.

Now you are just moving words around the page without really saying
anything remotely approaching common sense.
>
> With DP, only as much data is loaded into memory as is required

Is there any other way?

> and the events that change values often have no immediate effect.

There is no law or need that dictates that they have to.
Updates to weekly, monthly or annual totals are bread and butter events.
In any environment. language or methodology.

> This is because the effect of events that change states are
> synchronized to fit a schedule or are asynchronous on demand.

Whatever that means.

> I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL because the characteristics
> that make for 'good' OO applications are different than the
> characteristics of most DP.

If you are saying that OO is a buch of hoogy, I tend to agree,
if its proponents all talk like you.  No offense.
Also, the textbook you are quoting from is woefully behind the times.
It used to be EDP, then DP, then IMS.  Now it is IT.

> This does not mean that I feel there are no opportunities to
> use OO COBOL. It only means that I feel we must not jump into
> OO without first ensuring that an application is well-suited
> to OO.

"Make sure the solution is appropriate to the task in hand."
That is a startling concept.    I must write that down.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T23:46:13-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ulbn1obut2p2cd@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <aT259.5644$Ke2.462878@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

Topcoder <No@Spam.Com> wrote in message
news:aT259.5644$Ke2.462878@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
> Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
> news:ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com...
[snip]
> > I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL because the characteristics
> > that make for 'good' OO applications are different than the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> It used to be EDP, then DP, then IMS.  Now it is IT.

You are missing an essential concept -- DP is a subset of IT.

> > This does not mean that I feel there are no opportunities to
> > use OO COBOL. It only means that I feel we must not jump into
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> That is a startling concept.    I must write that down.

And repeat it when necessary. <g>

I believe that OO is not a bunch of hooey; only that it does
not fit everywhere. Read section 24.2.1 "Ignoring Classes"
in Bjarne Stroustrup, "The C++ Programming Language,"
Third Edition, Addison Wesley, 1997.

Page 725, "For many applications -- say, a traditional
sequential database update program -- [ignoring classes]
is quite reasonable, and the traditional techniques
developed over decades are adequate for the job."

Page 727, "... some problems are best solved by writing
a set of procedures. The point of an 'object-oriented'
approach to design is not that there should never be any
non-member functions in a program or that no part of a
system may be procedure-oriented. Rather, the key point
is to decouple different parts of a program to better
reflect the concepts of the application."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-08-11T04:21:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c240ee$700d2920$81c2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <aT259.5644$Ke2.462878@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <ulbn1obut2p2cd@corp.supernews.com>`

```
You might also want to read "Pitfalls of Object Oriented Development" by
Bruce Webster.  He discusses about 82 different pitfalls.  It is refreshing
to have a strong OOD advocate give a balanced picture.  Too often all you
get are "it slices, it dices, it ...."

<ulbn1obut2p2cd@corp.supernews.com>...

…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-11T04:27:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aj5ame02kk5@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <aT259.5644$Ke2.462878@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <ulbn1obut2p2cd@corp.supernews.com>`

```
> I believe that OO is not a bunch of hooey; only that it does
> not fit everywhere. Read section 24.2.1 "Ignoring Classes"
> in Bjarne Stroustrup, "The C++ Programming Language,"
> Third Edition, Addison Wesley, 1997.

Not every application I write uses objects to accomplish its
goals.  An object-oriented design approach, though, is still
valid in those cases.  You have consistently tempered your
statements, but I suspect you see no _practical_ use for OO.
This is the apparent source of much of this debate.

Some context has been added to your excerpts -- not as a
refutation of your point, but rather because it's an
interesting topic.

24.2.1. Ignoring Classes

Consider design that ignores classes.  The resulting C++
program will be roughly equivalent to the C program that would
have result from the same design process -- and this program
would again be roughly equivalent to the COBOL program that
would have resulted from the same design process.  In essence,
the design has been made "programming language independent" at
the cost of forcing the programmer to code in the common subset
of C and COBOL.  This approach does have advantages.  For
example, the strict seperation of data and code that results
makes it easy to use traditional databases that are designed
for such programs.  Because a minimal programming language is
used, it would appear that less skill -- or at least different
skills -- would be required from programmers.

> "For many applications -- say, a traditional
> sequential database update program -- [ignoring classes]
> is quite reasonable, and the traditional techniques
> developed over decades are adequate for the job."

However, suppose the application differs sufficiently from
traditional sequential processing of records (or characters) or
the complexity involved is higher -- say, in an interactive
CASE system.  The lack of language support for data abstraction
implied by the decision to ignore classes will hurt.  The
inherent complexity will show up in the application somewhere,
and if the system is implemented in an impoverished language,
the code will not reflect the design directly.  The program
will have too many lines of source code, lack type checking,
and will in general not be amenable to tools.  This is the
prescription for a maintenance nightmare.

A common band-aid for this problem is to build specific tools
to support the notions of the design method.  These tools then
provide higher-level constructs and checking to compensate for
deficiencies of the (deliberately impoverished) implementation
langugage.  Thus, the design method becomes a special-purpose
and typically corporate-owned programming language.  Such
programming languages are in most contexts poor substitutes for
a widely available, general-purpose programming language
supported by suitable design tools.

The most common reason for ignoring classes in design is simple
inertia.  Traditional programming languages don't support the
notion of a class, and traditional desgin techniques reflect
this deficiency.  The most common focus of design has been the
decomposition of the problems into a set of procedures
performing required actions.  This notion, called procedural
programming in Chapter 2, is in the context of design often
called 'functional decomposition.'  A common question is, "Can
we use C++ together with a design method based on functional
decomposition?"  You can, but you will most likely end up using
C++ as simply a better C and will suffer the problems mentioned
previously.  This may be acceptable in a transition period, for
already completed designs, and for subsystems in which classes
do not appear to offer siginficant benefits (give the
experience of the individuals involved at this time).  For the
longer term and in general, however, the policy against
large-scale use of classes implied by functional decomposition
is not compatible with effective use of C++ or any other
language that has support for abstraction.

So why prefer "object-oriented design" over the traditional
design methods based on functional decomposition?  A
first-order answer is that functional decomposition leads to
insufficient data abstraction.  From this, it follows that the
resulting design is:

    -- less resilient to change,
    -- less amenable to tools,
    -- less suited for parallel development, and
    -- less suited for concurrent execution.

The problem is that functional decomposition causes interesting
data to become global because when a system is structured as a
tree of functions, any data accessed by two functions must be
global to both.  This ensures that "interesting" data bubbles
up toward the root of the tree as more and more functions
require access to it (as ever in computing, trees grow from the
root down).  Exactly the same process can be seen in
single-rooted class hierarchies, in which "interesting" data
and functions tend to bubble up toward a root class (�24.4).
Focussing on the specification of classes and the encapsulation
of data addresses this problem by making the dependencies
between different parts of a program explicit and tractable.
More important, though, it reduces the number of dependencies
in a system by improving locality of reference to data.

However,

> "... some problems are best solved by writing
> a set of procedures. The point of an 'object-oriented'
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> reflect the concepts of the application."

Typically, that is best done when classes, not functions, are
the primary focus on the design effort.  The use of a
procedural style should be a conscious decision and not simply
a default.  Both classes and procedures should be used
appropriately relative to the application and not just as
artifacts of an inflexible design method.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-11T13:58:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uld8vjk2q23jbe@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <aT259.5644$Ke2.462878@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <ulbn1obut2p2cd@corp.supernews.com> <aj5ame02kk5@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```

Grinder <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote in message
news:aj5ame02kk5@enews1.newsguy.com...
> > I believe that OO is not a bunch of hooey; only that it does
> > not fit everywhere. Read section 24.2.1 "Ignoring Classes"
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> This is the apparent source of much of this debate.

I previously listed, as "practical" uses: simulation, modelling,
word processing, speadsheets, and computer-aided
design. I explained that I see such applications as
well-suited to object-orientation.

The "source of much of this debate" is the differing opinions
of what constitutes object-oriented programming. I feel
that object-oriented programming is defined by its
principles and practices; not the mere use of language
features. This stems from acknowledgement by Stroustrup
and many others that object-oriented programming can be
done in non-OO languages.

There is an implication in paragraph 1 that a C++
program is not object-oriented if the the program is
roughly equivalent to a C program that is not derived from
object-oriented design. The also applies to COBOL.
In my opinion, placing COBOL records in a class with
some methods is not enough to be object-oriented
programming. I see it as modular programming and have
previously said that I like the use of OO COBOL features
for modular programming. Furthermore, it is possible to
write non-OO applications while using language features
that support object-oriented programming (see para 4).
Ultimately, whether one is doing object-oriented
programming depends upon the design of the application.

Paragraph 1 also has something interesting about
COBOL; that is, COBOL is a model language for record
processing applications. C and C++ languages, when
used for record processing, use a subset of C and COBOL.
As COBOL programmers, we are not limited to that subset;
hence, COBOL for record processing applications is not a
"minimal programming language" nor is it "impoverished."

In my opinion, the use of "sequential" in paragraphs
1 and 2, does not apply to the full set of features available
in COBOL and should include traditional "non-sequential"
record processing, as well.

In the thread "Network database and OO," I was attempting
to show that primary, alternate, and foreign keys are
"relation objects" and are separable from the data that
is related to other objects through that relation. Interesting
methods for relation objects are "setKey" and "getData."
These methods are equivalent to MOVE and READ.
In the end, we can do a lot of object-oriented programming,
in COBOL, that has no advantage in the "real world" of
business data processing.
```

```
You missed a very big point Rick. Object Oriented Programming is not always a good solution, no argument there.

However, there are no problems where OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN is *not* a good solution. None of the situation you detailed below even
begin to contradict that.

The difference between understanding Object Oriented Design and Object Oriented Programming usually is the difference between a
Programmer and a Software Engineer. And yep, most S.E.s can program, but not all Programmers can handle the scope of the tasks a
Software Engineer does.


-Paul
```

```
Paul Raulerson <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote in message
news:jgy59.74966$Yd.3414498@twister.austin.rr.com...
> You missed a very big point Rick. Object Oriented Programming is not
always a good solution, no argument there.
>
> However, there are no problems where OBJECT ORIENTED DESIGN is *not* a
good solution. None of the situation you detailed below even
> begin to contradict that.

I had no desire nor any reason to contradict it. It is
reasonable (and in many cases desireable) to use
OOA and OOD as part of creating non-OO
applications. That is because using OOA and OOD
gives a different, and often better, view of the
application than SA and SD.

What I said later was, "Ultimately, whether one is
doing object-oriented programming depends upon
the design of the application."

If the design of the application, using OOD, results
in record sets suitable for traditional record processing,
the application will not use OOP, even when the language
is an OOPL.

If the design results in classes derived from data
abstractions and methods, with composition by
inheritance and aggregation, the application will use OOP,
even if the language is not an OOPL. (Without OO
support, in the language, inheritance is simulated.)

> The difference between understanding Object Oriented Design and Object
Oriented Programming usually is the difference between a
> Programmer and a Software Engineer. And yep, most S.E.s can program, but
not all Programmers can handle the scope of the tasks a
> Software Engineer does.
```

```
Grinder <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote in message
news:aj6gf8015gc@enews4.newsguy.com...
> Grinder previously wrote:
> [snip]
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> at least the ones you're designing.

I talked about practical uses for OO in Payroll more than
two years ago. In many COBOL records there are fields
that hold coded values and fields that are redefined. The
field, holding a code, may be seen as representing an
abstract class and the coded values may be seen as
type-ids for actual classes. The redefined area may be
seen as a data abstraction and the redefinitions as
"object" data. I also discussed this more than two years
ago. Notice that, at the record level, each of these is
composition by aggregation while the area reserved for
the data abstraction is completed through composition
by inheritance.

> > The "source of much of this debate" is the differing opinions
> > of what constitutes object-oriented programming. I feel
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> document parser could be written as a COBOL++ precompiler?

Or by redefinition and EVALUATE.

> > In the end, we can do a lot of object-oriented programming,
> > in COBOL, that has no advantage in the "real world" of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> prerequisite for object-oriented programming.)

I can't give a percentage because I don't have enough
information.

I have looked at full-blown OOD, in the form of UML.
I don't go that far. What I have used are simple graphic
designs to shown where relationships exist; but I do not
enumerate all object data and methods.

OOD is not a prerequisite for OOP, where XP is used.
```

```
if the language you are coding in does not have explicit support for them.

The book "Object Orientation An Introduction for COBOL Programmers" by
Raymond Obin, chapter 4 "A COBOL Implementation" shows how you can use
copybooks and the CALL statement to take a step toward the OO approach.  I
found the example really helpful when I was first trying to learn OO
concepts.  It was sort of a roll your own approach.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I am not enthusiatic about OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-11T13:37:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208111237.3ddfb4c1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ujnff1lh53f23f@corp.supernews.com> <aT259.5644$Ke2.462878@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <ulbn1obut2p2cd@corp.supernews.com> <aj5ame02kk5@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
"Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote 

> Consider design that ignores classes.  The resulting C++
> program will be roughly equivalent to the C program that would
> have result from the same design process -- and this program
> would again be roughly equivalent to the COBOL program that
> would have resulted from the same design process.  

Well, no.

The resulting C program would not be like a Cobol program because of
the limitations of C.  For example in Cobol all variables are handled
the same way: MOVE, ADD, COMPUTE can work on any mix of decimal, comp,
fixed and floating point.  MOVE is also used for records, groups and
elementary items.  Files are handled the same way regardless of
whether there are line sequential or indexed.

In C the operators can only work on the built-in types. To have a
decimal fixed point would require that there be a set of routines
created to handle these with functions to add, subtract, etc, and a
different routine for each different set of parameters. Another type
(different fixed point length) would require yet another set of
routines of different name.

Files are either handled by one set of system calls or another set of
stdio functions or by add-in sets of routines.

Along comes C++ to solve all _these_ problems in C.  Now one set of
names can be overloaded to handle any of these problems.  The
operators can be overloaded to present the appearance of these
invented types being handled alongside built-in types and files can be
handled in a consistent way using the same function names regardless
of the underlying mechanism.

At a higher level these OO techniques can also be useful, but the
primary reason for C++ is to solve the problems of C, problems that
Cobol never had.
```

```
>
> > Consider design that ignores classes.  The resulting C++
> > program will be roughly equivalent to the C program that
would
> > have result from the same design process -- and this
program
> > would again be roughly equivalent to the COBOL program that
> > would have resulted from the same design process.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The resulting C program would not be like a Cobol program
because of
> the limitations of C.  For example in Cobol all variables are
handled
> the same way: MOVE, ADD, COMPUTE can work on any mix of
decimal, comp,
> fixed and floating point.  MOVE is also used for records,
groups and
> elementary items.  Files are handled the same way regardless
of
> whether there are line sequential or indexed.
>
> In C the operators can only work on the built-in types. To
have a
> decimal fixed point would require that there be a set of
routines
> created to handle these with functions to add, subtract, etc,
and a
> different routine for each different set of parameters.
Another type
> (different fixed point length) would require yet another set
of
> routines of different name.
>
> Files are either handled by one set of system calls or
another set of
> stdio functions or by add-in sets of routines.
>
> Along comes C++ to solve all _these_ problems in C.  Now one
set of
> names can be overloaded to handle any of these problems.  The
> operators can be overloaded to present the appearance of
these
> invented types being handled alongside built-in types and
files can be
> handled in a consistent way using the same function names
regardless
> of the underlying mechanism.

Just so there's no misunderstanding -- I didn't say this, it's
a quote from Bjarne Stroustrup.


As he proceeds, though, you can see that he lumps C and COBOL
together because of their "separation of data and code."  C and
COBOL are both contrasted against the data + code mentality of
class abstractions.

> At a higher level these OO techniques can also be useful, but
the
> primary reason for C++ is to solve the problems of C,
problems that
> Cobol never had.

Perhaps for some, though I admit I've not heard this before.
For others, strong types are a beneficial constraint, not a
limitation.
```

## Thread: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: I need help with reading multiple records off one data file

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T03:41:20-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D54C3A0.40506@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<7d57a35f.0207280252.3bb96ddc@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0207281206.5f56a0ae@posting.google.com> <93dfc7c7.0208040513.2bab448f@posting.google.com> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.h0fma90.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <217e491a.0208061130.60692e91@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com> wrote > 
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> operation but there is a valid duplicate alternate key.

I wouldn't test for only '00' after a READ. I'd define an 88 for use 
after READs which includes the '02' value, as well as any others which 
also represent valid READs with some special attribute.

If you don't like 88's, you can code an EVALUATE which treats '00' & 
'02' as valid READS, e.g.:

EVALUATE 
	file-status
WHEN 
		ZEROS
WHEN 
		'02'
WHEN 
		<any others>
	continue
WHEN 
		OTHER
	<handle the error here>
END-EVALUATE
```

## Thread: COMP item.. — 5 msgs

### COMP item..

- **From:** nithi_rajagopal@rediffmail.com (Nithya Nandan Rajagopal)
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T01:53:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fb8f6b1a.0208100053.1461debd@posting.google.com>`

```
1.Memory storage for a comp  data item: 
Can any one explain in detail about the storage & maximum number of
digits it can store in the following cases.(platform:AS/400,
COBOL/400)
Please give examples also.
for Comp,Comp-1,Comp-2,Comp-3,Comp-4 items. 
In comp it can store either one word or half word.in that case how
many digits it can store at the maximum???
for declaring half word binary number,we specify 
  01 var1 pic s9(4) usage is comp.
for declaring a full word binary no.,
  01 var2 pic s9(8) usage is comp.
since comp can occupy only half/full word,at the most we can specify
s9(8)..is it correct..

But, if i specify s9(18) it takes & i can move the value
to that variable..how is it possible???
 

thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP item..

- **From:** kamur <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T20:46:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d55c1da$1_9@news5.nntpserver.com>`
- **References:** `<fb8f6b1a.0208100053.1461debd@posting.google.com>`

```
Not sure of As/400 , but here's what's byte allocations in OS/390/MVS:

1) S9(3) COMP-3 = 2 bytes
2) S9(5)V99 COMP-3 = 4 bytes
3) S9(4) BINARY = 2 bytes
4) S9(8) BINARY = 4 bytes
5) S9(6) = 6 bytes

Hope this helps.. Kumar
```

## Thread: compiler coming with a book — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: compiler coming with a book

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T11:25:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oK659.1365$yt3.413967@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<5SZ39.8435$Xf7.823073@news20.bellglobal.com> <217e491a.0208070016.3d2c2bae@posting.google.com> <DJt49.9572$HH2.1329823@news20.bellglobal.com> <3D535D66.AB888777@Azonic.co.nz> <3D52C6F1.5B83892B@shaw.ca> <aXD49.7434$2k.553225@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3D5366B0.B431D192@worldnet.att.net> <7f64e2ff.0208090730.1aee864b@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0208091615.6a6904dd@posting.google.com> <2XZ49.24923$Z7.3476371@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:2XZ49.24923$Z7.3476371@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com...
>
> Based on performance tests I have done between MF COBOL and C,
> I would be surprised if there is more than a small amount of interpreted
> code in a MF COBOL generated EXE....

While your statement is - rightly - careful to qualify you were testing for
the amount of 'interpreted'  vs. 'machine' code in the two compilers'
outputs, I am generally quite chary about trying to compare compilers for
*performance* when the two compilers' use different source languages. As
building the software which runs these comparisons is subjective, based on
the skill of the author, you never end up comparing apples for apples in
these cases.

That is, I hope no one infers that one source language may be generally
considered 'superior' to another for performance, generated code size or
other measure, as there is no such thing as a fair test.

Comparing "brand-X COBOL" to "brand-Y COBOL"  or "brand-X C" to "brand-Y C",
otoh, is fair.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: compiler coming with a book

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T08:24:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tr859.27506$lP6.1749734@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<5SZ39.8435$Xf7.823073@news20.bellglobal.com> <217e491a.0208070016.3d2c2bae@posting.google.com> <DJt49.9572$HH2.1329823@news20.bellglobal.com> <3D535D66.AB888777@Azonic.co.nz> <3D52C6F1.5B83892B@shaw.ca> <aXD49.7434$2k.553225@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3D5366B0.B431D192@worldnet.att.net> <7f64e2ff.0208090730.1aee864b@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0208091615.6a6904dd@posting.google.com> <2XZ49.24923$Z7.3476371@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <oK659.1365$yt3.413967@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I agree, Michael.  Certainly, languages should not be judged on such a
narrow example.  But my purpose was to see how efficient the MF
COBOL generated code was, using a known efficient C as a benchmark.

What I did was to code a very CPU intensive algorithm, the Sieve of
Erasthones (prime number generator, for those not familiar), in both
languages, using logic as close to identical as possible.  The MF
COBOL program was somewhat slower, but by less than one order
of magnitude (around 2:1 to 4:1, IIRC).  Knowing the C compiler as
well as I do, for such a compute intensive algorithm, it seems a bit
unlikely that a MF Virtual Machine would be quite that efficient.  If
it is, I applaud them.

If anyone is interested, I'm looking for the COBOL program.  Haven't
found it yet, but it may be on a backup.  Can't understand where it went,
I'm a packrat for such things, and keep them pretty well organized.  You
can download the C program (primes.zip) from my website, though
that program is dressed up a bit from the benchmark version.  It can
generate every prime number < 999,999,999 in a few seconds.
```

## Thread: Testing a date age by month — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Re: Testing a date age by month

- **From:** Doug Scott <dwscott@ieee.org>
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T12:56:12+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VA.00000240.0076164e@ieee.org>`
- **References:** `<20020808131830.17610.qmail@nym.alias.net> <3D52A292.6C8A4E8F@shaw.ca> <aiu93r$2u3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3D52B4AC.F2644D6E@shaw.ca> <VA.0000023b.03646ff3@ieee.org> <aiumjl$nmg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <UfO49.20069$Z7.1112060@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <VA.0000023f.08e18691@ieee.org> <dEZ49.24698$Z7.3471506@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```
Judson,

> Why can we 'assume' that?  Have we seen the system design specs?

We have been given a spec. A reasonable person would say that if the 
timescale was six months, then five or seven months would be tolerable. You 
could always ask.

> The programmer who posted the original question may not have realized
> that it does matter, and my post may have alerted him to the possibility.

This particular application - that of file retention times - is pretty 
flexible usually, but we don't know if it's a tax-sensitive application (for 
instance). If it were, then the writer would surely have included DD in his 
calculations. Since he didn't, there is an implicit statement (actually it's 
explicit - the code is there for all to see) that days don't matter.

> Either way, the information could be useful to others reading these posts.

This information is, surely, but not for this application. We can flog 
ourselves to death providing systems that exceed the spec by an order of 
magnitude with no payback at all. You didn't ask about hours and minutes, 
for instance, so you've drawn your own conclusins about the precision 
required.

---

Doug

dwscott@ieee.org
```

#### ↳ Re: Testing a date age by month

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Testing a date age by month

- **From:** Doug Scott <dwscott@ieee.org>
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T16:33:26+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VA.00000243.013cf783@ieee.org>`
- **References:** `<20020808131830.17610.qmail@nym.alias.net> <3D52A292.6C8A4E8F@shaw.ca> <aiu93r$2u3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3D52B4AC.F2644D6E@shaw.ca> <VA.0000023b.03646ff3@ieee.org> <aiumjl$nmg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <UfO49.20069$Z7.1112060@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <VA.0000023f.08e18691@ieee.org> <dEZ49.24698$Z7.3471506@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <VA.00000240.0076164e@ieee.org> <S%759.27299$lP6.1740397@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
Judson,

> I showed him a "better way."  He may not need to use it, or
> may not want to use it.  But why in the world does it bug you that I
> gave it?

Well.

I'm not bugged that you gave it at all. You seem to be bugged that I 
criticised your offer, which is I guess a natural reaction. The 
programming world is full of sensitivities like this.

My point which you seem to ignore is that an overspecification of a 
solution can cause increased complication while implementing the 
solution. It can take longer and be more difficult to test IN GENERAL. So 
it's always worth while to review what you're delivering so as to ensure 
that you're not delivering a Rolls Royce solution for a Ford problem. 

---

Doug

dwscott@ieee.org
```

```
"Doug Scott" <dwscott@ieee.org> wrote:
>
> My point which you seem to ignore is that an overspecification of a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> that you're not delivering a Rolls Royce solution for a Ford problem.


I'm not ignoring your point, I'm saying it is fallacious, and obviously so
(to everyone apparently, but yourself).  I'm not "sensitive", in that I am
happy to receive valid criticism, and quick to acknowledge a true mistake.
What bugs me is when somebody criticizes a post, mine or anyone else's,
from an indefensible point of view, such as you have done.  You act as if
offering a more detailed method somehow needs to be 'corrected'.  Your
paragraph above has to be one of the most contrived explanations I have
seen in a long time.  In other words "Don't tell them anything they don't
*need* to know - knowledge can be dangerous."  I wasn't trying to force
the guy to implement my suggestion, for heaven's sake, I was only offering
information (which was entirely correct, by the way).  Wow, you are a
"Solutions Nazi!"  Are you going to suggest book burning next, because
someone might be confronted with an algorithm more powerful than
absolutely necessary, and find themselves powerless to resist using it,
thereby wasting enormous amounts of human and computer resources?

Hint: Offering more complete information is *not* a bad thing, at least
for everyone but yourself.  Were you frightened by excessive information
when you were a child?  ;-)
```

<a id="t-72c3ef158d"></a>
## Thread: Testing a date age by month — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Re: Testing a date age by month

- **From:** Doug Scott <dwscott@ieee.org>
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T12:56:13+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VA.00000241.00761a91@ieee.org>`
- **References:** `<20020808131830.17610.qmail@nym.alias.net> <aiumjl$nmg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <UfO49.20069$Z7.1112060@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <VA.0000023f.08e18691@ieee.org> <aj1s6e$r4c$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
> Of course... it is so simple, so clear!  While you're at it you might 
> consider assuming there's no such thing as a user who will whine 'I know 
> it's what I *told* you... but it's not what I *want*!'..

Bullshit. I've been around the block more than a few times, and this is 
typical of the reasons why projects overrun. 

Let's re-state the problem. A programmers (not a user) wants a replacement 
for his code. I have provided an equivalent replacement. Not more precise, 
nor less. IF he had a problem with precision, I'd expect a programmer to 
state that in his statement of requirements.

In the case of users, you MUST know the users and you must know his 
application. And yes, if he says six months I'd have made it accurate to 
within the run cycle of the system (if it runs monthly, then we're arguing 
about nothing). An online system needs timestamping to within the 
resolution of the hardware clock (and even CICS hit a problem with that as 
machines got faster). That's part of the fundamental design of the system. 
In this case, it's not required and not dealt with.

---

Doug

dwscott@ieee.org
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Testing a date age by month

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Doug Scott <dwscott@ieee.org>
- **Date:** 2002-08-11T20:24:31+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VA.00000247.011d22af@ieee.org>`
- **References:** `<20020808131830.17610.qmail@nym.alias.net> <VA.00000241.00761a91@ieee.org> <aj30nd$f26$1@panix1.panix.com> <VA.00000244.013cfb59@ieee.org> <aj4bvn$9i9$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
> Now... care to address what was presented or would you rather snip and 
> run?

OK. I did explain my position, but you read right past it. One last try.

I know who you are. You've got around three to five years' experience in 
the industry, and you're pretty good at your job. And you're good because 
you care enough to research such arcane stuff as date routines and care 
about them being right. Would I hire you? Yes I would (but you'd probably 
refuse through injured pride).

I'm approaching forty years in the job, and I can say that, in the next 
three to five years, you're going to go through a Revelation that forces 
you to discover that, for all your knowledge and enthusiasm, the IT world 
is bigger than you know. When that happens, you'll be onto the web, 
surfing all sorts of sites trying to learn all you need to know about 
your new environment (and you'll do it because you're good and you care) 
and if you're good enough, you'll establish a new reputation as a 
designer/project manager (whatever opportunity presents itself).

And if you're good enough, that will continue for another eight or so 
years. You'll be an experienced project manager/ lead designer or 
whatever, but you'll be bothered by people not doing what you think is 
essential to see your project/your design go in easily - because of what 
you think is their own stupidity. But you can't do everything, so you 
have to live with it.

After eight years of that, you may find yourself as Programme Manager. 
This is tough, because you've got layers of people underneath you who 
won't do what you want. But with a sense of humility (which you'll have 
developed by now) you might be able to persuade them to follow your 
vision of how a system should evolve.

And sometime during that period, you'll be screwed by some programmer 
deciding to take your specs and expand it beyond all recognition, 
creating his own masterpiece. It takes longer, and is difficult to debug, 
difficult to commission, and costly to maintain. And these are your 
problems, not his - he thought he was doing the Right Thing.

As his manager, you need to be able to tell him in the fullness of time 
that he screwed your project because that little piece of code has caused 
overruns because everyone else was waiting for it, despite the hours of 
overtime he did and the nervous breakdown he suffered.

And then you'll decide that it's better engineering to build something 
tailored to the requirement, rather than complicate things beyond what 
was needed.

Has this happened to me? Not in such terms - my problem has always been 
finding people good enough to build the designs I've done. But I've been 
there when I was a junior - I've built routines that you wouldn't 
believe, then been faced with maintaining them long after I should have 
moved off the job onto other work. And in the end, following some 
Revelation, I rewrote them to be more mundane and merely meet the 
requirement established by the system.

So. Build away. It's not my project, and there's nothing wrong with 
knowing more than you code. But do no more than is necessary, because 
added complication will come back to bite you.

---

Doug

dwscott@ieee.org
```

<a id="t-ea6dd28af4"></a>
## Thread: Power Cobol Using Janus Dbgrid — 1 msgs

### Re: Power Cobol Using Janus Dbgrid

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T06:01:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0208100501.2e64c0f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3D528AE5.6E7D6D76@attglobal.net>`

```
I do have some experience with this control using Fujtisu COBOL with
COBOL sp2 from Flexus and their ActiveX support.  I don't know how
PowerCOBOL handles events, and populating the grid is all tied up in
the events.  With this control when you do not have an associated
database, you need to setup the grid - and then wait for the
"UnboundReadData" event.  BEFORE you return from that event you have
to do your set's for the values for the grid.  The cell needing
population will be passed by the event.  The setting of the values
requires using the object reference returned by the event and is only
valid within the context of the event - once you return from the
event, using that object reference that was returned is invalid.
```

## Thread: Testing a date age by month — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Re: Testing a date age by month

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T06:11:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0208100511.653323d7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20020808131830.17610.qmail@nym.alias.net>`

```
Well.... I have done this an entirely different way.  For example - I
needed to know how OLD someone was.  "Have they reached their 20th
birthday" or not.  This really is related.  The logic I used could
easily be translated to months.

I took the days between their dob and "today".  

Then I converted THAT number to a gregorian date using the function
"integer-to-date".  If someone was 20 - I got a year of 1621.  This
accounts for all the leapdays and such -- FAIRLY CLOSELY, but not
perfectly (because of the 400 year rule).  This could be used to see
how many "months" old data is.

…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
```

### Re: compiler coming with a book

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T13:48:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208101248.7f49942f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5SZ39.8435$Xf7.823073@news20.bellglobal.com> <217e491a.0208070016.3d2c2bae@posting.google.com> <DJt49.9572$HH2.1329823@news20.bellglobal.com> <3D535D66.AB888777@Azonic.co.nz> <3D52C6F1.5B83892B@shaw.ca> <aXD49.7434$2k.553225@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3D5366B0.B431D192@worldnet.att.net> <7f64e2ff.0208090730.1aee864b@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0208091615.6a6904dd@posting.google.com> <2XZ49.24923$Z7.3476371@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote 

> > MF Cobol can be compiled to intermediate code, even if this is buried
> > within a Windows .EXE or .DLL.  RM Cobol and Accu Cobol also use a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> code in a MF COBOL generated EXE.  

You should note that I said 'can' not 'is'.

MF Cobol can output files as .int, .gnt or .obj/.exe (depending on
version and platform).  When outputting .obj/.exe the content _can_ be
intermediate code by specifying OPT"0".

Using .int/.gnt gives portability to different machines and operating
systems.  Using .exe enforces DOS/Windows but intermediate code, while
being rather slower, is much more compact.  For interactive systems
CPU performance is usually not an issue in most of the code.

> I haven't tried this in Net Express, but I used to write some small utility
> programs using MF COBOL 3.2 (16 bit) instead of Microsoft C (16 bit)
> or QuickBasic, because the I/O performance on line sequential files in
> MF COBOL blew away QuickBasic and MS C, even when using large
> (64k) I/O buffers.  Never knew why.  Anybody know that? :-)

I have found MF Cobol line sequential files to be very slow compared
to the equivalent in other languages, but then I don't use MSC or MS
BASICs.
```

<a id="t-7900e0eeeb"></a>
## Thread: OO Exception Handling — 35 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### OO Exception Handling

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-10T22:10:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D5590B9.B38DF038@shaw.ca>`

```
Donald and Grinder,

Thought we should make this a specific thread. Don, wasn't clear where
you were describing EXIT METHOD to Grinder - so did the following to
clarify that which I believed to happen, in my own mind.

EXIT METHOD is an implied instruction prior to the line 'End Method' if
not explicitly included. Much of the code we see as examples, has as a
'pair',  'Exit Method' and 'End Method" - but in fact the 'Exit Method',
most of the time, is superfluous and can create the condition you see in
Method 4 Para-4A below, (assuming you are using paragraphs). Plus it's
also part of the category 'red tape', if you want to keep the coding
short and clear.

It does have ONE advantage, and I'm guessing this also applies to
Fujitsu. You can't PAUSE the Animator on 'End Method', but you can pause
on 'Exit Method' if it is the preceding line. As the final 'instruction'
line of a method the pause then allows you to query any variables or
objects that you are suspicious about in the Method.

Hopefully following shows all possible permutations - any comments ?

The GOOD NEWS. Bill may recall that I wrote a while back that using
'EXIT PROGRAM' just didn't do anything for me. God knows what I did, and
haven't got the code, but it led me down the wrong path.

EXIT PROGRAM or GOBACK will do EXACTLY the same as Procedural as you can
see in Method 4 below. You'll see I've deliberately commented out the
explict EXIT METHOD in Method 4, to prove it gets back to the 'caller'
DIRECTLY. (Obviously if Paragraph-4A didn't indicate some sort of error,
then Method 4 would function exactly like Method 3).

That's a big step forward in my mind. So if I go back to my original
example of nested Classes and Methods - Method3B WILL  (using GOBACK)
cause an exit from Class 3 - and we are back in Class 2 Method 2C.

With that concept firmly entrenched - now I put on the thinking cap, and
perhaps using the existing Net Express Exception Class, containing
callbacks, exception objects etc. - I can start to figure a way to tell
Class 2, 'You should be doing an automatic  GOBACK to Class 1". (????).

The ideal is to make any Exception Handling 'dual' feature, (and is
available within Net Express). Currently we can't cover fatal errors, (
messages to wrong objects, a field contains non-numerics etc.), and the
Runtime kicks in with a Messgebox and STOPS RUN. However if Exceptions
can be made to operate in two ways :-

1- 'semi-failure' - can't provide records for a list, missing info
invoking other languages etc., then we can signal via a GOBACK 'Get out
of this sub-suite' and let the user select one of the other 19 items
from the Master Menu that we know still work. (Could also be applied to
ISAM crashed indices.)

2- 'success/failure' - when reading a File/Table - did we establish
RecordFound/RecordNotFound - this info needs to be fed back to the
invoking class/method.

BTW - Do you have an Exception Handler in Fujitsu ?

Jimmy

*>----------------------------------------------------------
Program-id. excptn01.
Class-control.              Program02 is class "excptn02"
Procedure Division.
invoke Program02 "begin"
STOP RUN.
*>----------------------------------------------------------
*>-------------------excptn02.cbl-----------------------------

Class-id.        Exception02
                 inherits from Base.

Class-control.   Exception02 is class "excptn02".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
FACTORY.
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "begin".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
*> This doesn't have an EXIT METHOD - it's implied

Procedure Division.
 invoke self "Method1"
 invoke self "Method2"
 invoke self "Method3"
 invoke self "Method4"
End Method "begin".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "Method1".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
*> This has no EXIT METHOD - it's implied

Procedure Division.
    display "This is Method1 Line 1A"
    display "This is Method1 Line 1B"
End Method "Method1".

*> This Method displays  :-
*> This is Method1 Line 1A
*> This is Method1 Line 1B
*> and then automatically Exits
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "Method2".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
*> This has an EXPLICIT EXIT after the 'mainline'

Procedure Division.
    perform PARA-2A
    perform PARA-2B
    .
EXIT METHOD.

PARA-2A.
display "This is Method2, Paragraph 2A".

PARA-2B.
display "This is Method2, Paragraph 2B".

End Method "Method2".

*> This Method displays and exits :-
*> This is Method2, Paragraph 2A
*> This is Method2, Paragraph 2B

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "Method3".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
*> Note the EXIT METHOD, immediately prior to "End Method"

Procedure Division.
    perform PARA-3B *> I've switched the perform paragraphs
    perform PARA-3A
    .
PARA-3A.
display "This is Method3, Paragraph 1".

PARA-3B.
display "This is Method3, Paragraph 3B".
display "I don't do Method3 Paragraph 3A"

EXIT METHOD.
End Method "Method3".

*> This Method displays and exits :-
*> This is Method3, Paragraph 3B
*> I don't do Method3 Paragraph 3A

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "Method4".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
*> Testing for EXIT PROGRAM or GOBACK, but not using Exception
*> Handling techniques. Also note the EXIT PROGRAM/GOBACK works,
*> even without an explicit EXIT METHOD

Procedure Division.
    perform PARA-4A
    perform PARA-4B
    .
*> EXIT METHOD. I've deliberately commented this out to check
*>                          the GOBACK

PARA-4A.
display "This is Method4, Paragraph 4A".
display "Becuase of GOBACK/EXIT - I don't do Method 4 Para 4B"
*>EXIT PROGRAM.
 GOBACK.

PARA-4B.
display "This is Method4, Paragraph 4B".

End Method "Method4".

*> This Method displays
*> This is Method4, Paragraph 4A
*> Becuase of GOBACK/EXIT - I don't do Method 4 Para 4B

*> Then using either EXIT PROGRAM/GOBACK, via the Animator,
*> I see it takes me back to STOP RUN in the trigger program

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
END FACTORY.
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT.         *> Don't need either
END OBJECT.     *> of these lines for this demo
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
End CLASS Exception02.
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: OO Exception Handling

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-13T22:43:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D598D16.9905DC31@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3D5590B9.B38DF038@shaw.ca>`

```


"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> Donald and Grinder,
>

This is a follow up to my previous. Recall I wrote EXIT PROGRAM/GOBACK
didn't do it for me in OO - I was right. It did in the methods I showed -
BUT not in the following sequence, where I've introduced a Method 5 :-

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "begin".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Procedure Division.
 invoke self "Method1"
 invoke self "Method2"
 invoke self "Method3"
 invoke self "Method4"
 invoke self "Method5"
End Method "begin".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "Method4".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Procedure Division.
    perform PARA-4A
    perform PARA-4B
    .
 EXIT METHOD.

PARA-4A.
display "This is Method4, Paragraph 4A".
display "Because of GOBACK/EXIT - I don't do Method 4 Para 4B"
GOBACK.

PARA-4B.
display "This is Method4, Paragraph 4B".

End Method "Method4".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "Method5".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Procedure Division.
    display "I'm in Method 5"
End Method "Method5".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------

I automatically get the display from Method 5, (GOBACK or no GOBACK) - oh
hum, back to the drawing board. That's been my problem all along; *every*
Method will action all statements until it drops through to End Method,
unless you are testing for some condition and explicity instruct it to EXIT
METHOD.

Jimmy
```

```
That's Step 2. Having initially dismissed my earlier assumption that
GOBACK wouldn't work (purely in OO Class syntax), and now confirmed it
doesn't work - now I'll move over to dabbling with the existing
Exception Handler. What hasn't yet twigged is that you can use features
to propagate up through a nested series of classes/methods, (and even
much more comprehensively in the draft Standard) - but I'm still looking
for the Nirvana, which avoids the necessity of individually checking
within each method, 'in the chain', with an automatic feature that takes
you straight back to Class 1 Method 1.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Exception Handling

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T10:25:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajgh5q020hg@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3D5590B9.B38DF038@shaw.ca> <3D598D16.9905DC31@shaw.ca> <ajd33502tvi@enews4.newsguy.com> <3D5B08B7.1446FEF5@shaw.ca> <KuE69.6405$2T2.1121915@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
> What is wrong with "EXIT METHOD", or "STOP RUN" if you want
an abort?

Can't speak for Jimmy, but both of these have problems
(conceptually) if they work as I think they do.

EXIT METHOD
This would just exit the current method -- wouldn't execution
resume at the next statement after the invocation of the exited
method?

STOP RUN
Does this stop the entire process?  Since there may be cleanup
to be done in the calling methods, they would be denied that
opportunity.  In short, the method does not have enough context
to decide if the error is fatal to the run.

I hope I properly understand the nature of these statements.
```

```
functionality.  This is an "auto-method" that gets  control as an object
instance is "going away".
```
