<a id="t-076b596364"></a>
## Thread: Microfocus Rebuild Utility, RT 9/007 Error, (Just FYI) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Microfocus Rebuild Utility, RT 9/007 Error, (Just FYI)

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-12T13:19:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0PS59.1103$g47.38214@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<e87297f1.0208082015.6ac8225@posting.google.com>`

```
Is this file very static, or is it updated more than seldom?  I would hesitate
to use standard MF Indexed Files for that much data unless it is not often
updated, or it isn't critical.  I would use something with a robust rollback
and recovery capability.  MF COBOL and Net Express can be configured
to use Btrieve as an Indexed File handler.  Then you can use transaction
processing, and Btrieve (Provantage SQL 2000 now, but still provides the
underlying Btrieve API to the engine) is bulletproof.  I wrote a substantial
(>100k LOC) multi-user client server inventory control system in 1986-87
using MF COBOL and Btrieve (DOS then, Win32 now) for a client.  This
system has more indexes per file than any I have ever done, typically 4 to
6, some more than 10 (with 1 to 8 fields per index)  The database is well
over 500MB, and we have never had to restore the database because of
data corruption, except when a drive bit the dust.

This was the last system I wrote (begun in 1986) using 2 digit years, so
I had to expand the dates for Y2K.  I wrote programs to copy the old data
files to flat files, then reload the flat files into the new date files.  Total
conversion time (several million records) was only about 2 hours on a
Dell Pentium II 550, 256MB.  Considering the number of indexes that had
to be populated, I thought Btrieve's performance was very good.
```

---

<a id="t-4aeb4407d4"></a>
## Thread: DB2 joins w/"standard" working-storage — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Re: DB2 joins w/"standard" working-storage

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <NoPersonalMailPlease@MyDot.Com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-12T21:40:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3XV59.9039$Ke2.776765@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<da3c2186.0208050512.8270455@posting.google.com> <20020805094706.00688.00001072@mb-df.aol.com> <da3c2186.0208060434.4b676b0f@posting.google.com>`

```

robert <gnuoytr@rcn.com> wrote in message
news:da3c2186.0208060434.4b676b0f@posting.google.com...

> the data IS coming from DB2.  but it is still in the VSAM structure; not
> a relational one.  they say the data can't be made relational because
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> in working-storage, maintained in copybooks.  that's the crux of it.

#1  Consider this data structure definition

      01  BOM-ASSY-COPYBOOK.
            05  BOM-ASSY-KEY.
                  10  BOM-ASSY-ID-CORP.
                        15  BOM-ASSY-ID-DIVISION                    PIC X
                        15  BOM-ASSY-ID-COMPANY                 PIC 9(05).
                  10  BOM-ASSY-CD-MFG                                 PIC
X(03).
                  10  BOM-ASSY-ID-PARTNO                           PIC
X(40).
            05  BOM-ASSY-DATA-AREA.
                  10  BOM-ASSY-QT-COMPONENT-COUNT PIC S9(03) COMP-3.
                  10  etc etc etc
            05  BOM-COMP-RECORD                                     OCCURS 1
TO 999 TIMES

DEPENDING UPON

BOM-ASSY-QT-COMPONENT-COUNT.
                 10  BOM-COMP-ID-CORP.
                        15  BOM-COMP-ID-DIVISION                  PIC X
                        15  BOM-COMP-ID-COMPANY                PIC 9(05).
                  10  BOM-COMP-CD-MFG                                PIC
X(03).
                  10  BOM-COMP-ID-PARTNO                          PIC X(40).
                  10  BOM-COMP-DATA-AREA.
                       15  etc etc etc

            The above example is offered strictly as an example of a way to
deal with variable length records
            in working-storage, maintained in copybooks.  Is this what you
are looking for?
            Perhaps the inclusion of the BOM-ASSY-QT-COMPONENT-COUNT control
field
            is the kicker which makes this come together for you?

#2  If "they" say that a Bill of Material database cannot be relational,
then "they" are wrong.
      You can guarantee it.  Bill of Material structures have a tendency of
being beyond the grasp
      of many otherwise highly competent people.  That, however, doesn't
make them "numbskulls".

#3  Regarding your question "the number of parts is not fixed.  (OR IS
IT???????)",
      I feel I need to differentiate clearly between parts and components
here.

      The number of parts on a part master is limited only by physical
storage limitations,
      e.g.  a 4GB dataset size limitation on an IMS database, to quote one
example.

      The number of components in a specific Bill of Material is a system
design issue.
      One company may only need the capability of having 9 or less, while
another company
      may need 9999 or less.

      So, you may have millions of parts on a part master database, and
hundreds of thousands
      of segments on a Bill of Materials database, yet only have a few
thousand unique part numbers
      on the Bill of Materials database.  In other words, the potential for
data redundancy is huge,
      as the same component part may appear as a component of thousands of
Assemblies and/or
      Sub-Assemblies.

I'd better quit now, before I write a book.  I hope I have been of some
help.

Feel free to ladle on the details if you want to discuss this further.
```

#### ↳ Re: DB2 joins w/"standard" working-storage

```
"Hugh Candlin" <NoPersonalMailPlease@MyDot.Com> wrote in message news:<3XV59.9039$Ke2.776765@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>...

<snip>

> #1  Consider this data structure definition
> 
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>             is the kicker which makes this come together for you?

except, how would it be derived at runtime?  count(*), perhaps.  not
sure
about that part.

> 
> #2  If "they" say that a Bill of Material database cannot be relational,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

it was something of a rhetorical exclamation:  i suspect that some
folks
who still think that KSDS are the height of technology don't realize
that
...
   05 widget01
   05 widget02
   .
   .
   05 widget10
...

is a REALLY bad idea.  but it is simplistic, and therefore appeals to
numbskulls; or at least inbred hillbilly cousins (technically
speaking,
of course) who haven't considered that the guy who first designed this
thing in 1975 just MIGHT have missed something (IMS, for example).  
denormalized data (before the relational model defined its opposite),
aka flat files, seem to be the only data these cousins understand.  it
could be just my burden here, but i'm beginning to suspect that it is
a generally COBOL mindset problem.  could be the semantics of the 
language require it.  don't know.  but it is really irritating.

anyway, my question boiled down to:  is there a simplistic way to
build a copybook structure that:

i) supports the downstream COBOL which is looking for that fixed
number
   of widgets in working-storage
and
ii)  supports getting the widget data from a non-fixed number of
joined
     widget rows.

i thought the answer was no, but not knowing enough about COBOL
syntax,
thought i'd ask.

> #3  Regarding your question "the number of parts is not fixed.  (OR IS
> IT???????)",
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> example.

well, as i alluded to above; this application is a Bill of Material 
problem, though the cousins don't see it.  and it was first built on
370 ~1975.  IMS was the proper technology.  but it cost more than the
COBOL complier and VSAM.  

> 
>       The number of components in a specific Bill of Material is a system
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>       may need 9999 or less.

in the relational (unix) systems i've worked on before this COBOL, Big
Iron stuff, the notion of having to specify the number of "things"
just
doesn't come up.  the number of lines on an order is nowhere specified
in the code.  join the order table to the order-line table and loop 
through them.  end of story.  a colleague at a previous job once
complained
about having to deal with COBOL static storage.  perhaps that's the
answer.

<snip>

toodles,
robert
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DB2 joins w/"standard" working-storage

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-08-13T13:03:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020813090316.17907.00000016@mb-da.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<da3c2186.0208130430.55fb201a@posting.google.com>`

```
robert writes ...

[big snip]

>in the relational (unix) systems i've worked on before this COBOL, Big
>Iron stuff, the notion of having to specify the number of "things" just
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>answer.

Well, I'm still at a loss to understand your problem. You declare a cursor that
specifies the join and loop through the result table. COBOL does that. It
doesn't require knowing in advance how big the result table will be.

This is not a Big Iron problem; it is not a COBOL problem; it is not a DB2 /
SQL problem.

I seem to recall the old code built a table of widgets, with a predteremined
[maximum] number of lines and the code looped through it. But then I got
confused when you said they converted to DB2 but really used VSAM. Now under
the covers, DB2 uses VSAM files to hold data and control information, but
that's not relevant to the application programmer. From an applications
perspective, you either use QSAM or VSAM to build your in-memory table and loop
through it, or you use DB2 and SQL; in the second case you define a structure
that describes one row from the result of the join, declare a cursor, loop
through the cursor one row at a time, populating your single row description
with each FETCH.

BOM or not, it shouldn't be that hard.

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DB2 joins w/"standard" working-storage

- **From:** gnuoytr@rcn.com (robert)
- **Date:** 2002-08-13T11:59:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<da3c2186.0208131059.53c8cf96@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<da3c2186.0208130430.55fb201a@posting.google.com> <20020813090316.17907.00000016@mb-da.aol.com>`

```
scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote in message news:<20020813090316.17907.00000016@mb-da.aol.com>...
<snip> 
> Well, I'm still at a loss to understand your problem. You declare a cursor that
> specifies the join and loop through the result table. COBOL does that. It
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> [maximum] number of lines and the code looped through it.

this is the problem:  the current working-storage (and the table/file
from which it is built) has one row/record with a fixed number of widget
columns/fields,  not lines.  call them part fields.  call the record an 
order record.  the current structure has 5 parts/order.  no more.  
not ever.

so, now i wish to introduce the concept of an order table joined to
an orderline table.  the 5 part fields go away.  these are no longer
defined in the order table.  the working-storage declaration for the 
order record can no longer be fixed at 5 parts.  parts are no longer
declared as part of an order record; they are orderline rows.  
could be 33. could be 3.  could be 99.  who knows.  

so, what i wondered is:  can these variable number of parts remain
declared in a working-storage declaration for an order??  others have
mentioned the DEPENDING UPON phrase as an alternative.  which would
work if: i) i'm willing to accept a maximum number of parts per 
order; ii) the DEPENDING UPON value can be determined at runtime.  this
is out of my ken.   

 But then I got
> confused when you said they converted to DB2 but really used VSAM. Now under
> the covers, DB2 uses VSAM files to hold data and control information, but
> that's not relevant to the application programmer. 

it shouldn't be, but these guys still talk about "primary index" and 
"alternative index"; and refuse to define any but the former.  they use
something called SPUFI to generate the table defs from the VSAM file
defs.  needless to say, this drives me up the wall. 

From an applications
> perspective, you either use QSAM or VSAM to build your in-memory table and loop
> through it, or you use DB2 and SQL; in the second case you define a structure
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> BOM or not, it shouldn't be that hard.

i don't think so either, but getting these guys to think relationally
(primary keys, foreign keys, referential integrity, etc) has been
a bit of a hair pull.  the existing paradigm remains File Oriented.

(has anyone successfully gotten such an application, and its
programmers, from VSAM to DB2?)

toodles, 
robert

> 
> Kind regards,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DB2 joins w/"standard" working-storage

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-08-13T19:24:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020813152410.00656.00000052@mb-cf.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<da3c2186.0208131059.53c8cf96@posting.google.com>`

```
robert writes ...

>scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote in message
>news:<20020813090316.17907.00000016@mb-da.aol.com>...
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

So, maybe, in a simplified form:

01 order-record.
    02 part-1 pic x(12).
    02 part-2 pic x(12).
    02 part-3 pic x(12).
    02 part-4 pic x(12).
    02 part-5 pic x(12).
    02 other-stuff pic x(500).



>so, now i wish to introduce the concept of an order table joined to
>an orderline table.  the 5 part fields go away.  these are no longer
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>could be 33. could be 3.  could be 99.  who knows.  


So you would get, perhaps,

01 order-part-record.
    02 part-no  pic x(12)
    02 other-stuff pic x(500).


>
>so, what i wondered is:  can these variable number of parts remain
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>is out of my ken.   

Well, you could have something like:

01 order-record.
    02 num-parts   pic s9(9) binary.
    02 parts occurs 1 to 10000 times depending on num-parts.
         03  part-no   pic x(12).
         03  other-stuff pic x(500).


Or, something like this:


01 order-record.
    02 num-parts   pic s9(9) binary.
    02  other-stuff pic x(500).
    02 parts occurs 1 to 10000 times depending on num-parts.
         03  part-no   pic x(12).

And, yes, the value in num-parts can be filled in dynamically for each query.

>> But then I got
>> confused when you said they converted to DB2 but really used VSAM. Now
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

SPUFI doesn't generate table defs from VSAM file defs. SPUFI generates COBOL
(or PL/I or C) storage structures in language-specific terms, given a DB2 table
name.

>> 
>> BOM or not, it shouldn't be that hard.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

primary key when they say primary index, well, that's a little irritating but
let it roll.

Frankly, part of the problem sounds like it resides in your lack of COBOL,
ISPF, and DB2 skills as well as their lack of relational concepts. It would
also help if you would stop calling them numskulls. [Even if you don't do it to
their face, don't you think they can sense your feeling of superiority and
arrogance? How does that help you win them over?]

Now I have a hammer (training), so every problem looks like a nail (training
need). I would be delighted to teach a series of courses at your shop on COBOL,
or ISPF, or DB2, or some combination of these topics with an eye to lay out the
concepts and vocabulary and to give some hands on experience to all
```

```
robert writes ...

>scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote in message
>news:<20020813152410.00656.00000052@mb-cf.aol.com>...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ah, well, good point. The simplest option is to build the table
programatically:
* define a cursor describing the join
* open the cursor
* if sqlcode=100, no rows, get done with this part
* otherwise use a loop to FETCH each row; as you
  look at each row you add one to num-parts and move in part-no(num-rows)
* when sqlcode=100, close the cursor
* now your code can process the COBOL table

There are other options, partly dependent on your application needs, partly
dependent on your version of DB2 (use WITH HOLD option in cursor declare;
OPTIMIZE FOR n ROWS; FETCH FIRST n ROWS ONLY; scrollable cursors)

[snip]

>> 
>> Frankly, part of the problem sounds like it resides in your lack of COBOL,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>> 

Sorry, I can't make anybody do anything. I have resigned my former post of
being in charge of the Universe.


>> Now I have a hammer (training), so every problem looks like a nail
>(training
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

above, for example) or DB2 or COBOL or ISPF, we'll do things the old,
inefficient way. A real money saver(!) Nice long range view of management.
```

```
I guess I'm still a bit lost on this thread but I have noticed that a join has
been referenced to several times.  I find this interesting as my group has just
spent the better part of a month removing most if not all joins from our DB2
programs to increase efficiency.  Theoretically we've saved the company about
$250,000 in mainframe fees.  Sounds a bit weird to me, but that's what they are
saying.

Any comments?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DB2 joins w/"standard" working-storage

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T12:40:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020815084005.08596.00000001@mb-da.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20020814214240.22319.00000474@mb-cl.aol.com>`

```
YukonMama (Eileen) writes ...

>I guess I'm still a bit lost on this thread but I have noticed that a join
>has
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any comments?

It's all about functionality. If a join can get you the information you need,
then the DB2 bind optimize process will almost certainly give you better
perfomance than you can do by programmatically simulating a join. But if you
were doing a lot of joins where simply adding a column to a table puts the info
you need into a single table, then single table access will be faster. That's a
database design issue.

Joins can be expensive, and if a naive programmer sets up an unneccessary join,
or a poorly structured join, then you can pay a high price in performance. So
it makes sense to visit those programs that use joins to see if the join: 1) is
necessary at all; 2) could be avoided by a small database redesign; 3) could be
coded more effectively. But to unilaterally say "Let's get rid of all joins"
seems a bit extreme.

Kind regards,



Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DB2 joins w/"standard" working-storage

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** gnuoytr@rcn.com (robert)
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T07:40:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<da3c2186.0208150640.14c69a8a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<da3c2186.0208141320.7f8a5b88@posting.google.com> <20020814214240.22319.00000474@mb-cl.aol.com>`

```
yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama) wrote in message news:<20020814214240.22319.00000474@mb-cl.aol.com>...
> I guess I'm still a bit lost on this thread but I have noticed that a join has
> been referenced to several times.  I find this interesting as my group has just
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any comments?

standard "wisdom" has been that VSAM -> DB2 in best case scenario
is 50% decrease in raw throughput (seen this number on DB2 forum).  
worst case can be a lot worse.  what a DataBase buys is flexibility,
not raw speed.  go faster:  get closer to the iron.  TPF is faster than
VSAM is faster than DB2.  on the other hand, rewriting the code (COBOL
i suppose) to dump the hand built concurrency and transaction and data
management (those pesky joins) will get you close to a wash.  leave that
code in, and get the data from DB2 in its previous VSAM form, you win 
the worst of both worlds:  DB2 protects the data, and so do your COBOL
routines.  low level tuning, so the gurus say, can get one back to
near VSAM throughput.  it's also worth remembering, at least with DB2,
that the physical files are still (somewhat altered) VSAM.  what seems
to be most signficant to me is:  lots of physical files, indexing has
to fit the VSAM paradigm.  other kinds of DB's that don't map tables to
physical files can store joined rows together.  don't think DB2 can.  

not to be catty, but lots of DBA's (alas) who grew up on file system
systems tend not to fully understand the power of keys and relational
structures in DataBase engines.  this seems more true in the MainFrame
world than Unix.

yes, gathering data from two physical files can be more work than reading
a record from contiguous space on one physical file.  now what do you
do with it?  if it's data from a file system file, you have to manage
concurrency in your code, or let some transaction processing monitor
do it.  when you write it out, the code has to make sure that nothing
mucky has happened.  if you want to delete data, same thing.  

anyway, it comes down to philosophy:  do you want a data-centric system
where an intelligent database engine does most, if not all, of the 
management of the data; or a code-centric system where the data is just
some lump on the file system which is managed by code (COBOL, for 
example).  with a data-centric system, any application can use the data
without mucking it up.  some code shops are threatened by this, and
contend that the "business rules" embedded in those millions of lines of 
code could never be replaced by primary keys, foreign keys, referential
integrity, triggers, and stored procedures.  "and all this code works
just fine on flat files, why do we have to change?"

Peter Dashwood (either on this forum, or DB2) has pointed out that the
file system is the Achilles heel of COBOL.  i suppose that's what this 
missive says, too.

toodles,
robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DB2 joins w/"standard" working-storage

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** James Johnson <jamesj55@spam-not.telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-16T21:01:54-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1e7rlu01iudkb2psojshvvmt8hkjm5hlv0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<da3c2186.0208141320.7f8a5b88@posting.google.com> <20020814214240.22319.00000474@mb-cl.aol.com>`

```
A few years back I worked for a gas/electric utility and were having to
deregulate the business and give customers the choice of different suppliers.
One program I wrote was to pass new customer info on to the appropriate supplier
and drop notifications back to us.  

The first production run with a pilot of just 50,000 accounts it took over 35
minutes to complete.  The performance monitor showed almost 90% of execution
time was in one paragraph that was just a select with a join.  Before the job
had completed I had drafted a change to get rid of the join and just do two
seperate selects.  

Made the change in time for the next nights run and it completed in less that 5
minutes.  Joins are nice but I learned that they can be quite resource
intensive.

JJ

On 15 Aug 2002 01:42:40 GMT, yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama) wrote:
```

- **References:** `<vvriju00g37bq8slba4sk0udlap5sfi4mu@4ax.com> <r64qju0sdbb6mgpe9296h98ascgl6vn3c1@4ax.com> <7mb%8.97429$88.1595090@twister.austin.rr.com> <esosjuce65pk2nk29lq6l25ufn1atvovc7@4ax.com> <ahlt7n$kv0$1@panix1.panix.com> <56W59.9057$Ke2.777659@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <72S3HDAqiSW9EwY6@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
It is true that every 01-level under an FD is an "implicit" redefines.  What
differs from compiler to compiler is whether they allow this (superfluous)
clause to be explicit.  Some do (as an extension) - most don't.
```

```
news:ahlt7n$kv0$1@panix1.panix.com...

> >?
> AFAIR every 01 level in an FD is an implicit redefines.

That doesn't negate my implication.  You can use multiple 01-levels in an
FD,
and they are indeed implicit redefines, but I cannot remember ever
being able to use the REDEFINES clause explicitly while defining them.

I could be wrong, as my standard procedure is to define a single FD
01-level,
and READ the file record or segment INTO a Working-Storage copybook
definition,
which contains any necessary REDEFINES (but not at the elementary-level).
```

<a id="t-bdb1693935"></a>
## Thread: Yippee! Fujitsu 6.1 works with Linux most popular RDBMS MySql!!!!! — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Yippee! Fujitsu 6.1 works with Linux most popular RDBMS MySql!!!!!

- **From:** maross@texoma.net (Mike)
- **Date:** 2002-08-12T21:02:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2763b81a.0208122002.ad61113@posting.google.com>`

```
Yippee! Thanks to Katie Parker (cobolnotdead@hotmail.com). The problem
clicked after Katie's response to Richard's inquiry in my previous
thread. I got Fujitsu 6.1 to work with the most popular Linux RDBMS
MySql http://www.mysql.com and this means it probably works with
PostgreSQL as well as any with ODBC. Fujitsu doesn't even realize this
according to their support pages at http://www.adtools.com. This truly
shows the importance of companies supporting user forums in addition
to service plans as expert users (like Katie) help tech support iron
out complaints of users. This means Fujitsu's COBOL for Linux should
enjoy functionality with the fastest growing OS and RDBMS upon launch
soon. My apologies for insulting FSC 6.1 drivers previously. The
problem was in my checking for a SQLSTATE = "00000" MySql returned
"s1c00" and the program went to exit verses reading a row. It is not
perfect, but at least there is communication.  I will post it here if
anyone wants to see how it worked? Forgive the awful "go to" it was
minimally modified from Fujitsu's examples till testing was passed.
   Mike

File is a 1 row(age) InnoDB table(testt) under DB(test) (row =
[name=age, type=int(2),] innodb. Data=1,3,5,7,17,27,12.
Using MySQL ODBC 3.51 System DSN with "Data Source Name:=TestDB"

  COBOL code/files
               cobol85.cbr (@ODBC_Inf=age.inf)

age.inf 
[default]
@SQL_DATASRC=testDB  (name of 
@SQL_USERID=myself
@SQL_PASSWORD=COHJLKHJEDANJIHPMLPHNK
@SQL_ACCESS_MODE=READ_ONLY
@SQL_COMMIT_MODE=MANUAL
@SQL_CONCURRENCY=READ_ONLY
@SQL_ODBC_CURSORS=USE_DRIVER
@SQL_QUERY_TIMEOUT=0

              [SQL_DEFAULT_INF]
@SQL_SERVER=default
@SQL_USERID=myself
@SQL_PASSWORD= COHJLKHJEDANJIHPMLPHNK

[CONNECTION_SCOPE]
@SQL_CONNECTION_SCOPE=THREAD

c. COBOL source code:
IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID.    row1.
 ENVIRONMENT    DIVISION.
 DATA           DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
  01 ageWK          PIC S9(2). *> COMP-5.

     EXEC SQL BEGIN DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC. 
 01  age          PIC s9(2).
 01  SQLSTATE     PIC X(5).
 01  SQLMSG       PIC X(128).
     EXEC SQL END DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
  P-START.
* CONNECT TO THE DATABASE ON THE DEFAULT SERVER.
    
     EXEC SQL
       CONNECT TO DEFAULT 
     END-EXEC.

*need for mySql to enable transactions/ SET autocommit=0;

     EXEC SQL
       WHENEVER NOT FOUND GO TO :P-END
     END-EXEC.
* DECLARE CURSOR
     EXEC SQL
       DECLARE cur1 CURSOR FOR SELECT * from testt 
     END-EXEC.
*        IF SQLSTATE = "01000" THEN
     IF SQLSTATE = "00000" then  *>Mike toggle SQLSTATE-COMP/ =
"S1COO" THEN
       DISPLAY "SUCCESSFUL CONNECTION WITH DATABASE."
       DISPLAY " "
     ELSE
       DISPLAY "UNSUCCESSFUL CONNECTION WITH DATABASE."
       DISPLAY "       SQLSTATE : " SQLSTATE
       DISPLAY "       SQLMSG   : " SQLMSG
*> Problem statement        GO TO END-PROC
*> above statement would go to stop run without 
     END-IF.
     
* OPEN THE CURSOR. 
     EXEC SQL
       OPEN cur1
     END-EXEC.  
 P-LOOP.
* FETCH THE DATA.
     EXEC SQL
       FETCH cur1
       INTO :age
     END-EXEC.  
     move age             to ageWK.
     DISPLAY " age    =  "  ageWK.
     GO TO P-LOOP.

 P-END.
* CLOSE THE CURSOR.
     EXEC SQL
       CLOSE cur1
     END-EXEC.  
* END THE TRANSACTION.
     EXEC SQL
     ROLLBACK WORK
     END-EXEC.
* DISCONNECT FROM THE DATABASE.
     EXEC SQL
       DISCONNECT DEFAULT
     END-EXEC.

   END-PROC.
     DISPLAY "END OF SESSION".
     STOP RUN
```

#### ↳ Re: Yippee! Fujitsu 6.1 works with Linux most popular RDBMS MySql!!!!!

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-13T19:09:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208131809.185fb15e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2763b81a.0208122002.ad61113@posting.google.com>`

```
maross@texoma.net (Mike) wrote 

> Yippee! Thanks to Katie Parker 
> ... I got Fujitsu 6.1 to work with the most popular Linux RDBMS

I can get FJ6 to connect to MySQL-Max/InnoDB on Linux but couldn't get
transactions to work in any way. Not by doing an SET AUTOCOMMIT=0 as
an EXECUTE IMMEDIATE or by putting this in the driver startup.

COMMIT/ROLLBACK makes no difference all SQL commits.

More useful is that I can get it to connect to PostGreSQL on Linux and
this does work with Transactions.  I have to do a BEGIN WORK as an
EXECUTE IMMEDIATE but this makes no odds.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Yippee! Fujitsu 6.1 works with Linux most popular RDBMS MySql!!!!!

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-14T09:57:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cQp69.2313$yt3.793054@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<2763b81a.0208122002.ad61113@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0208131809.185fb15e@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0208131809.185fb15e@posting.google.com...
> maross@texoma.net (Mike) wrote
>
> I can get FJ6 to connect to MySQL-Max/InnoDB on Linux but couldn't get
> transactions to work in any way. Not by doing an SET AUTOCOMMIT=0 as
> an EXECUTE IMMEDIATE or by putting this in the driver startup.

> COMMIT/ROLLBACK makes no difference all SQL commits.

Did you check the DBMS/Driver  for transaction (DML)  support?  I am not a
Linux guy, so I'm not even sure it works this way on Linux, but on
Windows(r) systems using ODBC, any application which wants to do
transactions is at the mercy of the database/driver capabilities.

> More useful is that I can get it to connect to PostGreSQL on Linux and
> this does work with Transactions.  I have to do a BEGIN WORK as an
> EXECUTE IMMEDIATE but this makes no odds.

The fact that transaction support seems to work with your PostGreSQL (that's
a new name on me) DBMS - if that is what the above means - suggests the
difference is in the capabilities of MySql-Max/InnoDB.

What do the MySQL folks say? Does the DBMS support transactions when running
on Linux? If so, are there any special configuration steps which must be
taken? Do you need to use some kind of 'EXEC SQL SET AUTOCOMMIT OFF
END-EXEC' statement when using embedded SQL?


MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Yippee! Fujitsu 6.1 works with Linux most popular RDBMS MySql!!!!!

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-14T14:03:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208141303.2557305f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2763b81a.0208122002.ad61113@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0208131809.185fb15e@posting.google.com> <cQp69.2313$yt3.793054@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote 
 
> The fact that transaction support seems to work with your PostGreSQL (that's
> a new name on me) DBMS 

Postgress (now PostgreSQL) had been around for decades, growing out of
the same public domain base as Ingress (post-ingress!). Free, fast,
full featured, reliable.  Just not as 'popularist' as MySQL.

> - if that is what the above means - suggests the
> difference is in the capabilities of MySql-Max/InnoDB.

Until recently MySQL did not support transactions at all, this made it
useful as a toy or, for example, providing fast data for web pages but
not appropriate for heavy data processing.

MySQL-Max/InnoDB is an update that supports transactions.  I have
tried it with CobolScript and Python and it does work.
 
> What do the MySQL folks say? Does the DBMS support transactions when running
> on Linux? If so, are there any special configuration steps which must be
> taken? Do you need to use some kind of 'EXEC SQL SET AUTOCOMMIT OFF
> END-EXEC' statement when using embedded SQL?

That is why I had:

> > Not by doing an SET AUTOCOMMIT=0 as
> > an EXECUTE IMMEDIATE or by putting this in the driver startup.

The SET is as specified in the documents and what works with
CobolScript to turn transactions on.
```

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message 
>MySQL-Max/InnoDB is an update that supports transactions.  I have

Thanks for you response Richard and sorry for my late reply, busy in
the physical world. I was premature in trying transactions with COBOL
as I just attempted MySql transactions from MS-DOS without success, I
don't use transactional mode and will need to study it deeper soon. In
my above Read_Only, 1-column file-age (sorry above says row), if you
use my example above you will need to at least change the SQLODBCS
created file (age.inf) statement @SQL_ACCESS_MODE=READ_ONLY to
READ_WRITE and maybe @SQL_COMMIT_MODE=MANUAL? I was given a patch from
FSC support for the SQLODBCS 6.1 utility that corrects password
encryption, free without support if you email them. I use 5.0
SQLODBC.exe and port to my W2k 6.1(that aborts) I was told FSC 7.0 is
due in November. FSC Linux is ready and cost $1,500.00 + $300 support,
which you will need due to update at end of year. Considering they are
using an OSS IDE, with the same features as the lowers M$
version($750), and better OSS Web options available for free or low
cost; this is going to be a hard sell to the OSS group. I wonder if a
simple FSC-Windows-developed 6.1 "Hello World" would port to Linux via
Wine? The FSC-Linux has almost no GUI abilities that should interfere
with Windows-X?  Mike
```

## Thread: How to put OLEsafearray in OLEsafearray? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to put OLEsafearray in OLEsafearray?

- **From:** aherking@web.de (Alexander Wittka-Herking)
- **Date:** 2002-08-13T00:56:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4e1d4a90.0208122356.22a62fe6@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello,

I have to put an OLEsafearray (Type: VT-BSTR) into another
OLEsafearray (Type: VT-VARIANT).
All of my ideas didn't work. The result is everytime not succesfully.

Here is my code:

The first OLEsafearray:
           MOVE VT-VARIANT             TO vType
           MOVE K-EINS                 TO intDimensions
           MOVE 30                     TO cElements OF saBound1
           MOVE ZERO                   TO llBound OF saBound1

           INVOKE OleSafeArray "New" USING BY VALUE vType
                                           BY VALUE intDimensions
                                           BY REFERENCE saBound1
                                 RETURNING saFirst

The second OLEsafearray:
           MOVE VT-BSTR                TO vType
           MOVE K-ZWEI                 TO intDimensions
           MOVE K-DREI                 TO cElements OF saBound2(1)
           MOVE ZERO                   TO llBound OF saBound2(1)
           MOVE K-EINS                 TO cElements OF saBound2(2)
           MOVE ZERO                   TO llBound OF saBound2(2)

           INVOKE OleSafeArray "New" USING BY VALUE vType
                                           BY VALUE intDimensions
                                           BY REFERENCE saBound2(1)
                                 RETURNING saSecond

Now I want to put the second array into the first:
           MOVE 1                      TO intIndex1
           INVOKE saFirst "putOLEObject" USING intIndex1
                                               BY VALUE saSecond
                                     RETURNING lngResult

After this operation lngResult belongs to 2147942487.

I reach the same result when using:
           "putElement"
           "putOLEObjectAsVariant"
           "putVariant"

Is somebody able to fix this problem???

Thanks a lot for your help

Alexander Wittka-Herking
```

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-08-13T14:35:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020813103553.09188.00000038@mb-ml.aol.com>`

```
I am a COBOL applications programmer looking for a job or contract. 
I know DMS II, SQL in general and Oracle 8i in particular, and VSAM. 
But I am getting a lot of doors slammed in my face because I have
never touched DB2.  How big of a hurdle is DB2?  My guess was
that it would just be a handful of extra verbs to handle reading,
writing, deleting, updating, locking, perhaps transaction states,
auditing, and rollbacks. 
Several places give pre-tests on DB2 before proceeding further.  
Is there an on-line tutorial?  How long do you think it would take
an experienced programmer to learn enough to pass an entrance
exam? 


Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

```

<corek@nospam.hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:vh4jluo8rf16s5fo2map22r1qnea7gdmpp@4ax.com...
> On 13 Aug 2002 14:35:53 GMT, klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Huh?  Boy am I confused by that answer!  SQL "is" the language used to
access DB2 tables in a COBOL program.

Chapter 21. Programming for a DB2 environment

In general, the coding for your COBOL program will be the same whether or
not
you want it to access a DB2 database. However, to retrieve, update, insert,
and
delete DB2 data and use other DB2 services, you must use SQL statements.

To communicate with DB2, you need to do the following:
  () Code any SQL statements you need, delimiting them with EXEC SQL and
END-EXEC statements.
  () Use the DB2 precompiler or compile with the SQL compiler option if
using DB2
for OS/390 Version 7 or later.

   http://www.newsfeed.com       The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
```

#### ↳ Re: DB2 and COBOL

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <NoPersonalMailPlease@MyDot.Com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-14T07:01:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tfn69.11681$Ep6.926391@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<20020813103553.09188.00000038@mb-ml.aol.com>`

```

Ross Klatte <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20020813103553.09188.00000038@mb-ml.aol.com...
> I am a COBOL applications programmer looking for a job or contract.
> I know DMS II, SQL in general and Oracle 8i in particular, and VSAM.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> exam?

James Martin's book "DB2 Concepts, Design. and Programming
is relatively short (325 pages), and covers DB2, SQL,
QMF (Query Management Facility) and DXT (Data Extract).

An oldie (1989) but goodie, it has a chapter on Embedded SQL statements,
with examples in COBOL, PL/I, C, FORTRAN and Assembler,
so I'd recommend that to get you up and running.

For a more comprehensive User Guide, I also have Don Chamberlin's
"Using the New DB2".  681 pages.  Practical tips.  100s of tested examples.
Dr Chamberlin is a co-inventor of the original SQL database language.

For a certification guide, I've got the 4th Edition of IBM's
"DB/2 Universal Database v7.1 for Unix, Linux, Windows and OS/2",
written by George Baklarz and Bill Wong, DB2 experts in IBM's Toronto Lab.
All 790 pages of it.  Comes with a CD-ROM containing a trial version
of DB/2 UDB v7.1 for Windows NT, Windows 2000 and Windows 95/98.
```

```
(Mike Murach pub.), Volume 1 Introduction,  and it is getting pretty dry.  
The first part was pretty cool, and had a lot of fully coded programs
suitable for plagiarizing.  Then it gets into DB2I and TSO and JCL
and I am starting to get glassy-eyed.  Has anyone taken an entrance
exam on DB2?  Did the test include questions on TSO, etc.?  Or was
it strictly on SQL and COBOL usages?

book to use in crossing the TSO Bridge when I come to it.  Would
that damage my chances on a DB2 job applicant's exam?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DB2 and COBOL

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <NoPersonalMailPlease@MyDot.Com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-18T08:12:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1GI79.17436$Ke2.1449684@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

Ross Klatte <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20020817203902.00708.00001225@mb-cf.aol.com...
>
> I am on page 307 of Steve Eckols's "DB2 for the COBOL Programmer"
> (Mike Murach pub.), Volume 1 Introduction,  and it is getting pretty dry.

I feel the same way when first reading about any new technology.
At a certain point, I feel the need to logon and have at it with some actual
examples.
I need to use the stuff, get my feet wet if you will, to help me understand
it.
C/C++/C#, HTML/DHTML/XML, Java/Visual J++, or VB.
Regardless of topic, you need to use it or lose it in terms of following the
textbook.
You can read all you like about VB, but until you have dragged-and-dropped
those tools onto your project, and written some code, and set your
breakpoints,
and walked through the code to analyse it and see what is going on,
you just don't get the flavor.

> The first part was pretty cool, and had a lot of fully coded programs
> suitable for plagiarizing.  Then it gets into DB2I and TSO and JCL
> and I am starting to get glassy-eyed.

TSO is fairly simple IF you have used it on a daily basis,
although I'll admit that there are sections I never venture into,
because I simply have no need to.  I don't know of a better resource
for learning TSO than the TSO online Help itself, which puts me back
where I started - you have to use it to learn it.

JCL is something you need to understand at better than beginner's level.
You wouldn't be the first programmer to leave of a few critical characters
and end up deleting every member of a production Generation Data Group,
or every member in a test copy library.  I've always used a book by
Gary De Ward Brown "System 370 Job Control Language".
The first edition came out in 1977 and sold over 250,000 copies
before the second edition came out in 1987.
I don't know what the current edition is.

>Has anyone taken an entrance
> exam on DB2?  Did the test include questions on TSO, etc.?  Or was
> it strictly on SQL and COBOL usages?

I don't know.  I still have that windmill to tilt at.  But my certification
guide
states that coverage include:
* DB2 administration: populating tables with import and load utilities,
   monitoring, logging, maintenance and tuning
* Security: controlling data access through authentication, assigned
authorities,
   user/group privileges and more
* Database backup and recovery, step by step
* DB2 SQL, in depth: database objects, Data Definition Language (DDL),
   Data Manipulation Language (DML), triggers, outer joins, OLAP SQL,
   tble hierarchies, concurrency, and embedded SQL

You can see that it's a little more than throwing a few SQL statements
into a host program.

> I think my preference would be just to keep the book as a reference
> book to use in crossing the TSO Bridge when I come to it.  Would
> that damage my chances on a DB2 job applicant's exam?

As I said, the online Help is all the reference I have ever used or needed.
If push comes to shove on an interview, be frank in admitting to
what you don't know, especially if you can honestly reassure the employer
that you are a very quick study and will pick it up in no time.
>
> Does Expeditor work on DB2 programs?

As of March 15, 2001.  XPEDITER/TSO version 7.0.

http://www.compuware.com/pressroom/news/2001/2001031501.htm

Take care.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DB2 and COBOL

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-08-22T03:51:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020821235154.03694.00002510@mb-fq.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20020817203902.00708.00001225@mb-cf.aol.com>`

```
>From: klatteross@aol.commmm  (Ross Klatte)
>Date: 8/17/02 8:39 PM Eastern Daylight Time

> Has anyone taken an entrance
>exam on DB2?  Did the test include questions on TSO, etc.?  Or was
>it strictly on SQL and COBOL usages? 

I learned DB2 at a Roscoe shop.  TSO was used for QMF and some DBA activities
which I learned at the knee of another programmer.

The shop I'm currently at uses both Roscoe and TSO depending on the preference
of the programmer and being a Roscoe body I use that almost exclusively for
most of my work but still go into TSO for DB2 stuff and some other things that
can't be done in Roscoe.

I think if you get the basic idea down you should be ok.  That is know the
basic commands for getting into QMF or what have you.  As for JCL we have a
proc that we use and probably most other shops have as well so just the basic
parameters should have you covered.  The strength is in the SQL and COBOL the
rest just mechanics kinda like learning to drive a stick after driving an
automatic.  You know how to drive a car - it's coordinating everything else
that you have to get down pat which takes nothing but practice.
```

#### ↳ Re: OO and Components - WAS Re: MF Licensing

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-14T07:49:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A9s69.21395$g47.1151685@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3D48304A.5BBC408F@shaw.ca> <1028203538.201781@newsmaster-03.atnet.at> <bfdfc3e8.0208011712.19d16180@posting.google.com> <OaU29.99811$ND5.8994381@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D4C1A3B.F2A45399@shaw.ca> <%Eu39.127203$ND5.10660295@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D4F567C.66FE1EC4@shaw.ca> <aiokjg$iv4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3D5012A0.6F150F2@shaw.ca> <OOV39.6889$Xf7.743946@news20.bellglobal.com> <3D503A80.EB879F0C@shaw.ca> <y4749.159341$Og3.40540322@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <tx969.10544$Ep6.837589@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
> The Net Express editor does have column block abilities.  If this is
> a bottom of the rung editor, than a good one (Multi-edit?) must be
> incredible.

You're right, Net Express does have has an option to switch between line
and column blocks.  But you can't select streams of text at all, which is
extremely useful (I'll give examples if anyone wants).  And you must use
the mouse to use both on the fly, and using the mouse for editing functions
in a programmer's editor makes little sense.  Unless you're a hunt-and-peck
typist, taking your hands off the keyboard to do any editing function is far
slower than using the keyboard.  You can type dozens of keystrokes in the
time it takes to move your hand to a mouse, move the mouse, click, drag or
select, then move your hand back to the keyboard.  It is intuitively obvious
and easily verified, and is one reason why GUI interfaces don't make sense
for some applications.

Believe me, compared to a top flight programmer's editor (Multi-Edit is
only one of many), any IDE editor I have ever seen is like using crayons,
by comparison; no hooey.  Any programmer's editor worth the name will
have regular expression search and replace, block, column and line mark,
copy and move, convert case to upper/lower, file compare, keyboard
macros, a macro language, full keyboard remap, and a vast array of other
functions.  The last hardcopy manual printed for Multi-Edit is two major
releases ago, and it is over 1" thick.  The macro language in Brief was so
powerful there were games written using it.  Brief set the standard for
DOS based programmer's editors.  Unfortunately, Borland bought Brief to
get the technology, then let it die on the vine more than 10 years ago.  Even
now, Brief is better than many if not most of the current editors, and almost
all good programmer's editors still offer a Brief keyboard emulation.

By 'bottom rung' I mean that the NE IDE is poor in text editing functions
when compared with other IDEs, such as Visual Studio (which has such
features as regular expressions and keyboard emulation for a few big
name editors, such as Brief) or even old DOS IDEs such as QuickBasic
and the Borland products.  The Net Express IDE's debugging features and
integration with the compiler are okay.
```

<a id="t-08aa56258d"></a>
## Thread: IBM (z/OS) relase announcements — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM (z/OS) relase announcements

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-13T14:54:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajbo6c$qee$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI, (if you are in or care about "IBM mainframe" environments),

There were new announcements today for the next releases of z/OS and z/OS.e

See:

IBM z/OS V1.4: Enabling and Protecting Your e-business and Preview: z/OS
V1.5 (129.8KB)
    http://isource.ibm.com/cgi-bin/goto?it=usa_annred&on=202-190

IBM z/OS.e V1.4 and z/OS.e 1.5 -- Brings Affordability to Enterprise and
e-business Applications (53.3KB)
    http://isource.ibm.com/cgi-bin/goto?it=usa_annred&on=202-191

***

Of most interest (to me) is the fact that V1.4 will be available this Sept;
there will be NO releases in 2003; there will be two releases in 2004; then
IBM plans on going to an annual (rather than the older semi-annual) release
schedule.

Second (interesting to me) was the lack of any ANNOUNCED enhancements to LE.

Final (not so interesting) point.  The z/OS.e announcement maintains the
same "odd" support for COBOL compiled applications, (i.e. they are supported
*if* you precompile them on a z/OS machine *and* include specific calls to
CEEPIPI for LE initialization).  It is still unsupported (violation of the
license) to run any COBOL compiler under z/OS.e (you MUST use z/OS for this)
```

#### ↳ IBM (z/OS) release announcements

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-13T15:43:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajbr2l$slr$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ajbo6c$qee$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-08-14T11:28:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020814072809.20360.00000303@mb-fv.aol.com>`

```
As I understand it, Microfocus COBOL requires a "run-time fee." 
How much is it?  Is it a one-time payment?  Or do you pay monthly? 
Does the size of the payment vary with the size of the program?  Or vary
with the size of the customer's ability to pay?  

If it's a one-time one-rate-for-everybody payment...
Suppose you install a second program?  Is there another fee to pay? 

If I write a program and sell it to a customer, and that one customer
runs the program on four stand-alone PC's, is that four run-time fees? 
Is there a discount for additional copies at the same site?  
Suppose the customer installs one program on a server and ten
PC's run it off the network, is that one run-time fee or ten? 



Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

#### ↳ Re: How much is a run-time fee?

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-14T14:24:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208141324.68fa8446@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20020814072809.20360.00000303@mb-fv.aol.com>`

```
klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte) wrote

> As I understand it, Microfocus COBOL requires a "run-time fee." 
> How much is it?  Is it a one-time payment?  Or do you pay monthly? 
> Does the size of the payment vary with the size of the program?  Or vary
> with the size of the customer's ability to pay?  

You purchase run-times (or are supposed to) in 10 user packs for a
site. Annual support is extra at around 20% of purchase or so.

Up until the most recent versions of MF the developer licence has
allowed construction of run-time free distribution as long as only a
subset of the facilities are built into the program.  For example on
DOS/Windows the program must be built from .EXEs and .DLL and not .int
or .gnt. Things like callable rebuild, panels, fileshare server, some
called routines meant that run-time licence was required.

While this free subset was specifically in the manual and in the
licence most MF sales people categorically denied that this existed or
meant what it stated and/or didn't apply in any case and had been
superceeded and replaced (even if the developer hadn't upgraded).

On Unix it seems that there is a way of static linking a system to
avoid run-times but it is quite restrictive.

> If it's a one-time one-rate-for-everybody payment...
> Suppose you install a second program?  Is there another fee to pay? 

The run-time licence is per user.  However if the user is running two
applications (or one appication twice) at the same time then that
counts as two users - apparently.

> If I write a program and sell it to a customer, and that one customer
> runs the program on four stand-alone PC's, is that four run-time fees? 

Yes.  Or 10 as user licences come in 10 packs.  Allegedly small
developers can split packs for small users as a 'concession'.

> Is there a discount for additional copies at the same site?  
> Suppose the customer installs one program on a server and ten
> PC's run it off the network, is that one run-time fee or ten? 

One pack of 10.  Or more if users want to run multiple copies of an
application on their screen.  However if at one time only 5 of those
machines run two copies each then that too, is 10 (allegedly).
```

<a id="t-13b414bfb3"></a>
## Thread: SafeArray in SafeArray? — 2 msgs

### SafeArray in SafeArray?

- **From:** aherking@web.de (Alexander Wittka-Herking)
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T02:14:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4e1d4a90.0208150114.173ad81b@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello!

I try a second time.

How am I able to store an OLESafearray into another.
The first array is typ VT-VARIANT, the second is type VT-BSTR.
I want to store the second into the first at position 2 (Index 1).

Using "putElement", "putOLEObject", "putOLEObjectAsVariant" and
"putVariant" causes an error.

Does somebody has an idea how to solve this problem?

Thanks a lot

Alexander Wittka-Herking
```

#### ↳ Re: SafeArray in SafeArray?

- **From:** "David Sands" <david.sands@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T17:18:39+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajgjsb$ri1$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<4e1d4a90.0208150114.173ad81b@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Alexander,

I think you need to put a Variant in the first safearray so you need to wrap
the second safearray as a variant. Then put the variant in the safearray.

Your code could be something like:-

ws:-

01 anOLEVAR object reference.
01 ptr-Variant pointer.
01 ptr-array pointer.

01 intIndex1 PIC 9(9) COMP-5.

01 lngresult PIC 9(9) COMP-5.

linkage:-

01 aSafeArray object reference.
01 lnk-Variant variant.

proc div:-
invoke sasecond "getsafearray" returning ptr-array

MOVE VT-BSTR TO vType
ADD VT-ARRAY TO VType
invoke OLEVariant "newwithdata" using vType
returning anOLEVAR
invoke anOLEVAR "getVariantAddress" returning ptr-variant
set address of lnk-variant to ptr-variant
set VARIANT-VT-SafeArray of lnk-variant to ptr-array

MOVE 1 TO intIndex1
INVOKE saFirst "putVariant" USING intIndex1
lnk-variant
RETURNING lngResult
---
Regards
David

"Alexander Wittka-Herking" <aherking@web.de> wrote in message
news:4e1d4a90.0208150114.173ad81b@posting.google.com...
> Hello!
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Alexander Wittka-Herking
```

## Thread: Using VB with COBOL on multiple platforms. — 6 msgs

- **From:** "Henry Nelson" <email@minsterlogistics.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T13:10:10+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajg5lt$dac$2@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi,

At work we (not me) use:-

net express Cobol on windows
server express Cobol on Unix
cobol400 or ILE Cobol on AS400

We are looking to do new development in visual basic (thats me:-) and the
above languages.
I have seen some posts discussing net express and RPG using VB but I was
wondering if
there was a universal solution to communicate between VB and the above Cobol
versions?

If not what is the best approach for each individual platform and are there
any limitations?

We would like to be able to call COBOL programs and pass parameters from VB
and visa versa.

Our AS400 is running V4R5 but would like a solution for older and newer
operating systems.

I am currently using an SQL database to pass info between ASP/VB and net
express.
Someone has a java interface for passing info to and from server express
and I am accessing db2/400 from VB using IBMDA400 client access driver.

Any snippets of code or links would be most helpful.

thanks,

Henry
```

```
Henry:

I know that it's not VB, but COBOL sp2 supports the ability to build a
full GUI interface (complete with embedded OCX Controls).  When
combined with our Thin Client product, the COBOL program runs on a
variety of different server types while displaying the screens scross
a TCP/IP network.

The nice thing about it is that it allows you to maintain a single set
of source code and still support multiple server environments.

Please let me know if I can be of assistance.
```

## Thread: DYNAMIC ALLOCATION OF A DATASET — 8 this month / 11 total

### DYNAMIC ALLOCATION OF A DATASET

- **From:** spafa9 <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T12:33:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d5be5d9$1_9@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
I NEED TO KNOW HOW TO DYNAMICALLY ALLOCATE A DATASET. THE GUYS HERE AT
WORK SAID IT CAN BE DONE WITH THE PUTENV FUNCTION. HOW DO I SET UP THE
WORKING STORAGE AREA? THIS IS WHAT I HAVE DONE?

HERE IS WHAT I AM TRYING TO DO


SELECT NDM-FILE-OUT ASSIGN TO DYNFILE

 FD NDM-FILE-OUT RECORDING MODE F LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD RECORD
 CONTAINS 1850 CHARACTERS BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS DATA RECORD IS
 NDM-FILE-OUT-REC.

 01 NDM-FILE-OUT-REC PIC X(1850).

******************************************************************
*        DYNAMIC FILE ALLOCATION WORKING STORAGE AREA *
******************************************************************
 01 NDM-FILE-STATUS PIC 9(02) VALUE ZEROS. 01 WS-RC PIC 9(09) BINARY. 01
 WS-ADDRESS-POINTER POINTER. 01 WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-VAR. 05
 WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-DSN PIC X(38) VALUE
 'DYNFILE=DSN(ECBS053.ACVAF625.OUT)'. 05 WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-VAR-DISP
 PIC X(39) VALUE ' NEW DISK SPACE(CYC,(5,1),RLSE) CATALOG'. 05
 WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-VAR-UNIT PIC X(32) VALUE 'DISK
 SPACE(CYL,(5,1),RLSE)'. 05 WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-VAR-DCB PIC X(50) VALUE
 '(SYS2.DSCB,RECFM=V,LRECL=1854,BLKSIZE=0)'.


i AM PERFORMING THIS PARAGRAPH THEN GOING BACK TO OPEN MY FILES...

  SET WS-ADDRESS-POINTER TO ADDRESS OF WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-VAR. CALL
  "PUTENV" USING BY VALUE WS-ADDRESS-POINTER RETURNING WS-RC. IF WS-RC
  NOT = 0 THEN DISPLAY 'PUTENV FAILED' STOP RUN END-IF.
```

#### ↳ Re: DYNAMIC ALLOCATION OF A DATASET

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T18:36:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajgsab$nul$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3d5be5d9$1_9@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID. COBDYNAL.
000300*===============================================================*
000400* THIS PROGRAM DYNAMICALLY ALLOCATES AND DEALLOCATES A FILE
000500* USING THE BPXWDYN IBM SUBROUTINE. NO DD STATEMENTS FOR THE
000600* FILE SOULD APPEAR IN THE JCL:
000700*===============================================================*
000800 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000900 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001000 SOURCE-COMPUTER.
001100****           IBM-390 WITH DEBUGGING MODE.
001200               IBM-390.
001300
001400 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
001500 FILE-CONTROL.
001600     SELECT FILEIN  ASSIGN TO FILEIN
001700         FILE STATUS IS FILEIN-STATUS.
001800     SELECT FILEOUT ASSIGN TO FILEOUT
001900         FILE STATUS IS FILEOUT-STATUS.
002000     SELECT PARMIN  ASSIGN TO PARMIN.
002100/
002200 DATA DIVISION.
002300 FILE SECTION.
002400 FD  FILEIN
002500     RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS
002600     RECORDING MODE IS F
002700     LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
002800     BLOCK CONTAINS 0 CHARACTERS
002900     DATA RECORD IS FILEIN-REC.
003000 01  FILEIN-REC.
003100     05 FILLER             PIC X(80).
003200
003300 FD  FILEOUT
003400     RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS
003500     RECORDING MODE IS F
003600     LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
003700     BLOCK CONTAINS 0 CHARACTERS
003800     DATA RECORD IS FILEOUT-REC.
003900 01  FILEOUT-REC.
004000     05 FILLER             PIC X(80).
004100
004200 FD  PARMIN
004300     RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS
004400     RECORDING MODE IS F
004500     LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
004600     BLOCK CONTAINS 0 CHARACTERS
004700     DATA RECORD IS FILEIN-REC.
004800 01  PARM-REC.
004900     05 FILLER             PIC X(80).
005000
005100 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
005200 01  FILEIN-STATUS           PIC X(02)   VALUE '00'.
005300     88 FILEIN-STATUS-OK                 VALUE '00' '10' '97'.
005400     88 FILEIN-AT-END                    VALUE '10'.
005500
005600 01  FILEOUT-STATUS          PIC X(02)   VALUE '00'.
005700     88 FILEOUT-STATUS-OK                VALUE '00' '97'.
005800
005900 01  INPUT-REC             PIC X(80).
006000 01  IN-COUNT              PIC 9(9)   BINARY VALUE 0.
006100 01  SUB-PGM               PIC X(8)   VALUE 'BPXWDYN '.
006200
006300 01 COMMAND-STRING.
006400**** THIS FOLLOWING APPEARS TO BE OPTIONAL
006500**** COMMENT IT OUT, KEEP IT AT 100, OR PUT IN THE LENGTH!!
006600     05 C-S-LEN            PIC S9(4)   BINARY VALUE +100.
006700     05 C-S-DATA.
006800         07 FILLER         PIC X(100).
006900
007000 01  WS-INPUT-REC                 PIC X(80).
007100 01  MYINFILE                     PIC X(73)
007200                         VALUE 'UMS.D44201.COBOL(COBDYN)'.
007300 01  MYINFILE-LENGTH              PIC S9(2)   VALUE 24.
007400 01  MYOUTFILE                    PIC X(72)
007500                         VALUE 'UMS.D44201.TEST'.
007600 01  MYOUTFILE-LENGTH             PIC S9(2)   VALUE 16.
007700 01  SWITCHES.
007800     05  SW-PARM-EOF-SWITCH       PIC X(01)   VALUE 'N'.
007900     88  SW-PARM-EOF                          VALUE 'Y'.
008000     05  SW-FILE-EOF-SWITCH       PIC X(01)   VALUE 'N'.
008100     88  SW-FILE-EOF                          VALUE 'Y'.
008200
008300 01  PARM-RECORD.
008400     05  INFILE-PARM.
008500         10  INFILE-PARM-NAME     PIC X(06).
008600         88  INFILE-PARM-FOUND                VALUE 'INFILE'.
008700         10  FILLER               PIC X(01).
008800         10  INFILE-NAME          PIC X(73).
008900     05  OUTFILE-PARM             REDEFINES INFILE-PARM.
009000         10  OUTFILE-PARM-NAME    PIC X(07).
009100         88  OUTFILE-PARM-FOUND               VALUE 'OUTFILE'.
009200         10  FILLER               PIC X(01).
009300         10  OUTFILE-NAME         PIC X(72).
009400/
009500 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
009600 0000-MAIN.
009700     PERFORM 0100-INIT.
009800     READ FILEIN INTO WS-INPUT-REC,
009900         AT END SET SW-FILE-EOF TO TRUE.
010000     PERFORM 1000-READ-LOOP UNTIL SW-FILE-EOF.
010100     PERFORM 9000-CLEANUP.
010200     GOBACK.
010300
010400 0100-INIT.
010500     PERFORM 0200-READ-PARMS.
010600     PERFORM 0300-OPEN-INPUT.
010700     PERFORM 0400-OPEN-OUTPUT.
010800
010900 0200-READ-PARMS.
011000     OPEN INPUT PARMIN.
011100     READ PARMIN     INTO PARM-RECORD
011200         AT END SET SW-PARM-EOF TO TRUE.
011300     PERFORM 0210-READ-PARM
011400         UNTIL SW-PARM-EOF.
011500     CLOSE PARMIN.
011600     PERFORM VARYING MYINFILE-LENGTH FROM 73 BY -1
011700               UNTIL MYINFILE-LENGTH < 3
011800                  OR MYINFILE(MYINFILE-LENGTH:1) > SPACE
011900     END-PERFORM.
012000
012100     PERFORM VARYING MYOUTFILE-LENGTH FROM 72 BY -1
012200               UNTIL MYOUTFILE-LENGTH < 3
012300                  OR MYOUTFILE(MYOUTFILE-LENGTH:1) > SPACE
012400     END-PERFORM.
012500
012600 0210-READ-PARM.
012700     IF INFILE-PARM-FOUND
012800         MOVE INFILE-NAME         TO MYINFILE
012900     END-IF.
013000
013100     IF OUTFILE-PARM-FOUND
013200         MOVE OUTFILE-NAME        TO MYOUTFILE
013300     END-IF.
013400
013500     READ PARMIN     INTO PARM-RECORD
013600         AT END SET SW-PARM-EOF TO TRUE.
013700
013800 0300-OPEN-INPUT.
013900     MOVE SPACE                TO C-S-DATA.
014000     STRING ' ALLOC DD(FILEIN) '
014100            'DSN(' MYINFILE (1:MYINFILE-LENGTH) ')'
014200            ' SHR          '
014300         DELIMITED BY SIZE
014400                         INTO C-S-DATA
014500     END-STRING
014600     PERFORM 9000-CLIST.
014700     OPEN INPUT FILEIN.
014800     IF FILEIN-STATUS-OK
014900         CONTINUE
015000         DISPLAY 'OPENED "' MYINFILE (1:MYINFILE-LENGTH) '"'
015100     ELSE
015200         DISPLAY 'VSAM STATUS "' FILEIN-STATUS '" OPENING "'
015300          MYINFILE (1:MYINFILE-LENGTH) '"'
015400         MOVE FILEIN-STATUS TO RETURN-CODE
015500         GOBACK
015600     END-IF.
015700
015800 0400-OPEN-OUTPUT.
015900
016000     PERFORM 0410-IEFBR14-DELETE.
016100
016200     MOVE SPACE                TO C-S-DATA.
016300     STRING ' ALLOC DD(FILEOUT) '
016400            'DSN(' MYOUTFILE (1:MYOUTFILE-LENGTH) ')'
016500         ' NEW UNIT(DEVEDISK) CYL SPACE(05,05) CATALOG '
016600         DELIMITED BY SIZE
016700                         INTO C-S-DATA
016800     END-STRING
016900     PERFORM 9000-CLIST.
017000
017100     OPEN OUTPUT FILEOUT.
017200     IF FILEOUT-STATUS-OK
017300         CONTINUE
017400         DISPLAY 'OPENED "' MYOUTFILE (1:MYOUTFILE-LENGTH) '"'
017500     ELSE
017600         DISPLAY 'VSAM STATUS "' FILEOUT-STATUS '" OPENING "'
017700          MYOUTFILE (1:MYOUTFILE-LENGTH) '"'
017800         MOVE FILEOUT-STATUS TO RETURN-CODE
017900         GOBACK
018000     END-IF.
018100
018200 0410-IEFBR14-DELETE.
018300**** DELETE IF IT EXISTS.
018400     MOVE SPACE                TO C-S-DATA.
018500     STRING ' ALLOC DD(FILEOUT) '
018600            'DSN(' MYOUTFILE (1:MYOUTFILE-LENGTH) ')'
018700         ' MOD UNIT(DEVEDISK) CYL SPACE(05,05) DELETE  '
018800         DELIMITED BY SIZE
018900                         INTO C-S-DATA
019000     PERFORM 9000-CLIST.
019100
019200     MOVE 'FREE DD(FILEOUT)' TO C-S-DATA.
019300     PERFORM 9000-CLIST.
019400**** THE ABOVE ALLOWS THAT FILE TO BE OPENED AGAIN.
019500
019600 1000-READ-LOOP.
019700     ADD +1 TO IN-COUNT.
019800D    DISPLAY FILEIN-REC.
019900
020000     WRITE FILEOUT-REC FROM WS-INPUT-REC.
020100
020200     READ FILEIN INTO WS-INPUT-REC,
020300         AT END SET SW-FILE-EOF TO TRUE.
020400
020500 9000-CLEANUP.
020600     CLOSE FILEIN.
020700     IF FILEIN-STATUS-OK
020800         CONTINUE
020900     ELSE
021000         DISPLAY 'VSAM STATUS "' FILEIN-STATUS '" CLOSING "'
021100          MYINFILE (1:MYINFILE-LENGTH) '"'
021200         MOVE FILEIN-STATUS TO RETURN-CODE
021300         GOBACK
021400     END-IF.
021500
021600     MOVE 'FREE DD(FILEIN)' TO C-S-DATA.
021700     PERFORM 9000-CLIST.
021800**** THE ABOVE ALLOWS THAT FILE TO BE OPENED AGAIN.
021900     CLOSE FILEOUT.
022000     IF FILEOUT-STATUS-OK
022100         CONTINUE
022200     ELSE
022300         DISPLAY 'VSAM STATUS "' FILEOUT-STATUS '" CLOSING "'
022400          MYINFILE (1:MYINFILE-LENGTH) '"'
022500         MOVE FILEOUT-STATUS TO RETURN-CODE
022600         GOBACK
022700     END-IF.
022800
022900     MOVE 'FREE DD(FILEOUT)' TO C-S-DATA.
023000     PERFORM 9000-CLIST.
023100**** THE ABOVE ALLOWS THAT FILE TO BE OPENED AGAIN.
023200     GOBACK.
023300
023400 9000-CLIST.
023500*****  I DON'T THINK C-S-LEN IS NEEDED
023600     PERFORM VARYING C-S-LEN FROM 100 BY -1
023700               UNTIL C-S-LEN < 2
023800                  OR C-S-DATA (C-S-LEN:1) > SPACE
023900     END-PERFORM.
024000D    DISPLAY 'C-S-DATA="' C-S-DATA (1:C-S-LEN) '"'.
024100     CALL SUB-PGM USING COMMAND-STRING.
024200     IF RETURN-CODE = 0
024300         CONTINUE
024400D        DISPLAY 'RETURN-CODE ="' RETURN-CODE '"'
024500      ELSE
024600         DISPLAY 'RETURN-CODE ="' RETURN-CODE '" FOR "'
024700                 C-S-DATA(1:C-S-LEN) '"'
024800         DISPLAY '**** ERROR ABORTING ****'
024900         MOVE '35'           TO RETURN-CODE
025000         GOBACK
025100      END-IF.
025200****** COBDYNN ENDS HERE.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DYNAMIC ALLOCATION OF A DATASET

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T14:47:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajh0gi$ne4$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3d5be5d9$1_9@news5.nntpserver.com> <ajgsab$nul$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard,
  This is NOT the answer to someone who wants to (and CAN) use "putenv" and
"genenv".  This *is* a solution for people using "down-level" IBM mainframe
COBOLs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DYNAMIC ALLOCATION OF A DATASET

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T19:59:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajh15r$psq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3d5be5d9$1_9@news5.nntpserver.com> <ajgsab$nul$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ajh0gi$ne4$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On 15-Aug-2002, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> Howard,
>   This is NOT the answer to someone who wants to (and CAN) use "putenv" and
> "genenv".  This *is* a solution for people using "down-level" IBM mainframe
> COBOLs.

Two questions:

What is the advantage to using "putenv" and "getenv"?

What does "down-level" mean?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DYNAMIC ALLOCATION OF A DATASET

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T16:45:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajh7e3$8ks$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3d5be5d9$1_9@news5.nntpserver.com> <ajgsab$nul$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ajh0gi$ne4$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <ajh15r$psq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
The advantage of using putenv and getenv is that they are the DOCUMENTED (in
the COBOL) way of doing dynamic allocation. If you do a search in the
current IBM COBOL documentation for "dynamic file allocation" - this is what
you will find.

Down-level is anything *below* IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM V2R2 (where this
capability was introduced).
```

#### ↳ Re: DYNAMIC ALLOCATION OF A DATASET

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T14:42:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajh07t$7tc$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3d5be5d9$1_9@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
After you stop SHOUTING at us, (even COBOL doesn't require capitals any more
<G>)

Check out the example at:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/3.4.2.4?

Things to remember,

1) You need to be using a COBOL compiler that supports this, IBM COBOL for
OS/390 & VM V2R2 or above

2) The program names should be in lower-case.

3) These programs need PGMNAME(LONGMIXED) and NODYNAM
```

```
for what to put in your environment variable
```

## Thread: DYNAMIC ALLOCATION OF A DATASET — 1 msgs

### DYNAMIC ALLOCATION OF A DATASET

- **From:** spafa9 <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T12:38:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d5be70d$1_4@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
I NEED TO KNOW HOW TO DYNAMICALLY ALLOCATE A DATASET. THE GUYS HERE AT
WORK SAID IT CAN BE DONE WITH THE PUTENV FUNCTION. HOW DO I SET UP THE
WORKING STORAGE AREA? THIS IS WHAT I HAVE DONE?

HERE IS WHAT I AM TRYING TO DO


SELECT NDM-FILE-OUT ASSIGN TO DYNFILE

 FD NDM-FILE-OUT RECORDING MODE F LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD RECORD
 CONTAINS 1850 CHARACTERS BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS DATA RECORD IS
 NDM-FILE-OUT-REC.

 01 NDM-FILE-OUT-REC PIC X(1850).

******************************************************************
*        DYNAMIC FILE ALLOCATION WORKING STORAGE AREA *
******************************************************************
 01 NDM-FILE-STATUS PIC 9(02) VALUE ZEROS. 01 WS-RC PIC 9(09) BINARY. 01
 WS-ADDRESS-POINTER POINTER. 01 WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-VAR. 05
 WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-DSN PIC X(38) VALUE
 'DYNFILE=DSN(ECBS053.ACVAF625.OUT)'. 05 WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-VAR-DISP
 PIC X(39) VALUE ' NEW DISK SPACE(CYC,(5,1),RLSE) CATALOG'. 05
 WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-VAR-UNIT PIC X(32) VALUE 'DISK
 SPACE(CYL,(5,1),RLSE)'. 05 WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-VAR-DCB PIC X(50) VALUE
 '(SYS2.DSCB,RECFM=V,LRECL=1854,BLKSIZE=0)'.


i AM PERFORMING THIS PARAGRAPH THEN GOING BACK TO OPEN MY FILES...

  SET WS-ADDRESS-POINTER TO ADDRESS OF WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-VAR. CALL
  "PUTENV" USING BY VALUE WS-ADDRESS-POINTER RETURNING WS-RC. IF WS-RC
  NOT = 0 THEN DISPLAY 'PUTENV FAILED' STOP RUN END-IF.
```

- **From:** spafa9 <member@mainframeforum.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d5c130a$1_7@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
Hi I need help with the environment variable: Right now I am created a
file using putenv

and this environment variable

 01 WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-VAR PIC X(50) VALUE
 'DYNFILE=DSN(ECBS053.ACVAF625.OUT) NEW CATALOG'.



I need to be able to make it in to a tape how do I do this? I have tried



 01 WS-NDM-FILE-ENVIRON-VAR PIC X(50) VALUE
 'DYNFILE=DSN(ECBS053.ACVAF625.OUT) NEW CATALOG TAPE'.
```

#### ↳ Re: dynamic tape allocation

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-15T16:50:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajh7n4$8ji$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3d5c130a$1_7@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
Check out the FORMAT of what you can use (and how you use it) in the table
called

" Environment variable format (QSAM files)--DSN option"

at

http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3lr00/4.2.3.1

 The bottom-line is that you need to figure out what the JCL would look like
if you did this via JCL and "basically" put that in the enviornment
variable.
```

- **References:** `<20020808131830.17610.qmail@nym.alias.net> <3D52A292.6C8A4E8F@shaw.ca> <aiu93r$2u3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3D52B4AC.F2644D6E@shaw.ca> <VA.0000023b.03646ff3@ieee.org> <aiumjl$nmg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <UfO49.20069$Z7.1112060@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <3D54070E.B3CC96CE@shaw.ca> <aj1bgq$mb7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> In the case where
> the application DOES want to treat this as "1 month" - then it is true that
> the Standard doesn't provide a solution.  

And it doesn't need to.  It is trivial to multiply year numbers by 12
and add the months for those that wish to do that. The date routines are
only required because the calculations are complex and difficult to get
right.

Back when we used Pounds shillings and pence there were routines
provided to take the difference between two, convert to pence and back. 
With Dollars and cents this is trivial and unrequired.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Pre-decmilazation (was: Testing a date age by month

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-16T18:16:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajk1bo$ek4$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20020808131830.17610.qmail@nym.alias.net> <3D52A292.6C8A4E8F@shaw.ca> <aiu93r$2u3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3D52B4AC.F2644D6E@shaw.ca> <VA.0000023b.03646ff3@ieee.org> <aiumjl$nmg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <UfO49.20069$Z7.1112060@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <3D54070E.B3CC96CE@shaw.ca> <aj1bgq$mb7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3D5C9942.B92F8273@Azonic.co.nz> <3D5C93D6.62D608D3@shaw.ca> <217e491a.0208161505.21ccf61b@posting.google.com>`

```
From the true world of "trivia".  IBM's COBOL compilers actually INCLUDED
full support for Pounds, Shilling, Pence as "USAGE DISPLAY-ST".  This
included support for the Report Writer "SUM" function.  This feature was
last documented in their ANS COBOL V4 product - but the support remained in
OS/VS COBOL *and* such programs still work when run with LE.   The most
interesting part of this (to me) was the distinction between upper- and
lower-case "D/d" in the PICTURE clause (and this was BEFORE other parts of
the source code could be lower-case).
```
