- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-17T01:59:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D5DAF17.BB9325A5@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<20020808131830.17610.qmail@nym.alias.net> <3D52A292.6C8A4E8F@shaw.ca> <aiu93r$2u3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3D52B4AC.F2644D6E@shaw.ca> <VA.0000023b.03646ff3@ieee.org> <aiumjl$nmg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <UfO49.20069$Z7.1112060@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <3D54070E.B3CC96CE@shaw.ca> <aj1bgq$mb7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3D5C9942.B92F8273@Azonic.co.nz> <3D5C93D6.62D608D3@shaw.ca> <217e491a.0208161505.21ccf61b@posting.google.com> <ajk1bo$ek4$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> From the true world of "trivia".  IBM's COBOL compilers actually INCLUDED
> full support for Pounds, Shilling, Pence as "USAGE DISPLAY-ST".  This
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Don't want the Standard's God rushing off researching - but - did IBM allow for
following in the character set :-

1/4d = farthing,  1/2d = halfpenny (ha'penny) and 3/4d = three farthings ?

Along with the pound symbol we had those three characters on our early printer
barrels.

You are probably 'happier' with Standard's God rather than Horace Rumpole <G>.
Poor old Horace - Australian actor Leo McKern at 82 has recently died in
Bristol. Apart from his lovable performance in Rumpole, great movie actor -
stubborn cardinal in 'Shoes of the Fisherman' along with another 'favourite',
Anthony Quinn as the Ukrainian Pope and Laurence Olivier. As the Tudor era
Thomas Cromwell, (no relation to Oliver), the prosecutor of Sir Thomas More in
one of my *very* favourite movies 'A Man for all Seasons'.

Seeing Doc's quote - made me think he was thinking of a different tune - not so
- for the 'Uniformed', the following from an American site WASP Songs (White
Anglo Saxon Protestants ?) :-

                        I've got sixpence, jolly, jolly sixpence,
                                 I've got sixpence to last me all my life.
                             I've got twopence to spend and twopence to lend
                            And twopence to send home to my wife, poor wife.

                         No cares have I to grieve me, no tall and handsome man
                            To deceive me, I'm happy as a queen, believe me,
                                    As we go rolling, rolling home.

                          Rolling home, rolling home, rolling home, rolling
home,
                                    By the light of the silvery moon,
                            Happy is the day when the Air Corps gets its pay,
                                    As we go rolling, rolling home.

                                I've got fourpence, jolly, jolly fourpence,
                                 I've got fourpence to last me all my life,
                            I've got twopence to spend and twopence to lend,
                            And no pence to send home to my wife, poor wife.

                                 I've got twopence, jolly, jolly twopence,
                                 I've got twopence to last me all my life,
                             I've got twopence to spend and no pence to lend,
                            And no pence to send home to my wife, poor wife!

***  'twopence' is pronounced 'Tuppence' in the UK.

Did I ever tell you about the lady who walked into the Debenhams store in
Taunton, two days after we switched to decimal currency, ('73 ?). She looked at
the handful of 'strange' change she was given and mumbled, "I'll give this
system about a month, before it blows up !".

What's all this got to do with COBOL ? Absolutely bugger all ! But it's FUN <g>

Jimmy
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-17T12:33:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajm1da$efs$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
This is a "follow-up" from a thread a while ago about how to "specify" the
debug dataset (created with SYM,SEPARATE) at run-time.   I just received the
following from the LE documentation people (probably NOT the answer you
want, but ....)

"Thank you for bringing this comment to our attention.

The z/OS Language Environment Debugging Guide will be updated in the next
edition to add information, including that the user cannot change the DDName
or DSName specified <at compile-time> as SYSDEBUG at run time for Language
Environment dumps."

PLEASE NOTE WELL:
  That is the way things work today.  I believe that IBM would seriously
LOOK AT changing this, if an "enhancement request" came in via proper
channels (i.e. SHARE, or service REQUEST).
```

<a id="t-b76e728187"></a>
## Thread: Procedure-Pointers (IBM) (was: Cobol and Performance — 1 msgs

### Procedure-Pointers (IBM) (was: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-17T12:42:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajm1ti$db2$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254> <aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <6t3tlugko1ijnk8sv3odua2215jtfhev9e@4ax.com>`

```
Anyone thinking about using PROCEDURE-POINTERs should review the information
at:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/4.1.6

Given the fact that this is being talked about in relationship to programs
that call the same subprogram 2 or more times, you should PARTICULARLY note
the restriction that says,

"If you set your pointer item to an entry address in a dynamically called
load module and your program subsequently cancels that dynamically called
module, then your pointer item becomes undefined. Reference to it thereafter
is not reliable."
```

<a id="t-03648e0da1"></a>
## Thread: What does GoBack do? — 8 msgs

### What does GoBack do?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-17T15:41:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajmckh$2i9$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I wanted to start a new thread that is a follow-up on the OO Exception
Handling and GoBack discussions.

It seems to me that there is some confusion about MIXING "procedural" and
"OO" COBOL source code.

The draft Standard FULLY allows an INVOKED method to CALL a "procedural"
subprogram *and* vice versa

Every implementation that I know of (so far) also allows this.

THEREFORE, when you are in a "subprogram" - you don't know if you got there
from a method or another "procedural program".  Similarly, when you are in a
"method" - you don't know if you got there from another "procedural program"
or method.

This is why the draft standard introduces the concept of "activating
entity".

Please, when you are talking about either GoBack or "exception handling",
remember that you don't know "how you got here".  Any "solution" you talk
about MUST take this into consideration.
```

#### ↳ Re: What does GoBack do?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-18T02:05:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D5F0252.C8830EC8@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ajmckh$2i9$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> I wanted to start a new thread that is a follow-up on the OO Exception
> Handling and GoBack discussions.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Trust you to stir the mud <G>.

I can't disagree with what you have written, but back to my three types of COBOL
'applications' :-

- Procedural - no OO
- Procedural - invoke OO support classes as necessary
- OO Classes ONLY

You start 'shoving' procedural calls into OO classes will *certainly* offend
Rick's pure OO. Dammit it even offends ME <G>. I think you are going to finish
up with a real mish-mash. Design wise I think you should be making a firm
decision  between using #2 or #3 above.

#2 presents a 'reasonable' approach, and is probably the route most COBOLers
will go, IF they switch to OO at some time. Choice #3 presents a different
conceptual approach - with a tremendous amount of planning of the support
hierarchies up front before you get into your business classes, (Edit
files/transactions/printing etc.).

If you look at Michael Todd's message 'Migrating IBM programs....", some 18,000
- phew ! - could possibly be a candidate for migrating to OO - IF there were the
IBM tools to do it. With something that size, are they splitting business
'units' down into 'sub-programs' for convenience or is it in large part calling
generic sub-routines.
If  'generics' - a good candidate for thinking at the hierarchical level in OO
support classes, plus as you'll recall from our INVOKING UP/DOWN - the ability
to 'attack' (invoke a method) either 'upwards' from the lowest level of the
hierarchy or 'downwards' from the prime class.

Another interesting point - relating to #3 (totally OO) - your comment about
procedure pointers :-

"If you set your pointer item to an entry address in a dynamically called
load module and your program subsequently cancels that dynamically called
module, then your pointer item becomes undefined. Reference to it thereafter
is not reliable."

That problem just doesn't exist in OO. You determine at the design stage that
the invoked class will :-

- contain only Factory methods - invoke something/return something - finito -
the class is no longer 'active' after the invoke

- create an Object instance :-

        1 - do something, store something in global W/S,  return something
        2 - do something, get the stored something in W/S from #1 and return
                    something
        3 - after #'s 1 and 2 the class instance is still 'active' until
explicitly 'destroyed'

I'm not trying to suggest that switching those 18,000 programs to OO is a piece
of cake. Procedural or OO there is still a LOT of work. And in a transitional
stage you may well still be calling procedural programs. However, in the long
term,  I think they will have lost the overall benefits of OO constructs, if
those calls are not converted to OO invokes.

Now the interesting thing is where you point out :-

"THEREFORE, when you are in a "subprogram" - you don't know if you got there
from a method or another "procedural program".  Similarly, when you are in a
"method" - you don't know if you got there from another "procedural program"
or method."

Do you realize you're making my case for the suggested syntax  EXIT CLASS ?
Procedural source - GOBACK/EXIT PROGRAM and for OO source (#3) - EXIT CLASS <G>

Trust me - any solution I'm thinking of, applies to Option # 3 above.

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What does GoBack do?

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-18T02:18:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1uD79.57132$eK6.2060284@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<ajmckh$2i9$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3D5F0252.C8830EC8@shaw.ca>`

```
Oh, I just have to throw in an oar here.... <grin>

It doesn't matter one bit whether or not you are in a PROCEDURE or a CLASS when a GOBACK is invoked, since BOTH types of software
modules are handled with the equivalent of a BALR (i.e. subroutine) call. At least on the mainframe. Now on something like a SPARC,
which is MUCH more of a stack based machine than a 370 based mainframe, it may be a different story, but not if the compiler
writers are really smart.

Much as it shows my age, there are certain things that are just done the same way as they have been done for decades, simply because
they work. And BALR and it's descendants work well. You can't get a whole lot more efficient than the way they operate, and almost
every application out there uses the "standard" parameter storage and R1 parameter passing conventions. They work very well.

When you get down to nuts and bolts implementation, it doesn't matter much if you are in Java or in COBOL or in almost any other
language; the method invocations or subroutine calls or LINKS or PERFORMs or whatever mechanism you choose to implement for software
modules will operate like that.
<grin>

As an aside, I know a certain very vocal Java Evangelist who was literally MORTIFIED to discover this truth, and note that a COBOL
PERFORM was being implemented almost exactly like a OO method invocation. Mortified is not really adequate - he was actually struck
speechless for a full 30 seconds.
(Were I to fill in a name here, you would might recognize it and have a great big belly laugh. :)

Okay - I'll shut up now and go back to listening.... Both you guys are people whose opinions I respect. One day I will have to
journey to your home territories and purchase you quantities of your favorite brew...

-Paul


"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:3D5F0252.C8830EC8@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[98 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Paul Raulerson wrote:

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

I agree Paul, and once thought of attempting to illustrate that to Grinder. A Procedural program with a set of perform Paragraphs is no
different to invoking methods. (It would be a slovenly way of doing it, but you could turn Paragraphs into methods). Similarly using
entry points to call from another program is parallel to invoking methods in another class.

It's this damned 'fuzzy' exiting a class QUICKLY (not aborting) that's the problem.

> PERFORM was being implemented almost exactly like a OO method invocation. Mortified is not really adequate - he was actually struck
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Traditional <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What does GoBack do?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-18T12:56:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208181156.5d55c1c2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ajmckh$2i9$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3D5F0252.C8830EC8@shaw.ca> <1uD79.57132$eK6.2060284@twister.austin.rr.com> <3D5F165C.3E886E8B@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote 

> I agree Paul, and once thought of attempting to illustrate that to Grinder. A Procedural program with a set of perform Paragraphs is no
> different to invoking methods. (It would be a slovenly way of doing it, but you could turn Paragraphs into methods). Similarly using
> entry points to call from another program is parallel to invoking methods in another class.

It seems to me that methods are not like paragraphs at all, but are
nested programs.
 
> It's this damned 'fuzzy' exiting a class QUICKLY (not aborting) that's the problem.

I am not sure at all what would constitute 'exiting a class'.  It is
only the methods that are invoked.  It seems that what is wanted is
like doing an EXIT PROGRAM after several layers of perform of a
procedural program, but this is an invalid model. 'Exiting a Class' is
nonsense because the class is not called, the methods are.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What does GoBack do?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-19T02:41:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D605C63.A407109A@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ajmckh$2i9$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3D5F0252.C8830EC8@shaw.ca> <1uD79.57132$eK6.2060284@twister.austin.rr.com> <3D5F165C.3E886E8B@shaw.ca> <217e491a.0208181156.5d55c1c2@posting.google.com>`

```


Richard wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The above was a 'loose' comparison for convenience. Richard you are correct to a degree, and picking up on something Donald wrote earlier,
methods are in fact 'mini programs', and as per the current state of the Standard, function as mini programs with the exception of File
definitions, Declaratives and Working Storage. Haven't looked, but logically Report Writer and Screen Section would also be excluded, needing
to be 'globally' accessed within a particular class from specific methods.

Methods aren't automatically 'nested' programs, but *could* be :-

- Not nested - invoke CharacterArray using this string returning thisDisplayObject.
(In and Out from the Class CharacterArray)

- Nested - invoke Class1 "begin", invoke self "Method2" or invoke Class2 "begin",
etc.

It would be daft to do it but you could take a full-blown Procedural program and immediately prior to MAINLINE, insert :-

*>------
Factory.
*>------
Method-id. "mainline".
*>----------------
Procedure Division.
MAINLINE.
perform Para1
perform Para2 etc....

End Method "mainlihne".
*>------
END FACTORY

END CLASS XYZ.

>
> > It's this damned 'fuzzy' exiting a class QUICKLY (not aborting) that's the problem.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> nonsense because the class is not called, the methods are.

Semantics - classes are called as evidenced by referencing objects in the Animator :-

CLASS-ID.        MaterialsEdit  inherits from Base.
                           (8D 01 00 00 Class MaterialsEdit)
                            (04 00 00 00 Class Base)

FACTORY
*>-------
Procedure Division using lnk-App.
 invoke super   "new" returning ls-self
 invoke ls-self "begin" using lnk-App
 ls-self (92 01 00 00 instance of the class MaterialsEdit - but this reference is lost on exiting this method)
End Method "new".
*>-----------------
END FACTORY.
*>-----------
OBJECT.
*>----------------------------------------------
Method-id. "begin".
*>----------------------------------------------
Linkage Section.
01 lnk-App                  object reference.
Procedure Division using lnk-App.

 set os-App to lnk-App
 invoke self "getHandles"
        self =  (92 01 00 00 instance of the class MaterialsEdit)
 invoke self "createDialog"
 set ContinueProgram to true
etc.....

Exactly how the compiler/runtime handles this, I have no idea. But the start point, whether using methods solely in FACTORY or using "new" to
create an Object Instance,  is the Class.

The Animator shows no object references for Method names. A guess - using dictionary techniques, methods are qualified from a dictionary for
this class - which may mean 'extracting' the information from the compiled source residing on disk, or if already activated and cached, based
on previous usage, a 'pointer' to the start of the method detail held in memory.

*Knowing* the class identity, (Factory or Instance) - hence my tentative suggestion of  'EXIT CLASS'.

However, I've concluded - it's not worth the hassle - I'll stick with what I have, "If Continue Program.....do this......".

Jimmy
```

<a id="t-0a2e95815d"></a>
## Thread: Migrating IBM COBOL from old levels to newer levels — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Re: Migrating IBM COBOL from old levels to newer levels

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-18T09:38:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D5F6B00.6006DC34@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8n5tlu4udn0dthot3jt063afae7mrun9su@4ax.com>`

```
Michael Todd wrote:
> 
> It seems that a lot of IBM shops have COBOL programs that haven't been compiled in a while (more than a decade?).  I
> thought it might be useful to start a thread about the experiences (both good and bad--well, especially the bad) of
> those who have moved COBOL code from older compilers to new.

This year, my shop (three 9672 G6 boxes) upgraded from OS/390 2.8 to
2.10, turned on Work Load Manager in Goal mode for all
CICS/Transaction Server regions, and just completed upgrading to LE
ALL31(ON) as the default in all LPAR's.  We had been converting OS/VS
COBOL to COBOL II or higher beginning in 1999 (for Y2K, of course). 
We had to add the ALL31(OFF) override to several COBOL programs,
generally because they used assembler modules that had 24-bit
restrictions.

> (snip)
> SO, ALL YOU FOLKS THAT HAVE EXPERIENCE MIGRATING OLD COBOL TO NEW COBOL, PLEASE respond with your successes and
> tragedies!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

Okay.

> 
> We converted all of our OS/VS COBOL to COBOL for MVS 5 years ago.  Yes, there is a lot of work, but we used the IBM
> conversion utility to do more than 95% of the source code changes.  We were also changing from DYNAM to NODYNAM, so we
> wrote a clist to do make those source code changes for us.

Well, you were ahead of us.  We had a lot of groups that were very
resistant to leaving OS/VS COBOL behind.  On the other hand, our Tech
Services gurus have decreed that the COBOL default will by DYNAM
forevermore (except for CICS/Transaction Server, which absolutely
requires NODYNAM).

> 
> PROBLEMS?  Only 1, computes work differently.  Any/all computes with more than 2 terms can produce different results.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>         COMPUTE s9(5)v9(4) = (s9(5) / 100.0000) + 1

That's very interesting.  I don't recall running into this [particular
problem, but I will be looking for it in the future.

I seem to recall there were some differences with PERFORM VARYING
VARYING between OS/VS COBOL and COBOL II, but we don't seem to have
any problems.

> (snip)
> Considering we changed over 9,000 modules from OS/VS COBOL to COBOL for MVS and the compute was the only problem
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> conversion).

I came to this shop in 1987 after being in a VM & VSE shop.  The first
time I saw "BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS" I thought it looked a little
peculiar, but I copied it.  It worked well with OS/VS COBOL.  I have
seen problems with a couple of programs that omitted BLOCK CONTAINS
for QSAM files (defaulting to BLOCK CONTAINS 1 RECORD) and those
programs had long runtimes.  This is easily fixed, once you understand
the problem.  But it's not strictly a new COBOL issue.
 
> 
> When migrating from COBOL 2 prior to v3, we had another issue.  We had less than a dozen modules with variably located
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> fields which precede it.

We have been very lucky in my shop.  I can't even recall seeing OCCURS
DEPENDING ON in a production program.  There are just some constructs
that no one here ever uses, like DECLARATIVES, or MOVE/ADD
CORRESPONDING.

> (snip)
> WHAT ABOUT AMODE 24?  Well, that's been a headache all along.  We have already migrated most of our amode 24 assembler
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> amode24 callers, the called routines are compiled with RMODE24.

Assembler has been our biggest problem getting to ALL31(ON).  Compared
to ten years ago, there are hardly any applications programmers left
with any depth in assembler programming.  I've done some, but I am no
expert at assembler.  Converting a fifteen or twenty year old
assembler subprogram to AMODE(31),RMODE(ANY) can range from easy to
very tricky to downright impossible.  In some cases, the best that
could be done with certain subprograms was AMODE(31),RMODE(24).  

Sometime in the early to mid 1980's, before I got to this shop, they
converted from DOS/VSE and ISAM to MVS and VSAM.  They had an
assembler subprogram used to load COBOL subprograms dynamically, and a
general purpose subprogram to access VSAM files.  I didn't understand
it at the time, but everyone used these routines with OS/VS COBOL
programs even though there was no need to.  OS/VS COBOL was perfectly
capable of calling a parogram dynamically (even with the NODYNAM
compile option) by using a variable containing the name of the called
subprogram.  OS/VS COBOL was perfectly capable of accessing VSAM files
with native COBOL verbs.  But everyone continued to use the old
assembler subprograms, which were AMODE(24),RMODE(24) because they
were last assembled before MVS/XA existed.  The lesson here is that
bad practices can endure for a very long time, and can be very
difficult to eliminate. 


> 
> Keeping track of what can call what (amode and rmode wise) has been a huge effort, but it is paying off.  A few years
> ago we had relatively frequent, but sporadic abends for "insufficient below the line storage available."  Now, it's
> unheard of.  Additionally, a lot of programs are now getting more file buffers than before (improving both VSAM and QSAM
> IO).

One of our prime motivators for getting to the LE ALL31(ON) option is
the shrinking amount of below-the-line storage available to COBOL
applications with each new release of MVS/OS390/zOS.  We had some
COBOL programs with Working-storage close to 8 megabytes due to large
internal tables.  Some of these programs would ceased running with
OS/390 2.10.

We also had one *very* large COBOL application that was rewritten in
such a way that it could *not* run or be tested in an LPAR with
ALL31(OFF).  Tech Services also told us that 31-bit COBOL programs
would run faster that 24-bit COBOL programs once we got to ALL31(ON)
in every LPAR.  I converted an OS/VS program to COBOL for OS/390 & VM
2.2 earlier this year, and TS is recommending we recompile all COBOL
II programs with the current compiler.  At least the COBOL II programs
should not need any source code changes for that.  I believe a large
part of the resistance to change was that our OS/VS COBOL programs
typically would not compile under COBOL II or higher.  Typical issues
were CURRENT-DATE (the old special register), EXAMINE/TRANSFORM,
EXHIBIT NAMED, and BEFORE/AFTER POSITIONING.

> (snip)
> ADVICE?  While I'm understandably reticent to offer advice to anyone outside of my shop, I will say a few things.
> (snip)
> I had expected thousands of issues that simply never materialized.  I'm not saying that migrating old code is painless
> and "hazard free."  I am saying that I no longer fret over the risks.  It is doable.  

I agree with you.  It is doable, but there will be some problems.  The
kind of problems you have will depend on how you have been using COBOL
in your shop.

> (snip)
> What do I know now that I wished I had known then?  Compile options, for one.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> would have been very, VERY few.  

TRUNC(OPT) is our mandated default.  I have used it with CICS and, to
a lesser extend, DB2 (I don't do a lot of DB2 programming myself).  I
have never experienced a problem caused by using TRUNC(OPT) rather
than TRUNC(BIN).

> (snip)
> NUMPROC.  OS/VS COBOL didn't have this compile option either.  According to IBM, OS/VS COBOL always generated
> NUMPROC(NOPFD) code.  I haven't been able to verify whether that statement is 100% correct.  NUMPROC(MIG) generates more
> efficient code than NUMPROC(PFD)  Both MIG and PFD are ANSI compliant.  NUMPROC(NOPFD) generates the most efficient
> code, though it can produce non ANSI compatible results.  We are switching to MIG, but I wished we had converted with

My understanding is that NUMPROC(NOPFD) is less efficient than
NUMPROC(MIG), because it generates more sign fixup code.  Nevertheless
I prefer NUMPROC(NOPFD), which is also our default, because the output
is better.  I have had at least one COBOL II Program that abended with
S0C7 under NUMPROC(MIG), but completed successfully with
NUMPROC(NOPFD) and produced correct results.

Given the nature of our processing, we could not ensure that input
data had valid signs according to the PICTURE clause in all cases. 
Therefore, NUMPROC(PFD), which actually generates the most efficient
code would also generate more incorrect results, like -0 or unsigned
zero not beintg equal to +0.

And while I may appear to contradict myself here, I agree with you
that I would rather have the program abend with S0C7 than complete
successfully with "unexpected results".


> NOPFD.  The main reason I like NOPFD over MIG over PFD isn't performance (though that's my cost justification).  The
> real reason is because MIG and especially NOPFD encourage S0C7 abends when working on invalid numeric data.  YES I SAID
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> mainframe machines, you probably need ANSI compatible signs, i.e., use NUMPROC(MIG) or NUMPROC(PFD).

Remember that no matter what NUMPROC compile option you use, the
TARGET field will always have a sign that matches the PICTURE clause. 
The problem is:  what kind of results will you get if your SOURCE
field has an invalid sign, or even non-numeric data?

While we're on compile options, some others that we use are NOSSRANGE
and OFFSET.  Batch programs are typically NORENT.

> 
> I wish we had done more in the way of training.  Two weeks after converting a program from OS/VS COBOL to COBOL for MVS
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> they had a problem which went unnoticed for a while because it didn't abend.  This created a huge data clean up issue.

Well, if you had used TRUNC(OPT) you might not have had a problem at
all.  But it's very difficult to foresee these kinds of problems.   

> (snip)
> I recommend that every shop "look" at migrating their old code.  The newer compilers simply generate faster and more
> robust code.  We're now finding a greater percentage of issues on our test platforms rather than in production.  If
> you're concerned that recompiling old code under a newer compiler might cause problems, you may be fretting over it more
> than it deserves; at least I was.

I agree, every shop should recompile OS/VS COBOL programs with the
most current COBOL compiler.  On those rare occasions when something
blows up in the middle of the night and the only solution is a program
change, you will be very unhappy if the program also requires syntax
changes for the current COBOL compiler.

> 
> I stated at the very beginning that old load modules can have issues under z/OS.  This is what we have found so far.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> So, be careful using newer COBOL options to invoke old COBOL modules.

This is a good point.  We are fortunate that we didn't start using
procedure pointers until we got to COBOL for MVS & VM.  And so far
only that one *very* large COBOL application, the one that was
rewritten, is even using procedure pointers.

> 
> We have also migrated our LE runtime to LE for z/OS v1r3.  We're still running OS390 v2r10, but we upgraded LE in
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> copy the SYSOUTs that were never opened.

One thing we do with our batch programs is that every program displays
three lines at the start of execution, something like this:

WS921010-0000 UPDATE BILLING MASTER FILE
WS921010-0001 COMPILED 2000-03-15 AT 15:35:57
WS921010-0002 EXECUTED 2002-08-17 AT 01:00:03

If you're called in the middle of the night, you can look at the
SYSOUT and immediately tell if a program has been recently changed. 
On the other hand, we don't have programs that read or copy COBOL
SYSOUT.  I suppose you could DISPLAY a blank line, or some other
innocuous message, during program initialization.  That would at least
guarantee that your SYSOUT is allocated.

> 
> We originally reported this as a bug, but IBM responded that it wasn't a bug, it's a feature.  I can't argue with their
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> as we go.  If this thread gets much attention, I'll post other issues as we find them.

I would certainly be interested in hearing about them.  We're
replacing two of our 9672's with 2064's this month (z900), but we
probably won't get to z/OS until next year.

Thanks much!
```

## Thread: Cobol and Performance — 2 msgs

### Re: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-18T10:02:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D5F709F.89D88CFC@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254> <aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <6t3tlugko1ijnk8sv3odua2215jtfhev9e@4ax.com>`

```
Michael Todd wrote:
> 
> Well, since so many people have added to this thread I thought I would throw in our most recent "performance" discovery.

According to an applications performance guru in my shop, youi can get
even better performance on a DYNAM procedure-pointer call by omitting
the USING parameters and using EXTERNAL data to communicate between
the programs.

You must use the DYNAM compiler option to ensure the procedure-pointer
call is dynamic.  You must then add the "IS EXTERNAL" clause to the 01
leve of all shared records (in both the calling program and the called
program, which must have identical data definitions for the shared
EXTERNAL data).  Then your "CALL procedure-pointer USING parms"
statement can be changed to just "CALL procedure-pointer".

Once you do this, the CALL statement is not only faster than a static
CALL, it's equivalent to a PERFORM of a local subroutine!


> 
> Using procedure pointers reduces the overhead of a dynamic call by 48% to 52%.  It's pretty consistent.  48% benefit (to
…[66 more quoted lines elided]…
> >   http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igypg205/PREFACE.6.1
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-19T08:25:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4i689.23563$%P3.1611215@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254> <aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <6t3tlugko1ijnk8sv3odua2215jtfhev9e@4ax.com> <3D5F709F.89D88CFC@worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Arnold Trembley" <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>
> According to an applications performance guru in my shop, youi can get
> even better performance on a DYNAM procedure-pointer call by omitting
> the USING parameters and using EXTERNAL data to communicate between
> the programs.


Depends on the compiler a bit, but your guru is correct.  Unless you
need to pass the arguments on the stack for some reason, this is much
the better way.  And no shooting yourself in the foot by using explicit
pointers.
```

<a id="t-638fb68937"></a>
## Thread: Does Vax Cobol V4.4 comply with Cobol-85 standard? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Does Vax Cobol V4.4 comply with Cobol-85 standard?

- **From:** mor2346@gmx.net (morsi pan)
- **Date:** 2002-08-18T03:03:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.os.vms,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<792abe54.0208180203.1a199c95@posting.google.com>`

```
I am working on a project with OpenVMS Vax V5.5-2 and Cobol V4.4.
Because it is an old system and I cannot find any information about
Cobol v4.4.
Can anybody help me with the following question?
1. I want to know if vax cobol v4.4 comply with Cobol-85 standard?
2. If not, where can I find vax cobol v4.4 information?
3. If I compile cobol program in Compaq Cobol V5.7 with Vax OpenVMS
7.2,Can I port the program into OpenVMS with Vax Cobol V4.4 system?

Thanks in advance.

Morsi Pan
mor2346@gmx.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Does Vax Cobol V4.4 comply with Cobol-85 standard?

- **From:** Didier Morandi <Didier.Morandi@Free.fr>
- **Date:** 2002-08-19T10:16:29+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D60A95C.E8DF0E5C@Free.fr>`
- **References:** `<792abe54.0208180203.1a199c95@posting.google.com>`

```
morsi pan wrote:
> 
> I am working on a project with OpenVMS Vax V5.5-2 and Cobol V4.4.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1. I want to know if vax cobol v4.4 comply with Cobol-85 standard?

According to my V5.4 doc for VMS 6.1, COBOL prior to version 5 was COBOL 74.
VAX/VMS Cobol v5.* is conform to the 1985 ANSI COBOL Standard. To me, the answer
is no.

> 2. If not, where can I find vax cobol v4.4 information?

What for? All further releases of DEC compilers such like COBOL and FORTRAN are
downwards compatible, i.e. "old" features are present in the doc (and the
compiler) of any new version.

> 3. If I compile cobol program in Compaq Cobol V5.7 with Vax OpenVMS
> 7.2,Can I port the program into OpenVMS with Vax Cobol V4.4 system?

Compiling and linking produces binary images which deal with the operating
system, not the compiler anymore. As far as I know, all and every programs
successfully build with VMS 1.0 should be able to be recompiled and linked
successfully for any furhter version of VMS (VAX *and* Alpha). The answer is yes.

VMS is VMS.

The best.

Period.

D.
```

#### ↳ Re: Does Vax Cobol V4.4 comply with Cobol-85 standard?

- **From:** don.braffitt@hp.com (Don Braffitt)
- **Date:** 2002-08-23T05:58:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.os.vms,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e5c311b8.0208230458.415318af@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<792abe54.0208180203.1a199c95@posting.google.com>`

```
mor2346@gmx.net (morsi pan) wrote in message news:<792abe54.0208180203.1a199c95@posting.google.com>...
> I am working on a project with OpenVMS Vax V5.5-2 and Cobol V4.4.
> Because it is an old system and I cannot find any information about
> Cobol v4.4.
> Can anybody help me with the following question?
> 1. I want to know if vax cobol v4.4 comply with Cobol-85 standard?

Yes.  VAX COBOL V4.4 supported the ANSI-85 standard and some
differences related to the ANSI-74 standard.  See HELP COBOL /STANDARD
for some details.  These /STANDARD differences are still supported in
Compaq COBOL V5.7 for OpenVMS VAX.  If you have the .COM files used to
build the COBOL applications with VAX COBOL V4.4, you should recompile
using the same /STANDARD option (if any) used when the applications
were built with V4.4.  The defaut in both V4.4 and V5.7 is
/STANDARD=85.

VAX COBOL V5.0 added support for the 1989 intrinsic functions addendum
to the ANSI-85 standard, but this should not impact the upgrade
project you are doing now.

> 2. If not, where can I find vax cobol v4.4 information?

The best starting point for details on changes to COBOL on VAX since
V4.4 is the release notes:

  http://www.openvms.compaq.com/commercial/cobol/VAX_COBOL_RN_V57.htm

What we've tried to do in these release note is give a comprehensive
summary of all changes which have gone into the product on VAX over
the last 8 releases since V4.4.

The Compaq COBOL V5.7 for OpenVMS VAX documentation (User Manual and
Reference Manual) is available at

  http://www.openvms.compaq.com/doc/#cobol

This documentation covers V2.7 on Alpha (OpenVMS and Tru64 UNIX) and
V5.7 on OpenVMS VAX.
  
> 3. If I compile cobol program in Compaq Cobol V5.7 with Vax OpenVMS
> 7.2,Can I port the program into OpenVMS with Vax Cobol V4.4 system?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> mor2346@gmx.net

Yes.  As stated in the earlier replies, the intent with Compaq COBOL
V5.7 for OpenVMS VAX is that you can recompile and relink applications
which had previously been built with an older version of VAX COBOL.

For performance reasons, you should look at the /INSTRUCTION_SET
qualifier as part of your recompilation effort.  COBOL applications
generally perform best on modern VAX systems when compiled with the
NODECIMAL option.  The DECIMAL option (still the default for
compatibility with VAX COBOL V4 and earlier) was designed for early
VAX systems like the 780 and 750 with non-emulaed decimal
instructions.

I am assuming you are upgrading to a version of OpenVMS more recent
than V5.5-2.  Compaq COBOL V5.7 requires OpenVMS VAX V6.2 as a
minimum.  Compaq COBOL V5.7 is the single version of COBOL on VAX
currently supported by HP engineering meaning there is no support
today by HP engineering for any version of VAX COBOL, DIGITAL VAX
COBOL, or Compaq COBOL on VAX/VMS V5.5-2.

- Don Braffitt
  Compaq COBOL and SORT project leader
```

- **From:** FAN_CN <member@mainframeforum.com>

#### ↳ Re: console command

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-08-19T19:14:49+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<190820021914494915%josephk@iinet.net.au>`

```
In article <3d606d84$1_7@news5.nntpserver.com>, FAN_CN
<member@mainframeforum.com> wrote:

> hi who can tell me how can I issue console command with COBOL, thanks.
> 
> 
Do you mean an z/OS or MVS console command?
If so, you cannot do it from COBOL natively.
You will have to either:
1. Invoke a TSO CONSOLE command via the TSO Interface API.
2. Attach an EMCS console and issue the command via MGCR system macros
in an assembler subroutine - which must run authorized.
```

- **From:** "Rick S." <ricks123@hotpop.com>

```
"Rick S." <ricks123@hotpop.com> wrote:
>
> I thought that all those YR2K problems where for
> big old COBOL programs ?  Guess not...

The only reason COBOL was such an issue for Y2K is because the
number of old programs in COBOL dwarfed the number of old
programs written in every other language put together.  There is not
one construct in COBOL 85/89 of which I am aware that has in any
way made COBOL more vulnerable to Y2K than any other language.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: funny VC++ version 4 YR2K bug

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-19T08:47:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajqt4v$ir$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<21790eb9.0208190130.3b5a89f@posting.google.com> <9o689.23598$%P3.1612455@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
I guess that I (very SLIGHTLY) disagree with you on this Judson.

The fact that the "Standard" ACCEPT from DATE/DAY *only* got a 2-digit year
in all existing ANSI/ISO Standards (and most implementations WELL into the
90's) did "encourage" the use of 2-digit years.  (Many implementation did
add the YYYYMMDD and YYYYDDD extensions before the year 2000 - but not by
"much")

Having said that, I don't think that this was the MAJOR reason why COBOL got
so much of the focus for Y2K issues.  I do think that the "majority" of
COBOL problems were based  on the fact that many COBOL programs were written
when "storage" was expensive and/or the programmer never thought their
programs would stay in production long enough to need to think about a
4-digit year.  Those that didn't have problems because of those two things
were PROBABLY either started from a "stub" program that did have one of
those problems or were written by a programmer who "grew up" with those
issues.
```

```
written

> 4-digit year.

The limited 80-column capacity of punched cards was a, if not the, major
factor.

Some programmers and analysts did foresee this, and did try to include the
century.
Management would invariably veto the idea.

I'm supremely curious as to when the Y2100 issue will become a major issue
and start to impact companies using 20 or 25 as their windowing control
factor.
```

- **References:** `<21790eb9.0208190130.3b5a89f@posting.google.com> <9o689.23598$%P3.1612455@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <ajqt4v$ir$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <W%a89.20018$Ke2.1628798@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <01c247d5$7e844900$c4c2f943@chottel>`

```
Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:01c247d5$7e844900$c4c2f943@chottel...
> It won't take that long.  I know of one Y2K conversion that left six digit
> dates on all the CICS screens.  Many of the 5000+  programs have a lot of
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank God I'll be retired by then.

The board members at a lot of companies are going to hit the roof
when they find out that the "millennium" problem, on which billions was
expended to resolve, will soon return, as it was a "century" problem all
along.

Postulating a worst case scenario (I hope), change 39 in your example to 20,
and take the case where a company needs to quote and book sales orders
10 years into the future. That scenario leaves such a company with
less than 8 years to remediate all over again.  It truly boggles the mind.

The significant issue is, you cannot add any "I hope I'm wrong" disclaimer.
The question isn't "Will it happen?".  The question is "When will it
begin?".

This also doesn't address an issue which was not a Y2K factor.
Next time around, there will be 3 possible century years to cope with,
namely 19, 20 and 21.

On the PC side of the house, last I heard, MS software has a default window
of 30.  To their credit, the MS site is plastered with cautions not to use
windowing
due to the possibility of ambiguity in century date calculations.

IMO, that probability can safely be upgraded to a guarantee.
```

```
"Liam Devlin" <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote in message
news:kVg89.74153$EJ4.2206521@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net...
> Hugh Candlin wrote:
> > Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
> > news:01c247d5$7e844900$c4c2f943@chottel...
> >
> >>It won't take that long.  I know of one Y2K conversion that left six
digit
> >>dates on all the CICS screens.
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> cheaply. Nothing personal, BTW, just business.

You reminded me of the following joke:

There was an engineer who had an exceptional gift for fixing all things
mechanical. After serving his company loyally for over 30 years, he happily
retired.

Several years later, the company contacted him regarding a seemingly
impossible problem they were having with one of their multi-million dollar
machines. They had tried everything and everyone to get the machine fixed,
but to no avail. In desperation, they called on the retired engineer who had
solved so many of their problems in the past.

The engineer reluctantly took the challenge. He spent a day studying the
huge machine. At the end of the day, he marked a small "x" in chalk on a
particular component of the machine and proudly stated, "This is where your
problem is."

The part was replaced and the machine worked perfectly again. When the
company received a bill for $50,000 from the engineer for his services they
were reluctant to pay it. "This seems a bit excessive for one chalk mark,"
he was told, "Perhaps you'd better itemize your charges."

Within a few days, they received the following itemized bill:

One chalk mark . . . . . . . . . . . . . $1
Knowing where to put it . . . . . . . $49,999
```

<a id="t-796fb287d6"></a>
## Thread: Cobol and Performance — 21 msgs

### Re: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-19T08:21:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<He689.23544$%P3.1610722@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254> <aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <6t3tlugko1ijnk8sv3odua2215jtfhev9e@4ax.com>`

```
"Michael Todd" <mikltodd@comcast.net> wrote:
>
> Using procedure pointers reduces the overhead of a dynamic call
> by 48% to 52%.  <snip>

Okay, but I am very happy I won't have to fix the unbelievable number
of incredibly subtle bugs you are *definitely* going to create using this
methodology.  If you don't believe it, then you simply don't have the
experience to know.  If the ALTER statement was bad (and it was),
this will be a disaster.  A typical large C program is several times as
difficult to debug as a similarly complex COBOL program.  Pointers
are at the top of the reasons why this is so.  How many 'blue screen'
hang-ups and GPFs have you seen in Windows?  Well, errant pointers
are what cause those.

You use pointers when there is no better way.  Anything else is shooting
yourself in the foot (or head) on purpose.  I say this simply to establish
my credibility, but as one who has written maybe a million lines of
assembly and C (much of which has been system level code), and
maintained that code over lots of years (I 'cut my teeth' on assembly),
I have used pointers and pointers to pointers countless times, and have
forgotten more about using pointers than most of my fellow COBOL
programmers know.  Pointers can turn and bite you in the butt quicker
than just about anything.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-19T16:53:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Fo989.3203$yt3.1352827@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254> <aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <6t3tlugko1ijnk8sv3odua2215jtfhev9e@4ax.com> <He689.23544$%P3.1610722@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:He689.23544$%P3.1610722@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com...
> "Michael Todd" <mikltodd@comcast.net> wrote:
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> How many 'blue screen'  hang-ups and GPFs have you seen in Windows?  Well,
errant pointers
> are what cause those....
> You use pointers when there is no better way  Anything else is shooting
> yourself in the foot (or head) on purpose.....

Excuse me?  You, sir, have  been around long enough to know that it's not
errant pointers which cause GPF and BSOD errors; it's errant PROGRAMMERS.

Pointer variables are IMNSHO an EXCELLENT way to enhance performance.

That said, I do not believe a whole lot of shops would want their code
written that way, since 'recent grad' and 'body shop' maintenance will have
been effectively foreclosed.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

```

On 19-Aug-2002, "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

> Excuse me?  You, sir, have  been around long enough to know that it's not
> errant pointers which cause GPF and BSOD errors; it's errant PROGRAMMERS.
>
> Pointer variables are IMNSHO an EXCELLENT way to enhance performance.

I've been around long enough to remember when CoBOL manuals recommended using
Alter Go To's to improve performance.

It really doesn't matter whether a problem who you blame for a problem.  If the
actual results have costs greater than benefits - don't use that technique.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-20T08:17:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sIr89.1$CD2.78@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254> <aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <6t3tlugko1ijnk8sv3odua2215jtfhev9e@4ax.com> <He689.23544$%P3.1610722@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <Fo989.3203$yt3.1352827@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> > "Michael Todd" <mikltodd@comcast.net> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> errant pointers which cause GPF and BSOD errors; it's errant PROGRAMMERS.

Michael, I'm surprised at you!  *Every* bug is caused by programmers.
The question is: "Which techniques can be helpful decreasing programmer
errors, and which ones are harmful and increase programmer errors?"  Do
you use GO TO?  Do you use ALTER?  Why not?  Because those constructs
have been demonstrated beyond question to increase programmer errors and
make programs using them more difficult to safely modify and debug.  There
are two reasons why those constructs are harmful: they are "open ended"
and their function precludes much compiler support in catching errors.  On
the other hand, a procedure CALL (PERFORM) is *not* open ended, and the
compiler can easily generate code to detect errant subscripts.  But pointers
are the most "open ended" things there are, and generating code to test for
errant pointers is almost never practical.  Even if it were, that would more
than negate any performance gained by using them.

> Pointer variables are IMNSHO an EXCELLENT way to enhance performance.

Driving to work in a jet powered dragster gives you more performance too,
but such vehicles don't belong on the street.  Same with pointers, particularly
procedure pointers.  This is why there are so many debugging tools for C, a
language *requiring* pointers for any non-trivial task.
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:sIr89.1$CD2.78@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com...
> "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
> > "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> > > "Michael Todd" <mikltodd@comcast.net> wrote:
> > > >
> > > > Using procedure pointers reduces the overhead of a
dynamic call
> > > > by 48% to 52%.  <snip>
> > >
> > > Okay, but I am very happy I won't have to fix the
unbelievable number
> > > of incredibly subtle bugs you are *definitely* going to
create using this
> > > methodology...
> > > How many 'blue screen'  hang-ups and GPFs have you seen
in Windows?
> > > Well, errant pointers are what cause those....
> > > You use pointers when there is no better way  Anything
else is shooting
> > > yourself in the foot (or head) on purpose.....
> >
> > Excuse me?  You, sir, have  been around long enough to know
that it's not
> > errant pointers which cause GPF and BSOD errors; it's
errant PROGRAMMERS.
>
> Michael, I'm surprised at you!  *Every* bug is caused by
programmers.
> The question is: "Which techniques can be helpful decreasing
programmer
> errors, and which ones are harmful and increase programmer
errors?"  Do
> you use GO TO?  Do you use ALTER?  Why not?  Because those
constructs
> have been demonstrated beyond question to increase programmer
errors and
> make programs using them more difficult to safely modify and
debug.  There
> are two reasons why those constructs are harmful: they are
"open ended"
> and their function precludes much compiler support in
catching errors.  On
> the other hand, a procedure CALL (PERFORM) is *not* open
ended, and the
> compiler can easily generate code to detect errant
subscripts.  But pointers
> are the most "open ended" things there are, and generating
code to test for
> errant pointers is almost never practical.  Even if it were,
that would more
> than negate any performance gained by using them.
>
> > Pointer variables are IMNSHO an EXCELLENT way to enhance
performance.
>
> Driving to work in a jet powered dragster gives you more
performance too,
> but such vehicles don't belong on the street.  Same with
pointers, particularly
> procedure pointers.  This is why there are so many debugging
tools for C, a
> language *requiring* pointers for any non-trivial task.

Pointers are double-edged.  Absent strongly-typed pointers,
though, is the only alternative to copy the data instead of
referencing it?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-21T04:25:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wQI89.10664$CD2.1248064@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254> <aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <6t3tlugko1ijnk8sv3odua2215jtfhev9e@4ax.com> <He689.23544$%P3.1610722@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <Fo989.3203$yt3.1352827@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <sIr89.1$CD2.78@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <aju77r023vt@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
"Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote:
>
> Pointers are double-edged.  Absent strongly-typed pointers,
> though, is the only alternative to copy the data instead of
> referencing it?


The comments I made here are about explicit pointers.  Using implicit
pointers, where the language itself makes their use transparent, such as
passing parameters by reference instead of by value, are a different
animal altogether.  They are not 'open ended', and the compiler can
provide close scrutiny of their use.  Personally, I don't see any problem
with those.  But even 'strongly typed' pointers are still 'open ended', as
far as where they can point.  Storing the correctly formatted data in the
wrong place is still a Bad Thing. :-)
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:njU89.6949$%v4.130111@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com...
> "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote:
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'm not sure what you mean?  An object reference made using a
pointer
> that could be set to an invalid address would be open ended.

This depends on the system of course, but quite a few allow an
object to be destroyed, leaving dangling pointers behind.  You
wouldn't really be setting it to an invalid address, but it
would end up that way.  I guess I've answered my on question,
though.
```

- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254> <aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <6t3tlugko1ijnk8sv3odua2215jtfhev9e@4ax.com> <He689.23544$%P3.1610722@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <Fo989.3203$yt3.1352827@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <sIr89.1$CD2.78@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <aju77r023vt@enews1.newsguy.com> <wQI89.10664$CD2.1248064@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ak0rrp01u64@enews2.newsguy.com> <njU89.6949$%v4.130111@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <ak167n07p8@enews3.newsguy.com> <ffW89.4533$sY3.1195984@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:ffW89.4533$sY3.1195984@news20.bellglobal.com...
> "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote in message
> news:ak167n07p8@enews3.newsguy.com...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > I'm not sure what you mean?  An object reference made
using a
> > pointer
> > > that could be set to an invalid address would be open
ended.
> >
> > This depends on the system of course, but quite a few allow
an
> > object to be destroyed, leaving dangling pointers behind.
You
> > wouldn't really be setting it to an invalid address, but it
> > would end up that way.  I guess I've answered my on
question,
> > though.
> >
> >
> In fact, this goes back to our discussion of the value of a
"stop run". <G>.
>
> You have, as you stated, now answered your own question.
When a "run unit"
> ends, all pointers that were created in the run unit are
cleaned up.  Thane
> is the guy to talk to regarding how the specification is
actually worded.  I
> got lost about 3/4 of the way through the finalizer
discussion when the spec
> was being set.

The pointers themselves are cleaned up, or is it the other way
around?

It's my understanding that OO-COBOL will have garbage
collection.  When the last reference to an object goes out of
scope (or is explicitly cleared,) the object becomes eligible
for collection.  In this scenario, you don't have to worry
about dangling object references, as you would in a scheme that
employes explicit creation _and_ destruction of objects.

Certainly the memory for those references would ultimately be
de-allocated by the end of the run unit, but hopefully there's
no need for logic that hunts down and clears (nils, nulls, ...)
the actual pointers.

I can't see a condition that would require you to force a
finalization outside of the reference counting (or trace
mapping) that's normally used to expire an object, but there
are those that try.  Even so, having the runtime clip dangling
references would appear to be a logicstics nightmare.

___________________

This area of system design is interesting from a developmental
point of view, and also promises to play a key part in the
debates over secure computing.  The open-ended pointers that
Judson refers to are even closer to the heart of it.  Even if
they aren't strictly bad form (also suggested by Judson,) they
are powerful tools in scrutinizing a system.  As such, they
present a danger to secure computing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OO, Garbage Collection and draft Standard (was: Cobol and Performance

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-21T22:29:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ak1lrk$aa7$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254> <aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <6t3tlugko1ijnk8sv3odua2215jtfhev9e@4ax.com> <He689.23544$%P3.1610722@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <Fo989.3203$yt3.1352827@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <sIr89.1$CD2.78@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <aju77r023vt@enews1.newsguy.com> <wQI89.10664$CD2.1248064@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ak0rrp01u64@enews2.newsguy.com> <njU89.6949$%v4.130111@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <ak167n07p8@enews3.newsguy.com> <ffW89.4533$sY3.1195984@news20.bellglobal.com> <ak1efa02vfs@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
I just HAD to quote exactly what the draft standard says about "garbage
collection" (and this is the ONLY thing that it says),

"The timing and algorithm for the mechanism that determines whether or not
an instance object can take part in the
continued execution of the run unit is implementor defined.

NOTE The process of determining whether or not an instance object can take
part in continued execution and reclaiming
resources unique to the object is generally referred to as garbage
collection."
```

```


Grinder wrote:

> "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
> > In fact, this goes back to our discussion of the value of a
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Feel a bit like Clint Eastwood's orangutan on this one, "Which Way is Up
?" <G>.
Put it this way, using either Procedural COBOL or OO COBOL issue a STOP
RUN and it's like pulling the three pin plug out of the wall !

You know from the source I sent you that I use the interim Micro Focus
methods,
"finalize"(one object), "deepFinalize" (a colleciton of n elements) or
"deepFinalizeWithDepth" (a collection of n elements which are
x-dimensional), each time I finish a 'Business' module. Now when I
return to the Master Menu, I may still have a fair number of object
references still active, and not associated with the Business module I
have just left. If the user selects 'EXIT' from the menu I don't even
bother to clean them up - OnEvent Exit I just use the code STOP RUN.

>
> It's my understanding that OO-COBOL will have garbage
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well we will have to wait and see. But loosely, a TR(Technical Report)
called 'Finalizer' has been specified to cover your auto garbage
collection requirement. So that it doesn't hold up the Standard spec,
due December 2002, this topic was written 'outside' of the Standard to
be used as a 'trial addendum'. Obviously the J4 objective is to try and
be as near perfect as possible. Assume it has a 90% plus success rate -
based on feedback from developers to compiler vendors - the intent would
then be to fine-tune it even further. Given it makes, say a 99% level,
then the revised TR can be submitted from ANSI (J4) to ISO (WG4) for
official inclusion in the COBOL Standard. That suggests COBOL 2002 would
be re-issued as COBOL 2004 - assuming the above could happen in a
two-year time frame.

I'll leave it to Bill to dot the i's and cross the t's on the above.

>
> I can't see a condition that would require you to force a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> present a danger to secure computing.

You are the one who keeps asking questions - so let me now ask you as it
relates to Pascal <G>.

- Exiting the Master Menu above - what happens in Pascal - what is your
equivalent of STOP RUN.

- Auto Garbage Collection - can you put your hand on your heart and say
that it is always 100% in Pascal. That is, if I create a Master Menu
creating  50 objects, I now invoke a Business module adding 270 more,
and when I exit this particular Business module the count is back to 50.
(This assumes the 270 objects created for this particular Business
module are not required elsewhere).

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-22T02:48:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ak250401rli@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254> <aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <6t3tlugko1ijnk8sv3odua2215jtfhev9e@4ax.com> <He689.23544$%P3.1610722@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <Fo989.3203$yt3.1352827@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <sIr89.1$CD2.78@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <aju77r023vt@enews1.newsguy.com> <wQI89.10664$CD2.1248064@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ak0rrp01u64@enews2.newsguy.com> <njU89.6949$%v4.130111@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <ak167n07p8@enews3.newsguy.com> <ffW89.4533$sY3.1195984@news20.bellglobal.com> <ak1efa02vfs@enews1.newsguy.com> <3D6460B3.9823F334@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3D6460B3.9823F334@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > In fact, this goes back to our discussion of the value of
a
> > "stop run". <G>.
> > >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> > The pointers themselves are cleaned up, or is it the other
way
> > around?
> >
>
> Feel a bit like Clint Eastwood's orangutan on this one,
"Which Way is Up
> ?" <G>.
> Put it this way, using either Procedural COBOL or OO COBOL
issue a STOP
> RUN and it's like pulling the three pin plug out of the wall
!
>
> You know from the source I sent you that I use the interim
Micro Focus
> methods,
> "finalize"(one object), "deepFinalize" (a colleciton of n
elements) or
> "deepFinalizeWithDepth" (a collection of n elements which are
> x-dimensional), each time I finish a 'Business' module. Now
when I
> return to the Master Menu, I may still have a fair number of
object
> references still active, and not associated with the Business
module I
> have just left. If the user selects 'EXIT' from the menu I
don't even
> bother to clean them up - OnEvent Exit I just use the code
STOP RUN.

That's characteristic of a garbage collections scheme.  Without
one, you would be generating memory leaks by not explicitly
destroying the objects.  The description of finalize, though,
does not mention if you must call explicitly finalize to
de-allocate the object, or if there's some form of reference
counting going on...

(from MFNXCL31.CHM)
Destroys the object to which this message is sent. The result
is always set to null. Therefore invoke anObject "finalize"
returning anObject. Will destroy anObject and set the reference
to null.

> You are the one who keeps asking questions - so let me now
ask you as it
> relates to Pascal <G>.
>
> - Exiting the Master Menu above - what happens in Pascal -
what is your
> equivalent of STOP RUN.

For VB, .net, Java and Object Pascal, there's no need to ever
have a hard termination of your application.  There are various
constructs in the frame works that look like this --
Application.Terminate in Delphi, for instance.  This doesn't
stop the run directly, though.  It posts a message of sorts,
that will, when handled, initiate an orderly shutdown of the
application.

Many applications expire from "natural causes," though.  When
the last form closes, an application-level object realizes
this, and initiates the cleanup.  For more procedural
applications -- like a console app -- your program expires when
you run out of code.  You can issue a Halt, but I consider it
better form to have a single exit point.

> - Auto Garbage Collection - can you put your hand on your
heart and say
> that it is always 100% in Pascal.

Object Pacal does not have any provision for garbage
collection.  Delphi's framework, has some gc-like behaviors
built into objects like forms and application objects, but
there's no fundamental manager for object de-allocation.
Indeed, if/when gc comes to Delphi (looks like it will with
delphi.net,) it will be part of a framework, rather than part
of Object Pascal.

> That is, if I create a Master Menu
> creating  50 objects, I now invoke a Business module adding
270 more,
> and when I exit this particular Business module the count is
back to 50.
> (This assumes the 270 objects created for this particular
Business
> module are not required elsewhere).

I'll skip to C#.net, as it has gc -- and makes for a better
target for your question.  (There's some dispute over
reference-counting mechanisms employed by COM/ActiveX (VB being
an example) and if it's truly garbage collected.  Even though
this qualifies, in my opinion, as gc under this discussion,
I'll not gum up the description.)

There is hardly any provision to explicitly finalize objects in
.net.  You can do it, but it's clearly not the intention of the
design to do so -- it's just there for the psychos, I guess.

You just go about your business of creating an using objects as
you need them.  If you need them to persist beyond your current
scope, you just declare it in the next highest one up.  I can't
easily describe "scope" in terms of OO-COBOL, because of my
unfamiliarity with the terrain, so let me know if this is
opaque.

When the an object, or a group of related objects, are no
longer in scope, the garbage collector is free to reclaim that
memory.  When this happens, though, is considered to be
non-deterministic.  Understandably, this has generated quite a
bit of confusion and controversy.

The "problems" generated by this approach, though, are largely
ones generated from trying to shift into that paradigm.
```

- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254> <aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <6t3tlugko1ijnk8sv3odua2215jtfhev9e@4ax.com> <He689.23544$%P3.1610722@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <Fo989.3203$yt3.1352827@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <sIr89.1$CD2.78@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <aju77r023vt@enews1.newsguy.com> <wQI89.10664$CD2.1248064@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ak0rrp01u64@enews2.newsguy.com> <njU89.6949$%v4.130111@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <ak167n07p8@enews3.newsguy.com> <ffW89.4533$sY3.1195984@news20.bellglobal.com> <ak1efa02vfs@enews1.newsguy.com> <3D6460B3.9823F334@shaw.ca> <ak250401rli@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```


Grinder wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> news:3D6460B3.9823F334@shaw.ca...
…[131 more quoted lines elided]…
> ones generated from trying to shift into that paradigm.

I was anticipating you would qualify 'auto garbage collection' somewhat.
While it isn't per se part of your system design it is however a very
critical feature to avoid memory leaks. However the topic is just so
complex. A fairly simple example :-

Class A - I create a CustomerCollection (object reference plus object
references for each element). Just as in other languages, within COBOL I
can pass this object via linkage to other classes, so we get :-

Linkage Section.
01 lnk-CustomerCollection                    object reference.
Procedure Division using lnk-CustomerCollection.

Class B - set ObjectB to lnk-CustomerCollection
Class C - set ObjectC to lnk-CustomerCollection
Class D - set ...............

We could finish up with the original os-CustomerCollection and ObjectsA
through ObjectH. Different references, but they all 'point' to the same
object. The problem I see with 'automation' is where the object, (AND
that includes all references to it) gets destroyed. As is, within Net
Express, rather than use "set....." I can  :-

    invoke lnk-CustomerCollection "deepcopy" returning aNewCollection

a new copy and distinct reference, not associated to
lnk-CustomerCollectiion

That's a fairly simple example- but it shows the problems. Then throw in
languages communicating with one another and passing objects - which
language should do the auto garbage collection ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-22T14:44:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ak3ev201fb7@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <Xns925DE82A6F2B8walkerbjoptusnetcoma@210.49.20.254> <aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <6t3tlugko1ijnk8sv3odua2215jtfhev9e@4ax.com> <He689.23544$%P3.1610722@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <Fo989.3203$yt3.1352827@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <sIr89.1$CD2.78@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <aju77r023vt@enews1.newsguy.com> <wQI89.10664$CD2.1248064@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ak0rrp01u64@enews2.newsguy.com> <njU89.6949$%v4.130111@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <ak167n07p8@enews3.newsguy.com> <ffW89.4533$sY3.1195984@news20.bellglobal.com> <ak1efa02vfs@enews1.newsguy.com> <3D6460B3.9823F334@shaw.ca> <ak250401rli@enews1.newsguy.com> <3D651950.C1DF50BC@shaw.ca>`

```
> I was anticipating you would qualify 'auto garbage
collection' somewhat.
> While it isn't per se part of your system design it is
however a very
> critical feature to avoid memory leaks. However the topic is
just so
> complex. A fairly simple example :-

Just so I've not cast the wrong impression.  I understand
"garbage collection" to be intrinsically automatic.  In other
words, if you have garbage collection, your code does not have
to explicitly decide when to destroy an object -- that's the
job of the garbage collector.

> Class A - I create a CustomerCollection (object reference
plus object
> references for each element). Just as in other languages,
within COBOL I
> can pass this object via linkage to other classes, so we get
:-

You're passing a _reference_ to the object in to another class?

> Linkage Section.
> 01 lnk-CustomerCollection                    object
reference.
> Procedure Division using lnk-CustomerCollection.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> We could finish up with the original os-CustomerCollection
and ObjectsA
> through ObjectH. Different references, but they all 'point'
to the same
> object. The problem I see with 'automation' is where the
object, (AND
> that includes all references to it) gets destroyed.

Any of the schemes I've seen for garbage collection intend to
keep track of the references to an object.  When there are no
references left, the object may (and will) be destroyed.  In
your example, an object is created, and a referenced is passed
(through the linkage section) into a sub-program.

What happens in this sub-program is trivial from a garbage
collector's viewpoint.  You may reference the object
(originally referenced) any number of times, but you'll not be
able to clear that last reference -- there's still one left in
the calling program.  When your sub-program terminates, any
references you've made will be released, so the overall number
of references will be the same as it was before the call to the
sub-program.

Really, it makes quite a bit of sense.  The sub-program did not
create the object, and it's actions will not result in its
destruction.

> As is, within Net
> Express, rather than use "set....." I can  :-
>
>     invoke lnk-CustomerCollection "deepcopy" returning
aNewCollection
>
> a new copy and distinct reference, not associated to
> lnk-CustomerCollectiion

In this case, you are creating a new object.  Unless you call
another sub-program, all references to that object will be
contained within this sub-program.  When the sub-program
terminates, those reference will be released, and the object
will be destroyed.  As you point, out this new object bears no
(memory management) relationship to the one copied.

> That's a fairly simple example- but it shows the problems.
Then throw in
> languages communicating with one another and passing
objects - which
> language should do the auto garbage collection ?

Whatever "language" -- or more specifically memory manager --
that allocated the object would ultimately be responsible for
destroying it.  It is complicated from the perspective of the
memory manager -- practically magic.  From the developer's
perspective, though, it's easy -- you just handle objects the
way you would basic data types.
```

```


Grinder wrote:

> > I was anticipating you would qualify 'auto garbage
> collection' somewhat.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Correct. Introducing the 'Finalizer' the intent is to relieve me, the
programmer, of the onus of worrying abut destroying objects. I said
tongue in cheek it could be incorporated in the Standard giving us COBOL
2004. Seeing Bill's other comments, I think he is thinking something
like COBOL 2006 or later, if ever ! (As yet I don't recall seeing
anything for a target finish date on this particular TR). New
applications will be written according to what is available in the
latest Standard, subject to implementation by vendors. However given
some five years or more of code using the current "do your own
finalizing", unless it is transparently obvious, people just wont revamp
old applications to use a new auto garbage collection facility.

>
> > Class A - I create a CustomerCollection (object reference
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> You're passing a _reference_ to the object in to another class?

Yes. Switch from CustomerCollectioin to say SimulatedEventsCollection,
with say Simulation/Forecasting. Again we pass the initial collection to
subsidiaries which execute specific tasks on the original. Given Class A
(originator) and B through G, we might want to 'kill' all classes but
retain Class F - for additional processing. Class F could even
"reactivate" some of the others.

Going from memory, Bill quoted, "when the class is out of
scope........". That's a 'beaut'. I wonder how that would fit in with my
Classes A to G above ?

>
> > Linkage Section.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Depends upon what "when there are no references left" - indicated as
above "when out of scope......". Will it always work satisfactorily. The
real stinker is if you find yourself attempting to invoke an object
which the Auto Garbager has determined is no longer required - hence a
null or nil reference.

>
> What happens in this sub-program is trivial from a garbage
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> destruction.

> > As is, within Net
> > Express, rather than use "set....." I can  :-
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> way you would basic data types.

Believe me, I don't intend getting heartburn over this topic. I'll go
along with what J4 determine and see how it pans out. You use the phrase
above 'practically magic'. Under some circumstances I can foresee when
it is more likely to be a 'bloody miracle' <G>

Jimmy
```
