```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3D65562E.1AB074AD@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > critical feature to avoid memory leaks. However the topic
is
> > just so
> > > complex. A fairly simple example :-
> >
> > Just so I've not cast the wrong impression.  I understand
> > "garbage collection" to be intrinsically automatic.  In
other
> > words, if you have garbage collection, your code does not
have
> > to explicitly decide when to destroy an object -- that's
the
> > job of the garbage collector.
> >
>
> Correct. Introducing the 'Finalizer' the intent is to relieve
me, the
> programmer, of the onus of worrying abut destroying objects.
I said
> tongue in cheek it could be incorporated in the Standard
giving us COBOL
> 2004. Seeing Bill's other comments, I think he is thinking
something
> like COBOL 2006 or later, if ever ! (As yet I don't recall
seeing
> anything for a target finish date on this particular TR). New
> applications will be written according to what is available
in the
> latest Standard, subject to implementation by vendors.
However given
> some five years or more of code using the current "do your
own
> finalizing", unless it is transparently obvious, people just
wont revamp
> old applications to use a new auto garbage collection
facility.

It is a little dicey.  If you convert your explicit
destructions of objects to setting the object reference to nil,
you'll only get into trouble in one spot.  If you're accustomed
to leaving object references pointing out into space, that
could (temporarily) keep the object from being destroyed.

Doing that isn't good practice, but I acknowledge that good
programmers might do this.

> > > Class A - I create a CustomerCollection (object reference
> > plus object
> > > references for each element). Just as in other languages,
> > within COBOL I
> > > can pass this object via linkage to other classes, so we
get
> > :-
> >
> > You're passing a _reference_ to the object in to another
class?
>
> Yes. Switch from CustomerCollectioin to say
SimulatedEventsCollection,
> with say Simulation/Forecasting. Again we pass the initial
collection to
> subsidiaries which execute specific tasks on the original.
Given Class A
> (originator) and B through G, we might want to 'kill' all
classes but
> retain Class F - for additional processing. Class F could
even
> "reactivate" some of the others.
>
> Going from memory, Bill quoted, "when the class is out of
> scope........". That's a 'beaut'. I wonder how that would fit
in with my
> Classes A to G above ?

As you re-reference lnk-CustomerCollection with a pointer to a
different object, the previous object reference is dropped.
This could result in the object that it _was_ pointing to being
destroyed.  As for the original object you passed into the
sub-program, though, it won't be destroyed by doing this, as
you have a reference to it in the calling program.

> > > Linkage Section.
> > > 01 lnk-CustomerCollection                    object
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > We could finish up with the original
os-CustomerCollection
> > and ObjectsA
> > > through ObjectH. Different references, but they all
'point'
> > to the same
> > > object. The problem I see with 'automation' is where the
> > object, (AND
> > > that includes all references to it) gets destroyed.

I may have mispoken from a misunderstanding of your example.

If lnk-CustomerCollection points to an object, setting another
object reference to it, is no guarantee that you'll be pointing
to the same object.  That will only be true if ObjectB points
to that same object already.

> Depends upon what "when there are no references left" -
indicated as
> above "when out of scope......". Will it always work
satisfactorily. The
> real stinker is if you find yourself attempting to invoke an
object
> which the Auto Garbager has determined is no longer
required - hence a
> null or nil reference.

Defects in the implementation of the gc can be a problem, but
the goal is to destroy objects when there are no longer any
references to it.  Dropping a reference can happen a couple of
ways:

1)  You explicitely clear the reference -- set it to nil.

2)  The run unit in which your reference variables are declared
terminates.

In either case, the object is never pulled from under the
object reference -- you've either divorced it yourself, or it's
no longer accessible because the run has ended.

> Believe me, I don't intend getting heartburn over this topic.
I'll go
> along with what J4 determine and see how it pans out. You use
the phrase
> above 'practically magic'. Under some circumstances I can
foresee when
> it is more likely to be a 'bloody miracle' <G>

5 or 6 years ago, when I first started getting acquainted with
garbage collection, I was dubious of it.  I've seen it work,
though, and now qualify as a convert.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-08-21T22:45:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0208212145.120af95e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6MD09.12382$cU1.412067@news.chello.at> <aibulq$e9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <6t3tlugko1ijnk8sv3odua2215jtfhev9e@4ax.com> <He689.23544$%P3.1610722@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <Fo989.3203$yt3.1352827@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <sIr89.1$CD2.78@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <aju77r023vt@enews1.newsguy.com> <wQI89.10664$CD2.1248064@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ak0rrp01u64@enews2.newsguy.com> <njU89.6949$%v4.130111@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
There has been some significant work done in the standard to prevent
this "invalid pointer" situation for object references.  I'm sure
there's some way to mess it up, but the aim was to prevent it from
happening.  Object references can have "types" (bad term) that allow
conformance checking.  You can specify that an Object Reference data
item can only reference an object of a certain class or interface - or
you can make it a "Universal" Object reference allowing it to contain
any type of Object reference.  Doing so requires more runtime
conformance checking and might be slower depending on the
implementation.  I think this is what is meant by "Open Ended" object
references.


"Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message news:<njU89.6949$%v4.130111@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>...
> "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> that could be set to an invalid address would be open ended.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and Performance

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-22T02:49:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ak252e01rmp@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
news:bfdfc3e8.0208212145.120af95e@posting.google.com...
> There has been some significant work done in the standard to
prevent
> this "invalid pointer" situation for object references.  I'm
sure
> there's some way to mess it up, but the aim was to prevent it
from
> happening.  Object references can have "types" (bad term)
that allow
> conformance checking.  You can specify that an Object
Reference data
> item can only reference an object of a certain class or
interface - or
> you can make it a "Universal" Object reference allowing it to
contain
> any type of Object reference.  Doing so requires more runtime
> conformance checking and might be slower depending on the
> implementation.  I think this is what is meant by "Open
Ended" object
> references.

Can you finalize/destroy an object and leave references to that
object dangling?
```

<a id="t-f484764e25"></a>
## Thread: Shared Memory — 5 msgs

### Shared Memory

- **From:** eckleder <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-19T09:18:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d60fe35$1_1@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
I need to write a program that will write a data string to memory so it
can be read by another program. I tried using shared memory
(CBL_ALLOC_MEM, CBL_PUT_SHMEM_PTR, CBL_GET_SHMEM_PTR, CBL_FREE_MEM). But
I don't understand how I can tell the CBL_PUT_SHMEM_PTR function what
data it write to the memory. The other problem is, how can I get the
data from the memory with the CBL_GET_SHMEM_PTR function. Please send me
code examples if you have them.

THANKS!
```

#### ↳ Re: Shared Memory

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-19T18:09:37+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajr8kg$vn1$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<3d60fe35$1_1@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
The sequence of actions to do this is as follows :

To set up the shared memory.

1) Decide on a name for your shared memory. This is what is used in
CBL_PUT_SHMEM_PTR
2) Allocate some memory to store your data string in using CBL_ALLOC_MEM.
Remember to set the flag to say that the memory is shared.
3) Set the address of a linkage item to the pointer returned by
CBL_ALLOC_MEM
4) Store your data string in this linkage item.
5) Pass the pointer returned by CBL_ALLOC_MEM to CBL_PUT_SHMEM_PTR using the
name you decided on in 1).

To access the shared memory

1) Call CBL_GET_SHMEM_PTR using the same name you decided on previously.
2) Set the address of a linkage item to the pointer returned.
3) Access the linkage item to obtain the data string.

Note that you can only access shared memory if the second program was
started by using CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT. Programs that are completely separate
cannot use this mechanism.

I hope this helps.



"eckleder" <member@mainframeforum.com> wrote in message
news:3d60fe35$1_1@news5.nntpserver.com...
> I need to write a program that will write a data string to memory so it
> can be read by another program. I tried using shared memory
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> www.MainFrameForum.com - USENET Gateway
```

#### ↳ Re: Shared Memory

- **From:** eckleder <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-20T01:17:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d61df13_2@news5.nntpserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3d60fe35$1_1@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
Thanks for your reply! This helps me, but how can I share memory for two
seperate programs?

THANKS!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Shared Memory

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-08-20T10:07:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0208200907.4d7fb676@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3d60fe35$1_1@news5.nntpserver.com> <3d61df13_2@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
Hello,

You don't state the compiler, operating system or hardware that you
use, but on IBM mainframes you can use batchpipes to achieve something
along these lines.  Otherwise, you may be able to use shared files to
achieve your objective.  If you get down to the assembler level, you
may find facilities that allow you to allocate and share memory
between independent run units.

Regards, Robert

eckleder <member@mainframeforum.com> wrote in message news:<3d61df13_2@news5.nntpserver.com>...
> Thanks for your reply! This helps me, but how can I share memory for two
> seperate programs?
> 
> THANKS!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Shared Memory

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-20T13:45:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aju2vc$n4a$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3d60fe35$1_1@news5.nntpserver.com> <3d61df13_2@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
If (and only if) the two programs are run in the same "run unit" (under the
same "CALL" structure), then you can use the EXTERNAL attribute on the
definition of the data in both programs' working-storage and everything will
be done for you. This is true for any '85 Standard compiler.
```

<a id="t-ffcacafcd0"></a>
## Thread: IBM COBOL to HP-UX COBOL — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### IBM COBOL to HP-UX COBOL

- **From:** "Shyam" <shyamsunder.puranam@digital.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-19T15:50:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3t889.20$h_5.584751@news.cpqcorp.net>`

```
Hi:

I am a new comer to this group. Please excuse me for my ignorance and any
questions that may sound very trivial.

One of my customer is wanting to move his COBOL applications from IBM to
HP-UX. Currently he is using CICS on IBM. Though I have worked with COBOL,
my knowledge is limited to Tandem NonStop platform. Hence, I do not know
much about compatibility of COBOL on other platforms, the semantics...

I request the following information from you

(1) What is the compiler used on HP-UX? Does HP-UX has its own compiler? Or
does it use MicroFocus or Fujitsu to compile COBOL programs?
(2) If I have to migrate these Screen Maps in IBM - CICS to HP-UX, can I use
the Screen Section on HP-UX? Are there any restrictions on using it? Can any
one please direct me to some documentation of COBOL on HP-UX.
(3) Are there any specific compiler directives I need to use on HP-UX? I
wrote a simple COBOL program and tried compiling, it said cannot
find -lFutil and then -lcob. (really my efforts were futile)

I appreciate your help on this and thanking you in advance.

Regds,
Shyam
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM COBOL to HP-UX COBOL

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-19T15:58:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oA889.68395$eK6.2375534@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3t889.20$h_5.584751@news.cpqcorp.net>`

```
It depends upon why you are migrating; if the goal is just to port it to a new platform as quickly as possible, then you want to
look into MicroFocus and using CICS (well, Transaction Server) under HP/UX.

If the goal is to move away from CICS and IBM, then yes, you will have to recode to use another compiler or screen vendor's display
capability.  HP has their own screen capability, and Microfocus has a more generic screen capability and runs on many platforms.

If the goal is to port it quickly and save money... well... have you instead looked at porting to AIX and the pSeries machines?
pSeries runs, TxServer (i.e. CICS) and IBM COBOL natively, so the port would be more or less trivial. Cost effective as well. And as
well, it runs MicroFocus. HP/UX is a platform in decline at the moment, what with it being a direct competitor to the Alpha machines
at Compaq. AIX/pSeries is definitely on the rise, and what's more, it is one of the most well supported platforms for porting on the
planet, as well as a wonderful platform for Web Services with WebSphere. Oh yes, and a $2800 machine (new price from IBM) can handle
an astonishing workload. (The B50 model server.)

-Paul


"Shyam" <shyamsunder.puranam@digital.com> wrote in message news:3t889.20$h_5.584751@news.cpqcorp.net...
> Hi:
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
compiler. I was a bit concerned and worried whether the equivalent of Screen
Maps in IBM/CICS is Screen Section on HP-UX/COBOL.

Regds,
Shyam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM COBOL to HP-UX COBOL

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-19T16:45:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qg989.68411$eK6.2380439@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3t889.20$h_5.584751@news.cpqcorp.net> <oA889.68395$eK6.2375534@twister.austin.rr.com> <nW889.23$Q_5.602137@news.cpqcorp.net>`

```
This is the only thing I have bookmarked.

http://devresource.hp.com/STK/crossplatform.html

Note that the Microfocus compiler itself can be ran on any platform; all you need for the HP./UX is the runtimes. Also, you should
probably contact both HP and Microfocus directly about the latest licensing policies.

Now, you should realize that porting from BMS maps to a typical screen section COBOL is
NOT going to be easy. Most CICS programs are written in PsuedoConversational mode for efficiency.
MicroFocus COBOL will not support that method of programming.

<grin> Do not say you were not warned of this. :)

-Paul


"Shyam" <shyamsunder.puranam@digital.com> wrote in message news:nW889.23$Q_5.602137@news.cpqcorp.net...

…[72 more quoted lines elided]…

```

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2002-08-20T14:27:52+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3d62502b.2570676@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<3t889.20$h_5.584751@news.cpqcorp.net> <oA889.68395$eK6.2375534@twister.austin.rr.com> <nW889.23$Q_5.602137@news.cpqcorp.net> <qg989.68411$eK6.2380439@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
Paul:

We have conversion tools which will automatically convert the BMS map
to COBOL sp2 GUI screens, but you are absolutely correct in that the
procedural code to display the screens must be modified to operate in
a conversational mode.

We have a few consulting affiliates who have assisted in CICS
conversions.  I think that they can assist Shyam with his project.

This approach would also provide them with many more choices.

They could convert the application to run:

- in HP-UX character mode (ASCII terminal display)
- on an HP-UX server displaying screens on a remote PC through a
Win32 GUI thin client
- on an HP-UX server displaying screens on a remote PC through a web
browser

We have found that not all companies want to run their user interface
environment through a web browser.  Sometimes the web browser is a
"less than ideal" user interface environment.

More choices are always better.
```

## Thread: OT - Humor — 4 msgs

### OT - Humor

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-19T21:08:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ajs8au$r5i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For our Australian friends (and those who admire them),  some humo<ur> sent
to me recently.

    ***

Qantas Airline Humor
Never let it be said that Australian ground crews and engineers lack a sense
of humor.

Here are some actual logged maintenance complaints by QANTAS pilots and the
corrective action recorded by mechanics.

(P stands for the Problem the pilots entered in the log, and S stands for
the Corrective Action taken by the mechanics.)

P: Left inside main tire almost needs replacement.
S: Almost replaced left inside main tire.

P: Test flight OK, except auto land very rough.
S: Auto land not installed on this aircraft.

P: Something loose in cockpit.
S: Something tightened in cockpit.

P: Dead bugs on windshield.
S: Live bugs on backorder.

P: Autopilot in altitude-hold mode produces a 200-fpm descent.
S: Cannot reproduce problem on ground.

P: Evidence of leak on right main landing gear.
S: Evidence removed.

P: DME volume unbelievably loud.
S: DME volume set to more believable level.

P: Friction locks cause throttle levers to stick.
S: That's what they're there for!

P: IFF inoperative.
S: IFF always inoperative in OFF mode.

P: Suspected crack in windscreen.
S: Suspect you're right.

P: Number 3 engine missing.
(note: this was for a piston-engine airplane; the pilot meant the engine was
not running smoothly)
S: Engine found on right wing after brief search.

P: Aircraft handles funny.
S: Aircraft warned to straighten up, fly right, and be serious.

P: Radar hums.
S: Reprogrammed radar with words.

P: Mouse in cockpit.
S: Cat installed.

By the way, Qantas is the only major airline that has never had an accident.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - Humor

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-20T03:23:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D61B2BD.53BABF7F@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ajs8au$r5i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> For our Australian friends (and those who admire them),  some humo<ur> sent
> to me recently.
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
> By the way, Qantas is the only major airline that has never had an accident.

Well we all know they've got a good sharp sense of humour Down Under. But
universally we know that Customs/Immigration officers are not imbued with a
'funny bone'.

Ivor my former DPM at Debenhams in Taunton, stayed on for several years after I
left UK. As a result of a merger/takeover he got the heave-ho and of all things
started up a company making computer circuit boards or some such down there in
beautiful but sleepy Somerset. (A definition of UK West Country folk - they have
two speeds, SLOW and STOP!). Don't know the specifics but appears he made a
bundle of money, enough to let him and his wife go gallivanting around the world
some three times each year. (Came here about three years ago - Seattle, across
WA and Montana, up into Alberta, across to Prince Rupert in BC, boat down to
Vancouver Island, back to Vancouver, Seattle, air to Toronto/Niagra Falls and
back to UK. Phew ! )

Anyway, as a result of his endeavours he latched on to one of those UK Board of
Trade selling 'missions. So they head for parts East, first stopping over in
Hong Kong where they get the grand treatment as befits a government marketing
party.
Next stop - Aussieland.

Hot, and very tired from the flight, he gets off and dutifully fills in the
temporary immigration form. A female immigration officer says, "You haven't
completed it - you missed this". He looks at the missed question :-

    Do you have a criminal record  ? - Yes/No plus details

Tired Ivor responds, "I didn't know you still needed one after all this time".
She would have liked to have nailed him there and then - but from an 'official
party' ? He related the event to his Australian hosts over drinks and they
roared with laughter.

He was always a quiet bugger. Back to his grammar school days at Exeter in
Devon. They had to fill in a form of subjects they wanted to take and they were
given an additional optional subject. Something piqued his interest, so he
entered it.

Having read his application, his tutor with a look of disgust said, "Carpentry
?" "C-A-R-P-E-N-T-R-Y ?".  Ivor gave the only response he thought was
appropriate. "Well, it was good enough for Jesus Christ". <G>

Jimmy
```

```
Henry Blofield, known as "Blowers".  Picture the upper class English accent,
the brightly coloured bow tie, the dapper dress, the round, florid face
```

## Thread: Effective use of DOS base memory for COBOL programs — 11 msgs

### Effective use of DOS base memory for COBOL programs

- **From:** calvinchin@fm333.com (Calvin)
- **Date:** 2002-08-20T02:18:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<62843e96.0208200118.5e0f7207@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi there,

I have MF COBOL programs running under MS DOS. What I understand is
that Micro Focus COBOL is using the base memory (640K) to run the
program.

Thus, when loaded with DOS, with some networking protocols, there will
only left about 600K base memory. Some of my programs are quite big
that required more than 600K base memory. At the end, the program is
unable to load and giving errors.

I have to separate those BIG programs into few smaller programs. But
the processing will be a bit slower and less efficient.

Please, is there anyone can help me to run the MF COBOL programs under
base memory, either by using less base memory, or how to use less
memory?

Thank you.

Regards,
Calvin
```

```
programs requiring less memory.

MCM
```

```
Calvin,
The 16bit Micro Focus COBOL systems came with a "DOS extender" named 
"XM.EXE". This program will run your existing programs in extended 
memory. You should find documentation in your COBOL System Reference 
Volume 2. Some experimentation may be required to get all the switches 
set corectly. The most recent versions (3.2, 3.4) are probably the 
most likely to work well with Microsoft's most recent versions of DOS.
-magee
```

#### ↳ Re: Effective use of DOS base memory for COBOL programs

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2002-08-20T09:27:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0208200827.1d0b3532@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<62843e96.0208200118.5e0f7207@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello Calvin,

You could consider using the segmentation facility, if your compiler
has it, or preferably subdivide your program into separately compiled
modules and use dynamic linkage with CALL/CANCEL, if that is an option
provided by your compiler and linkage editor.  In either case, your
program would have to be organised such that the amount of swapping in
and out is minimised and that its nature is appropriate to your task. 
Much better, if possible, is to design your solution to use smaller
programs by subdividing the component tasks differently.  There may be
techniques to use the high memory area, extended and expanded storage,
but I don't know them myself and you would have to read the compiler
documentation to see whether they are available and how to use them.

Regards, Robert

calvinchin@fm333.com (Calvin) wrote in message news:<62843e96.0208200118.5e0f7207@posting.google.com>...
> Hi there,

…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
```


#### ↳ Re: Effective use of DOS base memory for COBOL programs

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-20T17:46:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D628200.679FE9AE@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<62843e96.0208200118.5e0f7207@posting.google.com>`

```


Calvin wrote:

> Hi there,
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> memory?

Good old Patrick, (Micro Focus) jumped in and referred you to the XM
solution; similarly Thane said that was the solution. It can be but not
necessarily so

Let's go back to your statement, "Some of my programs are quite big....".
I hate that.
Starting with only 64K on a Radio Shack some 20 years ago I was a Nervous
Nellie about size. I always come back to this one, having toured a Weston
biscuit factory when I was a 17-year old kid in the RAF.

Making chocolate covered meringue biscuits :-

- Flour, sugar, powdered milk, lard etc., dumped into huge vats to mix
into pastry
- the above put through a series of heavy duty rollers, then rolling out
the pastry to a specific thickness.
- cookie cutters descended on the pastry, the residual dropping off at the
end of the conveyer belt
- cut cookies partially heated
- blob of jam
- blob of meringue
- coated with liquid chocolate
- partially re-cooked
- finished cookies slipped into packaging trays
- packages sealed

At any stage that process can go wrong - like the nozzle squirting the jam
gets gunged up. Although the process stops, the maintenance engineers can
zero in on the 'jam squirting process' - sub programming.

Programming is essentially the automation of human clerical processes.
Your BIG programs - are they 'all singing all dancing', referencing files,
performing calculations, printing reports. Can they be identified to
discrete processing units and be sub or called programs ?

Certainly one solution would be to hive off your files as separate called
programs. Do you have a copy of Micro Focus Mudemo.cbl (Mullet User demo)
which calls file programs - this illustrates how you can do this.

If you don't want to get involved in a major change then XM could well be
your current solution - but I still hate BIG ! The other problem with BIG
is that any innocent change in the source could trip you up all over the
place, elsewhere in the program. Business logic sub-divided into
sub-programs or calls allows you to test as discrete units, and going back
to your prime program, the change may affect sub G,
but subs A through F still retain their integrity.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Effective use of DOS base memory for COBOL programs

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-21T02:53:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhD89.120051$Yd.5311367@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<62843e96.0208200118.5e0f7207@posting.google.com> <3D628200.679FE9AE@shaw.ca>`

```
Big is not necessarily nor automatically bad. For example, a very small and simple program can have a BIG memory footprint, and with
good reasons. There is also a lot of be said for having all the parts of a program
in a small number of source files. The single biggest nuisance with Java (or even more so, Smalltalk) is the vast number of parts
and pieces scattered around. It makes it difficult to get a big picture.

Even IBM fell victim to that one - I think youcould not view an entire Java source file until VA Java 3.01 was released; you could
only part of the source file (i.e. a method or the class definition) at a time. Most hatefully annoying!
-Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: Effective use of DOS base memory for COBOL programs

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-20T12:44:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208201144.c3a1876@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<62843e96.0208200118.5e0f7207@posting.google.com>`

```
calvinchin@fm333.com (Calvin) wrote 

> I have MF COBOL programs running under MS DOS. What I understand is
> that Micro Focus COBOL is using the base memory (640K) to run the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> unable to load and giving errors.

There are several ways of improving the situation:

   1.  Use XM to allow use of several megabytes of memory

   2.  Compile with OPT"0" to create byte coded programs rather
       than generated code - approx 1/3rd code size.

   3.  Optimize CALL/CANCEL operation to keep maximum free space.

   4.  Use the RTS and build your systems as .INT/.GNT as the 
       RTS (chargable runtime) will shuffle memory.

   5.  Use overlays. In the largest programs reorganise the 
       program into SECTIONs that operate at different times
       and give them section 'priorities'.  eg 50 for code
       that only runs at initialisation, 51 for code that is
       only done at end of run, 52 for phase 1 processing, etc.

   6.  Replace SORTs with ISAM files. 

I have systems that total several megabytes of .EXE that will run in
600Kb or less.

If sounds like (due to "I have running .." and "at the end,..") that
you have a system structure that does CALL/CANCEL to run various parts
of the system and this eventually runs out of memory.

CALL works by filename but CANCEL works by PROGRAM-ID.  If the
identity is not the same as the filename then the CANCEL will not
work.  This will eventually lead to running out of memory.

If there is a hierarchy of CALLing then ensure that CANCELs are done
as the hierarchy collapses to retain maximum contiguous memory.  If
there are called routines that are to remain during several successive
programs then add dummy CALLs to load these into memory early rather
then have them lodge in memory.

The basic structure that I use is a very small control program which
basically sits in a loop doing CALL/CANCEL of whatever some other
program tells it to. This starts with the name of the menu program to
start off with.  Before it CALLs this it does a DISPLAY..AT to load
the ADIS.EXE module, oepns a dummy ISAM file to load the EXTFH.EXE
module and dummy CALLs a control routine which stays in memory for the
whole run.  The idea is that these load into lower area of the 600Kb
leaving one contiguous area for application programs to be
CALL/CANCELed.

With Overlays you need to be careful about the structure of the system
and common routines performed from several overlays need to be in the
permanent part of the program, but if the program progresses through
several phases or has several sections that are executed at different
times depending on user control then it can reduce the run size
enormously.

> I have to separate those BIG programs into few smaller programs. But
> the processing will be a bit slower and less efficient.

This may or may not help if the smaller programs still have to be
loaded into memory by CALLing each other.
```

### IBM announces new release of Enterprise COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-20T14:20:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aju4r8$5a4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
A new release (to be available in Sept) of IBM's z/OS and OS/390 (still
*not* VM) COBOL compiler was announced today.  See:

 http://www.ibmlink.ibm.com/usalets&parms=H_202-195

For our "components" fans, it has the following enhancement mentioned,

"Simplified componentization of COBOL programs and interoperability enabled
with Java components across distributed applications"

P.S.  New releases of PL/I and the Debug tool were also announced.
```

<a id="t-67ecd7cd35"></a>
## Thread: OS/VS COBOL "conversion" and/or "debugging" tool — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### OS/VS COBOL "conversion" and/or "debugging" tool

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-20T14:37:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aju5qv$aho$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For those who may have missed it, from the announcement of

  "IBM Debug Tool Utilities and Advanced Functions V3.1 Helps Maximize
Availability of z/OS and OS/390 Applications"

at

 http://www.ibmlink.ibm.com/usalets&parms=H_202-193

which MUST not be confused with the announcement of:

 "IBM Debug Tool for z/OS and OS/390 V3.1"

which is at

  http://isource.ibm.com/cgi-bin/goto?it=usa_annred&on=202-194

you may well be interested in the following paragraph,

"Support for OS/VS COBOL and Other Old COBOL Programs: Source programs
written for OS/VS COBOL are supported for debugging by a "Convert and
Compile" option. This option includes source conversion during the compile
step. The compilation is done using either Enterprise COBOL for z/OS and
OS/390 or COBOL for OS/390 and VM and then the converted OS/VS COBOL source
programs can be debugged using Debug Tool. In addition, once the debugging
is completed you can choose to continue using the OS/VS COBOL compiler, or
since you have already debugged the new module and know it works, you can
use the new level of source code that was produced during the compilation
and use a newer compiler. One by one you will eventually convert all of your
OS/VS COBOL source code to the latest compilers! This feature will also
support ANY level of COBOL source program as input, including VS COBOL II,
COBOL for MVS and VM, and COBOL for OS/390 and VM programs that were
previously compiled with the CMPR2 compiler option. This source conversion
can also be done separately from the compile step if you choose. "

    ***

Now, please do NOT ask me what this "feature" does or how it relates to the
traditional "CCCA".  I am just the messenger, not the interpreter <G>
```

## Thread: Which cobol compiler to upgrade to? — 13 msgs

### Which cobol compiler to upgrade to?

- **From:** "Greg" <greg@primesoft.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-08-21T15:15:16+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ELD89.609$Fc4.85808@news.xtra.co.nz>`

```
Does anyone know which PCcobol compiler is the best to get to complile
source, which compiles correctly using microsofts v5.0 (MF 3.?) compiler? It
may need to be backward compatible to MS2.?. syntax. I need the quickest
cheapest upgrade to a 32 bit environment. I currently have about 10Mb of
source and am not too keen on a complete rewrite. Datafiles are ISAM and
user base is not too bright. :-) i.e. complicated data conversions need to
be avoided.

Any suggestions for best gui development tools to go with your
recommendation of compiler would also be appreciated.

Thank you in advance

G
```

- **References:** `<ELD89.609$Fc4.85808@news.xtra.co.nz>`

```
On Wed, 21 Aug 2002 03:15:16 UTC, "Greg" <greg@primesoft.co.nz> wrote:

> Does anyone know which PCcobol compiler is the best to get to complile
> source, which compiles correctly using microsofts v5.0 (MF 3.?) compiler? It
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Your immediate choices are Microfocus NetExpress at 
http://www.microfocus.com or perhaps PERCobol from LegacyJ at 
http://www.legacyj.com Both of those compilers support the MS Cobol 
/MF Cobol dialect used for those versions.
```

#### ↳ Re: Which cobol compiler to upgrade to?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-21T05:28:46-05:00

```
> Does anyone know which PCcobol compiler is the best to get to complile
> source, which compiles correctly using microsofts v5.0 (MF 3.?) compiler? It
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> be avoided.

I have migrated well over 100k lines of code from DOS MF COBOL
to 32 bit Net Express with very few code changes necessary.

> Any suggestions for best gui development tools to go with your
> recommendation of compiler would also be appreciated.

If you mean a GUI IDE, Net Express already has it.  If you mean GUI
application screens, go to http://www.flexus.com and get SP2.  You
can have text screens, GUI screens or HTML, all by simply re-linking
your program with the appropriate library.  Also check out FormPrint
for Windows printing.  Flexus are good folks with good products.  You
won't see anything but praise for them around here.  Bob Wolfe, Flexus
papa, keeps an eye on this newsgroup.
```

#### ↳ Re: Which cobol compiler to upgrade to?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-21T21:59:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D640DCD.668D59D@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ELD89.609$Fc4.85808@news.xtra.co.nz>`

```


Greg wrote:

> Does anyone know which PCcobol compiler is the best to get to complile
> source, which compiles correctly using microsofts v5.0 (MF 3.?) compiler? It
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> recommendation of compiler would also be appreciated.

There are several possibilities, but bearing in mind MS Version 5 is MF Version
3 - the quickest, easiest, but not cheapest - is go to Net Express. Set up a
project in Net Express, include all your source files xxx.cbl. Your copyfiles
automatically get added to the project when referenced in the xxx.cbl files. Hit
the "Rebuild All" button for a compile, and in theory at least, you should see
"All compiled without errors".

Using any other alternative or 'cheaper' compiler - I can't recall, but if MS
Version 5 allows you to use MF extensions like CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXISTS or Screen
Section attributes or Mouse 'sensing', then of course these are gong to hiccup
in any other compiler.

GUI tools - Hmmmm..... One good suggestion already made, SP2 from the 'Volunteer
Fireman' at Flexus <G>. You could use Net Express GUI classes - there's a
learning curve and it 'pushes' you towards using OO which you may not yet be
ready for. If you are familiar with the likes of VB or Delphi etc., you could
use those to build Dialogs and invoke from Net Express.

Jimmy
```

```
Bob Wolfe wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
```

```
Bob,

It helps <G>. But yours truly was a 'pen-pusher', but even from my early days I employed
W5 (Who, Why, What, When, How), even in a administrative vice like RAF administration -
first seeds of my interest in system design.

As for flying - Egypt ('51/'54) - well my wing commander, (a Lt. Col. doing a desk job),
to qualify for his flying pay had to put in 'x' hours flying per month. Once took me up
in a two-seater flying eastwards along the Sweet Water Canal, (anything but "sweet"
!!!). I guess it's an offshoot from the Nile somewhere outside of Cairo and flows
eastwards towards Ismailia and the Suez Canal.. A broad expanse of desert with a green
strip, not much more than an acre in width, either side of the Canal - industriously
cultivated by the nice simple folk, the felaheens.

He gave me the controls. What the hell. I put it into a dive. I'm sure he got more
excited than I did <G>.

>
> You are also correct that I wear eyeglasses, but I don't wear them for
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Here goes your memory lapse again. You know that guy Trubble, Rubble or whatever his
name is, born somewhere in East Montana between Butt and Bute and currently counts
armadillos lying on their backs on Texas roads. His photo was attached to an article
(cobolreport.com ???). You were unkind enough to say he looked 'funny'. I said he looked
OK to me - and you responded, "Having met him, without my glasses I would have
difficulty recognizing him from the photo".

If you check your local paper on Continuing Education - I'm sure somebody must have a
course on Memory 101 !
<G>

Jimmy
```

```
rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe) wrote:

>I wouldn't have used the word "funny" but I do recall the incident in

On May 8, 2000, Thane reported that Ken Foskey had congratulated him
on having the worst picture in the magazine.  Thane replied to him:
Garbage In, Garbage out.

You then chastised Ken for making such a comment......not me.

Because Howard Brazee had commented on how blurry the photo was in the
publication, I then commented....and I quote:

"I've met Thane in person.  If I remove my progressive lens bifocals
from my face, he looks exactly the same as he does in the picture!"

....meaning that when I remove my glasses, *everything* appears
blurry.

...no comments about anyone looking "funny."

Jimmy......I've signed us BOTH up for the Memory 101 class.  The
classes run weekly and start Tuesday next at 6:03:45 PM on the 4th
floor, room 405B of the Dogpatch Community College Student Union at
the Dogpatch Pennsylvania Community College.

pound loaf of Pennsylvania scrapple is included in the class fee.

;-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows DLL -&gt; load and use

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-08-21T22:42:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0208212142.50e9ef35@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3d633f0e_1@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
You just can't call a VB DLL directly - from ANYTHING besides VB. 
There are some tools (I don't have the reference but they have been
mentioned here before) that will convert a VB DLL such that it CAN be
called.  When you do this it's called like a C program.

If the VB code is an ActiveX control the world is more open - there
are various ways to use ActiveX controls from COBOL programs including
using Flexus Sp2 or Dialog System, or PowerCOBOL.

eckleder <member@mainframeforum.com> wrote in message news:<3d633f0e_1@news5.nntpserver.com>...
> Hello,
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for your help!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Windows DLL -&gt; load and use

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-22T02:14:47-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ak231g01mva@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3d633f0e_1@news5.nntpserver.com> <bfdfc3e8.0208212142.50e9ef35@posting.google.com>`

```
> > I have a Windows DLL (from a VB program). Now I want to use
this DLL in
> > Cobol. My Question: How can I load this DLL into my Cobol
program and
> > how can I use the Methodes? The DLL includes a Class and
three Methodes.

> You just can't call a VB DLL directly - from ANYTHING besides
VB.
> There are some tools (I don't have the reference but they
have been
> mentioned here before) that will convert a VB DLL such that
it CAN be
> called.  When you do this it's called like a C program.

You can use www.vbadvance.com to generate "standard" DLLs in
VB.

ActiveX DLLs are another matter, but they can be called by
COM-savvy enviroments or environment extensions.  I think
there's even a set of standard DLLs out there that allow you to
dynamically interface to a COM DLL.  (If it's important, I can
probably come up with a name.)

> If the VB code is an ActiveX control the world is more open -
there
> are various ways to use ActiveX controls from COBOL programs
including
> using Flexus Sp2 or Dialog System, or PowerCOBOL.

I would have to defer to your experience, though, in
determining if ActiveX DLLs (and ActiveX EXEs, for that matter)
can be interfaced by various COBOL devs.
```

- **From:** "Joanna ���wik���a" <asior@biezanow.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-21T09:46:57+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol,ibm.ibmvm.cobol.cforum
- **Message-ID:** `<ajvgid$gu3$2@news.tpi.pl>`

```
Hello,

I am not a COBOL programmer, and I don't know much about this language :-)
but there is a big problem with it. I'm working on a program in C, which has
to process data contained in COBOL indexed files. I have .DAT and .IDX file,
and file specification, i.e.
5 FIELD1
10 FIELD11    9(2)
10 FIELD12    9(2)
etc.
Now my question: how to translate DAT files into text files? Is this a valid
DAT specification? Are there any non-commercials translators? If I want to
write a parser myself, what should I do?
Please, help me, I really don't know how to start. I looked for information
with Google, but I found nothing that could help me understand the way this
files are created and the format, in which they are written.

Thanks for your help,

Joan
asior@visionsoft.com.pl
```

#### ↳ Re: DAT and IDX files

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-21T05:12:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PvJ89.11266$CD2.1254853@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<ajvgid$gu3$2@news.tpi.pl>`

```
"Joanna �wik�a" <asior@biezanow.net> wrote:
> Hello,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> etc.

There isn't "a problem with" COBOL or .DAT files.  The problem is
that you are trying to look at .DAT files as if they were created by some
sort of database program, such as Microsoft Access or Oracle.  COBOL
has a built-in indexed (keyed) file system, called 'Indexed Files'.  You
can think of it as something like a b-tree library used in a C program.
C has no such built in capability, so you should not think of COBOL as
"having a problem" because it offers capability not found in C.  If one
doesn't like it, one does not have to use it. ;-)

Specifically, .DAT and .IDX files are created by COBOL programs
compiled using Micro Focus COBOL of one version or another, and I
strongly suspect your files were created in this way.  From what you
wrote above, it appears that you have the format of the .DAT file
records.  The .IDX file contains the index data.

Here are a few possible solutions to your conversion problem:

1. If you have access to a Micro Focus compiler compatible with the
file, create a small COBOL program to read the file and write out
the data in 'Line Sequential' format, converting any COMP fields to
DISPLAY (printable ASCII), then compile and run the program.
If there are any signed or non-integer numeric fields in the file, you
should use 'numeric edited' pictures in the output, to make the data
easier to parse.  You can add comma delimiters, and enclose alpha
data in quotes too, if you like.  This is your 'best' option, in that it
is guaranteed to work, you can make your output data look any way
you like, and avoid having to convert non-integer BCD data into
floating point in a C program.  Decimal arithmetic is another facility
COBOL has that C doesn't, which is one reason why there is about
100 times more business software written in COBOL than in C
(10 million copies of MS Access is still only one C/C++ program).

2. If your file has *no* binary data (i.e. no COMP fields), you can use
the Micro Focus utility program REBUILD to write the file out into
'Line Sequential' (normal text) format.  REBUILD is considered part
of the Micro Focus COBOL runtime, and can be distributed with the
application software created using the compiler.  If you can legally
run the application, you can legally have and run REBUILD.  Check
with Micro Focus if you can't find it.

3. If your file contains any binary data, you can't use Line Sequential
format, but you can use REBUILD to convert the data to  'Sequential'
format.  Each record of a Sequential format file has a record length
field preceding each record.  Either get the record length format
from the Micro Focus documentation, or examine the file enough to
figure it out.  Then write a C program to extract the data into any
format you like.
```

#### ↳ Re: DAT and IDX files

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-08-21T12:14:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020821081432.28902.00002223@mb-ci.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<ajvgid$gu3$2@news.tpi.pl>`

```
>From: "Joanna ï¿½wikï¿½a" asior@biezanow.net 
>Date: 2002-08-21 03:46 Eastern Daylight Time
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It seems to me that you ought to be able to just read it directly.  

If the COBOL layout says
FD something...first file
01 RECORD-1-EMPLOYEE.
   05 FIELD1-EMP-NAME.
      10 FIELD1-A-NAME-FIRST                PIC X(10).
      10 FIELD1-B-NAME-LAST                 PIC X(10).
   05 FIELD2-EMP-NUMBER.
      10 FIELD2-A-EMP-NUMBER-FIRST   PIC 9(4).
      10 FIELD2-B-EMP-NUMBER-LAST    PIC 9(6).

FD something else...next file

then you may rest assured that Record 1 will always have
30 bytes.  That is to say, there are no INT's or VARCHAR's 
or stuff like that.  There are no field delimiters.  If your data has: 
JOE       BLOW      0000123456          SMITH     7986000000SAMU...etc.
Then 
       Firstname Lastname ID-prefix  ID-suffix
1st guy Joe Blow <no prefix> 123456
2nd guy <first name unknown> Smith 7986 <no suffix> 
3rd guy Samu...etc.

You just pull the first 10 characters into your First_Name variable, then
the next 10 characters into your Last_Name variable, etc. 

When I say "always" I am, of course, lying.  Some formats will have
one or two characters at the end of each record just to make
it easy for you.  I believe the Microfocus .DAT format will have
two characters marking the end of each record.  So the record
would actually be 32 characters long instead of the 30 indicated
by the file layout.  

Some formats will have spaces counted and summed, instead of
having every single space put into the data.  Some formats will
have numbers in a different order that what you might expect, and
you have to rearrange the digits. 
I don't think .DAT files do either of those.  If you look at the data,
you should be able to tell if anything unusual is going on. 

I don't think you need to look at the .IDX files.  Presumably, you
are going to move this data to a database.  Near the beginning of
the COBOL program, in the SELECT statement, it should tell you 
what the (Primary) Key is.  Most likely, you will want to keep this 
for the Primary Key in your database.  So, if the COBOL program
says KEY IS FIELD1-EMP-NAME, then you would designate
your Primary Key as First_Name + Last_Name. 

It doesn't seem all that hard to me, so I am probably missing
something obvious. 

Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

```
Ross Klatte Hi, if you dont have any fields  with  comp-3, comp-5 etc
and your  files was  created  by MicroFocus and you have a utility REBUILD.EXE,
use this, works fine

rebuild /V /T:LII INPUTFILE.DAT,OUTPUTFILE.DAT
THIS CREATE A FILE OUTPUTFILE.TXT AND OUTPUTFILE.IDX

I hope help you

Carlos Lages
```

```
table from one system to another you need to re-generate the indices in the new system.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DAT and IDX files

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-08-21T19:43:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020821154308.00669.00002142@mb-cf.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<QrR89.24064$Ke2.1873039@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
>From: "Hugh Candlin" NoPersonalMailPlease@MyDot.Com 
>Date: 2002-08-21 15:01 Eastern Daylight Time

>Are you thinking of Little-endian?

I wasn't *thinking* of it, but googling tells me that's what it is. 
    Converting data between the two systems is sometimes referred to 
    as the NUXI problem. Imagine the word UNIX stored in two 2-byte words. 
    In a Big-Endian systems, it would be stored as UNIX. In a little-endian 
    system, it would be stored as NUXI. 
    The bit ordering within each byte can also be big- or little-endian, 
    and some architectures actually use big-endian ordering for bits and 
    little-endian ordering for bytes, or vice versa. 

That looks familiar.  Apparently the situation is still alive and well. 

    
Ross
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

```
And if anyone thinks this gets "fixed" when the FINALIZER specification
becomes a TR (and who knows if it EVER will become a standard),  this is
what it says about that same section,

    ***

Replace 9.3.14.2, Life cycle for instance objects, which says:

"An instance object is created as the result of the NEW method being invoked
on a factory object.
An instance object is destroyed either when it is determined that the object
cannot take part in the continued execution of the run unit, or when the run
unit terminates, whichever occurs first.

The timing of and algorithm for the mechanism that determines whether or not
an instance object can take part in the continued execution of the run unit
is implementor defined.

NOTE The process of determining whether or not an instance object can take
part in continued execution and reclaiming resources unique to the object is
generally referred to as garbage collection."

with

"An instance object is created as the result of the NEW method being invoked
on a factory object.  The object returned is in reachable state.  An object
created in the range of finalization immediately transitions to finalizable
state.
An instance object is destroyed after it has been placed in unreachable
state.  The resources allocated for the object may be reclaimed when it is
destroyed.

The timing of and algorithm for the reclamation of resources are implementor
defined.

NOTE     The process of reclaiming resources unique to the object is
generally referred to as garbage collection."
```

#### ↳ Re: OO, Garbage Collection and draft Standard (was: Cobol and Performance

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-22T02:52:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ak257l01rrc@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<ak1m4d$th7$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in
message news:ak1m4d$th7$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...
> "An instance object is created as the result of the NEW
method being invoked
> on a factory object.
> An instance object is destroyed either when it is determined
that the object
> cannot take part in the continued execution of the run unit,
or when the run
> unit terminates, whichever occurs first.

This sounds very cutting edge.  On problem with
reference-counting is that strong circular references can block
automated destruction.  This language strongly suggests that a
trace is done to avoid that problem.
```

### Re: Migrating IBM COBOL from old levels to newer levels

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-22T05:59:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o5%89.25442$Ke2.1936689@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8n5tlu4udn0dthot3jt063afae7mrun9su@4ax.com>`

```
<SNIP>
> PROBLEMS?  Only 1, computes work differently.  Any/all computes with more
than 2 terms can produce different results.
> Example:  COMPUTE s9(5)v9(4) = (s9(5) / 100) + 1
>
> Under the newer compilers (which follow the ANSI 85 standard) the
s9(5)/100 produces an integer result (because both
> terms are integers).  OS/VS COBOL produced an s9(5)v9(4) result.  So we
lost four digits of precision.  The solution was
> simple.  We changed the code to
> COMPUTE s9(5)v9(4) = (s9(5) / 100.0000) + 1
>
    Is this true?  This seems horribly counter intuitive.  I
would expect compute to use maximum precision internally,
and round or truncate to the destination, like Microfocus.

    Do most compilers that follow the above "Standard"
have options that allow more precise math?
```

## Thread: RM Cobol archive files — 8 this month / 10 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RM Cobol archive files

- **From:** "Gabriele Boccone" <gabriele.osi@libero.it>
- **Date:** 2002-08-22T09:03:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eN199.85736$n04.2474592@twister2.libero.it>`

```
I'm trying to decode some RM-Cobol archive files, to import them into a
database.

I've found some specification for this file format on the net, but all of
them are wrong. So I'm asking if anybody has the *real* specification for
this kind of files.

The hardest part is: what happens in an archive when I delete or modify a
record? I suppose my Cobol program "marks" that record as "deleted" rather
than actually rewriting the whole file without that record.

Does anybody have more information? There seems to be no "official"
specification available on the net.

Of course, when this work is finished, I'd be glad to share my code
(although not strictly "COBOL", since it's written in Delphi, anyway it's
somewhat Cobol-related) and knowledge with everybody.
```

#### ↳ Re: RM Cobol archive files

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-22T17:20:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D651F0B.2E48B5CA@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<eN199.85736$n04.2474592@twister2.libero.it>`

```


Gabriele Boccone wrote:

> I'm trying to decode some RM-Cobol archive files, to import them into a
> database.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> somewhat Cobol-related) and knowledge with everybody.

Not a solution - just a couple of points.

- Do you know which version of RM/COBOL ? If *really old* around Version 2 which
was ANSI 74 compliant, the file formats differ from RM/COBOL ANSI 85.

- Deleted records -  correct "Deletions' are marked as such. You only truly lose
them when you use RM/COBOL file REBUILD routines. Assuming same record size, for
a REWRITE the 'new' replaces the 'old'.

If you do have access to the appropriate RM compiler then you could write your
own CSVs ( in COBOL parlance, Sequential files).

Hopefully others will jump in indicating routines that could create CSVs that
you can import into Delphi.

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: RM Cobol archive files

- **From:** "Gabriele Boccone" <gabriele.osi@libero.it>
- **Date:** 2002-08-23T07:22:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Mol99.92077$lu5.2559673@twister1.libero.it>`
- **References:** `<eN199.85736$n04.2474592@twister2.libero.it> <3D651F0B.2E48B5CA@shaw.ca>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> ha scritto nel messaggio
news:3D651F0B.2E48B5CA@shaw.ca...
>
> Not a solution - just a couple of points.

Thank you, anyway, it has been very helpful.

>
> - Do you know which version of RM/COBOL ? If *really old* around Version 2
which
> was ANSI 74 compliant, the file formats differ from RM/COBOL ANSI 85.

It's version 6, for DOS/Win

>
> - Deleted records -  correct "Deletions' are marked as such. You only
truly lose
> them when you use RM/COBOL file REBUILD routines. Assuming same record
size, for
> a REWRITE the 'new' replaces the 'old'.

Ok. And how do I tell a record has been deleted? "Same record size" is not a
starting point: you can have records of the same size in memory, but when
you write them to file they are compressed and their size is going to
change.

>
> If you do have access to the appropriate RM compiler then you could write
your
> own CSVs ( in COBOL parlance, Sequential files).

That's how the "old" COBOL program works. But we are going to add new
modules to this program, and these modules are written in Delphi. We have
some "exported data files", that are ASCII CSV. These files are some 700k-1M
records (100+ MB) and we need to import them into a DB. Now it takes a whole
night to just export from COBOL into CSV. Then we use linked tables in M$
Acce$$, to avoid importing them (we just don't have enough [time*computing
power] to do it), but in this way queries on the DB are almost impossible.

We'd like to have some external program read the archives, and import them
directly into the DB (M$ Acce$$ or Borland InterBase), it would probably
take just as long as exporting them to CSV's, but then we would be able to
query the DB for reporting.

>
> Hopefully others will jump in indicating routines that could create CSVs
that
> you can import into Delphi.
>

We already know how to do it. We simply wanted to avoid doing it, but if
there's no other way...

On the other hand: our "import routines" in Delphi seem to work fine for
small files. Records are readable, and all records in the archive is read.
But when files grow, we find some "strange" characters. If we just ignore
them, the program outputs more records (and if we treat all of them as
"deleted" records, the program reads fewer records) than it's supposed to.

Thank you for your help.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM Cobol archive files

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-23T08:54:18+01:00

```
On Fri, 23 Aug 2002 07:22:20 GMT, "Gabriele Boccone"
<gabriele.osi@libero.it> wrote:

>
Snip

>> If you do have access to the appropriate RM compiler then you could write
>your
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>query the DB for reporting.
For what you said, I assume you are continuing to use the COBOL
programs at the same time as the new modules, so this will need to be
a ongoing thing.

For this type of situations I propose the following options.

1-Change the COBOL files to BTRIEVE files if you can. (RM/COBOL works
fine with them except for a few quircks).

2-Because you are using a program that can access a database using
ODBC you can use Liant's Relativity to give your Delphy direct access
to the RM files. You can even import/link them directly in the
Access/interbase databases. This works very well.

3-Speak with www.rldt.fr and see if they have their product available
to the databases you can use. If so them this might be a better option
than the previous ones as no conversion is required, and changes are
normally very small or even none at all. 


If you go with option 2 or 3 you will have to set up all types of
files/tables relationships which may be hard sometimes. If help is
required on this area, my company does supply the knowledge and
personnel for it.


Regards


FF

>
>>
>> Hopefully others will jump in indicating routines that could create CSVs
>that
>> you can import into Delphi.
The easiest way is to write a COBOL program to do it.
```

```
>> is not a starting point: you can have records of the same size in
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

The problem is: we have HUGE archive files, exporting them requires both a

>>> Hopefully others will jump in indicating routines that could create
>>> CSVs
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> sequential ?

We read COBOL data files directly from Delphi, we found some (incomplete and
not even correct) specification on the net, and with trials-and-errors we
succeeded in reading *some* of them. Our Delphi program now reads the
archive (ARC) files, and creates the same CSV's that COBOL creates, but
takes just half the time.

If we used the CSV's created by COBOL the procedure would be:

1 - Read ARC files into COBOL
2 - Write CSV from COBOL
3 - Read CSV into Delphi
4 - Write DB from Delphi

This would be *slow*, and we'd like to leave it as a last resort. We'd
prefer reading directly ARC files into Delphi. We already know *almost*
everything about these archive files. Our program works fine for small
files, but stops working properly when files are 64k records or more. Our
biggest archive files are 1M records.

So we want to know: is there an OFFICIAL specification for RM-COBOL archive
files, or do we just have to guess and try?

>> Thank you for your help.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

Yes, we are stuck, but we are so close to the solution that we don't want to
throw away all our work.
```

```
I can't tell you how Micro Focus did conversions on the Version 2 files, but

        if in-record = Low Values
            go to process-next
        end-if

I never had an occasion where I picked up deleted records.

As you say you are so close, obviously having figured out what comp-1, comp-3
and comp-6 need to be for Delphi. Suggestion - contact Tom Morrison at Liant and
see if he will play ball and give you the information you are missing.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
10. How do I find out more about converting my Micro Focus code
to NetCOBOL?

Answer: Call us to discuss how we can help you make the
transition to NetCOBOL in a cost-efficient manner (800-545-6774
```

```
I think that Fujitsu Cobol comes with a MF to Fujitsu converter called
MFTO85 on the START menu. Otherwise it is known as MF Cobol/2 Source
Conversion Utility. This contains all the help text you should need.

Fujitsu Cobol and PowerCobol come with their own manuals online (or you
can download them from www.adtools.com). These include quickstart
guides.

Fujitsu Cobol is Cobol 2 (i.e. Cobol 85) compatible so any book about
Cobol II syntax from IBM should help.
```

## Thread: C Datatype BYTE in COBOL — 3 this month / 6 total

### C Datatype BYTE in COBOL

- **From:** eckleder <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-22T09:18:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d64f29f_2@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```
Hi,

how can I define the C Datatype BYTE in COBOL?


Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: C Datatype BYTE in COBOL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-22T12:16:36-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uma337r8egipe5@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3d64f29f_2@news5.nntpserver.com>`

```

eckleder <member@mainframeforum.com> wrote in message
news:3d64f29f_2@news5.nntpserver.com...
> Hi,
>
> how can I define the C Datatype BYTE in COBOL?

First of all, BYTE is not a C type! It is more likely a macro
for 'char' and may be 'signed' or 'unsigned' depending
upon the macro or the implementation. These things are
important!

In terms of physical representation, PIC X is the
closest; however, arithmetic cannot be done, directly,
on PIC X.

Standard COBOL has no equivalent for PIC X as a
numeric. The draft standard provides BINARY-CHAR
as either SIGNED or UNSIGNED..

Many implementations do provide an equivalent. It will
depend upon the implementation you are using. The one
you did not mention. This too is important!
```

#### ↳ Re: C Datatype BYTE in COBOL

- **From:** "R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@kub.nl>
- **Date:** 2002-08-22T18:55:46+02:00

### OT (sort-of) - Does it take math skills to do data processing?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-22T23:04:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I received the following humorous item in my email today.  For those of you
who haven't seen it before, I thought that it MIGHT explain some of the
things going on in data processing today.

    ***

Teaching Math;

Teaching Math in 1950:
A logger sells a truckload of lumber for 100. His cost of production is 4/5
of the price.
    What is his profit?

Teaching Math in 1960:
A logger sells a truckload of lumber for 100. His cost of production is 4/5
of the price, or $80.
    What is his profit?

Teaching Math in 1970:
A logger exchanges a set "L" of lumber for a Set "M" of money. The
cardinality of set "M" is 100. Each element is worth one dollar. Make 100
dots representing the elements of the set "M." The set "C", the cost of
production contains 20 fewer points than set "M." Represent the set "C" as a
subset of set "M" and answer the following question:
    What is the cardinality of the set "P" of profits?

Teaching Math in 1980:
A logger sells a truckload of lumber for 100. His cost of production is $80
and his profit is $20. Your assignment:
    Underline the number 20.

Teaching Math in 1990:
By cutting down beautiful forest trees, the Logger makes $20.
    What do you think of this way of making a living?
    Topic for class participation after answering the question:
    How did the forest birds and squirrels feel as the logger cut down the
trees?
        There are no wrong answers.

Teaching Math in 2002:
A logger sells a truckload of lumber for 100. His cost of production is
$120.
    How does Arthur Andersen determine that his profit margin is $60?
```

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote in message
> news:ak8str02n3i@enews4.newsguy.com...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Doesn't this depend on what the definition of "is" is?

Clever answer, but no. As I said above, no one's much interested in the 
facts of this matter, but I'll post some anyway:-)

Gore did not claim to have "invented the Internet", he *did* say, to 
Wolf Blitzer, that he "took the initiative in creating the Internet", 
which is a very different thing. See below how he actually "took the 
initiative in creating the Internet", i.e., recognized the 'Net's 
importance and worked in Congress to get it funded. Two people widely 
regarded as the fathers of the Internet, Robert Kahn & Vinton Cerf, 
published an article in 2000 to the effect that Gore's support and 
resulting funding were crucial to the development of the 'Net. Here's 
part of an article from:

http://199.97.97.16/contWriter/cnd7/2000/10/20/cndin/0737-0012-pat_nytimes.html

which explains Gore's role and quotes Vint Cerf:

   --------------------------------------------------------------

   Through the 1980s and early 1990s, as personal computers soared from 
a curiosity owned mostly by techie hobbyists and a few companies to a 
widespread commodity, anyone wanting to link a computer to a network had 
to choose from among the many private network systems available - 
Delphi, CompuServe, Prodigy, Genie, Bix, and a host of others. Each had 
its own e-mail system, its own bulletin boards to share information and 
search for answers on specific topics, and its own specialized services. 
None allowed any connection to the world outside the individual network 
and its subscribers.

       In addition, the Internet was still strictly limited to use on 
college campuses and in research labs. Private users did not have access 
to it, and no commercial uses were allowed.

       What changed?

       That's where Al Gore comes in. Technical histories of the 
Internet refer mainly to the technical milestones along the way. Among 
all the techno-whizzes that get the credit, only one legislator is 
mentioned - Gore - despite the fact that every stage of the Internet's 
evolution was funded and directed by federal grants and initiatives.

       Gore was widely credited in histories written long before the 
vice president's oft-derided comment to CNN reporter Wolf Blitzer that 
he ``took the initiative in creating the Internet.''

       Gore is credited by the technological cognoscenti for having 
sponsored legislation that helped launch the expansion of the fledgling 
Internet to ever-wider uses. As early as 1986, Gore articulated a vision 
of widespread connected computing. In 1989, he said that ``the creation 
of this nationwide network . . . will create an environment where work 
stations are common in homes and even small businesses.''

       Two years later, he introduced a followup bill to expand access 
to the network, saying, ``In the future, I think we will see computers 
and networks used to teach every subject from kindergarten through grade 
school.''

       None of these histories comes close to giving him credit for the 
``creation'' of the Internet. One account, written by Vinton Cerf 
(widely known, though he eschews the title, as ``the father of the 
Internet''), states: ``I think the vice president is very deserving of 
credit for his active support for the Internet and the businesses that 
depend on it daily.''

       Cerf, now a vice president at MCI-Worldcom, added that ``his 
remark was almost certainly a slip of the tongue, because he'd be quite 
correct to say `I helped create the Internet' - because of his work to 
provide an environment of support for research, technology transfer and 
e-commerce initiatives so fundamental to the Internet today.''

   -------------------------------------------------------------

 From another article published by Vint Cerf:

"Then-Senator Gore's 1986 legislation calling for the interconnection of 
the Centers using fiber optic technology ultimately led the 
administration to respond with the High Performance Computing and 
Communications (HPCC) Initiative. "

http://www.cs.washington.edu/homes/lazowska/cra/networks.html

   -------------------------------------------------------------

Finally, from:

http://www.interesting-people.org/archives/interesting-people/200009/msg00052.html

an email from Kahn & Cerf addressed to Dave (Farber) and Declan (McCullagh):


Subject: IP: Al Gore's support of the Internet, by V.Cerf and B.Kahn [ I 
second this djf]

     * From: Dave Farber <farber@cis.upenn.edu>
     * Date: Sat, 30 Sep 2000 08:29:48 -0400

---------- Forwarded message ----------
Date: Thu, 28 Sep 2000 17:43:58 -0400
From: vinton g. cerf <vcerf@MCI.NET>
To: Declan McCullaugh <declan@well.com>, farber@cis.upenn.edu
Cc: rkahn@cnri.reston.va.us
Subject: Al Gore and the Internet

Dave and Declan,

I am taking the liberty of sending to you both a brief
summary of Al Gore's Internet involvement, prepared by
Bob Kahn and me. As you know, there have been a seemingly
unending series of jokes chiding the vice president for
his assertion that he "took the initiative in creating
the Internet."

Bob and I believe that the vice president deserves significant
credit for his early recognition of the importance of what has
become the Internet.

I thought you might find this short summary of sufficient
interest to share it with Politech and the IP lists, respectively.

==============================================================

Al Gore and the Internet

By Robert Kahn and Vinton Cerf
Al Gore was the first political leader to recognize the importance of 
the Internet and to promote and support its development.

No one person or even small group of persons exclusively "invented" the
Internet. It is the result of many years of ongoing collaboration among
people in government and the university community.  But as the two 
people who designed the basic architecture and the core protocols that 
make the Internet work, we would like to acknowledge VP Gore's 
contributions as a Congressman, Senator and as Vice President.  No other 
elected official, to our knowledge, has made a greater contribution over 
a longer period of time.

Last year the Vice President made a straightforward statement on his
role.  He said: "During my service in the United States Congress I took 
the initiative in creating the Internet."  We don't think, as some 
people have argued, that Gore intended to claim he "invented" the 
Internet. Moreover, there is no question in our minds that while serving 
as Senator, Gore's initiatives had a significant and beneficial effect 
on the still-evolving Internet. The fact of the matter is that Gore was 
talking about and promoting the Internet long before most people were 
listening.  We feel it is timely to offer our perspective.

As far back as the 1970s Congressman Gore promoted the idea of high 
speed telecommunications as an engine for both economic growth and the
improvement of our educational system.   He was the first elected 
official to grasp the potential of computer communications to have a 
broader impact than just improving the conduct of science and 
scholarship. Though easily forgotten, now, at the time this was an 
unproven and controversial concept.  Our work on the Internet started in 
1973 and was based on even earlier work that took place in the mid-late 
1960s. But the Internet, as we know it today, was not deployed until 
1983. When the Internet was still in the early stages of its deployment, 
Congressman Gore provided intellectual leadership by helping create the 
vision of the potential benefits of high speed computing and 
communication.  As an example, he sponsored hearings on how advanced 
technologies might be put to use in areas like coordinating the response 
of government agencies to (sic) natural disasters and other crises.

As a Senator in the 1980s Gore urged government agencies to consolidate
what at the time were several dozen different and unconnected networks 
into an "Interagency Network."  Working in a bi-partisan manner with 
officials in Ronald Reagan and George Bush's administrations, Gore 
secured the passage of the High Performance Computing and Communications 
Act in 1991.  This "Gore Act" supported the National Research and 
Education Network (NREN) initiative that became one of the major 
vehicles for the spread of the Internet beyond the field of computer 
science.

As Vice President Gore promoted building the Internet both up and out, 
as well as releasing the Internet from the control of the government 
agencies that spawned it.  He served as the major administration 
proponent for continued investment in advanced computing and networking 
and private sector initiatives such as Net Day. He was and is a strong 
proponent of extending access to the network to schools and libraries. 
Today, approximately 95% of our nation's schools are on the Internet. 
Gore provided much-needed political support for the speedy privatization 
of the Internet when the time arrived for it to become a 
commercially-driven operation.

There are many factors that have contributed to the Internet's rapid 
growth since the later 1980s, not the least of which has been political 
support for its privatization and continued support for research in 
advanced networking technology.  No one in public life has been more 
intellectually engaged in helping to create the climate for a thriving 
Internet than the Vice President.  Gore has been a clear champion of 
this effort, both in the councils of government and with the public at 
large.

The Vice President deserves credit for his early recognition of the 
value of high speed computing and communication and for his long-term 
and consistent articulation of the potential value of the Internet to 
American citizens and industry and, indeed, to the rest of the world.


Version 1.2
Word count: 709


=================================================================
WorldCom
22001 Loudoun County Parkway
Building F2, Room 4115, ATTN: Vint Cerf
Ashburn, VA 20147
Telephone (703) 886-1690
FAX (703) 886-0047
```

- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <Aly99.132217$EJ4.4122114@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <RDL99.165558$2p2.7398483@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ak8nbs024qs@enews4.newsguy.com> <ak8str02n3i@enews4.newsguy.com> <0FW99.171049$m91.7446878@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <r7ya9.8694$yl3.130521@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```

"Liam Devlin" <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> > >
> >
> > Gore: "...took the initiative in creating the internet..." vs. Reporter:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> which is a very different thing.

No it's not. "Took the initiative in creating the Internet" is Gore-speak
for "(I) invented the internet." Let's parse:

"Took the initiative" = start by taking the first step
"creating" = cause to come into being, construct from nullity

vs.

"Invent" = produce or contrive something previously unknown by ingenuity or
imagination

The distinctions I see are "imitative" implies others followed after the
first step was taken whereas "invent" implies the whole item came into being
without further modification: "initiative in creating" is the first step of
"invention," and "imitative" is a moment, an instant, whereas "invention"
implies a discrete time interval.

As far as determining whether Gore is a lying weasel, the difference between
"invent" in "initiate" is vanishingly small.
```
