"Gabriele Boccone" <gabriele.osi@libero.it> wrote:
> >> My 2 cents from Italy:
> >>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> after that war.

Wrong.

> That means:
> - We used that kind of bomb on Japan, and that kind of chemical weapon
>    in Vietnam, because we are good.

Wrong.  We used it to remove the foliage, not as a 'chemical weapon',
you ignoramus.  We did it because the NVA was using the jungle as
cover, and lives are more important than vegetation, and also because
the long-term effects of  the chemicals wasn't known then. It's one thing
to make a mistake, it's quite another thing to intentionally commit an
evil act.  Even good people with good intentions can make a mistake.
And it's a third thing to be an ignoramus and intentionally distort facts
to fit your own personal bias.

> - Nobody should use that kind of weapons again, because they are bad.

If anyone has passed any kind of restrictions on using defoliants to
remove foliage, either in wartime or peace, I have never heard of it.

Do you equate the use of a firearm by a policeman to shoot someone
who is trying to murder a little child, with a criminal using a firearm
to shoot am innocent robbery victim?  If you do, then you are *way*
too simple. It is the act that creates the results, but it is the intention
that must be judged. Only fools look simply at an act without regard
for cause or intention.

People such as yourself who have an 'anti U.S.' mentality like to
conveniently forget that the use of the atomic bomb in WWI actually
*saved* literally millions of *Japanese* lives. I have seen actual
video footage, taken in the 70's (see "The World at War", a 26 part
documentary of WWII by the BBC), of high officials in the Japanese
government in 1945 (including the man who signed the surrender
agreement on the USS Missouri), and they said that if we had not
dropped the atom bombs, it would have been impossible for them to
have surrendered, and it was nearly so as it was. Many Japanese
fought on, even after the surrender. Had the U.S. been forced to
invade Japan, literally millions of innocent civilians would have
been inevitably killed.  The same documentary also shows young
Japanese elementary school children being taught to use knives and
sharp sticks to fight if the U.S. did invade. You also like to forget
that on one night a bombing raid of 2,000 B29's struck Tokyo,
burning the city to ground and killing over 170,000, more than the
150,000 who were killed by *both* atom bombs combined. (You
do that because it is Politically Correct to imagine there is a moral
difference between killing 100,000 people with conventional
weapons and killing 100,000 people with nuclear weapons.) It is
not *how* you kill people, but *why* you kill people that is the
moral difference. The how is a practical, not a moral, difference.

You also like to forget that at the time, Japan was occupying vast
areas of Asia and Indonesia, keeping the populations down with
such brutality that even the Nazis complained (e.g. the slaughter in
broad daylight of 135,000 innocent Manchurian civilians in one
day and the Bataan Death March). Even with nearly 60 years of
hindsight, the U.S. had only three real options at the time:
1. Cease hostilities against Japan, with no surrender, leaving
Japan to keep the territory they had already conquered and, except
for England and China who were also at war with Japan, free to
rebuild, reinforce and take more territory, also leaving the ones in
power who started the Pacific war (in 1933, not 1941) in the first
place.
2. Invade Japan conventionally. In early 1945 plans were already
on the table for this option. The estimates were that there would
be at least one million American casualties. Based on figures
from earlier campaigns with the Japanese, their casualties would
have been several million, most of them fatalities. Many people
today do not realize what an implacable foe the Japanese were,
often refusing to surrender, fighting literally until the last man.
Even after the official surrender, some Japanese units refused to
surrender, and had to be completely destroyed. Japanese civilians
were brainwashed to believe they would be tortured and killed by
victorious Americans, and many of them committed suicide, some
by jumping of cliffs while holding their children, before the
Americans got to them.  The Americans were horrified by this.
3. Use the atomic bomb to destroy two Japanese cities, killing
150,000 Japanese and no Americans.

Check your history, you will discover that all of the above is pure
fact. Now genius, which option is the more moral?

> That's what I mean with "too simple".

That's what I mean by 'being an ignoramus'.

> > Iraq: Iraq invaded Kuwait.  The U.S., along with many other allies,
> >     kicked the Iraqis out of Kuwait, and did severe damage to their
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> So why not kill him, or hurt him directly?

Fine, genius. Explain how one would go about doing all that without
having a war. Even if the U.S. could have Sadam assassinated without
a war, you would be bitching about the cruelty of the CIA by doing it.
If you were more than a blindly biased ignoramus, you would have
figured that out for yourself.

> > Kosovo: European countries, such as Italy, sat on their butts while
> >     virtual genocide was being conducted in a neighboring country.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> And what about Cuba?

What about Cuba? Last time I checked, it was a Caribbean island about
90 miles south of Key West Florida, ruled by an oppressive Communist
dictator named Castro. So oppressive, that thousands of Cubans have
risked their lives (and many have died) in overloaded boats, trying to
make the 90 miles to the U.S. coast. Florida, particularly south Florida,
is almost inundated with them. Not that I blame them, I would rather
live in the U.S. than Cuba myself.

> > Yes, the Italian army conquered Ethiopia in WWI, as I recall.  But the
> > Italian armed forces were so incompetent that the Brits kicked their
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> there's a lot of difference between WWII, Vietnam, Iraq and so on.

Yes, after Italy surrendered (in hopes of getting rid of the Nazis, a good
idea), the Americans then led a force composed of many nationalities to
free Italy of the Nazis. Just like Americans fought in Viet Nam, to defend
South Viet Nam from Communist aggression, as we had previously signed
a treaty to this effect. In Iraq we were fighting to get the Iraqi invaders
out of Kuwait. In every case, Americans were fighting and dying defending
the freedom of others. Only an ignoramus would be glad for U.S. help in
defending *their* homeland against aggression, and then criticize the U.S.
for doing the same thing for others. A hypocritical ignoramus.

> In 1945 ALL Italian people *wanted* the Nazis to be sent away from here, and
> American soldiers HELPED them. We had a monarchy, and a Fascist government,
> but we didn't want it to continue. The same in Germany. When Hitler was
> killed, German people rejoiced. They WANTED to get rid of that government.

So did the Kuwaitis and Afghans rejoice, for exactly the same reason.
Now, what was your point?

> In Iraq, people still worship Sadam,

What planet are you from? There are about 40,000 people in Iraq who do
support Sadam: the ones dependent on his continued rule. All the rest hate
him with a bitterness you or I probably can't conceive. Only an ignoramus
would think those demonstrations of support for Sadam are real.  Those
propel would be shot if they expressed anything less than absolute fidelity
to Sadam, who has executed a number of his own family members who
weren't as supportive of him as he liked. Why do you think so many Iraqi
soldiers surrendered in the Gulf War, even to news helicopters?  Because
they like the news?

> and in Yugoslavia people liked Milosevic.

Sure, the ones he wasn't murdering. The ones he was murdering did not
like him so well. Do you think the UN charged him for war crimes
because he didn't floss regularly?

> They didn't see American soldiers come and help them be free.

The ones being murdered did. The ones doing the murdering didn't,
I'll agree on that.

> Bin Laden escaped and is still alive, you just don't know where he is

Right. And he is purposely not encouraging his 'troops' by failing to
make any more videos of himself, showing he is still alive. Uh huh.

> >> U.S. Army bases, and U.S. fighter planes, piloted by people who like
> >> to fly very low (and sometimes, kill people by "accidentally" cutting
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> nobody will ever prosecute them.

And other pilots will *continue* to fly like that, because it is people like
that who make the best fighter pilots. People who are less aggressive and
more willing to 'play by the rules' generally get their butts shot out of the
air by those who are more aggressive and less willing to 'play by the rules'.
Like it or not (you obviously don't know it), that's just the way it works.

> And I think that *at least* they should be sent to jail. They were doing
> something highly DANGEROUS and ILLEGAL, and they were released,
> as if they were innocent.

It was a very tragic accident, involving serious loss of innocent life. But
in reality, like I said above, if you want the most effective fighter pilots
you can get (and U.S. fighter pilots are the best, as has been demonstrated
many times), having pilots who are 'cowboys' that 'push the envelope' is
simply something you have to live with. You don't have to like it, but you
do have to live with it, or have incompetent fighter pilots. In peacetime,
incidents like that are hard to stomach. But when the Enemy is knocking
on your door, you had better hope that you have the most aggressive,
pedal-to-the-metal pilots in the sky. Reality is not always pleasant, but
it is always reality.

> >> Of course terrorists are bad, but you simply can't kill 300 innocent
> >> people to get 1 terrorist. You are putting it like it was *too
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> you can use a hammer, why use dynamite?

A military isn't like a hammer or a screwdriver, or even dynamite.  You
can go down to the hardware store and buy those any time you need them,
or you can buy them ahead of time and keep them in a drawer or garage,
without having to maintain them. When you need a military, you can't just
go down to the store and buy one. You have to spend lots of money and
it takes a long time to build one. And you have to spend a lot of money
and time maintaining a military, or it will be useless when needed. It is
fashionable in the world today to think a strong military isn't needed.
But there has never been a time in history when countries neglected their
defense without paying a price, eventually. Human nature hasn't changed
in recorded history. Sadam is virtually a clone of Stalin. Both cold and
heartless and absolutely ruthless. And there were people like them 5,000
years ago. As long as people like that continue to exist and have power,
a wise country keeps a well maintained military. A wealthy and wise
country keeps a military that can kick anybody's butt any time. In the long
run, it is the cheapest and easiest thing to do. No sane enemy attacks
someone who can clean their clock without breaking a sweat. But you
need to let them know you can do that, or your fine military is not a
deterrent at all. Then you have to really use it to clean their clock when
the time comes, and war is not fun, even when you win. Deterrence is
much better, which is why the U.S. makes it known what we can do, and
sends a carrier group around when trouble is likely. Not for braggadocio,
but for deterrence. Better to intimidate than to clobber. But better to do
either than to fail to defend.

> And the last thing: you are probably 100% European blood, and the
> only people with a right to say "I am American" are the native
> American (indians).

At what point can you call yourself a 'native'? If you examine history,
you will find that most every place has been occupied from time to
time by various peoples. Once a culture is established and stable, then
the citizens of that culture are 'natives'. Any other interpretation does
not fit with the reality of history.

> Your family name tells your history. Your history, since probably
> 1700 or 1800, was in Scotland, then you "stopped" being European
> and begun being American.

Actually, Scottish and Welch.  I can trace my ancestry back to Welch
royalty. There's also a bit of American Indian in there too, for parts
of my family have been in America for a long time.

> Most American people tend to ignore history, since *their* history
> starts in 1700-1800.

Pardon. American recorded history (the Indians recorded no written
history) begins for earnest in the 1500's. In this particular discussion,
it is clearly apparent that at least one Italian is ignoring history.

>And their names are mostly English, Spanish, Italian, and even Chinese.
> Then: you don't know when, or how those wars started, and you think
> you can drop some bomb and have people stop fighting.

Sorry, your point (if any) escapes me. Odd that you mention ignorance
of history and say that (to paraphrase) making war on people won't stop
them from fighting. Actually, our experience in America has been that
annihilating someone's ability to threaten you is quite effective in
stopping them fighting. It sure worked in Japan and Europe, and
(unfortunately) with the American Indians. Only an ignoramus, entirely
ignorant of history, would claim that violence never solves anything.
No criminal, once executed, committed another crime.  And no country
whose entire military infrastructure had been destroyed, continued a
war. Often 'violence begets violence'. But sometimes the right kind of
violence results in peace. Just as the U.S. has been friends at peace
with Italy, Germany and Japan since WWII.

read on some newspaper (not only Italian newspaper) that UN meetings fail
because everybody agree except U.S.A. and Israel. It's very sad, indeed.

then. Are you REALLY REALLY REALLY sure? I don't think so.

See what I mean?  You are simply expressing what you want to believe,
but you have no evidence, other than your own feelings, to back it up.

philosophy?

> And since the same thing happened with atomic bomb before, and the

> think that "ignorance" is not real.

You evidently do not have experience in either science or engineering.
Humans in general have a very poor record of forseeing ahead of time
all the ramifications of changes. Even with something that can be tested
in the lab it often takes a long time to get it right. Rocket science is one
good example, environmental science is another.

> > thing to make a mistake, it's quite another thing to intentionally
> > commit an evil act.  Even good people with good intentions can make a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> newspaper and never listen to ony 1 radio. You understand?

So, I have lived in America for 54 years, but you know better than I what
the philosophy of our people and government are? You can bet your bippy
that I have more information than you do! My background is in the sciences,
where you are trained to be skeptical, and I am by nature a skeptical person.
I am rarely conned, and never twice by the same person. And neither do I
trust any single source, or even a few sources, for my information.

> >> - Nobody should use that kind of weapons again, because they are bad.
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Then you say that EVERY policeman is good? I think this is wrong too.

We may be having a language problem here. I'm not sure how good your
English is (I don't speak Italian at all), but your responses show that you
either do not clearly understand what I'm saying, or you are intentionally
distoring what I say. Nothing I said even implies that every policeman
is good. Since your response is a non sequitur, I don't know how else to
answer it.

> > People such as yourself who have an 'anti U.S.' mentality like to
> > conveniently forget that the use of the atomic bomb in WWI actually
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> about new born babies...

If you believe we live in a world without risk, you are very mistaken.
There is no such thing as complete security from risk. People have to
do the best they can do, and sometimes mistakes happen. That's life,
it doesn't mean people are 'bad', or even careless, because someting
unexpected happens. People aren't God, and do not, and will notever,
know everything.

> > weapons and killing 100,000 people with nuclear weapons.) It is
> > not *how* you kill people, but *why* you kill people that is the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> and *when*.

That doesn't matter. It is *still* the *why* that matters. You can kill someone,
or even a thousand people, but if you do it completely by accident, you are
*not guilty* of a crime. But if you *try* to kill someone, but fail, you *are*
guilty of attempted murder. Guilt and blame are entirely about *why*.

> >> So why not kill him, or hurt him directly?
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> people will continue to starve, and what else?

Again, we seem to be having language difficulties. I never implied that
if we do go in there and have a war that Sadam would survive. I said
that we would have to have a war to ensure his death.

> >>> Kosovo: European countries, such as Italy, sat on their butts while
> >>>     virtual genocide was being conducted in a neighboring country.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> No comment. You have answered.

That's amazing, because I have no idea what the question was. What did
the U.S. do to Cuba that you are complaining about? If you will tell me
*what about Cuba that concerns you*, I will try to answer.

> > Yes, after Italy surrendered (in hopes of getting rid of the Nazis, a
> > good idea), the Americans then led a force composed of many
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> be free and their government didn't want it.

For an Italian, you are not very familiar with what happened in Italy
during WWII. When Hitler suspected that Italy was about to surrender
to the Allies, he moved large numbers of troops into Italy. The Germans
took over Italy and forced the Italian military to disarm. Perhaps it was
not a conventional invasion, but the effect was the same. But no matter
how the German occupation came about, when you wanted them out, we
helped you to repel invaders, just as in South Viet Nam, South Korea
and Kuwait.

> >> In 1945 ALL Italian people *wanted* the Nazis to be sent away from
> >> here, and American soldiers HELPED them. We had a monarchy, and a
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> nation's people.

When people are under an opressive government, do you think such symantic
differences matter to the people being opressed? When they kill you just for
speaking against opression, it *does not matter* how the government got in
power. Opressed people want *freedom*.

> >> In Iraq, people still worship Sadam,
> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> opinion.

What are you talking about? Do you know *anything* about war, or human
nature? Soldiers are supposed to *fight* the enemy, not surrender to a news
helicopter! Soldiers who are proud of their government will fight. Soldiers
who hate their government won't. Iraq's soldiers mostly caved in right away.
That means the soldiers were *unwilling* to fight hard for Sadam. Can't you
see that?

> >> and in Yugoslavia people liked Milosevic.
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> independence, and other people will fight to get their land.

As I said, you try to do the best you can do. Sometimes it works out well,
sometimes it works out poorly. So you want to assign all these nefarious
intentions to the U.S. because we aren't God, and can't ensure the happiness
of everybody. When your neighbor's house is on file, you try to help. If
you get your neighbor's furniture wet while putting out the fire, it is a very
ungrateful neighbor that gets angry at you for the wet furniture. It is the
attitude of an ungrateful child.

> Milosevic was not the problem. Other people will take his place and keep
> doing the same things.

Are you blaming the U.S. because there is evil in the world?

I'm not responding any further to this person, because I am beginning

On Fri, 30 Aug 2002 09:18:03 GMT, "Gabriele Boccone"
<gabriele.osi@libero.it> enlightened us:

>>> That means:
>>> - We used that kind of bomb on Japan, and that kind of chemical
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I can't comment on the atom bomb because the U.S. dropped it on the
two Japanese islands knowing its effects and wanting to send a signal
to Japan that continued war making would result in this type of
devastation to the entire country.  While a terrible thing, I believe
it saved many more lives than it actually took.  But our Italian
friend is pretty much right on about the use of Agent Orange and
Judson is maybe not aware of the facts.  

Agent Orange -- so called because it was shipped to
Vietnam in 55-gallon drums marked with an orange stripe
-- was the principal defoliant used in what the Pentagon
called Operation Ranch Hand. It was a potent witches'
brew that contained equal measures of two powerful
chemicals, whose effectiveness had been tested at the
Pentagon's War Research Service at Fort Detrick,
Maryland, and field-tested first in the Florida Everglades
and Puerto Rico, and later in Thailand and Vietnam.
Though scientists, including 17 Nobel laureates, and
organizations like the Federation of American Scientists
and the American Association for the Advancement of
Science had been calling for a halt to Operation Ranch
Hand for years during the Vietnam conflict -- branding it
"barbarous" and a dangerous precedent for biological and
chemical warfare -- the U.S. military repeatedly
dismissed concerns about possible health and
environmental effects. Then, in 1969, scientists
discovered that one of the components of Agent
Orange, known by the shorthand notation 2,4,5-T,
caused birth defects in laboratory animals. In December
1970, President Richard Nixon announced a halt to the
use of Agent Orange in Vietnam. 

Further research showed the dioxin in Agent Orange led to a wide range
of diseases including birth defects and cancers.

But it was not only the Vietnamese people who suffered from Agent
Orange's use.  Many, many soldiers who were exposed to it have had
health problems and it took the Veterans Administration years before
the Pentagon would finally admit the diseases suffered by these
soldiers was caused by Agent Orange.

Just recently the U.S. held meetings with Vietnam and has agreed to
help them treat their citizens and lands that have been effected by
Agent Orange.


<<much snippage>>


((some information taken from a much longer and interesting article on
this issue at:
http://www.motherjones.com/mother_jones/JF00/orange.html  ))

Regards,

In article <5U5b9.32943$Hd4.7502031@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>, Judson
McClendon <jud@sunvaley0.com> writes
>Iraq: Iraq invaded Kuwait.  The U.S., along with many other allies, kicked
>    the Iraqis out of Kuwait, and did severe damage to their military in the
>    process.  The U.S. would have loved to take out Sadam Hussein at the
>    same time to prevent his doing it again, but because our allies would not
>    support that, we didn't.  
Actually, wrong. Towards the end of the war the road to Basra had been
intensively bombed. Images released by journalists were just becoming
available. The politicos deemed that furtherance of the war would be
politically inappropriate in light of forthcoming bad publicity and in
the best interests of the administration they pulled out. This came to
light in a recent documentary on British TV where American politicos
were interviewed regarding their roles in the war.

>When Iraq invades Kuwait or Saudia Arabia
>    (assuming we don't take him out first), they will realize how mistaken
>    they were to oppose our taking him out in 1991.  Sadam Hussein is a
>    man such as Joseph Stalin; he has the conscience of a rock.
I am not aware of any hectoring voices from Saudi, et al, raising any
opposition to taking out Saddam at that time. The only voices raised
were within various allied administrations (UK and USA) saying that
individuals should not be targeted. 

>
>If you
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>whining and complaining all the time.
When I was 18 I despised America as much as any one else. I have since
grown up, as has the rest of the world. However, I am not going to give
your nation state blind and total adulation in all things any more than
I have to any British government since 1979. Your nations'
administration makes mistakes just as ours does, has and will continue
to do so. Both our nations have long histories of error and crass
stupidity and the world has long memories. 

I am grateful that America became involved in two world wars. I am
grateful that America remained in Europe after WW2 and helped maintain
the balance of peace. I am grateful that America is an active member of
the United Nations and it's world conflicts. It is just unfortunate that
the good deeds are poorly publicized and often nullified by
egocentricism and the less enlightened acts.

>
>> U.S. Army bases, and U.S. fighter planes, piloted by people who like
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>steel cable on purpose, just to kill those poor people?
I agree with you on this one. An unfortunate and ill thought argument by
our friend.

Incidentally, my spell checker gave me an alternative to your spelling
of Saddam with one 'd' - Sadism.

"Alistair Maclean" <ld50macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> Judson McClendon <jud@sunvaley0.com> writes
> >Iraq: Iraq invaded Kuwait.  The U.S., along with many other allies, kicked
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> were interviewed regarding their roles in the war.

That's very interesting, and believable.  I would really like to see that
documentary.

> >When Iraq invades Kuwait or Saudia Arabia
> >    (assuming we don't take him out first), they will realize how mistaken
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> individuals should not be targeted.

I don't think the Middle Eastern countries really objected to taking out
Sadam himself, though they may have said so publicly. What they did
object to, I understand, was the amount of collateral (civilian) damage
that would inevitably result from having to go in there and dig him out,
and they were concerned that Iran might awallow a defeated Iraq and
become an '800 pound (weight) gorilla', going wherever and whenever
it wanted, doing anything it wanted.

> >If you
> >Europeans were as eager to show some gratitude as to criticize, we
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> I have to any British government since 1979.

I can agree with that.  Neither do I. :-)

> Your nations'
> administration makes mistakes just as ours does, has and will continue
> to do so. Both our nations have long histories of error and crass
> stupidity and the world has long memories.

Unfortunately, true. I can live with someone pointing that out, though it is
annoying when they constantly harp on it. What I object to is the frequent
practice of non-Americans assigning all these nefarious intentions to any
U.S. action, regardless of what our real intent was. There are undoubtedly
evil people inside the U.S. government, and certainly in the U.S. population.
But it is a gross injustice to say that the entire U.S. government itself is evil,
or that the American people are evil. Sometimes bumbling and stupid, yes.
Sometimes too self-centered, yes. But evil or nefarious, no.
```

On Wed, 28 Aug 2002 10:18:19 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
<jud@sunvaley0.com> enlightened us:

>"Gabriele Boccone" <gabriele.osi@libero.it> wrote:
>> > It is in the U.S. national interest (and everyone else's) for there
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

SEATO was an excuse and not a valid one since it was made only with
South Vietnam which in reality was a puppet government of the CIA.
But your other assertions (vital interest not threatened etc.) are
correct.


>Iraq: Iraq invaded Kuwait.  The U.S., along with many other allies, kicked
>    the Iraqis out of Kuwait, and did severe damage to their military in the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

At that time, they could have gone on their own to take Saddam out.
They would have had the support of a few of the partners and it may
have been enough.  It was a missed opportunity for which the current
administration is mistakenly trying to rectify.

>Kosovo: European countries, such as Italy, sat on their butts while virtual
>    genocide was being conducted in a neighboring country.  Reluctantly
>    (and inadvisably IMO), the U.S. attempted to do what it could to stop
>    the slaughter.
>

If I recall correctly this turned out to be a U.N. or NATO exercise
and not soley a U.S. mission.  The same apathy shown towards the
citizens of Kosovo by the bulk of the European populace was also shown
by their dragging their collective butts in the sand when atrocities
were being committed in Bosnia and Herzegovina, and various other
Yugoslavian provinces.


>> What's happened in Italy? We have American bombs in our sea (because
>> "intelligent" bombs dropped on Kosovo didn't always reach their target),
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>The pilot was on a training flight, it had nothing to do with terrorists.

Also, there were 20 people killed, not 300.  The pilot was tried and
found not guilty of involuntary manslaughter.  The pilot's defense
attorney said the military map issued to Captain Richard Ashby did not
show the ski lift, and that key jet equipment (an altimeter for one)
was not working properly. Defense witnesses also said Ashby could have
been drawn too low by a visual illusion that made him think he was
higher. 

It was a terrible accident, but the pilot was probably the least to
blame.  Others were far more culpable but have yet to be brought to
trial.

Regards,

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

The United States has always prefered economic war to actual guns.  Your MBA
types call it "playing hardball", and have a saying ... if you do not want
to play in the big leagues, stay home. Unfortunately, they always try to
play these rules in my front room.

What it means basically, is either give us all your money and land, or we
will make sure you starve, and our CIA will destroy your country.

Are you really telling me that it is the desire for peace and justice United
States is indulging in?

Pardon my scepticism, but I think the events of the last twenty years have
been based on the desire for oil, and the desire for peace and justice has
been a *very* minor consideration.  In fact, the United States has supported
every single terrorist and totalitarian government that has gauranteed
United States economic "rights" to rape the land being ruled.  As long as
the oil flowed, they were right in there with their guns on the rapist side.

The entire "cold war" was about forcing an economic rule on the world, and
*has* been indulged in since the end of the second world war.  If the United
States had no desire to inflict that on the rest of the world, you would
have absolutely no reason for any armed forces.

Three quarters of the world is starving to support an SUV in every American
driveway, and the United States armed forces are there to protect that.  You
can tart that up with "protecting the American way" all you like, but
claiming they assisting the rest of the free world out of some sense of
noblesse oblige is pure naiveity.

Donald

"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
>
> What really pisses people off is the US running arround telling us that they
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> tend not to trust people that blind/stupid/immoral/untruthfull.

In my opinion, this is looking at an action and assigning what one's own
motives would have been in taking that action. This phenomenon is very
real; I have seen it happen many times.

> Little things, like claiming the US got into the second world war to save
> our ass, when in fact, they stayed out regardless of the morality, and
> regardless of their allies, until not only had the brunt of the first attack
> been beaten off, but in fact they were forced into war by being attacked.

I certainly did not claim that the U.S. got into WWI for the sole purpose
of saving anyone elses butt. We got into WWII because we were attacked.
I said that their butts were saved *by* our coming into the war, which
is a fact. But long before we did get into the war, we were giving serious
material assistance to England. Enough so that Germany started sinking
our ships. And we did that *precisely* to help someone else. Like I have
said before, I think the U.S. should have gotten involved in WWII much
earlier. Staying out was a mistake, but going to war was not politically
possible until Pearl Harbor. I'll give FDR one thing, he knew we should
have been in that war sooner, and did the best he could to help England.

> Then, after the war, they make a hundred films claiming to have been in from
> the start, won every battle, even ones they had no troops in, and deny the
> contribution of anybody else.

Neither the U.S. government, nor the American people are responsible
for what movie producers do. Blame the producers for those things.

Actually, I've never seen a movie that portrayed the U.S. being in the
war from the start. All the movies I have seen, including a number of
films produced by the U.S.Government, got that part correct. I clearly
remember one film made by the U.S. Army that explained in accurate
detail how the Pacific and Eoropean wars started and progressed.

> It is indeed a religious issue.  You worship your own government, believe
> your own dogma, and still think god is on your side.

Don't you? One would certainly get that impression from reading what
you have written here. I have seen not one single indication that your
mind is open on this issue at all. :-)

Believe our own dogma? What would you say about anybody who did
not believe what they said they believed? ;-)

This comment from Judson, I do need to respond to.  He said,

 "You think our objection to Joseph Stalin, who shot and starved millions of
his own people, was a  *religious issue*? You can look at the social,
economic and ecological  wasteland that the USSR made of western Europe, and
say that our  objections were a *religious issue*?"

I don't think that it was "religious" in the sense of OUR *religion* versus
theirs.  But I certainly do believe that the US position toward the USSR was
treated with "religious furor" and not rational.  Remember most (certainly
NOT all) of the starving of Stalin's own people occurred BEFORE WWII when we
(the US citizens) were told what a GREAT guy he was.

We started treating the USSR as an "evil empire" (later term) about the time
that our "good friend" England was trying (and failing) to do to India,
exactly what USSR was trying (and succeeding) to do to much of Eastern
Europe.  As far as I can tell, the only REAL difference was that the US
"thought" that we would have equal influence in Eastern Europe after WWII -
while we "knew" that it was England that would have the influence in India.

"William M. Klein" wrote:

> It is EXACTLY the 1945 - 1947 period of time that I was talking about.  It
> was during this time that the US started to "hate" the USSR (and "good old
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well back to the Russkies - In defence of the US - there's a turn-up for the
book <G>.

I'm not familiar with the period, but I really can't believe that the US
population was being fed that Josef was a 'good guy' back in the Thirties. Like
the UK, US intelligence must have been blatantly aware of the horror side of
Communism.

Churchill had first hand knowledge when he was Home Secretary back in 1910-ish.
There was the 'Sydney Street incident' involving Marxists, (the mystery around
it suggests one of them was Lenin). Following on from the Communists early moves
in 1917, the British supported failed attempts by the White Russians. I'm sure
the US must have been cognizant of these events.

Communism even in its mildest idealistic form was seen as a threat to stability
in many European states. I've no idea but you probably also had, for a short
time, a US Communist Party. Logic would suggest so, as many US-Germans, with
Lindbergh in particular, were sympathetic to the Nazis, primarily because
something was being done for the Fatherland. We know Communism was a threat in
Germany, and we know what Hitler did about it.

My own conclusion - both US and UK were anti-Communist through the Thirties,
solidified by the Nazi-Soviet. pact that led to the carving up of Poland when
attacked in 1939. Britain entered WW2, still with that view - I don't know the
history - but I'm willing to bet the same attitude existed in the States.

For public consumption Hollywood produced one of Greta Garbo's last movies,
"Ninotchka" where she played an attractive but bureaucratic commissar. So Josef
and his gang were seen as a joke. Then Hitler, ignoring Napoleon's career,
decided to attack the Russians. Don't ask me the name of the movie - totally
forgotten. We now swing 360 degrees - the poor Soviets are our buddies. Walter
Huston playing the sage Russian doctor, and just to get the atmosphere right,
Erich von Stronheim, complete with shaven head, playing the Nazi Prussian
monster.

Then our good old buddy Winston Churchill gets rebuked for supporting the
Russkies. Can't remember exact quote, so a paraphrase, "I would give the Devil a
letter of reference if he would support us in getting rid of the Nazi terror..."

Were the Americans and British sheer hypocrites in supporting the Soviets. A
RESOUNDING NO. Expediency dictated that they should be used as a resource to
take on the Nazis and they bore the brunt of the battles until D-Day 1944. We
know only too well that the Soviets were spying on us, just as the OSS/CIA and
MI5/MI6 were spying on them. Back to the movies, a black and white, "House on
92nd Street".

We knew at the Yalta Conference that Josef was a double dealing bastard, and
poor old FDR was already too ill to be effective. Harry Truman took over, but
the
European game plan had already been set - the biggest mistake - the US standing
back and letting the Commies take Berlin.

We know the Soviets were constantly testing us - firstly Churchill coining the
"Iron Curtain", then Kruschev's 'Missles of October' to test the mettle of the
Irish-Americn President, followed by his "We will bury you", leading eventually
to the non-combatant movie star and his 'Star Wars" approach which was a
counter, "We will bury you with greenbacks".

I don't think western powers have anything to feel guilty about - they were
responding to real politick. If one does want to point the finger, yes the US
was guilty of allowing an opportunistic slime ball like McCarthy, to institute
his witch hunt.

The thing that always amazes me, considering the amount of money poured into the
intelligence agencies - when Mikhail Gorbachev announced the demise of
Communism, I think both the CIA and MI5 were just as surprised as I was ! I
don't once recall seeing a talking head on TV saying, "I worked for the CIA and
back in 19.. we knew Communism was gong down the tubes.............".

Jimmy

>

Donald Tees wrote:

> "Alistair Maclean" <ld50macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
> > May I remind you that Britain conquered a lot of territory with
> > ineptitude and cavalry charges.
>
> Yep.  But I think it ended with the light brigade

Most of this stuff I ad lib from memory, but went back to my kiddy encyclopedia
to confirm the reason for this one. Technically this was not 'Empire building'.
The Russians were putting the squeeze on the Turkish empire to protect orthodox
christians. The British with the phobia of the Russian Bear gaining access to
India via Afghanistan via the Kyhber Pass, or to the Middle East via Turkey,
along with the French and Sardinia, (I wonder what the hell the last
contributed !), supported the Turks and started the Crimean War, 1854-56 -
giving us Florence Nightingale and the fiasco of the Charge of the Light
Brigade, (Tennyson - "Half a league, Half a league, Half a league
onwards....").

Remember my granddaddy at Omdurman, Egypt, (or Sudan ?) in 1898 ? That was the
last 'Empire' cavalry charge which Winston Churchill participated in as a
junior officer.

Jimmy
```

old Blood and Guts looking his shiny best is stood with a US honour guard to
welcome the arriving and astonished Monty with British troops. Freddy de

I don't know if it has been released in the UK yet, but they are making
a 3-part movie of one of my favorite stories, "The Lord of the Rings",
by a countryman of yours, J.R.R. Tolkien.  Part 1 "The Fellowship of
the Ring" was released here last December, part 2 "The Two Towers"
will be released here this December, and part 3 "Return of the King"
will be released  December, 1993.  All three movies were shot at the
same time (took 2 years) in New Zealand.  The scenery is fantastic.

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
> "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> having been american.

I can't speak for the American press, and I don't like them, generally.
But I'm not ignorant of the situation, or the part others played in Bosnia.
The U.S. supplied over half the peacekeepers sent into Bosnia, and the
great majority of the airpower.  Since the EU is more populous then the
U.S., and has a larger economy than the U.S., and Bosnia is in Europe
after all, doesn't that sound a bit lopsided to you?  It does to me.

> Bosnia was a _NATO_ action, they acted in unison and it was _not_ the
> US that 'stepped' in, it was the whole of NATO in which the US is a part.

As I said, I understand it was a cooperative process.  But had it not
been for U.S. political pressure to deal with the situation, and our
willingness to take the majority of the load, I am very doubtful that
anyone in Europe would have intervened, unless the conflict spilled
over the borders.  The Europeans should have dealt with the situation
in their own backyard, whether the U.S. did anything or not.  I think
most Americans didn't want the U.S. to get involved in Bosnia at all.
Historically, if Japan had not attacked Pearl Harbor, it is very likely
that the U.S. would not have gone to war.  But it is also likely that
Germany would have eventually either declared war on the U.S., or
provoked us into declaring war on them, because we were deeply
involved in helping England (e.g. Lend-Lease and our using U.S.
warships to guard convoys going from here to England).  Actually,
you could say that Japan 'did us a favor' in a backhanded sort of way,
by dragging us into the war.

> The Americans expect their press to report how the 'US Calavary'
> charged in and 'saved the day', so they do - bugger the facts.

I certainly won't defend the American press, except to say that a very
few sources (e.g. Fox News) do try to show all sides of issues.  One
of my pet gripes is the bias and incompetence of most of the American
media.
```

members of NATO to form the security alliance.

the most.

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
> See my other reply. What you say above is true. UNFORTUNATELY
> it is that attitude which will NOT win the US any friends - least of all
> from the rag-heads in the Middle East!

No attitude, simply statement of fact. :-)  I've come to believe there
is nothing we can do to make the people in the Middle East like us.
But we *can* make them respect us, or at least respect our military
power.  And we *will* eliminate those who are terrorizing us.  The
terrorists are making the same mistake that Japan made in 1941.
Americans don't like war, and often put up with a lot to avoid one.
But any time U.S. citizens and territory are attacked (unlike e.g. in
Korea and Viet Nam), The U.S. will do *whatever is necessary*
to eliminate the threat.  God forbid that terrorists sneak a nuke into
a U.S. city, for not only will we lose a city, but at least one country
will become a glow-in-the-dark parking lot.  I really, really hope
the terrorists understand that.

This is a grim subject, but that is why the American Indians were
virtually wiped out.  Yes, I know, there were horrible atrocities on
both sides, and I'm not justifying it.  I'm just saying that when the
Indians started attacking houses at night to kidnap and kill women
and children, they were certainly doomed.  There is an attitude
deep in the U.S. psyche that can be expressed something like "Hey,
you leave me alone, and I'll leave you alone.  I'll even go way out
of my way to help you when you need it.  But you mess with me or
my people, and I will clean your clock, however long it takes and
whatever the cost."  The U.S. doesn't 'get even', we eliminate the
threat.  Which is why Lend-Lease, Normandy, Hiroshima, Bosnia,
Afghanistan, and unfortunately, the American Indian.  The terms of
"unconditional surrender" was not a slip of FDR's tongue, he was
just stating what is intuitively obvious to most Americans.
```

"Hugh Candlin" <NoPersonalMailPlease@MyDot.Com> wrote:
>
> Judson McClendon <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Respect, no.  Hate, yes.

Respect, yes.  I don't mean 'respect' in the sense of 'appreciate', but in
the sense 'wary of'.  Hate, already, what's to lose? ;-)

> > And we *will* eliminate those who are terrorizing us.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> always believe they are in the right.

Yes. Yes. Yes. Yes. Yes.  I didn't say we would elininate anyone
who *might* attack us.  But you can be sure we *will* eliminate
those who do.  The U.S. has never lost a war where our security
was threatened.

> > The terrorists are making the same mistake that Japan made in 1941.
> > Americans don't like war, and often put up with a lot to avoid one.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> So, who are you going to bomb?

You can be sure that, if someone nukes the U.S., there will be a
*severe* price to pay.  Perhaps it escaped your notice, but when
that 747 was bombed over Scottland, it took a long time, but the
perpetrators *were* found, and Libya was behind it.  When the
World Trade center was bombed in 1993, *all* the terrorists
were tracked down, even though many of them were finally found
in other countries.  You can bet your last cent that if the U.S. were
nuked, the perpetrators *would* be found, if it took 50 years.  All
of these major terrorists have to countries that protect and fund
them, or they could not operate for long.  In a case like that, any
country, and I mean *any* country, who interfered with locating
those responsible would regret it, big time.  Such interference
would be taken by the American population as an act of war.  The
fury felt and expressed by the U.S. population over 9-11 stunned
even those of us who live here, including jaded members of the
press.  You cannot imagine the rage that would result from a city
being nuked.  The U.S. population would almost be willing to
destroy themselves if necessary in order to obliterate those
responsible.  What Japan did in 1941 was trivial when compared
to nuking a city, and the U.S. virtually destroyed their civilization.
Before the decision was made to use the atom bomb on Japan,
plans were being made to invade the island, with one million
anticipated U.S. casualties.  But, once the threat from the Japanese
and Germans was eliminated, we spent $billions helping Japan
and Europe to rebuild.  Not many (any?) countries who have been
attacked show such mercy as victors as does the U.S.

> > This is a grim subject, but that is why the American Indians were
> > virtually wiped out.  Yes, I know, there were horrible atrocities on
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's all the Indians wanted.  To be left alone.

I agree.  I wasn't trying to excuse the massacre, just trying to make a
point about why it happened, moral judgments aside.
```

"Liam Devlin" <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote:
>
> Judson McClendon wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Laden dead or alive"?? What a sick joke.

In 1991, the clearly stated purpose of the U.S. led effort was to remove
the Iraqis from Kuwait.  This was done with a show of force that was
as overwhelming as ever seen in war.  You may have missed it, but
after the action the government did a statistical study and found that the
number of injuries and deaths to U.S. forces was less than would have
happened by accident if they had simply stayed home.  As much as I
and most other Americans would love to see Sadam Hussein dead,
that was never part of the plan.  Anyway, Iraq didn't attack us, they
attacked Kuwait.  And even if we definitely plan to take out Sadam, in
military operations you act when it is tactically and strategically best
for you, not the press.

How many terrorists attacks have Al Qaida conducted since we went
into Afghanistan?  Osama bin Laden is almost certainly dead, or he
would have made new tapes by now.  If you were paying the slightest
attention, you would have seen that nobody (including me) said this
would be quick or easy; quite the contrary.  The key word is *certain*.
If bin Laden is alive, it is only a matter of time until he will be dead
or in prison.  The U.S. government has stated clearly and repeatedly
from the very beginning that this effort will take years, and bin Laden
is only a tiny part of this.  There is no way the U.S. government is
going to announce that Osama bin Laden is dead until they have
absolutely incontrovertible proof.  That doesn't mean he is still alive.
```

Judson McClendon wrote:
> "Liam Devlin" <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> happened by accident if they had simply stayed home.

So you're recommending permanent war to reduce US military casualties?

I'm well aware of what was done in 1990-91 in the Gulf, my statement 
about Iraq has in reference to the current march to war. Either Baker or 
Cheney has talked about not only toppling Saddam, but a US occupying 
force! That's a very bad idea and shows both the arrogance & the 
ignorance of the current administration.

> As much as I
> and most other Americans would love to see Sadam Hussein dead,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> for you, not the press.

I thought Bush41 was right in halting the ground war when he did - we'd 
accomplished our objectives in Kuwait.

> How many terrorists attacks have Al Qaida conducted since we went
> into Afghanistan?  Osama bin Laden is almost certainly dead, or he
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> or in prison.

You're right, of course, Judson, it's only a metter of time until we're 
all dead. Personally, I hope we die of old age in our beds, not some 
nuclear or biological attack provoked by the worst US administration in 
memory; but the odds don't look so good anymore. Maybe when the next 
Congress is seated and the Democrats are back in control of both houses 
the Congress will stand up to this bellicose dweeb in the White House.

> The U.S. government has stated clearly and repeatedly
> from the very beginning that this effort will take years, and bin Laden
> is only a tiny part of this.

I would remind you of Bush's speech shortly after 09/11, which included 
the "bin Laden - Dead or Alive" bit. Bin Laden was the chief bad guy 
then and the stated target of our war in Afghanistan, i.e., we were 
going to get bin Laden and topple his terrorist network. It was only a 
month or two later that Rumsfield said he had no idea where bin Laden 
was or if we'd ever catch him.

> There is no way the U.S. government is
> going to announce that Osama bin Laden is dead until they have
> absolutely incontrovertible proof.  That doesn't mean he is still alive.

You seem to have a faith & belief in the current administration 
bordering on the childlike. The Bush administration quickly recognized 
the political value of the 09/11 attack and started talking about 
permanent war & issuing the useless terrorist alerts which serve no 
useful purpose other than to keep the electorate nervous and, thus, 
compliant. It's part of the GOP's reelection strategy.

you be happier if it had been carried out with less efficiency and skill? Or
do you want me to *say* it was carried out with little efficiency or skill,
whether it was or not? Or do object to my pointing out something that is
demonstrably true? ;-)

> I'm well aware of what was done in 1990-91 in the Gulf, my statement
> about Iraq has in reference to the current march to war. Either Baker or
> Cheney has talked about not only toppling Saddam, but a US occupying
> force! That's a very bad idea and shows both the arrogance & the
> ignorance of the current administration.

And you show complete ignorance of what would happen in the instant
power vacuum that would be created when a tyrant dictatorship like
Sadam Hussein's is taken out. Never anything good. Dictators like Sadam
or Stalin eliminate anyone who could even potentially threaten them.
This means that anyone who might be able to run the government without
Sadam has already been shot. There will be no government infrastructure
remaining, because anyone in government who hasn't already been shot
will either be killed in the process of removing Sadam, or will need to
be arrested and tried by the UN for war crimes in supporting such a
monstrous dictatorship. And have you thought of what would happen if
the radical Muslim (even by moderate Muslim standards) regime in Iran
took the opportunity to waltz right in and take over? From your comments,
you aparently have not.

> > As much as I
> > and most other Americans would love to see Sadam Hussein dead,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> accomplished our objectives in Kuwait.

I agree with what you said there. But in the whole picture, it was a mistake
not taking out Sadam Hussein at the time, because either somebody goes in
there and takes him out now (which will be far more difficult), or Sadam
is going to develop sufficient means of mass destruction to seriously threaten
the whole Middle East. Human suffering aside, more countries than the U.S.
depend on oil supplies from the Middle East. Sadam already set fire to all
the oil wells in Kuwait. Do you think he would hesitate to waste the whole
Middle East oil fields to keep himself in power, should he have the means
and opportunity? Or even do it for revenge, as he apparently did in Kuwait?

> > How many terrorists attacks have Al Qaida conducted since we went
> > into Afghanistan?  Osama bin Laden is almost certainly dead, or he
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> the Congress will stand up to this bellicose dweeb in the White House.

May I remind you that all the major wars the U.S. has been involved in
for a long time were initiated under the Democrats?

> > The U.S. government has stated clearly and repeatedly
> > from the very beginning that this effort will take years, and bin Laden
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> was or if we'd ever catch him.

You're nitpicking over symantics here. Nobody thought that taking out one
single individual would stop bin Laden's organization. Rumsfeld was
just being honest, not stating a change in U.S. policy.

> > There is no way the U.S. government is
> > going to announce that Osama bin Laden is dead until they have
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> bordering on the childlike.

Wow, are you off base there. I said that because of human nature,
not having anything to do with the Bush administration per se. If the
U.S. government announces that bin Laden is dead, then he turns up
alive, they look like fools. Nobody wants to look like a fool if they
can avoid it. It would be utterly stupid to announce his death without
absolute proof. Even if you believe Bush himself to be that stupid,
he is surrounded by people like Rumsfeld who clearly aren't. If bin
Laden is dead as I suspect, his body may be buried in a deep cave
somewhere, and we may never have truly absolute proof. I suppose
nothing less than a body with a DNA match to his family could do it.
```

```
"Liam Devlin" <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote:
>
> Do you remember that the Israelis unilaterally bombed a site years ago
> (80's?) at which Saddam was supposed to have been on the verge of
> nuclear weapons? Why wouldn't that be a useful tactic in 2002?

The nuclear reactor was easily found and targeted. The huge construction
process of such a thing, not even in secret, makes it a sitting duck.  But
bio and chemical warfare agents can be made in relatively small labs,
tucked away in any of millions of buildings. It takes hard, on the ground
intelligence to bomb those.

> Finally, where is it written that the heads of government of sovereign
> nations have to meet the approval of the US government? The arrogance is
> unbelievable, as is the hypocrisy, i.e., what about countries like China
> (the commies)? We never discussed declaring war on the USSR to topple
> "evil" leaders like Stalin.

Any government has it's own safety to consider, and when a foreign
leader is a threat, it is the business of government to deal with that
threat. This is the only legitimate reason countries maintain a military.
The U.S. is no different in that respect than any other government,
except that the U.S. has a bigger stick than anybody else, right now.

As far as China or the USSR are concerned, if a policeman catches a
drug pusher in the act, he might be able to simply grab and arrest him.
But if that same policeman becomes aware of a huge drug ring, that
approach won't work, even though the crime is the same. That also
goes for countries. This is only common sense, after all. Some things
are doable one way, other things require a different approach, even
if the reason for doing both is the same.
```

Judson McClendon wrote:

> "Alistair Maclean" <ld50macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
> Really?  Was that what the U.S. was doing when we saved your butts
> in WWI, WWII and the Falkland Island war?

You keep going off at a tangent about what the US did. Jeez does it piss me
off !. You saved nobody's butt. Foreign diplomacy is all about 'self
interest'. You obviously are looking at a different history book from the rest
of us. According to your logic the calendar dates for the two wars are :-

WW1 - 1917 - 1918
WW2 - 1941 - 1945

You saved nobody's friggin butt - you got into both wars because you as a
nation were provoked. Even as a child of 10 I recall picking up Life magazine,
or some other - full page ad, "Help Britain to help ourselves". Now there's a
really *noble* reason to save somebody's butt !

The Falklands - gimmie a break. I dislike the lady intensely but Maggie
Thatcher did it on her own, and being from her generation totally support her.

And in case some of the others think I'm a peacenik - after doing 12 years in
the RAF, I can assure you I am NOT.  I *fully* support the US taking out
Saddam Hussein. From a British TV documentary he has been a bloody menace
since his youthful days of exile in Egypt. But I do want the current coterie
in the White House to stop blathering about it - in SECRET, plan it and
execute it ! And the most *important* part of the plan has to be
RECONSTRUCTION of Iraq as a secular, democratic society, fully acknowledging
*true*  Islam in their culture - look to McArthur and Japan for a model., or
closer to home, Mustafa Kemal Ataurk's Turkey (Whatever you do - don't use
what's gong on in Afghanistan as a model - I have grave doubts the existing
*democractic* regime, comprised of many former war lords,  will last more than
two years).

Jimmy
```

"Alistair Maclean" <ld50macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
> Judson McClendon <jud@sunvaley0.com> writes
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes. If you can't see that then don't pontificate about politics.

We are no less secure because South Viet Nam was defeated years ago.
We would not have been less secure had South Korea been defeated
either. Our 'interests' would have been affected, but not our national
security. Korea was a UN action, and our defense of SVN was because
of treaty obligations. Neither was because our country felt directly
threatened. Believe me, I can see that very clearly.

> >Libya did not nuke a U.S. city.
>
> But you brought up the example of Lockerbie. I merely corrected your
> misrepresentation.

Your 'correction' was misapplied. I used Lockerbie as an example of
the lengths the U.S. will go to track down terrorists. Nuking a city was
an example of extreme threat and damage to the U.S. You are comparing
apples to oranges. :-)

> >Really?  Please supply references.
>
> Watch the news. Try BBC News 24.

That's a little vague, don't you think. ;-)

> >Really?  Was that what the U.S. was doing when we saved your
> >butts in WWI, WWII and the Falkland Island war?
>
> The US hung back from both world wars and had to be forced into war
> on both occasions.

You're correct. Unlike what has been stated in these threads, the U.S.
does not like to go to war. But if someone even accidentally knocks
you from in front of a truck about to crush you, they 'saved your butt',
did they not? When they contribute $billions from their treasury and
send millions of their soldiers to shed blood indefeating an enemy
that would have almost certainly defeated you without help, then yes,
they did 'save your butt'. The French helped 'save our butts' from you
guys in our Revolutionary War. Then they gave us a nice bronze lady
to stand watch over New York. :-)

> As for the Falklands War I remember a certain politician
> who spent much of his time flying across the Atlantic trying to convince
> the British not to mount an attack whilst the USA sat on the fence (so
> much for the special alliance). Is that really saving our (UK) butts?

No, but providing essential intelligence and munitions was.

> Thanks for the Big Bird intelligence.

You're welcome.

> IIRC, USA also was involved in demanding reparations.

Even if you're correct, it was still a bad idea. :-)  We did take another
view after WWII. The Marshall Plan was a stroke of brilliance, and
worth every penny.
```

> <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> writes

Alistair,

Give us a little more background on this one. I checked my kiddy
encyclopedia index and it said "Picts - People of disputed origin, early
inhabitants of Scotland...". Didn't pursue the reference but a Google search
showed up UK royal families with two trees for both the Picts and Scots
roughly 400 to almost 1,000, eventually integrated by Mac Alpin. (He's the
guy who finished up building roads isn't he <G>).

You seem to imply the Picts were not of Celtic stock - although we know the
Celts stretched originally all the way to Eastern Europe. This one kind of
ties in with Judson's comments about the inevitable demise of the N.
American Indian. Progressive invasions inexorably pushed the Celts west
until we finish up currently with, a small enclave in NW Spain, Brittany
(Bretons), Cornish, Welsh, Scottish and Irish. (Can't remember, does the
Isle of Man fit in this group ?).

See Bobbino - we are back to those Celts again <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
> Actually the Japanese attacked the Pearl Harbour fleet because the
> Americans were blockading the Japanese trading with Indoneasea.

It may have escaped your notice, but the Japanese were conducting a
war of agression in China.  The Japanese did things there that were
so brutal it shocked even the Nazis, such as executing 135,000
civilians in one day.  Which was why the U.S. was trying to put a
clamp on Japan's oil supplies.  The war in Asia started in 1933, not
in 1941.  The U.S. didn't start that war, we just ended it.

> > Americans don't like war, and often put up with a lot to avoid one.
>
> They are usually late, yes.

Oh, we're war prone, just late getting there, eh?  Your balanced
attitude is showing.

> > But any time U.S. citizens and territory are attacked (unlike e.g. in
> > Korea and Viet Nam), The U.S. will do *whatever is necessary*
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> doubt that you will _ever_ understand that.

Duh!  If the U.S. *were* threatening their lands or sovregnity, what
you said might make sense.  But since it is an outright fabrication, no
I will _never_ understand that.  It was the Europeans who were the
imperialists, not the U.S.  We would have never been in Viet Nam if
the French had not acted so arragantly and stupidly there after WWII.
It wasn't the U.S. who divided up the Middle East with such amazing
insensitivity to local feelings and ethnicity.  The U.S. had nothing to
do with making Kashmier such a point of contention.  All those things
were done by Europeans.

> > This is a grim subject, but that is why the American Indians were
> > virtually wiped out.  Yes, I know, there were horrible atrocities on
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> IT WAS THEIR BLOODY LAND THAT YOU STOLE, YOU IDIOT.

Yes, and I wasn't defending it, you moron.  I was simply describing one
of the mechanisms that caused it.  What do you think the phrase "I'm not
justifying it" means?  Duh!

> Typical American arrogance: we will steal your land and if you fight
> back we will eliminate you because we have bigger bombs.

Typical non-American stupidity: Americans are horrible, until we need
them to get us out of whatever trouble we've gotten ourselves into.  Then,
once they've spend their blood and money getting us out of our fix, we
are free to resume bashing them again.  Europe would have (and almost)
self-destructed twice in the 20th century, if the U.S. hadn't come to their
rescue.  If the U.S. had been imperialistic, we could have *owned* the
world after WWII.  Most of the rest of the world was in rubble, and we
had the atomic bomb.  We could have annexed Japan and crushed
Russia.  But instead, we disarmed and spent $billions helping both our
allies and former enemies to rebuild.  For decades we spent even more
$billions keeping the Soviet Union from immolating Europe.  You think
a country with 400 years of conquering territory, and consisting mostly
of conquered territory, led by a maniac like Joseph Stalin, and holding
an army that had just defeated Germany, would not have done the same
thing to Western Europe that it did to Eastern Europe?  Yet not one of
our allies, even England, paid even the interest on the money we lent
them for the war.  No country, and not even the several largest other
countries combined, give and do as much toward helping others in
need as do Americans.  And still we get this kind of stupid ignorant
crap from people living in places like Europe and New Zealand (Do
you think that a successful Japanese army would have stopped short
after conquering Australia?) that would have been dog meat except for
U.S. military intervention.  And we get it through a communication
medium that everyone benefits from which we created.  Thanks, it's
nice to be appreciated.  Especially by those who hold such 'well
informed' and 'balanced' opinions.  The U.S. isn't perfect, but it has
earned many times over a far better evaluation than people like you
are willing to offer.  It's easy to sit on the sidelines and criticize.  But
it is a far more difficult thing to lay it on the line in blood and money,
as the U.S. has done again and again, to little credit or appreciation.
It is because if this attitude that many Americans could care less when
those like you criticize.  We know what we've done, what we are
doing, and what we are willing to do.  And no amount of criticism
from ignorant, ungrateful morons is going to make us forget the good,
or the bad.  You may take that and shove it anywhere you like. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Presidential eligibility.

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-30T15:26:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208301426.342b398c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0208271936.1062236d@posting.google.com> <akif9c$s7o$1@panix1.panix.com> <3D6DCE40.3B4A00F3@Azonic.co.nz> <akk2t2$j91$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote 

> 
> I'm sorry, there seems to be some logic that escapes me... are you saying 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> discourse, please?

All elections have criteria for who is eligible to vote: age,
citizenship, residential status, etc.

Note that settlement didn't start in NZ until the 1840s (except the
Moari who started in approx 1000AD or so).

In the 1850s the criteria was that everyone who was over 21 and was a
land owner, or business owner or rent payer or some other minor
criteria could vote. This included Maoris and any other race but
excluded itinerants, visiting sailors, whalers and sealers.

Now it happened that women were not land owners or rent payers. That
is, it didn't necessarily exclude women because they were women, but
because they didn't meet the eligibility criteria.  This was corrected
by changing the eligability to one based on residency and specifically
including women.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Presidential eligibility.

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-28T05:44:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VD1b9.30917$Hd4.6575191@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <ak8str02n3i@enews4.newsguy.com> <qF1a9.50602$%v4.1456651@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D692000.194ECAAA@shaw.ca> <anfa9.5422$2E6.2196968@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <akddqe$7ok$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <yQra9.4995$yt3.2105167@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <akdn8r$d6v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ake0es$kjg$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <ake33b$jvs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jVza9.5996$p%3.454322@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0208271140.1539fdf7@posting.google.com> <BvRa9.78343$%v4.3258660@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D6BEEA6.A0532820@shaw.ca> <HfTa9.79357$%v4.3289441@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <DKZa9.35541$yl3.816531@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```
"Liam Devlin" <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> > This is a grim subject, but that is why the American Indians were
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> they attacked or who got scalped.

Conquer, yes.  Virtual genocide, no.  Their culture was doomed from
the start, yes.  Any time an advanced culture encroaches on a primitive
culture, this is (for good or ill) the inevitable result.  Had the Indians
accepted it, they would have been absorbed into the new culture.  They
would have (unfortunately) probably been second class citizens for a
while, but not forever.  And they would not have been slaughtered as
they were.  No moral statement, just making a point.  Slavery, another
black mark on American history, could have been eliminated without
bloodshed.  It is a little known but documented fact that before the
civil war the southern states proposed federal legislation that would
have eliminated slavery over several years (and in 1860 the Virginia
legislature came within one vote of abolishing slavery in Virginia).
But the northern states killed it.  I'm not sure exactly why, but I think
it was because they wanted it stopped *immediately*.  However, this
would have destroyed the economy of the southern states, because so
much capital had been paid to northern slave traders to buy the slaves.
No doubt the northern states waited until the market on slaves was
down before promoting this action.  Actually, slavery in the Americas
was started by the colonizing European countries hundreds of years
before the U.S. revolution.  By the time the U.S. became a country,
slavery was already well entrenched in the culture and economy by
the British.
```

"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Primitive?  You mean that they had not progressed to mechanized warfare?

In this case, I mean primitive technologically.

> The north american culture was an peacefull stable agricultural culture for
> 12,000 years or so, and had developed mathmatics, medicine, and most of the
> current world food crops (all the bean and corn crops, about have of our
> grain) before the spanish came.  In many ways, it was far more advanced than
> the war culture that superceded it.


It is also true than many Indian women were treated as virtual slaves,
spending maybe 18 hours a day cooking, chewing leather so the braves
could wear soft moccasins, and generally supporting the men while
they ran around and fought among themselves. If you think the Indians
were all peaceful and kind and just, you have been reading too much
revisionist history. Some tribes were peaceful, but some were very
warlike. The Indian cultures were no better or worse (morally) than
similar cultures of the times. The mathematics you speak of was not
developed or used by the North American Indians, but by the Incas
and Aztecs of Central and South America. In addition to mathematics,
they also practiced human sacrifice on a vast scale, and some
anthropologists believe it contributed to the eventual destruction of
their civilizations. I live in an area that is rich with Indian cultural
influences. Many of our place names are still Indian names (e.g.
Chattanooga, Chickamauga, Tallapoosa, Tuscaloosa, even Alabama).
There is a pretty thorough curriculum of local history in the public
schools here. There are Indian burial grounds a short distance away.
I am pretty sure that my ancestry includes at least a few Indians along
the way, based on old family photos.
```

"Liam Devlin" <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Judson, you are truly an embarrassment in front of the rest of the world!

I hope they are enjoying the show.

But why? For making observations without moral assessments? I neither
contributed to, nor approve of, the virtual genocide of the Indians. But
there is nothing wrong with making observations about the mechanics
of how attrocities occur, even when you find them abhorent. A truly vast
number of books have been written about the attrocities the Nazis
committed. Understanding the mechanics of something helps to better
understand why it happened, and hopefully prevent its recurrance. I
would certainly hope that any condemnation I would make about the
Indian massacres would be entirely redundant. Or do you think there
are still people out there who need to be informed that it was a Bad
Thing?

> > Slavery, another
> > black mark on American history, could have been eliminated without
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> so-called investigations.

No, Congressional Record documents currently residing in the Library
of Congress.

> And slavery in the US was all the fault of the Brits, right.

No, but the Brits started it here, and in other places like Jamaica and
other Caribbean islands. One of the most popular Christian hymns
of all time, one that is familiar to many non Christians (e.g. the movie
"Star Trek II") is "Amazing Grace", written by a (previous) British
slave ship captain named John Newton, who composed it around
1760-1770, before the American Revolution. You can find more
details at: http://www.anointedlinks.com/amazing_grace.html.
British slavery was well documented, even in the popular British
fiction of the day. (e.g. Jane Austen's "Mansfield Park", published
in 1814). But it is clear that slavery was firmly embedded in the
Colonial economy well before the U.S. became a nation. That does
not excuse the Americans from continuing the practice after British
rule was overthrown.
```

On 27-Aug-2002, "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

> No attitude, simply statement of fact. :-)  I've come to believe there
> is nothing we can do to make the people in the Middle East like us.
> But we *can* make them respect us, or at least respect our military
> power.

In general, whether they like us or not depends on two things:

1.  Whether they like their governments.
2.  Whether we are friends with their governments.

No + yes = dislike.
No + no = like
Yes + yes = like
Yes + no = dislike

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Presidential eligibility.

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T18:10:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mCTa9.79551$%v4.3303163@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <ak8str02n3i@enews4.newsguy.com> <qF1a9.50602$%v4.1456651@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D692000.194ECAAA@shaw.ca> <anfa9.5422$2E6.2196968@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <akddqe$7ok$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <yQra9.4995$yt3.2105167@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <akdn8r$d6v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ake0es$kjg$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <ake33b$jvs$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <jVza9.5996$p%3.454322@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0208271140.1539fdf7@posting.google.com> <BvRa9.78343$%v4.3258660@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <FxSa9.4387$uF.637551@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
>
> He can?  I thought he would need at least one other person to agree before
> he could kill anybody he wanted.  Perhaps the laws of the States should be
> ammended to at least the point where a madman cannot destroy the world.


For any extended action, the President needs approval from Congress,
because they can cut off the funding.  But for a limited action, all he has
to do is order it done.  In cases where the action would violate statutory
law, he can declare a 'True and Present Danger' to the security of the
U.S.  The U.S. Constitution places the military under the authority of the
President (he is the Commander in Chief of the armed forces)  The
current U.S. restriction on assassination is a Presidential Order (by
Gerald Ford, IIRC).  Any sitting President could undo that in 5 minutes.
When it gets right down to it, the President of the U.S. has the authority
to order a strike on just about anybody whenever he wants.  Of course,
he will pay the political costs eventually.  If a President tried to do
something seriously out of line, like nuking somebody without cause, it
is almost certain (IMO) that high level military and staff would refuse
the order and claim he was mentally imbalanced.
```

news:eZ4b9.32441$Hd4.7374906@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com...
> "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
