```
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No Judson.  They are enraged when it happens at home.  When it happens in
other countries, it is not even reported, and people like you are "not
familiar with what you refer to".  The incident I am refering to is 30 years
old, but since a few of my friends happened to die in it, I am still just a
little pissed off.

Very briefly, your CIA decided to infiltrate Canada, just in case they had
to bump off Trudeau for not liking either Johnson or Nixon.  As part of
that, they rigged an experiment at McGill to drug a bunch of french
separatists.  Thirty students either died or ended up in insane asylums. The
reasons stated for doing it in Canada were that they would have been
prosecuted if they did it to United States students.  The United States
refused to name anybody involved, because it was a matter of national
security.

> > How about like arming the Shah of Iran a few years back.  Judson,
> > it was your CIA that armed and created all those terrorists.  Now
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> USSR.  Are you saying that was a bad thing?  Should we have simply

Yes, I am.  But it started at least ten years before the soviets were even
involved. It started with the US supporting the dictatorships for oil.

> watched as the Soviets slaughtered those people and do nothing, as
> the rest of the world did?  If you don't like that tough, live with it.

No, I will not live with it.  I will not allow you the right to rule me
because you have better weapons.  How is it that the Soviets declaring war
on the Taiban is so incredibly evil that you were forced to go in and help,
but the US declaring war on the Taiban is so incredibly good that I have to
help or get threatened?

> Where the U.S. failed was not to do more to help stabilize the situation
> in Afghanistan after the Soviets left.  Does anyone think that the Afghans
> are worse off today than they were a year ago?
> --

Does anyone think an Afghan will get treated by the United States equally to
a United States citizen in either country? Even in Afganistan, US citizens
are not subject to Afghan laws.  That is the military system that you have
set up.  All of your rights only apply to you.  They do not apply to me, nor
to any other citizen of any other country in the world, including
Afganistan.  In fact, the US has placed their prisoners in Cuba, precisely
so that their own internal laws for their own citizens will not have to be
applied.  One set of rules for the yankees, another for the rest of the
world.

What I think is that United States foreign policy is designed to make a few
incredibly rich and powerfull people in the United States even richer and
more powerfull.

I think that is being done with close to a complete disregard for the rights
of anybody, and only lip service to the internal laws of the United States.
Fiasco's like Enron are symptomatic ... US bussiness runs the US government,
and there is a total disregard to any morality at all until someone inside
your country gets some flack over it.  If it is done outside the country, no
one in the states gives a rats ass.  Their attitude is "live with it ... as
long as I can sit in my comfortable pew, inside my little enclave, I am not
responsible.  I got mine, and I got the big guns to enforce it.".

Donald
```

```
Richard wrote:

> "Hugh Candlin" <NoPersonalMailPlease@MyDot.Com> wrote
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> is just another visitor.

That's tellin' 'em Ricardo <G>. Yes the US is the most POWERFUL country
in the world economically and militarily - and following from that the US
president is powerful - *subject* to a consensus - as FDR had from the
Congress and Senate following December 7th (8th ?), 1941. Bill Clinton's
ideals of Health Care reform went nowhere without the blessing of the two
Houses; similarly, although supported by Clinton, the former SEC
chairman's recommendations were negated by both Republicans  and
Democrats voting against 'their' president..

By comparison the UK model - A nominal head of state, (the monarchy, not
a president), means that in the UK and Canada, and I'm sure the same
applies to Australia and New Zealand - the PRIME MINISTER has more
day-to-day power than the US president. The Prime Minister having got his
cabinet to agree (1) Can call a snap election anytime before his/her
mandate runs out, (2) At cabinet level decide whether or not to support
the US in the Hussein/Iraq affair, (3) Introduce Sociall/Health schemes
or Budget as they see fit, (4) Strengthen/lessen military logistics
etc..., the list goes on an on. When I say 'at the cabinet level', more
often than not this is an 'inner circle' of the more powerful within the
cabinet - not quite the same, but a bit like George W's 'Texas cowboys'.

Having ensured they have their party MPs on a leash, they can push
through any legislation they want. Neviille Chamberlain didn't ask
parliament in 1939, and Tony Blair didn't ask parliament in 199? - before
getting into WWII or the Gulf War. (Same with Maggie Thatcher and the
Falkland crisis). They inform parliament that this is what is happening,
just as FDR did back in 1941. It was emotion that carried through the
last event, regardless of rules/regulations.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
for a year. That year gave Britain "some" time to ramp up war production and
prepare for what was to come. As it was, England was hard pressed to stave
off the Nazi juggernaut. Had England not had the 1000 Hurricanes and
Spitfires built during that year (for example), the results might have been
different.
```

```
That last is a valid interpretation. However, Chamberlain was an innocent, (from
memory), from an industrious merchant family, Edwardian moral values coupled
probably with a 'chapel' type of religion. It's also possible that he was
already suffering the first signs of cancer back in '38. His approach was pure
naivetï¿½, when he waved his piece of paper on returning from Berchesgarten
(phonetically that looks right <G>). Hitler and Ribbentrop guffawed at the
English fool they had to deal with.

As regards the Battle of Britain - in truth the British didn't win it, the Nazis
lost it. Having post-war spoken to people who were then youngsters in 1940, the
RAF was soorly pushed to survive. I think, resulting from a token propaganda
raid on Berlin, the Luftwaffe was directed to attack London - leaving the
fighter stations of No. 11 Group intact.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT (sort-of) - Does it take math skills to do data processing?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T23:56:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%eza9.5566$2E6.2297053@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <Aly99.132217$EJ4.4122114@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <RDL99.165558$2p2.7398483@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ak8nbs024qs@enews4.newsguy.com> <ak8str02n3i@enews4.newsguy.com> <qF1a9.50602$%v4.1456651@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3D692000.194ECAAA@shaw.ca> <anfa9.5422$2E6.2196968@newssvr28.news.prodigy.com> <akddqe$7ok$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <akddqe$7ok$1@peabody.colorado.edu>, "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
>
>On 25-Aug-2002, spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller) wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>President or Speaker of the House or even do a Gerald Ford?

(Brief mea culpa: above quotation was typed from an obviously imperfect 
memory. Quotations below are copied directly from an authoritative source.)

Amendment 22, Section 1: "No person shall be elected to the office of the 
President more than twice, and no person who has held the office of President, 
or acted as President, for more than two years of a term to which some other 
person was elected President shall be elected to the office of the President 
more than once... " (The remainder of Section 1 is a disclaimer stating that 
this does not apply to whoever is President at the time the article was 
proposed or enacted.)

Amendment 12, Section 1, final sentence: "But no person constitutionally 
ineligible to the office of the President shall be eligible to that of 
Vice-President of the United States."

By the strict letter of the law, I suppose it might be possible for a former 
two-term President to be elected Speaker of the House, and succeed to the 
Presidency again if the President *and* the VP die simultaneously. I'd expect 
to see a Supreme Court challenge to that, however. And since the line of 
succession after the VP is established by Federal law, not by the 
Constitution, it would not surprise me to learn that such a situation is 
already provided for under the law.
```

```
As for Bill, I don't see how.

Senate Document 103-6, "The Constitution of the United States:
Analysis and Interpretation, Annotations of Cases Decided
by the Supreme Court of the United States to June 29, 1992"
is available at:
< http://www.access.gpo.gov/congress/senate/constitution/toc.html >

This consists of approximately 50 each PDF and HTML files.

There are supplements for cases through 2000.

Article II and Amendments 12, 20, 22, and 25 address the
constitutional issues of the office of President of the United
States.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT (sort-of) - Does it take math skills to do data processing?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T01:23:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020826212317.00719.00003328@mb-cf.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<umlh5t4uaooma9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
>From: "Rick Smith" ricksmith@mfi.net 
>Date: 2002-08-26 20:24 Eastern Daylight Time
>

>For former President Clinton to become President again,
>she will have to run in her own name. <G>
>

Probably Nancy Reagan wielded a lot more power than the
woman from Arkansas.  

I am reminded of Lurleen Wallace.  When her husband George was
prevented by the state constitution from running for another term for
governor, she ran on a campaign pledge that she would do whatever 
her husband told her to do.  She won with 54%.  On those issues
which were attracting national news coverage, she apparently 
fulfilled her pledge.  On unimportant issues, such as care for
the mentally ill, she planned and was carrying out her own policy. 
Sooner or later, certainly, there would have been a clash.  Such 
an interesting situation was prevented by her death from
cancer at the age of 42.  

There is nothing in any constitution or statute that would prevent
this kind of shogun rule on a national basis.  If an outgoing 
two-term president were an admired ex-member of the television 
news media clique, then I think it could be very easily done. 
The constitution provides--in theory, at least--for temporary
replacement if the president is mentally or physically incapable
of carrying out his duties on the golf course.  But there is no
provision for dealing with a president who is fully capable of
doing his job but who voluntarily delegates it to someone else.  
The only thing that is actually required of a president is 
his signature. 



Ross
Roebuck, South Carolina
http://community.webshots.com/user/ross_klatte
```

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net...
> I received the following humorous item in my email today.  For those of
you
> who haven't seen it before, I thought that it MIGHT explain some of the
> things going on in data processing today.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> A logger sells a truckload of lumber for 100. His cost of production is
4/5
> of the price.
>     What is his profit?

[...]

In the book, "The Death of Common Sense" the author relates the following
story.

The city of San Francisco pays contractor $40,000 / year to remove dead
trees from city parks. One bureaucrat sent out a few letters and found that
there were companies that would PAY the city MORE than $40,000 / year to BUY
the dead trees, removal included!

Then it was pointed out to the bureaucrat that the city would have to spend
$100,000/yr in legal fees to defend the change in policy (citizen
complaints, environmentalists, losing bidders, etc.).
```

```
"Liam Devlin" <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>

> > In the book, "The Death of Common Sense" the author relates the
following
> > story.
> >
> > The city of San Francisco pays contractor $40,000 / year to remove dead
> > trees from city parks. One bureaucrat sent out a few letters and found
that
> > there were companies that would PAY the city MORE than $40,000 / year to
BUY
> > the dead trees, removal included!
> >
> > Then it was pointed out to the bureaucrat that the city would have to
spend
> > $100,000/yr in legal fees to defend the change in policy (citizen
> > complaints, environmentalists, losing bidders, etc.).
>
> Do you have any sort of cite for this at all?

Sure I have a cite for this. I 'cited' the source of the story in my opening
sentence! Did it ever occur to you, in your wildest dreams, that I might NOT
have a cite? Did you think maybe I made it up? Or, alternatively, are you
eager to get this reference material for your own library? Makes no
difference; the book is available at your local library or bookstore:

THE DEATH OF COMMON SENSE
How Law Is Suffocating America
By Philip K. Howard
Random House, 1994, 202 pages
ISBN: 0679429948

I would be happy to dig out the page number on which this story is related
and provide the author's footnotes if necessary.

Howard had an even more fascinating story on the painting of the Brooklyn
Bridge in time for the bridge's centiennial celebration. It seems the
planning for its 100th birthday began four years before the event. But the
bridge looked really scruffy and needed a coat of paint! Based on past
experience, it would take five years from the time the bids went out until
the bridge actually got painted! (Most of the five years was spent in
appeals from losing bidders and the eventual lawsuits.)

The borough president said, "screw this," and hired a local contractor on
cost-plus basis to paint the damned bridge. The bridge was completely
painted - way in advance of the centiennial birthday party - before the
dozens of lawsuits could even get their first day in court.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT (sort-of) - Does it take math skills to do data processing?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-25T04:31:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2r1a9.50474$%v4.1454120@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <1LL99.175378$SS.7195749@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <7WU99.168651$EJ4.4767931@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <m5X99.187722$SS.7669360@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Two more from N.Y.C. (apologies if anyone else has posted them on
this thread).  Sorry, I'm relating these from memory and don't recall
the references.

A portable toilet manufacturer, hearing that bathrooms were scarce
in downtown New York, approached the city with the intention of
donating hundreds of the things to be placed on street corners.  The
city said that by law each facility *must* have special accommodation
for the handicapped. The company installed a number of test facilities,
with the handicapped accommodation, and monitoring devices to count
how often the regular and handicap toilets were used.  Turned out, the
handicapped ones were virtually never used.  The city still insisted.
The company responded that the extra cost made the idea impractical,
and the project was scrapped, so nobody got extra toilets.

A company in New York owned an old office building, and rented it
cheaply to a religious order that did charity work.  The company gave
the building to the nuns.  The city said since the title was transferred,
the law required the building to have an elevator, because it was more
than three floors.  The nuns protested, saying they had been walking up
the stairs for years, their religious beliefs would prevent them using an
elevator even if it were installed, and certainly they could not afford to
install one.  The city condemned the building, and the community lost the charitable services the nuns had provided.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT (sort-of) - Does it take math skills to do data processing?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-28T02:20:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dZZa9.35707$yl3.819414@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`
- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <1LL99.175378$SS.7195749@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <7WU99.168651$EJ4.4767931@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net> <m5X99.187722$SS.7669360@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <2r1a9.50474$%v4.1454120@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> Two more from N.Y.C. (apologies if anyone else has posted them on
> this thread).  Sorry, I'm relating these from memory and don't recall
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> install one.  The city condemned the building, and the community lost the charitable services the nuns had provided.

I hate this senseless waste as much as anyone, but I think it's 
shortsighted to blame it on the bureaucrats who said they had to have an 
elevator. I browsed the NYC building code yesterday & today and, if I 
read it correctly, any building over 3 stories has to have an elevator. 
There's no provision in the code to exempt nuns from that requirement 
for a homeless shelter. There was probably a change in building class 
from the building's former use to being a homeless shelter. The 
bureaucrats are just following the rules. I believe this is a recent 
change to NYC's building code (the 3 story rule). However, I do find 
fault with the maze of laws & regulations, many of which are 
nonsensical; but they come from the lawmakers, not the bureaucrats.

I lived in the city for a while in a 6 story walkup and was told at the 
time that 6 stories was the limit for a walkup, i.e., 7 or more stories 
and the building had to have an elevator (there are lots of 6 story 
walkups around Manhattan). That would be for Building Class J, 
residential. The would be homeless shelter might have been classified 
some sort of medical facility, not residential, at any rate.
```

#### ↳ Re: Does it take math skills ... back on topic.

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T16:50:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ake7rb$p23$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Well getting back to the topic (sort of)...whenever people
ask me this question my answer is... It does not take math
skills other than simple algebra to do business programming,
it takes logic skills. The computer does the math. If you
did well in geometry with all the proofs and theorems and
progression of logical steps involved you will probably
be a good programmer. It does not matter if you need a
calculator to do arithmetic, it matters that you know
the steps and requirements necessary to perform or program
that arithmetic.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Does it take math skills ... back on topic.

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T21:56:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akepom02l71@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <ake7rb$p23$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com>`

```

"Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:ake7rb$p23$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com...
> Well getting back to the topic (sort of)...whenever people
> ask me this question my answer is... It does not take math
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> that arithmetic.

I believe this to be true for many disciplines.  When I was
doing my physics undergrad, a pattern developed in the
first-year students.  (There are a lot of pre-engineers in that
group, as the curricula is nearly identical for the first two
years.)

First semester, everyone had a calculator.  Second semester,
about half didn't even bother to bring them anymore -- those
are the ones that would make it to year 3.  The other half,
were fervently looking to their calculators for answers that
their knowledge could not provide.
```

```
I finally remembered.  Talk about stupid.  Go to file\print and then you can
decide what you want saved in a file.  You can choose Datablock, Validation,
Global Dialog, Error Messages, Window Dialog, Control Dialog, Window Image
and Control Image.

It is nice for documentation.

John
```

```
We just recently upgraded to OS/390 V2.10 (from 2.6) so that we can
migrate our DB2 from version 5 to 7

All parts of Boeing are not so slow, but the platform I support will
need major supplies of smelling salts to contemplate this upgrade.

	 --Susan A
```

## Thread: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux — 38 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-08-24T15:43:07+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
The web site now lists NetCobol for Linux as being released.  It has a
special price of $US 999 until August 31.


"""San Jose, CA, August 21, 2002 - Fujitsu Software Corporation, a
leading supplier of COBOL  compilers, today announced the general
availability of its popular NetCOBOLï¿½product on the Linux operating
system. In addition to this significant commitment to Linux, Fujitsu
Software Corporation also announces that the IDE (Integrated Development
Environment) for this product is built on the  Eclipse open source IDE. 

...

NetCOBOL for Linux was tested and developed on the Intel x86
distribution of Red Hat Linux. NetCOBOL for Linux has a special
introductory price of $999 worldwide when purchased  from the 
netcobol.com Web site. This introductory pricing is being offered until
August 31, 2002. Thereafter the price will be $1800 in the United
States."""

I suggest that you read the announcements and note that it doesn't, for
example, support screen section.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

- **From:** maross@texoma.net (Mike)
- **Date:** 2002-08-25T10:54:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz>...
> The web site now lists NetCobol for Linux as being released.  It has a
> special price of $US 999 until August 31.
> 
    The ugly Screen Section should have been dropped when
HTML-multimedia came out and replaced with it as a front-end. It is as
pleasing as asking your kids to watch a closed-caption only TV (no
video) to help their reading skills. Despite the fact COBOL-CGI has
been out for years, there still have no standards, nor has it been
popular (actually none I can find). Fujitsu effort is great, but its
CGI-extensions have been poorly accepted without a proven OSS RDBMS
(MySql)! This Linux version looks more like a Beta version till they
get the Screen-Section working and may end up as a trial version (like
FSC 3.0) in the future if Fujitsu marketing is smart. Fujitsu has good
service and documentation, but the WWW trend is just going for other
Languages and there is no turning back.  Many PHP/JAVA programmers can
also write C/C++ extensions to OSS when they need a intrinsic Function
enhancement. How many COBOL programmers can do that? PHP with 
RDMS/Java-extensions/Flash-swf (ming) built in and great service from
IDE's vendors like Israel's http://www.zend.com (I got 3 emails in
24hrs over a minor problem after midnight!) is just a superior
solution for a small WWW programmer like me that could care less to
work in a mainframe shop. I tried Kobol, http://www.theKompany.com ,
$39 for Win/Linux and the CEO replies to me via email from a problem
on Sunday, WOW! I hope they plan CGI/RDBMS or linkage to PHP via their
plugins and SOME REAL DOCUMENTATION? Their RDBMS plugins, nice
Multilanguage-WWW-editor "Quanta Gold", Linux/Win/MacOS multi-platform
apps and a dynamic CEO that actually reads threads and replies (see
interview http://www.osnews.com/story.php?news_id=1592 ) future looks
bright. Mike
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-25T16:24:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com>`

```
"Mike" <maross@texoma.net> wrote:
>
>     The ugly Screen Section should have been dropped when
> HTML-multimedia came out and replaced with it as a front-end. It
> is as pleasing as asking your kids to watch a closed-caption only
> TV (no video) to help their reading skills. in message

What does *that* have to do with anything?  Do people keep wrenches
and hammers because they're nice to look at?  If you have been around
this business more than a few months you should know that there are
literally millions of people whose jobs consist of keying in credit card
receipts, medical charges, etc.  Many of those type systems function
*better* as a character based application than GUI, by *every* standard
of measure, except perhaps 'pretty'.  They're cheaper to write, cheaper
to maintain, more robust, less complex, much more reliable, and much
more efficient in computer and human resources, in these applications.
A person can type dozens of keys in the time it takes to remove their
hand from the keyboard, locate the mouse, position the mouse, click the
mouse, and move their hand back to the keyboard.

Please understand, I have nothing against GUI, where it's appropriate.
But GUI *is not* the Be All, End All approach to every possible app.
Use GUI where it works best, use character where it works best.  But
please, please, stop this 'Throw Away Character Interfaces', simply
because you think GUI is prettier and haven't been around in the Real
World long enough to know it is sometimes the inferior interface.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T03:51:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net>`

```


lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca wrote:

> On Sun, 25 Aug 2002 21:24:23 UTC, "Judson McClendon"
> <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Believe me, I'm no proponent of GUIs - I liked Screen Section and wish I was
still using it. The fact is, many users are *demanding* GUIs, because that's
where their other applications are at. (I know, I know, Judson offers Screen
Section as a cheaper alternative. I've said before, he would be on a hiding
to nothing up here in "tecchie" Alberta).

Both you and Judson latched on to the obvious - Mr. Microsoft designed it so
that we *have* to use the TAB key - which ergonomically is 1,000% lousy -
(a) Use number pad to enter numerics and (b) now find the Tab key with your
left hand.

With effort you can avoid that - substituting the ENTER key but what a
hassle. Means checking after each entryfield - has user hit the Default
PushButtton (ENTER key) or are they terminating an entryfield with ENTER.

Note above I said I liked Screen Section - but for certain neither I in the
past, nor Judson currently, use the Screen Section which at long last is
officially in the Standard, when it's issued. I betcha Judson uses Micro
Focus CBL_SCREEN_ATTRIBUTES and CBL_MOUSE routines. Without those Screen
Section is one real big dud - and is only marginally better than RM/COBOL
Screens (ANSI 74) -  (not Screen Section as we now know it).

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T04:24:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<phma9.81488$CD2.5643517@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
> Both you and Judson latched on to the obvious - Mr. Microsoft designed it so
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> PushButtton (ENTER key) or are they terminating an entryfield with ENTER.

Note that this is true *only* if you avoid using the console interface, supported
by both Windows (all versions) and Net Express.  My point is that you don't
have to use GUI, even on Microsoft platforms.

> Note above I said I liked Screen Section - but for certain neither I in the
> past, nor Judson currently, use the Screen Section which at long last is
> officially in the Standard, when it's issued.

Actually, not quite true.  It is true that if I were currently writing new
systems I would be using SP2.  But it happens that for the last few
years most of my programming has been doing maintenance and making
enhancements to existing systems. ;-)   Actually, I would like to convert
all my PC COBOL apps to SP2, if for no other reason than to get out
from under my dependence on Micro Focus, who'se runtime licensing
policies are *not* doing me any favors these days.  But some of these
apps are so large I can't absorb the cost myself, and I hate to bill a
client for it, unless they get some other benefit.

> I betcha Judson uses Micro Focus CBL_SCREEN_ATTRIBUTES and
> CBL_MOUSE routines. Without those Screen Section is one real big
> dud - and is only marginally better than RM/COBOL Screens (ANSI 74)
> -  (not Screen Section as we now know it).

Sorry, I have never used either of those functions, though I have used a
few of the "CBL_..." functions for testing if a file is present, etc.  (As
an aside, one of my pet pieves is that every standard language should
have a built-in way to detect file presence.  After all, the concept of
determining a file's existence is not platform specific, and you just have
to do that from time to time.  I don't understand why it's often left out.)

I also don't use the mouse in charcacter based apps.  If you're going to
use the mouse, you might as well use GUI in the first place. :-)  I have
found the Micro Focus Screen Section facilities to be sufficient for
anything I have needed to do (though sometimes barely :), in a straight
character interface.  Unfortunately, there are features there that are not
in the new standard.  And, of course, it doesn't offer all the features that
SP2 does.

My feeling is that a language such as COBOL is not complete if it does
not provide for a user interface, both GUI and character based.  If you
can't write at least a simple application using the language alone, it is
just not a complete language.  I have written a lot (and I mean *a lot*)
of 'utility' programs over the years, many of them in COBOL.  Many of
them input and output only a couple of fields to the screen as their sole
function.  Having the Screen Section makes this easer and cleaner in
COBOL than in most other languages.  The *whole* application is
visible, right there in one printable source file.  Removing the Screen
Section would eliminate this whole class of application from COBOL's
preview.  My gosh, if we're adding OO, a huge monster which *has not
been proven* to be a true improvement, by *objective* industry wide
measure, such as development and maintenance cost and speed, and
especially not in learning curve, no matter what advocates claim, on
which the COBOL community is still deeply divided, and which even
advocated admit, the vast majority of COBOL programmers will never
use anyway, why would we want to *narrow* COBOL's effectiveness
by removing something that can be demonstrated to be useful?  With
Screen Section you can, for example, write a quick a program to test
a new subroutine in five minutes.  What other way is there to do this
as quickly and easily?  A much more sensible and practical approach
(IMO), would be to *expand* the Screen Section to provide GUI
capability as well.  Sure, it would never offer the features (GUI or
character) provided by third party products such as SP2.  But simple,
ubiquitously available tools have their purpose too, as I've been trying
to point out.  Remember my earlier posts about "planting COBOL
seed?"  Why in the world would anyone buy a COBOL compiler for
hobbyist use for even $49, if they could not write just a simple program
that inputs and displays some data without buying another product,
that certainly *is not* going to cost $49?  Don't just think about your
main bread-and-butter applications right now.  Think broadly.  Think
long term.  :-)
```

```
Yea, till retirement. Why hasn't COBOL engineers added Java
hooks/multimedia/direct- calls-WebSql-RDBMS/HTML-browser-applets and
let the users decide what to use so we can have choices? Yes the
problem is the mindset!  What monthly percent COBOL learning and Web
site deployment growth rate do you think is in the population of 20-40
year old programmers? By Netcraft's most recent survey PHP WWW sites
are growing are a phenomenal 6.5% a month rate and Apache with MySql
(transactions recently and foreign-keys soon) is the preferred
OS/RDBMS. http://www.netcraft.com/survey/Reports/200208/graphs.html
Please stop defending COBOL's "perfect methods and vendors" as this
just accelerates COBOL's decline!

>It's mid-morning in Israel when it's after midnight here. Try an
email >at noon and see what happens.
Jerry the time zone has little to do with it (I work late hours). The
point being this company cares with a follow up email to request a
reply to see if they corrected your problem; http://zend.com also
incorporates a forum that people who do not has service contracts ($49
a year) can get help and sometimes the forums are actually faster and
can teach their tech support the pro tips and if that doesn't answer
your questions go to about 30 popular WWW forums. This is the most
popular COBOL forum I found (and Tek-tips), however you only see about
20 regulars and few newbees.  Most Cobol vendors have no forums or
have taken them down when users complain of various shortcomings or
policies (runtimes), fearing new users will go to other vendors or
languages. However I must admit Fujitsu has sent me numerous emails
upon problems and "Most" of the time problem get corrected; But I like
to hear what the expert grunts are doing.

Judson McClendon wrote;
>receipts, medical charges, etc.  Many of those type systems function
>*better* as a character based application than GUI, by
*every*standard
>of measure, except perhaps 'pretty'.  They're cheaper to
write,cheaper
>to maintain, more robust, less complex, much more reliable, and much
Many studies have shown that people respond quicker to well done
graphical interfaces. It has been documented when they went to
universal street sign verses text that accidents rates went down. 60
years ago mostly text-based books with a few poor graphics were the
normal learning mode. Now we output mostly multimedia
(tv,WWW,music,pc-graphic-program) and when efficient voice-recognition
input methods are perfected, perhaps keyboard use will decline? Text
systems have only been the dominant human method for around a hundred
years (a millisecond in human history), in which 85% of the world was
illiterate.

>A person can type dozens of keys in the time it takes to remove their
>hand from the keyboard, locate the mouse, position the mouse, click 
>the mouse, and move their hand back to the keyboard.
Yea and this is because of poor graphical method programming. Graphic
programmers are from the same clay as COBOL text based. However text
base systems ar easier to maintain "For The Programmer".
       Regards Mike
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T12:36:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Auta9.2043$Hd4.896611@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca> <phma9.81488$CD2.5643517@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <2763b81a.0208260712.2ce34d05@posting.google.com>`

```
"Mike" <maross@texoma.net> wrote:
>
> >A person can type dozens of keys in the time it takes to remove their
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yea and this is because of poor graphical method programming.

Okay, I'll bite.  Now tell me how one can 'properly' program a GUI
interface so that moving the mouse and clicking on a button is as fast
as (or golly, faster than?) typing a character.  ;-)  Don't cheat now.
If you say not to use the mouse, then what does a GUI offer over a
character interface, other than looks?

In my experience (which I admit is only about 34 years), what people
(at least the ones who spend the money) really want is function.  Or at
least, they should want that.  If you don't know that a well designed
character interface literally trounces any GUI interface ever designed
by man, when used strictly as a data entry system, you need to get out
there and look around a bit more.  It's not a debatable point, fella.  It
can be *easily* shown that, in such applications, character interfaces
can be orders of magnitude more efficient, not only in people time, but
in computer resources (eg. memory, bandwidth, CPU, storage).  And
because they are simpler to design, program and implement, and the
necessary support software is also far less complex, it is a natural and
inevitable consequence that they are cheaper and faster to create, and
more reliable as well.
```

```
> JerryM$ouse wrote:

> those M$ shares after this sale quarter which M$ should do well (they

Ah ha! The disguise is ripped off! A Linux troll! (actually, an
anti-establishment troll opposed to all traditional solutions that work
(Windows/COBOL/hot water) - a modern Luddite). The idealism of youth is at
work here; the certitude that new solutions are better. Not to worry, as you
mature, you'll see the worth of the tried-and-true. By the way, it's
"you're" not "your."


>    I personally don't care if your input is text-based, graphical or
> you use a motion detector and fart into it. But the language users
> should have a choice.

They do have a choice. They can work somewhere else. No IT manager in his
right mind (non-sequiter?) is going to allow a vegetable-soup of languages
in his shop. Capitalism is not democracy and certainly not anarchy.


> We are going to embedded utensil hardware input
> in the future were Cobol will never go. I.E. a digital camera uses
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> netcarft survey?

SO WHAT if the language used to program a digital camera is not COBOL? COBOL
doesn't do digital cameras or fuel-mixture ratios for a Corvette. Java
doesn't do paychecks! Visual Basic doesn't do the space shuttle.

I don't know what percentage of web pages use COBOL-CGI methods; probably
only those that want to achieve serious results. We COBOLers tend to leave
the flames and pop-ups and chat-room stuff to the script-kiddies who are,
demonstrably, more interested in sizzle than steak.

>    Well there is only till August 31 for Fujitsu discount! Is anyone
> going to buy it here?
```

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0208271906.22186a13@posting.google.com...
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'traditional' ?

You have a point. I was using COBOL on a TRS-80 in 1986 and sixteen years is
but a blip. But, I wasn't offering COBOL + Windows, I was saying COBOL is a
traditional method of programming, Windows is a traditional - in the sense
of older - operating system (compared to Linux), and hot-water is a
traditional method of bathing when contrasted with water in its (usual)
normal state.

>
> > - a modern Luddite).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> which was the result of mechanisation.

I guess I should have use a lower-case "L" (Luddite = "One who opposes
technical or technological change."). Actually a common definition of
Luddite is one who opposes technology in the belief that machinery would
diminish employment. Probably the bosses of 'manual' production shops were
quite adept at generating sweat even without machines.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-28T18:59:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208281759.38bbdd92@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca> <phma9.81488$CD2.5643517@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <2763b81a.0208260712.2ce34d05@posting.google.com> <pLwa9.212003$m91.8779853@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <2763b81a.0208262008.2e82fed6@posting.google.com> <BDRa9.232925$m91.9553872@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <2763b81a.0208280733.260d6680@posting.google.com>`

```
maross@texoma.net (Mike) wrote

>   The thread is about FSC 7-linux which ONLY uses Web-browser to CGI
> for screen display, 

No.  SP2 is available which can use text terminals or graphics.

> so you "Text-Entry-Gurus" IT manager elitists
> shouldn't be upset if you walk into the employees coffee break room
> and hear negative comments about you. 

It isn't about 'ugly' text or 'pretty' grpahical.  It is about
creating screens which can be used efficiently to key in quantities of
data.  Most 'GUI' interfaces inhibit fast keying of bulk data because
of the way that the keyboard and mouse must be used.  Many text
interfaces are also bad too, usually for different reasons.

It is possible to design interfaces that can be used efficiently
irrespective of whether it is Windows or text mode.  It is possible to
easily have mouse/pull down menus/multiple windows/help using text
mode, even green screen terminals.

> I guess my inquiry to
> FSC-Linux's acceptance has been answered. However I must applaud 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> all his men!

This is more of your content free rambling.  What 'answer' did you
imagine ? What stone wall ?  Which monkey ?  Humpty Dumpty never had
any men.


>   Jerry if you need to update your Kobol Http://theKompany.com they

Not Jerry, me.

> just released a new version and plan to improve their documentation

It was the new (21st August) version 1.2 that I was using.

> soon to include Cygwin Windows installation, etc per Shawn Gordon-
> CEO.  Did you get it to work?   Regards Mike

Kobol includes cob2c and cob2c_mpe - I have no idea what the
difference is. The IDE tries to use cob2c but uses -3 parameter which
cob2c doesn't recognise.  Renaming cob2c_mpe to cob2c fixed that
problem.

Howver, Kobol, Tiny-Cobol and OpenCobol have no discernable mechanism
for file sharing and record locking.  Without this, and/or ODBC/SQL,
these will remain out of mainstream use.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T16:29:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208261529.402eac28@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca> <phma9.81488$CD2.5643517@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <2763b81a.0208260712.2ce34d05@posting.google.com>`

```
maross@texoma.net (Mike) wrote:

> >maintenance nightmare. There's a different mindset with COBOL
> >programmers; they're in it for the long haul.

> Yea, till retirement. 

It is actually the corporate IT that 'is in it for the long haul'. 
Businesses mostly change slowly over decades and their computer
systems are required to last for many years being updated (maintained)
as the business rules change.  Systems that are 20 or 30 years old and
have been maintained to keep up to date with needs are common place.

Academics can throw out everything each year and start again with the
latest new shiney, trendy languages, corporates are there to do
business, not to be computer consumers or to employ programmers.

> Why hasn't COBOL engineers added Java
> hooks/multimedia/direct- 

Why is it that you think they haven't ?  Is it just that you only read
PeeCee magazines that only push the latest trendy things ?

> calls-WebSql-RDBMS/HTML-browser-applets and
> let the users decide what to use so we can have choices? 

You _can_ have choices.  Cobol CGI is easy, Many Cobols do RDBMS and
have done for decades, since well before SQL even (IDMS, IMS, etc). 
You want client 'applets' ? see Accu, you want server side, see
NetCobol for .NET.

> Yes the problem is the mindset!  

And your 'mindset' seems to be: what isn't in the latest press
releases in PeeCee mags doesn't exist.

> What monthly percent COBOL learning and Web
> site deployment growth rate do you think is in the population of 20-40
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> OS/RDBMS. 

And the vast majority of Web sites are not business sites.  Most are
advertising, they are toys, they are magazines, they are brochures and
catalogues, but they are not business.

Of course a few are business, often these are closed sites that you
wouldn't find by browsing because they are for business partners only.

While MySQL _does_ have Transactions in some development versions it
is still not yet suitable for _business_.  PostgreSQL won't be
replacing Oracle any day soon, but will be growth from new users.

Cobol is a Mack truck for shifting containers. It is irrelevant
whether the number of skateboards built in a month is gorowing by by
6.5%, skateboards won't shift containers.

You also have absolutely _zero_ idea of how many web sites are using
Cobol or are being developed in Cobol and have merely extrapolated
this ignorence into assuming that there are none.

> http://www.netcraft.com/survey/Reports/200208/graphs.html
> Please stop defending COBOL's "perfect methods and vendors" as this
> just accelerates COBOL's decline!

Noone claims that Cobol vendors are perfect, or that Cobol is the best
language for _every_ task.

However, acting like a clueless newbie and making claims for toys like
PHP and MySQL does not gain you points about what is required for real
business needs.

> Jerry the time zone has little to do with it (I work late hours).

It has everything to do with it.  You were impressed that it was
'after midnight' while it was actually normal office hours for the
vendor.

When I was contracting to a UK company I would do 'overnight' changes
because I live at GMT+1200.  The downside is that US Companies
sometimes decide to give me a friendly ring to reply to an EMail as
they clear their mailbox in the morning. It is 2 am here so they get
told to piss off.

> Many studies have shown that people respond quicker to well done
> graphical interfaces. 

You confuse 'easy to learn' with 'efficient'.  Most studies of that
type take people who have never seen a system before and see how long
it takes for them to poke around until something useful happens.

Given a person with typing skills, understands the data and has had a
modicum of training they will get the job done _faster_ using a well
designed character interface.

If cars were designed like computer systems then many would all be
completely different to operate but if the driver poked around enough
with the 'easy to use controls' they would get it go enough in half an
hour to get it some shops and back without too many accidents.

However, actual cars cannot be learnt by 'poking around'.  Instruction
is needed, understanding of rules, complex coordinations learnt.

> It has been documented when they went to
> universal street sign verses text that accidents rates went down. 

Mainly because foreigners cannot read word signs.  Was that
'documentation' the study that was done in Europe where there are
dozens of different languages ?

> 60
> years ago mostly text-based books with a few poor graphics were the
> normal learning mode. Now we output mostly multimedia
> (tv,WWW,music,pc-graphic-program) 

And the education levels are _worse_.

> and when efficient voice-recognition
> input methods are perfected, perhaps keyboard use will decline? Text
> systems have only been the dominant human method for around a hundred
> years (a millisecond in human history), in which 85% of the world was
> illiterate.

Complete nonsense.  Text business records date back thousands of
years.

> Yea and this is because of poor graphical method programming. 

There are good and bad text based systems and good and bad graphical
systems.  A good text based input system can outperform most graphical
systems.  A good, fast, efficient 'graphical' system operates like a
good text based system.

In fact most times people say 'graphical' or 'GUI' when what they
really mean is pull down menus, overlapping windows and push buttons
etc.  There is no need for it to be actually graphical (in the sense
of it being built from pictures).

I use SP/2 and have UIs on text 'green screen' terminals and as actual
Windows programs, or over the internet. They look slightly different,
but only in the rendering, and work identically.

> Graphic
> programmers are from the same clay as COBOL text based. 

And in many cases are the same programmer.  A decade ago I started
development on a Windows 3.0 graphical application that displayed
graphic representation of container vessels showing bays and cells and
where containers were loaded, allowed drag and drop to load the
vessel, showed curves of stability, bending, shear and torsion, it
graphically represented the dangerous goods separations.  It even drew
a view from the helm position showing the containers, the calculated
horizon and the sight lines to ensure that visability was within the
requirements.  Actually the view point could be changed (for playing)
so that the program could do a 'fly by' of the container ship.

All done in Cobol.

> However text
> base systems ar easier to maintain "For The Programmer".

What actual basis do you make this claim on ?  I take as much care
over my pure text interfaces, my 'TUI' interfaces, my Graphical 'GUI'
interfaces, my HTML/CGI interfaces and my pure graphical (as in
drawing pictures) interfaces.

None are particularly difficult or less 'easy' as long as the correct
tools are used that are appropriate for the job.  If Cobol isn't
actually appropriate then I use C or Python or JavaScript or some
other - but that is less than 10% so far.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
> >
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Now there's one where we get caught up in COBOL being vanilla flavoured. I just read
all this mainframe jazz about DCOMMAREA (or whatever), JCL etc. It covers files but
has absolute zilch to do with COBOL coding for file handling. The mainframe O/S
approach is just as bad as Windows for being 'specific'. And just don't expect J4 to
address this one any time soon in the current century ! I once wrote something to the
effect, "It would be nice to have GET_CBL_DIRECTORY....". Howard's response, "Might
be nice for you. Useless for me". The twain shall ne'er meet <G>

> I also don't use the mouse in charcacter based apps.  If you're going to
> use the mouse, you might as well use GUI in the first place. :-)  I have
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> preview.

A recurring theme of yours, with which I agree. But remember Thane's "All or
nothing....". And so far J4 are determined 'It will be NOTHING', particularly as most
use is on the big beasties - back to vanilla flavoured.


>  My gosh, if we're adding OO, a huge monster which *has not
> been proven* to be a true improvement, by *objective* industry wide
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> long term.  :-)

Certainly OO works in the PC world. (That includes accepting your criticisms - so
many of the bright eyed and bushy tailed, while very, very clever - just don't know
which way is up when it comes to systems design, all those bloody silly little
windows popping up all over the joint !). To my mind the only concrete case that can
be made for OO is when somebody using a mainframe trumpets its success. However,
problem - which mainframe OO COBOL compiler are we talking about ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T12:03:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DUJa9.150764$Yd.6694422@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca> <phma9.81488$CD2.5643517@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <3D6AF8BC.4B053F3E@shaw.ca>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:3D6AF8BC.4B053F3E@shaw.ca...
>

>
> Now there's one where we get caught up in COBOL being vanilla flavoured. I just read
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> be nice for you. Useless for me". The twain shall ne'er meet <G>

Well, actually, this is a totally different area. DFHCOMMAREA refers to a CICS specific
interface and doesn't really have an exact analog in the COBOL world. CICS, if you are unfamiliar
with the mainframe world, is like a mini operating system running on top of the underlying O/S.
it is the same whether it is on OS/390, OS/2, Windows, AIX, or HP/UX.  Nothing really to do
with COBOL.

However, I find it a bit amusing that something as platform specific as GET_CBL_DIRECTORY
would be something we would want. Much better to call the underlying O/S in my opinion. Why
clutter us COBOL with OS dependencies?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T12:35:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208271135.7da8cab7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca> <phma9.81488$CD2.5643517@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <3D6AF8BC.4B053F3E@shaw.ca> <DUJa9.150764$Yd.6694422@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
"Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote 

> However, I find it a bit amusing that something as platform specific as GET_CBL_DIRECTORY
> would be something we would want. Much better to call the underlying O/S in my opinion. Why
> clutter us COBOL with OS dependencies?

You seem to have this completely inverted. MF's CBL_GET_DIRECTORY and
most other CBL_ routines are platform _independent_ where they are
available, they are OS _independent_.
It is 'Calling the underlying O/S' that is an 'OS dependency'.
But then you probably did want to be amusing    ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T23:39:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<W4Ua9.123456$eK6.3805098@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca> <phma9.81488$CD2.5643517@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <3D6AF8BC.4B053F3E@shaw.ca> <DUJa9.150764$Yd.6694422@twister.austin.rr.com> <217e491a.0208271135.7da8cab7@posting.google.com>`

```
Oh really? How does it work on platforms that do NOT have directories, such as say, the AS/400 or MPE?

No, I meant what I said, if you need to read a directory, that is an O/S dependent thing and it makes a lot more sense to call the
O/S call to do it. Very *very* few applications are spread on enough platforms to make this an issue to maintain. The same is true
for other things too, like allocating shared memory segments or checking the status of a process.

If you need to test for the existence of a file, that is NOT OS dependent; you can simply OPEN it for input and check the FILE
STATUS.

-Paul


"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:217e491a.0208271135.7da8cab7@posting.google.com...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T10:52:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QLna9.4968$yt3.2078645@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca...
>
> Both you and Judson latched on to the obvious - Mr. Microsoft designed it
so
> that we *have* to use the TAB key - which ergonomically is 1,000% lousy -
> (a) Use number pad to enter numerics and (b) now find the Tab key with
your
> left hand.
>
> With effort you can avoid that - substituting the ENTER key but what a
> hassle. Means checking after each entryfield - has user hit the Default
> PushButtton (ENTER key) or are they terminating an entryfield with ENTER.

I do not know how to do it with any of the "object oriented" COBOL or
GUI-helper tools (e.g., Net Express), but under Windows you can subclass the
controls and have the <enter> key function just like a standard <tab> key
and advance to the next entry field. I have done this with several
applications. No checking after each field: once you set it up, it just
happens automatically.

Maybe a search of the COBOL-compiler vendor's literature on "subclassing"
will turn up what you need for the particular product involved. Or, check
MSDN for subclassing, and look to see if you can get a Windows' "window
handle" for the controls involved. (Or check the vendor's literature for
"window handle" and contact my office.).

I also do not know if if this can be done under Linux (thread title,
although comment above was re Microsoft), but maybe there is some equivalent
there, too.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

- **Message-ID:** `<3D6AF3CE.8D03FB8E@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca> <QLna9.4968$yt3.2078645@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```


Michael Mattias wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> news:3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca...
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thanks Michael for the tip.

Essentially there are two problems :-

1. Disable the Tab Key - and the following GUI methods seem to apply :-

Class Abstract Window -  method-id. "KeyboardGroup".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
*> Ensure this window is in the keyboard group. This enables the
*> application end-user to set focus to the window by pressing
*> the tab-key.
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
and

 method-id. "NoKeyboardGroup".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
*> Ensure window is not in the keyboard group. This prevents the
*> application end-user from setting focus to the window by
*> pressing the tab key.
*>---------------------------------------------------------------

Looks like NoKeyBoardGroup will 'disable" the Tab key.

2. The second problem, which I'm currently handling is, when using the ENTER key
to terminate an entryfield - the ability to differentiate between an entryfield
and the Default (OK) PushButton.

The MS SDK that came with Net Express listed three possibles for recognizing the
ENTER as a terminator - needless to say all examples were coded in C ! (Now of
course had MS used your beloved BASIC for source code.....<G>). With some
effort, and help from Micro Focus in Newbury, I hit on this one :-

CLASS : My Dialog.
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "begin".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-Caller                        object reference.
Procedure Division using lnk-Caller.
  set os-Caller to lnk-Caller
  invoke self "wantAllKeys"
End Method "begin".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "checkCharacterCode".   *> PRIVATE
*>-------------------------------------------------------------

*> checks for backspace etc.....

Local-storage section.
copy "keys.cpy".
01 ls-code                              pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-object                            object reference.
Linkage section.
01 lnk-event                            object reference.

Procedure Division using lnk-event.

 invoke lnk-event "getObject" returning ls-object
 invoke lnk-event "getCharacterCode" returning ls-code

 Evaluate true
    when  ls-code = OVK-RETURN
          invoke self "returnEntryObject"
 End-evaluate

End Method "checkCharacterCode".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "defaultButton".          *> PRIVATE
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Procedure Division.
Evaluate true
  when os-CurrentObject = os-DefaultButton
       invoke os-Caller, ws-okMethodName
  when other
       invoke self "returnEntryObject"
End-evaluate

End Method "defaultButton".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "returnEntryObject".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
copy "dilgretn.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ls==.
01 n                             pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-object                     object reference.
01 ls-string                     object reference.

Procedure Division.

perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > MaxEntryFields

    invoke os-Collection(1) "at"
           using n returning ls-object

    if  os-CurrentObject = ls-object
        invoke os-CurrentObject "getText" returning ls-string
        invoke ls-string "trimblanks" returning ls-string
        invoke os-CurrentObject "Getlength" returning ls-length
        invoke ls-string "getValueWithSize"
             using ls-Length returning ls-text
        invoke ls-string "finalize" returning ls-string

        invoke os-Collection(2) "at"
             using n returning ws-MethodRec
        move ws-Line   to ls-Line
        move ws-Column to ls-Column
        invoke os-Caller ws-MethodName using ls-ReturnValues
        EXIT METHOD
    End-if
End-perform

*> The Line/Column above is so I can identify fields in a COBOL 'n' by 'x'
table.

End Method "returnEntryObject".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "setNextFocusEmpty2".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

*> See creation methods "zCreateEntryfield" and "zDynamicEntryfield".
*> This method merely supplies the Dialog-Params required for
*> "setNextFocusEmpty", above

Local-storage section.
copy "dilogprm.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ls==.
Linkage section.
01 lnk-field                     pic x(4) comp-5.

Procedure Division using lnk-field.

  initialize ls-Params
  move lnk-field to ls-Field
  invoke self "setNextFocusEmpty" using ls-Params

End Method "setNextFocusEmpty2".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "zCreateEntryfield".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-event                      pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-object                     object reference.

Linkage section.
copy "dilogprm.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.

Procedure Division using lnk-Params.

 invoke self "getObjectFromId"
        using lnk-ID returning ls-object

 Evaluate true
     when NumericK   invoke ls-object "numeric"
     when UpperCase  invoke ls-object "uppercase"
 End-evaluate

 if lnk-Font <> zeroes
    invoke ls-object "setfont" using os-Font(lnk-Font)
 End-if

 Evaluate true
     when Centered       invoke ls-object "centered"
     when RightJustify   invoke ls-object "rightjustified"
 End-evaluate

 Evaluate true
     when ReadOnly       invoke ls-object "readonly"
 End-evaluate

 if   lnk-length <> zeroes
      invoke ls-object "setLength" using lnk-length
      invoke ls-object "accessSystemEvents"
      move Button1Down to ls-event

      Evaluate true
       when EmptyField
         invoke ls-object "setEventto" using
           ls-event, self, "SetNextfocusEmpty2 ", lnk-field
       when other
         invoke ls-object "setEventto" using
           ls-event, self, "SetNextfocusInsert2 ", lnk-field
      End-evaluate

      move p2ce-functionKey to ls-event
      invoke ls-object "setEventto" using
          ls-event, self, "checkCharacterCode "

 else invoke ls-object "readonly"
 End-if

 initialize ws-MethodRec
 move lnk-MethodName to ws-MethodName

 if lnk-Line is numeric
    move lnk-Line       to ws-Line
    move lnk-Column     to ws-Column
 End-if

 invoke os-Collection(1) "add" using ls-object
 invoke os-Collection(2) "add" using ws-MethodRec

End Method "zCreateEntryfield".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

It is *more* than possible that I've got overkill with the above methods -
however it works - with one minor problem - if the user does use the TAB key
?????? That's where your suggestion - disabling the TAB comes in handy. Thanks -
I will pursue.

Jimmy
```

```
Slightly off topic but...

I was eating lunch at a local restaurant Friday, when a loud and boorish person came in escorting a customer to lunch. I wasn't
trying to listen, but this person went on to loudly regale their customer with how stupid people are to not move completely to
Microsoft. How mainframes would die out within a few years, and how Unix and Linux people just "didn't get it" and so forth and so
on. When he puffed himself up and declared these things were inevitable, and his customer asked him why, he proudly returned that he
knew these things because he was a professional.

That did it, I laughed so hard I started joking, and the fellow realized that I was laughing at him. And when I started, so did the
group of IBM techies at the next table. The fellow finally realized that everyone who had heard anything he was saying thought him a
total fool, and got embarrassed. He turned pink, dropped some money on the table, and told his customer to 'come on'. His customer
looked at him funny, and said he would call him later.  At least he had enough decency to pay the bill ...

... but he no longer has his customer. I have a note that just arrived (6:30am on Monday Morning) from his customer asking to meet
with me to take on his project.  He wants pricing on a mainframe, AS/400, or UNIX solution to his needs. Smart customer.

-Paul

>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T13:10:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208261210.699514ec@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote 

> With effort you can avoid that - substituting the ENTER key but what a
> hassle. Means checking after each entryfield - has user hit the Default
> PushButtton (ENTER key) or are they terminating an entryfield with ENTER.

I don't use Screen Section at all, but then I had developed my screen
handling without it being available.  I use 'field at a time' which
means that every field is terminated with an Enter or function key. 
This allows each field to be checked and validated before moving to
the next.  Nothing worse (IMHO) than filling out a whole screenful of
data only to find there was an early error.

While it may seem an excessive amount of code is required in fact it
is quite easy to generate cobol Code from text file screen layouts.

In extreme cases I build the actual screen display dynamically from a
field dictionary and control file layout definition.  For example I
have one program that maintains most master files (Product, Customer,
etc) based on control information specifying file module to call,
field dictionary to use, layout controls, validation and lookup tables
to use, etc.  It may be a large complex program, but it is a lot less
code than the dozens of simpler programs that would otherwise be
required.
 
> Note above I said I liked Screen Section - but for certain neither I in the
> past, nor Judson currently, use the Screen Section which at long last is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Screens (ANSI 74) -  (not Screen Section as we now know it).

I don't use any of that, to me Screen Section is a big dud anyway.  I
do attributes (colouration) with routines developed years ago that
allow the user to change the colours that will be used and that work
with Cobol 74 on dumb terminals to IBM PC and to Unix/Linux.  It also
make the code completely portable because I only need recode the
routines in one place, making them null in the worst case.

The mouse can be used with MF ADIS to just return as if a function key
and I have used this with pure text mode for allowing menu selecting.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T15:39:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sawa9.3375$Hd4.1698754@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca> <217e491a.0208261210.699514ec@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
> I use 'field at a time' which means that every field is terminated with
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> error.

I understand where you're coming from.  I do screens like that when
I need to.  But in my experience (and perhaps I'm just unlucky :), my
clients usually demand a significant amount of validation involving
multiple field relationships.  In such cases, you might as well wait until
the screen is full anyway, because there are abundant opportunities for
errors, even after each field is validated individually.  You folks may
disagree, but it seems counterintuitive to get errors while you're keying
the data, and then you can get an error between two or three fields at
the beginning of the screen after everything is entered.  Seems like the
typical user, used to errors being displayed on a field by field basis, is
inevitably going to ask "How can I get an error now, since it accepted
the fields when I keyed them in?"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T04:32:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D6B0162.3DE835EF@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca> <217e491a.0208261210.699514ec@posting.google.com> <sawa9.3375$Hd4.1698754@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```


Judson McClendon wrote:

> "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> >
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> the fields when I keyed them in?"

I'm inclined to veer towards Richard's approach be it RM/COBOL, Micro Focus
Screen Section or Net Express GUIs. The fact is - you can't make a solid case
for one approach. It largely depends upon the application.

I was poking around one of those genealogy sites - accidentally hit the SEND
key and it immediately spewed out one long messasgebox listing every empty
field !

My *major* input is a 10 x 24 table, but it also contains ancillary
information - like Date (YYmm). Key in "9914" and you could show it in  a
final error list. However what if you keyed in "9910" when you meant "9911" -
much easier if it is verified and displayed as "Oct 1999" or "Nov 1999" -
visually the user sees it straight away - otherwise they could miss it when
they go to press the OK Pushbutton.

I also have windows where the input for one entryfield can automatically
generate the values for other fields and display them. Using Business logic, I
then bypass these fields for input as they *now* contain values. Similarly
there are instances where I "hide" both radio buttons and droplists - which
are only triggered as being 'active' when some previous entryfield is entered.

Simple name and address stuff - easy - Screen Section or GUI - accept the
'lot'. Other than checking certain fields can't be empty,  the only likelihood
of error is non-selection of country or state/county/province codes.

As for line-by-line being counterintuitive - think back to the lassies
keypunching cards or paper tape; the second or 'verifier', came to a
screeching halt if she keyed something different from the originator. OK, so
we moved on to key-to-disk and the data entry equipment got more
sophisticated. Subject to how it was programmed, that too could Beep at the
operator, breaking her flow.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T13:29:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d6b7d05.973119@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-m3smRx4KWzdb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <3D69A696.9FA90AA5@shaw.ca> <217e491a.0208261210.699514ec@posting.google.com> <sawa9.3375$Hd4.1698754@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```
Judson:

User interface management systems, whether they support Windows,
character mode or both should give the programmer the choice to
support field-by-field processing or full screen processing.

You mentioned that your application has multiple field relationships
which does give credence to the need for full screen processing over
field-by-field processing.

I have spoken with programmers who have the need to process some
screens in their application on a field-by-field basis and others on a
full screen basis.

The key word is adaptability.  Regardless of where your application is
running, every application seems to have unique requirements.


"Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```
