###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T14:22:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d6a379e.4206899@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <aKba9.77533$CD2.4995905@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```

Judson:

You hit the nail on the head.  I can also confirm your comments.  We
have many customers who still use character mode screens.

If the objective is to enter data into fields on a screen, one should
ALWAYS ask the question, "which user interface environment will work
*best* in our specific circumstances?"  There are still a number of
user interface choices......character mode, Win32 GUI, web browser....

FAR too often, PHM types (pointy haired managers) declare that the
application must run with a web browser interface despite the fact
that there are few (if any) business, technical or ergonomic reasons
to support the decision.

Thank you for the refreshing moment of sanity in a world obsessed with
web browser user interfaces.



"Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"Mike" <maross@texoma.net> wrote:
>>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T07:53:01+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D69D04D.5E565E90@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com>`

```
Mike wrote:
>     The ugly Screen Section should have been dropped when
> HTML-multimedia came out and replaced with it as a front-end. It is as
> pleasing as asking your kids to watch a closed-caption only TV (no
> video) to help their reading skills. 

I quite agree that if you want to teach children to read then using
Cobol Screen Section may not be an appropriate solution.

> Despite the fact COBOL-CGI has
> been out for years, there still have no standards, 

Given that there are many 'standards' for connecting to Web servers
(CGI, Win-CGI, ISAPI, NAPI, JSP, ASP, PHP, ...) then there needs to many
different implementations of solutions.

> nor has it been
> popular (actually none I can find). 

I have several sites running Cobol CGI.  There is no need for any
routines or language changes at all, my code is just Cobol and it works
fine.  Use interface routines if you want to, otherwise just use Cobol,
and it works with several OS/Server combinations.

The whole point, of course, is that a Cobol web site looks just like any
other web site so how would you expect to 'find' them ?

> Fujitsu effort is great, but its
> CGI-extensions have been poorly accepted without a proven OSS RDBMS
> (MySql)! 

You seem to have extrapolated your inability to 'find' Cobol based Web
sites into 'poor acceptance'.  In many cases (such as some of mine) the
site is closed to employees, agents, and customers so it is not likely
that you would find them at all.  Sure, use flash and PHP/MySQL if you
want to put advertising on the web, but Cobol does do it nicely when
reliable data processing is required.

MySQL is great for serving out Web pages and being used as a toy, but
until it gets transactions and replication it is not much use for data
processing which is where Cobol is targetted.  Fujitsu does work well
with several proven RDBMS's including PostGreSQL (which is alomost
there).

> This Linux version looks more like a Beta version till they

It seems to be based on their other Unix compilers which have been
available and developed for several years.  What are you basing your
'beta' on ?  Have you been using it ?

> get the Screen-Section working and may end up as a trial version (like
> FSC 3.0) in the future if Fujitsu marketing is smart. Fujitsu has good

Screen Section is irrelevant (to me).  There are better ways to get a
user interface.

> service and documentation, but the WWW trend is just going for other
> Languages and there is no turning back.  

Different languages are needed for different purposes.  While Web
serving is a requirement for many organisations there is still a large
need for interactive systems, and indeed for batch processing.  In any
case using a tool like SP/2 gives the same user inteface for
applications from dumb terminals to web users.

> Many PHP/JAVA programmers can
> also write C/C++ extensions to OSS when they need a intrinsic Function
> enhancement. How many COBOL programmers can do that? 

How mant Cobol programmers need to ?

> PHP with
> RDMS/Java-extensions/Flash-swf (ming) built in and great service from
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> work in a mainframe shop.

So why are you still in the Cobol group ?  I won't be writing any
interactive Order Entry/Warehouse Management systems in PHP and Flash
just doesn't cut it on the RF (Radio) terminals used on the fork-lifts.

> I tried Kobol, http://www.theKompany.com ,
> $39 for Win/Linux and the CEO replies to me via email from a problem
> on Sunday, WOW! I hope they plan CGI/RDBMS or linkage to PHP via their
> plugins and SOME REAL DOCUMENTATION?

Have you tried CobolScript ?  It does CGI/RDBMS and it, too, is almost
Cobol.

> Their RDBMS plugins, nice
> Multilanguage-WWW-editor "Quanta Gold", Linux/Win/MacOS multi-platform
> apps and a dynamic CEO that actually reads threads and replies (see
> interview http://www.osnews.com/story.php?news_id=1592 ) future looks
> bright. Mike
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

- **From:** "Heikki Tuuri" <Heikki.Tuuri@innodb.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T20:25:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C8wa9.459$416.34936@read2.inet.fi>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz> <2763b81a.0208250954.53268a4d@posting.google.com> <3D69D04D.5E565E90@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Hi!

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3D69D04D.5E565E90@Azonic.co.nz...
> Mike wrote:
...
> > Fujitsu effort is great, but its
> > CGI-extensions have been poorly accepted without a proven OSS RDBMS
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> until it gets transactions and replication it is not much use for data

MySQL has supported replication for years, and it is actually in heavy use
at many MySQL sites.

MySQL supports transactions through InnoDB and BDB backends.

> processing which is where Cobol is targetted.  Fujitsu does work well
> with several proven RDBMS's including PostGreSQL (which is alomost
> there).
...

Best regards,

Heikki Tuuri
Innobase Oy
---
InnoDB - transactions, row level locking, and foreign key support for MySQL
See http://www.innodb.com, download MySQL-Max from http://www.mysql.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux

- **From:** "Reason" <steveno@nocfs.com.au>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T13:38:49+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nvCa9.10$FV3.5593@nsw.nnrp.telstra.net>`
- **References:** `<3D679B7B.99CC5BCE@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Hi Richard,

"Richard Plinston" wrote:
> The web site now lists NetCobol for Linux as being released.  It has a
> special price of $US 999 until August 31.
>
<snip>
>
> I suggest that you read the announcements and note that it doesn't, for
> example, support screen section.

How could we do screen I-O with a Cobol that offers only console-style I-O?

Due to big problems with Microfocus, we are intently evaluating Fujitsu
NetCobol for Linux. It looks lovely except that it has only console-style
display and accept.

Like you, we also have our own screen handler function. It currently uses
DISPLAY LINE...POS... at the low level. So we have plenty of flexibility.

Microfocus are making us struggle by tying us to a dinosaur in three ways:
- Many $000's for new Developer seats to simply move development from the
near-obsolete SCO OpenServer5 to either OpenUnix8 or Linux.
- Many, many $000's for new cobol runtimes to go with that.
- Charging for all the bells and whistles when we mainly just do ADDS and
MOVES.

Stephen
```

COBOL sp2 is a character mode user interface development system which
supports Linux and Fujitsu COBOL.  In addition, our Thin Client
product can allow you to run the COBOL application on the Linux server
and display 100% GUI COBOL sp2 screens on remote Windows PC Client
machines running on a TCP/IP network (Internet).  Please contact our
distribution agent for more information:

Our distributor for Australia is David Mowat.  You can contact Mr.
Mowat as follows:

Timaru S Canterbury

## Thread: Help request: Realia 16bit to 32bit conversion problem — 2 this month / 5 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help request: Realia 16bit to 32bit conversion problem

- **From:** maurice.northey@team.telstra.com (Maurice Northey)
- **Date:** 2002-08-25T23:43:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<697c3488.0208252243.3b3dcbbd@posting.google.com>`

```
I am converting a batch app from Realia Cobol v3 (DOS) to Realia
Workbench 3.1 (NT).
One program won't behave the same upon recompilation.
I have tried every trick in the book to find out why. 

I don't have any doco on what language-level Cobol version v3 is, so I
have tried recompiling in Realia4, Realia, ANSI74, 85, error checking
on/off etc without success.

So (1) does anyone know what options should be used with Workbench to
do the recompilation?

(2) Is it possible to trace a v3 program?

I reckon the problem is related to COMP SYNC usage.
(3) Does anyone know what COMP in v3 equates to in Workbench
(comp-3,4,5 etc)?

Any sggestions appreciated.
Maurice
```

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-08-26T08:53:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1030351773@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

<a id="t-00f43c2f7c"></a>
## Thread: IBM COBOL for AIX and Oracle — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### IBM COBOL for AIX and Oracle

- **From:** Ron <ron@yuma.acns.ColoState.EDU>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T09:31:09-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akg5vt$21iq@yuma.acns.ColoState.EDU>`

```
We are currently using Microfocus Cobol and Oracle's precompiler for accessing
Oracle databases on an AIX machine and looking for alternatives to Microfocus.
Our primary concern is the way Microfocus charges for licensing, not in its 
functionality.

When we have contacted IBM about their Cobol, it seems we get varying answers
as to whether they even sell it, or support it.  Yet, we were able to get a
copy of the IBM COBOL for AIX V1.1 for testing.  

My questions, is anyone using this COBOL on an AIX machine, and whether it
is going to continue to be supported by IBM.  And also, whether anyone is
using it for accessing Oracle dababases.  I'd also like to know of anyones
success with using someone elses COBOL, other than Microfocus, with Oracle.

Thanks,

Ron
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM COBOL for AIX and Oracle

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T23:35:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I0Ua9.123439$eK6.3804302@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<akg5vt$21iq@yuma.acns.ColoState.EDU>`

```
I use AIX COBOL a lot and am very satisfied with it. A quick check shows it is still orderable online, and I have not heard of any
withdrawals on it. Remember, most of the people you talk to at IBM Direct are less than knowledgeable about anything more  esoteric
than Windows 98. (*sigh*)

It does not support the SCREEN section, so if you are using that, you would have to port over to something else. Talk to Bob Wolfe
over a Flexus for some good ideas, or you can simply write the screens in C using Curses and CALL them that way, or you can do the
best thing, and use CICS. <grin> (Well, that idea of the 'best thing' may be a tad biased... :)

I do not know what is involved with using it for Oracle, but I am sure that Oracle does, so I would give them a call.

Speedwise, compile wise it is pretty efficient and comparable with Microfocus except when dealing with Operating System files
directly. (i.e. A Sequential File is not just a LF delimitated file under UNIX, however a LINE SEQUENTIAL file is.)

-Paul

- **References:** `<akg5vt$21iq@yuma.acns.ColoState.EDU>`

```
Ron <ron@yuma.acns.ColoState.EDU> wrote in message news:<akg5vt$21iq@yuma.acns.ColoState.EDU>...
> We are currently using Microfocus Cobol and Oracle's precompiler for accessing
> Oracle databases on an AIX machine and looking for alternatives to Microfocus.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ron

    We are using Acucobol(5.1.0.4) on AIX(4.3.3) with Oracle(8.1.7)
and have had very good luck. Acucorp's Acu4GL database interface
drivers allow you to access the database tables without changing the
COBOL read/write/rewrite/start/delete/etc statements to SQL. This
means that once the index files have been converted to database tables
there is no need to alter or rewrite any program code to perform IO
operations. The only problem we have encountered is doing some types
of read operations on alternate keys with duplicates. The performance
can be awful if the table is large. I have been told by Acucorp
support that this problem has been addressed in version 5.2 of the
runtime.
    One issue to be aware of though is cost. Oracle is rather
expensive and so are Acucobol's runtime and Acu4GL licenses.
```

## Thread: packed field handling - no sign — 9 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### packed field handling - no sign

- **From:** cougar400@hotmail.com (Kevin)
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T09:21:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3d6c1e7c.0208270821.5802dbbe@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi all..

Im having a problem handling the data in a file that I am trying to process.

The file stores a date(20020517) in the format below (in 4 bytes).

| byte 1 | byte 2 | byte 3 | byte 4 |
    2        0        0        1
    0        2        5        7

Since it is not signed, how do I define this in COBOL??

HELP!

thanks
Kevin.
```

#### ↳ Re: packed field handling - no sign

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <NoPersonalMailPlease@MyDot.Com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T18:27:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MwPa9.6884$p%3.533317@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3d6c1e7c.0208270821.5802dbbe@posting.google.com>`

```

Kevin <cougar400@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:3d6c1e7c.0208270821.5802dbbe@posting.google.com...
> Hi all..
>
> Im having a problem handling the data in a file that I am trying to
process.
>
> The file stores a date(20020517) in the format below (in 4 bytes).
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Since it is not signed, how do I define this in COBOL??

As you noted COMP-3 won't work as you get (and need) one byte for the sign.
Specify it as COMP.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: packed field handling - no sign

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T18:43:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yLPa9.5174$yt3.2232637@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3d6c1e7c.0208270821.5802dbbe@posting.google.com> <MwPa9.6884$p%3.533317@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <NoPersonalMailPlease@MyDot.Com> wrote in message
news:MwPa9.6884$p%3.533317@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
> Kevin <cougar400@hotmail.com> wrote in message
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> As you noted COMP-3 won't work as you get (and need) one byte for the
sign.
> Specify it as COMP.

Nope, that is not BCD storage, which is what the original poster wants. With
USAGE COMP ...

MOVE 20020517 to this-field

.. will give you, in hex representation, the value x'01317D25'   (what he
wants is x'20020517').

If you really need BCD format you will have to move the values into a PIC
X(04) field byte-by-byte. (this would be a good place for USAGE COMP-X , if
supported on the target platform).

MCM












will give you the binary equivaenlenlnet of the decimal number
```

#### ↳ Re: packed field handling - no sign

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T19:45:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kFQa9.231373$m91.9520644@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3d6c1e7c.0208270821.5802dbbe@posting.google.com>`

```

"Kevin" <cougar400@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:3d6c1e7c.0208270821.5802dbbe@posting.google.com...
> Hi all..
>
> Im having a problem handling the data in a file that I am trying to
process.
>
> The file stores a date(20020517) in the format below (in 4 bytes).
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Since it is not signed, how do I define this in COBOL??

Well, the short answer is: you can't.

But this should work:

01  BIG-FIELD    PIC 9(9) COMP-3.
01  FILLER REDEFINES BIG-FIELD.
   02  LITTLE-FIELD   PIC X(4).
   02  FILLER  PIC X VALUE X'0F'.

MOVE MYDATA TO LITTLE-FIELD.
COMPUTE MY-ANSWER = BIG-FIELD / 10
```

#### ↳ Re: packed field handling - no sign

- **From:** "Leahy, Don" <zzdlf@tdbank.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T15:48:28-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FHQa9.3577$z21.811743@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3d6c1e7c.0208270821.5802dbbe@posting.google.com>`

```
Here is one way to handle this situation (IBM COBOL for this-and-that):

05  WS-DATE-AREA.
 10  WS-DATE-AREA-X          PIC X(04).
 10  FILLER                               PIC X(01) VALUE X'0C'.

05  WS-DATE-9                      REDEFINES
      WS-DATE-AREA              PIC S9(09) COMP-3.

05 WS-DATE-NUM                PIC 9(08).

MOVE your date field to WS-DATE-AREA-X

COMPUTE WS-DATE-NUM = WS-DATE-9 / 10  (This last step gets rid of the X'0C'
in WS-DATE-AREA)

HTH

process.

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: packed field handling - no sign

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T15:10:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4vmnmu00cnbqno1lcm5as367hrh2kd6dfk@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3d6c1e7c.0208270821.5802dbbe@posting.google.com>`

```
On 27 Aug 2002 09:21:20 -0700 cougar400@hotmail.com (Kevin) wrote:

:>Im having a problem handling the data in a file that I am trying to process.

:>The file stores a date(20020517) in the format below (in 4 bytes).
:>
:>| byte 1 | byte 2 | byte 3 | byte 4 |
:>    2        0        0        1
:>    0        2        5        7

:>Since it is not signed, how do I define this in COBOL??

You will have to move it to an overlay field.

        05  in-numeric-form pic s9(8)V9 comp-3.
        05  in-character-group redefines in-numeric-form.
            10  in-character-form pic x(4).
            10  in-character-trailer pic s9 comp-3.

       move  field1 to in-character-form.
       move  +0 to in-character-trailer.
       in-numeric-form is now available.
```

#### ↳ Re: packed field handling - no sign

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <jud@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T15:35:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<alRa9.78246$%v4.3255313@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3d6c1e7c.0208270821.5802dbbe@posting.google.com>`

```
"Kevin" <cougar400@hotmail.com> wrote:
>
> Im having a problem handling the data in a file that I am trying to process.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Since it is not signed, how do I define this in COBOL??

On Micro Focus COBOL through 3.2, I believe COMP-0 handles that.
As far as I know, Net Express no longer supports unsigned BCD.

On Unisys A Series mainframes, that is the format for unsigned COMP.
```

#### ↳ Re: packed field handling - no sign

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T18:32:41-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akgu27$u2b$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3d6c1e7c.0208270821.5802dbbe@posting.google.com>`

```
Define it like this:

03  WS-DATE-PACKED PIC  9(08)V9 PACKED DECIMAL.
03  WS-DATE-PACKED-X REDEFINES WS-DATE-PACKED
                                            PIC  X(05).
03  WS-DATE-DISPLAY PIC  9(08).
03  WS-DATE-BCD         PIC  X(04) VALUE X'20020517'.

MOVE WS-DATE-BCD TO WS-DATE-PACKED-X (1:4).
MOVE X'0F' WS-DATE-PACKED-X (5:).
MOVE WS-DATE-PACKED TO WS-DATE-DISPLAY.

At this point, WS-DISPLAY-DATE contains 20020517 in Display-Numeric format.

HTH....

Bill

"Kevin" <cougar400@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:3d6c1e7c.0208270821.5802dbbe@posting.google.com...
> Hi all..
>
> Im having a problem handling the data in a file that I am trying to
process.
>
> The file stores a date(20020517) in the format below (in 4 bytes).
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Kevin.
```

## Thread: using "INVOKE" to mimic Java Interfaces — 5 msgs

### using "INVOKE" to mimic Java Interfaces

- **From:** rowntreerob@yahoo.com (rob rowntree)
- **Date:** 2002-08-27T13:08:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fc421fe6.0208271208.36b2d096@posting.google.com>`

```
In COBOL ( IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM  2.2.1 )i'm looking at a strategy
that implements the JAVA concept of interfaces allowing easy, cheap
extensions to an existing sub-system where no re-compiling of any
existing sub-system programs is required.

Im new to the COBOL "Invoke" statement and want some help with it.
Help me out with A practical example like that found at this link:

http://groups.google.com/groups?q=object+cobol+java+interface&hl=en&lr=lang_en&ie=UTF-8&oe=UTF-8&as_drrb=b&as_mind=12&as_minm=5&as_miny=2000&as_maxd=27&as_maxm=8&as_maxy=2002&selm=3C05A457.1142E5DE%40shaw.ca&rnum=9

Can anyone point me to COBOL code that accomplishes my technical
requirement below?

With an existing Interface that supports CALL-BACKS into generic
CLASSes conforming to , or implementing the Interface, as i extend the
system or as i want to introduce new functionality, i just write a new
implementation and use it according to the rules for "new
implementations that will call-back into their own methods".

The JAVA example of this and why you want to use something "so
complex" is described at the followin link:

http://www.ddj.com/documents/s=906/ddj9902j/9902j.htm

In other words using pseudo code, i want to do something like the
following:


START pseudo code
******
* existing sub system calls on the interface that do NOT Change.
* They never get recompiled when the implementation changes.
* They specify an instance of the interface in the method signature at
statement * 0001 below...

* ie. whenever "caller" is invoked, it will be invoked on an instance
of an  object conforming to the interface that includes method1, 2, 3
as shown below.
******

0001 method caller ( using  Implementation_of_the_Interface IotI )
   // A signature for getting an arg=IotI that is an implemntation 

0002 IotI.callback_to_method1() ;   
0003 IotI.callback_to_method2() ;
0004 IotI.callback_to_method3() ;


********
my New class or implementation that will accomplish my new work in
methods
1 2 and 3 
********

Class  aNewExtension implements MyInterface ...

method1    no args  // definition of method with signature as per the
interface

  0020     COBOL statements that do my work in the new way that i want
  0030
  0040

   ... 

method2    no args  // definition of method with signature as per the
interface

  0050     COBOL statements that do my work in the new way that i want
  0060
  0070

method3    no args  // definition of method with signature as per the
interface

  0080     COBOL statements that do my work in the new way that i want
  0090
  0100

* *** 
then the code to invoke the call-backs so the new statements in "0020"
thru
0100 above will get "performed" by the original code in "0001 method
caller" above
*****

ref1 = aNewExtension.newInstance();
caller(ref1);  // this invokes the method above at "0001 method
caller"
               // caller then calls-back into 3 methods in
Class=aNewExtension

******
end of pseudo-code
```

#### ↳ Re: using "INVOKE" to mimic Java Interfaces

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

Easy answer,

 Whatever you do, do *NOT* use the OO support in IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM
V2R2.

IBM has dropped that approach completely and a new (much easier to interface
with JAVA - but no SOM support) approach is available in IBM Enterprise
COBOL V3R1 and the newly announced V3R2
```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:<3D6C04A5.435CB9FA@shaw.ca>...
> rob rowntree wrote:
> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Java - is that a feature in  IBM OO COBOL ? As yet, I've stayed solely within the confines of OO COBOL - with no immediate requirement for the Web I haven't gotten into Java.

JAVA definitions to clarify the earlier analogy:
An Interface is a special class that specifies a set of behaviors
  these behaviors are like a list of methods 
   each method with a specific signature or parameter list
  methods in the interface have no source code or guts
  the guts of each method comes when you write an "implementation" 
  any implementation must contain each method listed in 
   the original interface

Java , as a good OO often uses objects in the P-List of methods

example: 
 0001 method caller ( using  Implementation_of_the_Interface IotI )

 here, i meant that the arg="IotI" in the signature specifies that:
    the argument must be an Implementation of a particular interface
    that it must implement 3 methods (M1, M2, and M3) the signatures 
      of which are specified by the orig. interface
    So, "Implementation_of_the_Interface" in the signature above
       means "an Object goes here"     

 Callbacks come into play when the original code for the method
"caller"
   which has an Interface class as an argument, then follows with code
   in the body of the method that mentions or uses methods from 
   the list of methods specified by the interface. 

> ref1 = aNewExtension.newInstance();  
> caller(ref1);  // this invokes the method above at "0001 method
  
and the body of caller has:

   IotI.M1()  |
   IotI.M2()  |  any new implementation of these methods  
   IotI.M3()  |  is what gets called back when you use an instance of 
                 the new class in place of 'ref1' as in :
                       caller(ref1)  above


In the 2.2.1 Cobol LE manual, i looked more at the "Invoke" 
and am not sure that it actually covers the practice of methods 
that take other objects as their arguments.

I think that i am going to wait for 3.1 and do an "in-line" API in
safe ole Cobol in the meantime.

> 
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: using "INVOKE" to mimic Java Interfaces

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-28T18:45:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D6D1C10.88B2748A@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<fc421fe6.0208271208.36b2d096@posting.google.com> <3D6C04A5.435CB9FA@shaw.ca> <fc421fe6.0208280120.191a2f38@posting.google.com>`

```


rob rowntree wrote:

>
> JAVA definitions to clarify the earlier analogy:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> safe ole Cobol in the meantime.

Well at least we are back on to the topic of COBOL. My fault, I shouldn't have suggested Bill Clinton could run again <G>

The solution is largely dependent upon what class support IBM provides - no details but I get the impression it is fairly *lean". Prototyping or whatever, you do require basic building bricks, just like Leggo. As it stands
the J4 Draft Standard hasn't yet addressed these 'building bricks' - so Fujitsu and  Micro Focus have currently provided their own. (Probably same applies with Hitachi who based their 'Standard Classes' on Smalltalk, just
like Micro Focus).

As an example, I can build a Dictionary (Key and Data) - a fancy name for an ISAM list, which I think Java calls a MAP. Currently the Dictionary can be made up of :-

Key        Data
----    --------
String    String                        Note : String = "ofValues" in example below
String    Object Reference
O/Ref    String
O/Ref    Object Reference

An example of constructing a Dictionary :-

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "initialize-dictionary".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-AssocTemplate                  object reference.
01 ls-DataTemplate                   object reference.
01 ls-KeyTemplate                    object reference.
01 ls-KeyLength                      pic x(4) comp-5.

Procedure division.

 if    ClientRecType
       move 3 to ls-KeyLength

 else  move 2 to ls-KeyLength
 End-if

 invoke CobolPicx "newclass"   using     ls-KeyLength
                               returning ls-KeyTemplate
 invoke CobolPicx "newclass"   using     ws-NameLength
                               returning ls-DataTemplate
 invoke Association "newclass" using     ls-KeyTemplate
                                         ls-DataTemplate
                               returning ls-AssocTemplate
 invoke Dictionary "ofValues"  using     ls-AssocTemplate
                                         ws-NameLength
                               returning
                                 os-Collection(ws-CurrentRectype)
End Method "initialize-dictionary".
*>------------------------------------------------------------

The important thing to note is that I'm using support classes CobolPicX and Association, (basic building bricks) to create the structure of the Dictionary. Those two 'building classes' are further assisted by additional
Micro Focus support classes.

You could take two approaches to the above, sub-class, or given the Net Express source, rewrite your own CobolPicX2 and Association2. Certainly, there's nothing to stop you writing classes with 'empty methods'.

Now I'm going into pseudo code because I don't know Java  <G> :-

*>----------------------
Method-id. "build-Java".
*>---------------------
Procedure Division.
 invoke JavaBuilder "new" using theseParameters returning aJavaBuilderInstance

In other words - IBM wise pass parameters but do the creation in Java. BUT - without knowing specifically what IBM does or *will* eventually provide, it's largely a guessing game.

Jimmy
```

with you about the word problems. I was always opposed to apptitude tests
too because most just test your ability to take a test. Riddles or questions
like what number comes next in the series don't tell you much. Sometimes
though the stress and time pressure of apptitude tests can also make them
unreliable. Good programmers can be lost because of the test itself.

of what you are talking about?

SIN
> or COSIN.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

and
> determine how to best derive that which we don't know.
>
> I could make those exact same statements about solving math word problems.
>
> By the way, my company uses an aptitude test for all new programming associates.  I was strictly opposed to
programming
> aptitude test until I took this one.  You don't have to know any programming language.  It tests your ability to
follow
> very detailed instructions.  It test your ability to follow sometimes conflicting instructions (they don't really
> conflict when you look at them closely).   It tests your ability to keep track of the "state" of things.  The test
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

If
> they do, then they probably won't like programming very much.  That's a huge generalization, but we all cling to
truisms
> when we find them worthwhile.

## Thread: PPT entry for Statically/Dynamically called subprograms in CICS. — 6 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### PPT entry for Statically/Dynamically called subprograms in CICS.

- **From:** nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas)
- **Date:** 2002-08-28T06:17:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c02fd744.0208280517.79feeb85@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
               First of all sorry for putting CICS question on COBOL
group.
My assumptions are as follows:
1. For Statically called module no PPT entry is required because
called module is part of main routine.
2. For Dynamically called module PPT entry is required as called
module is loaded at run time.Simultaneously we have to put called
module in CICS linked pack area.


Please reply me whether my assumptions are correct or not.


Regards.
Shyam
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PPT entry for Statically/Dynamically called subprograms in CICS.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-28T18:11:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akjljl$aie$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0208280517.79feeb85@posting.google.com> <akjfds$bah$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Is he correct that the dynamically called modules MUST be in the "CICS
linked pack area"?   It may be just the term used, but I thought that
application load modules could be DFHRPL or possibly STEPLIB.  But I could
be TOTALLY mistaken on this.
```

#### ↳ Re: PPT entry for Statically/Dynamically called subprograms in CICS. (2)

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-28T22:09:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akjv8q$9qc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0208280517.79feeb85@posting.google.com>`

```
A correction. You don't need to place Dynamically Called modules in the LPA.
They can be placed into your normal User Load Lib.

Dynamically Called module PPT entries can be Autoinstalled or explicitly
defined.

A heads-up for Dynamically Called modules. Try to keep your WS to a minimum
as Dynamically Called modules WS is not released until Task Termination.

A heads up for Statically Called modules. If the Caller's WS is reentrant
(or as in CICS/COBOL, serially reusable), try to pass a chunk of the
Caller's WS to the Static module as a parameter, ensuring that the Static
module will not be accessing storage defined to itself, which will render it
non-reentrant and cause those ever-popular "unpredictable results" as
mentioned frequently in the IBM Manuals. This would be similar if a Static
Assembler module were Called and it modified storage defined to its own
CSECT, which also renders it non-reentrant.

Thanks BK. I read this too fast initially as I was going out the door....

Bill

"shyam nivas" <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> wrote in message
news:c02fd744.0208280517.79feeb85@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>                First of all sorry for putting CICS question on COBOL
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Shyam
```

#### ↳ Re: PPT entry for Statically/Dynamically called subprograms in CICS. (2)

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-28T22:09:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akjvjh$bio$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0208280517.79feeb85@posting.google.com>`

```
A correction. You don't need to place Dynamically Called modules in the LPA.
They can be placed into your normal User Load Lib.

Dynamically Called module PPT entries can be Autoinstalled or explicitly
defined.

A heads-up for Dynamically Called modules. Try to keep your WS to a minimum
as Dynamically Called modules WS is not released until Task Termination.

A heads up for Statically Called modules. If the Caller's WS is reentrant
(or as in CICS/COBOL, serially reusable), try to pass a chunk of the
Caller's WS to the Static module as a parameter, ensuring that the Static
module will not be accessing storage defined to itself, which will render it
non-reentrant and cause those ever-popular "unpredictable results" as
mentioned frequently in the IBM Manuals. This would be similar if a Static
Assembler module were Called and it modified storage defined to its own
CSECT, which also renders it non-reentrant.

Thanks BK. I read this too fast initially as I was going out the door....

Bill

"shyam nivas" <nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com> wrote in message
news:c02fd744.0208280517.79feeb85@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>                First of all sorry for putting CICS question on COBOL
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Shyam
```

#### ↳ Re: PPT entry for Statically/Dynamically called subprograms in CICS.

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-29T01:22:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Icib9.10639$XV.352498@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0208280517.79feeb85@posting.google.com>`

```
shyam nivas wrote:
> Hi,
>                First of all sorry for putting CICS question on COBOL
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Please reply me whether my assumptions are correct or not.

Yes on no. 1.

For no. 2, you need a PPT entry for all dynamically called programs; but 
they don't have to be (and probably shouldn't be) in the LPA. They just 
have to be in the DFHRPL concatenation for the region. They're vanilla 
application programs, no special considerations, e.g., LPA.
```

## Thread: Does it take math skills ... back on topic. — 2 msgs

### Re: Does it take math skills ... back on topic.

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-28T14:52:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ls5b9.246010$m91.10102342@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<ak4c8s$1f0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <ake7rb$p23$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com> <akepom02l71@enews3.newsguy.com> <0n3omuk831ee7q7qdagldrec9kvgj5efd7@4ax.com>`

```

"Michael Todd" <mikltodd@comcast.net>
> >
> Actually, I have found that the aspect of math most valuable in
programming is the ability to solve word problems.  That
> doesn't require knowing your multiplication tables up to 100x100 or being
able to state the formula for calculating SIN
> or COSIN.
>
> The statement, "people that are good at math are typically good at
programming," I believe is better stated as "People
> that are good at solving word problems are typically good at programming."
When you look at our discipline
> (programming) IS solving word problems.  We typically receive requirements
in English (or should I say in a spoken
> language).  We have to break the requirements down into small, solvable
pieces.  We have to look at what we do know and
> determine how to best derive that which we don't know.

After my first semester of Law School, I was ranked 19th out of 180
freshmen. After the 2nd semester, I had moved up to 18th.

I visited with my advisor and asked what was going on? I skipped many
classes and didn't really study until the month before finals (although I
didn't admit my failings to my advisor!). He asked my undergraduate
background.

"I have  a Masters in Math" said I.

"Oh, you won't have any trouble with law school," he said. "The purpose of
law school is not to teach law - laws change every day, no one can keep up.
The purpose of law school is to teach you to THINK like a lawyer: logic,
deduction, comprehensive."

He went on: "Students who come to us from math, the physical sciences,
philosophy, engineering... they all do quite well. Those from business,
accounting, finance... those are your average law students. Those from the
liberal arts: English, music, history... they have the toughest time and
most don't make it."
```

```

> After my first semester of Law School, I was ranked 19th out of 180
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

I have often thought that the "art" of law consists of designing

## Thread: Visual Foxpro Date formatting via ODBC — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Visual Foxpro Date formatting via ODBC

- **From:** Billgentry2@comcast.net (Bill Gentry)
- **Date:** 2002-08-28T10:10:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6da089cf.0208280910.4b3c6b48@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi all,

I'm stumped on a problem, and I wonder if anyone else has worked
through this.

I have an app that writes to database tables via ODBC.  The current
data source is Microsoft Access.  I'm attempting to port to visual
foxpro.   I'm stuck on inserting/updating date fields.   Under Access,
I'd define a date as PIC X(10), with the date formatted in CCYY-MM-DD
format.   Foxpro wont accept it:  The sql error message is "data type
mismatch".   I've tried just about every kind of data format I could
think of to get this date inserted, to no avail.   My backup plan is
to just insert the dates as NUMERICs, but it'll be a lot easier
overall if I can just get the data inserted in the correct format.

In any case, if anyone has worked through this I'd appreciate the
input.

Thanks in advance,
Bill Gentry
```

#### ↳ Re: Visual Foxpro Date formatting via ODBC

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-28T23:19:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D6D5BBB.D8D044D9@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<6da089cf.0208280910.4b3c6b48@posting.google.com>`

```


Bill Gentry wrote:

> Hi all,
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't dabble with Dates in MS Access, (using only yymm or ccyymm), but
to try it out created a Table to do the following. This is the resulting
copyfile that Net Express ESQL Assistant generates :-
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

     EXEC SQL DECLARE
     `DatesF` TABLE
     ( "ThsID"               VARCHAR(8)
      ,"DateChar8"           VARCHAR(8)
      ,"DateLongInt"         INTEGER
      ,"DateSingle"          REAL
      ,"DateDouble"          DOUBLE
      ,"DateNoSpec"          DATETIME
      ,"DateGeneral"         DATETIME
      ,"DateLongDate"        DATETIME
      ,"DateMediumDate"      DATETIME
      ,"DateShortDate"       DATETIME
     ) END-EXEC.
****************************************************************
* COBOL DECLARATION FOR TABLE DatesF
****************************************************************
 01  DCLDatesF.
     03 DatesF-ThsID                     PIC X(8).
     03 DatesF-DateChar8                 PIC X(8).
     03 DatesF-DateLongInt               PIC S9(09)  COMP-5.
     03 DatesF-DateSingle                            COMP-1.
     03 DatesF-DateDouble                            COMP-2.
     03 DatesF-DateNoSpec                PIC X(19).
     03 DatesF-DateGeneral               PIC X(19).
     03 DatesF-DateLongDate              PIC X(19).
     03 DatesF-DateMediumDate            PIC X(19).
     03 DatesF-DateShortDate             PIC X(19).
****************************************************************
* COBOL INDICATOR VARIABLES FOR TABLE
****************************************************************
 01  DCLDatesF-NULL.
     03 DatesF-ThsID-NULL                PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
     03 DatesF-DateChar8-NULL            PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
     03 DatesF-DateLongInt-NULL          PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
     03 DatesF-DateSingle-NULL           PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
     03 DatesF-DateDouble-NULL           PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
     03 DatesF-DateNoSpec-NULL           PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
     03 DatesF-DateGeneral-NULL          PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
     03 DatesF-DateLongDate-NULL         PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
     03 DatesF-DateMediumDate-NULL       PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
     03 DatesF-DateShortDate-NULL        PIC S9(04)  COMP-5.
*>---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Having established what you want in MS Access, (one of the formats above),
exactly what is Foxpro anticipating. Perhaps if you attempt to Import a
dummy MS
Table *directly* into Foxpro you might get a clue. Alternatively if you
set up a dummy Table in Foxpro can you import it to MS Access and see what
you get ?.

Above is no solution - but it might help.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Bill

<a id="t-f8227f777e"></a>
## Thread: OT - My ### is bigger than yours - but is it better? — 40 this month / 58 total

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - My ### is bigger than yours - but is it better?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-28T19:05:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akjogh$tdj$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Starting a "new thread" for those who want to argue "might is right" versus
"right is might" versus "cultural relativism".
Let me preface this by my personal ethical "background".

I am of an age that I might have been subject to the US draft for Vietnam.
I applied for "conscientious objector" status with supporting documentation
from both my home rector and my bishop (Episcopal - not a common position -
but one supported for specific individuals in that denomination).  This
"claim" was denied by my draft board, but "luckily" I was declared 1F (not
as unfit as 4F - but close).  I had determined that I would go to jail
(probably NOT to Canada) if I were required to serve in a combatant role.

At that time, I considered what I would have done in WWII.  I come from a
"long line" (both sides of the family) of "Middle European" - often German -
Jews.  Given what we know now, how would I have felt about serving in WWII?
I didn't ever come to a final decision then, and I don't really know much
better now.

If WWII had ended some "other way" how would the winning side have
documented the American internment camps for Americans of Japanese descent -
as opposed to the Nazi concentration camps?  As is often pointed out, the
winning side DOES tend to write the histories.  (Ever read some of the
historical analysis of how Shakespeare might of written Richard III, if he
was NOT writing under a Tudor Queen?  And this doesn't even get into whether
his Henry VIII is even good reading/performing).

From my historical studies (not as great as others in this list), there is
no country/government that I would call "always perfect".  I don't even
think I know of any (or certainly not any of any length of time) that is
"always awful".  (I spent a summer in Leningrad - when it was Leningrad -
during its celebration of the 50th anniversary of the Communist revolution.
I wouldn't personally put either the Russian Communists, nor the Czarist
Russians; much less the current Russian government in either the "always
right" or "always wrong" category.) I know of many cultures that "thought
they were doing RIGHT" when history eventually judged them to have made
"unethical" decisions.  This might describe the European colonial powers in
"civilizing" both South America and Africa *OR* the American government in
"bringing the TRUTH" to either American Indians and former Spanish colonies
after the Spanish-American war.  The view that "our culture" is better than
yours and that we are "helping you" by FORCING our values and system on you
has happened time and time again.  I am personally too close to such cases
in the 20th (and 21st) centuries to give any "absolute" answer to when in
the past - or currently - this is still going on.

My PERSONAL ethics says that it is "wrong" to kill or wage war because you
think that "your group is GOOD" and "their group is EVIL".  This applies to
BOTH sides in Northern Ireland; BOTH sides (or all sides) in the Middle
East;  All sides in Sri Lanka, Philippines, Basque, and several regions in
Africa (to name just a few).  Furthermore,  I tend to lean more on Gandhi's
side than on either of the Bush's sides when it comes to "*IF* you attack us
or our friends, then we are JUSTIFIED in attacking you, your families, your
friends, and anyone who ever speaks up for you."  This is NOT a particularly
common view in the US (and I doubt that it is particularly common in many
other places in the world).  It is, nevertheless, where I stand on these
issues.
```

news:akjogh$tdj$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net...
> Starting a "new thread" for those who want to argue "might is right"

I have a very open question.

If we decided that a United States of the World were in order, today, and
could actually start it, who would be the founding states be?

The United States claims to be the best government in th world, and a
saviour to the rest.  If 11 new states applied for entry (say the provinces
of Canada) would they be welcome?  What about 40 new states?  What about 75?

Donald

```
So we should forget about a first strike ?
why certainly I say............
we should forget about the second strike too, yes
we should only forget until we have the Koran shoved down our throats
if we can stop choking on the poison gas first
the first strike could have been, say Kuwait in 1990
let's say the second was 9/11/2001
ok - you liberal America bashers say that can't be proven
well them let's look at all of the attacks on our peacekeepers - don't you
look at the news and see how many of our planes get shot at in the U.N.
mandated (not US, mind you) no fly zones ?
remember - the US has never been and likely never will be the head of the
U.N. though we contribute the most money, personnel and equipment
the U.S. does not want other states - if we did then Puerto Rico would be,
as would the Philippines who we helped at the turn of the century (the turn
of the 20th, not the 21st), then let's say we could have 'stated' Japan and
Germany, heck toss in Italy too........BUT WE DID NOT.
Democracies do not take over countries and keep them. Ok, so you say, but
England and France conquered nearly half the world hundreds of years
ago.........but they were monarchies (aka dictatorships)
I say we should engage in a second (or more depending on the count) strike
and make it NUCLEAR
I don't want my teenagers coming back in body bags any more than my granny
wanted grandpa coming back from Japan in one either

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - My ### is bigger than yours - but is it better?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-29T17:30:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3D6E5BED.133DE0F4@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<akjogh$tdj$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <%aib9.18917$n73.1060431@news20.bellglobal.com> <R_nb9.162308$Yd.7151472@twister.austin.rr.com> <bTrb9.31573$kp.342673@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net>`

```
You have the audacity to use the nom de plume 'Common Sense' and then use the
sabre-rattling phraseology you do below ???????

This is not 1945 - I'll take a bet, that with a heavy heart, Harry Truman
authorized the nuclear attacks on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. It justifiably saved
the lives of millions of Allied troops, (not just Americans) -  but the horrific
results on those two cities. And at that time no likelihood of reprisal from the
enemy. Nazi Germany, (with planned nuclear research), had already been defeated
and at that time the Soviets were still our war-time pals.

By all means get rid of Saddam Hussein with conventional weapons, if that's what
you are alluding to. Having carried out the war-like act, also plan for what
will replace his existing regime. A nuclear attack - think of the implications.
Any nutcase could retaliate with either biological or nuclear ! And which
country would they zero-in on ? (No prize for getting the right answer).

Your teenagers wouldn't have to come home in body bags. For convenience they
would be in your local morgue !

Jimmy

Common_Sense wrote:

> So we should forget about a first strike ?
> why certainly I say............
…[64 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

```
Wow Jimmy, what a response !

ok - it is not 1945 but if the Japanese and the terrorists of today did not
put their arms factories in cities then maybe there would be no collateral
damage.

As for reprisal - yes there could be reprisals but do you think if the US
performed a massive strike that wiped out the entire country that any others
would dare attack when they knew the result would be a firestorm ?
I think not - it worked with the Soviets after WWII. The Japanese still had
plenty of time, people and weapons for reprisal though. There were still
millions of citizens, capable of military service, alive. Plus Japan was
known to have and have used chemical weapons. Don't you think they could
have used some on us like they did the Chinese? Heck no - we would have
NUKED them again. So yes the fear factor worked and should continue to,
until man starts behaving like he should.

Look at this fact.....everyone was scared to death of the Soviets. Why ?
They had nuclear weapons though never used them in battle but they had a
huge conventional army too and did not hesitate to use them. Se habla
Hungary, Poland, Ukraine, Afghanistan, etc.....
Some people only respect force and must be forcibly removed or destroyed,
like you would a pit bull after your neighbor's kid was chewed up.

I think it is time the world realized what has always been true. The US is
not bent on world domination or empire building. The US comes to almost
everyone's rescue when disaster strikes yet we are always the first to be
bashed when someone cries wolf.

```
Oh my- an anonymous poster filled with patriotic fervor, who feels that a first strike is Ethical.
Well, you are entitled to your opinion.

However, I remember going to bed a night scared to death the Russians were going to drop the bomb
while I was sleeping, and I would not wake up.  I remember what "duck and cover!' meant too, from
first hand experience.

I personally believe a first strike is not ethical, nor can it ever be justified with the years old intelligence
we have now, and without international support. You can believe what you want.

Now the interesting point is - who are you? And why did you take off on a rant when I clearly was not referring about anything to do
with Iraq or any related issue the U.S. is facing?

- **Message-ID:** `<%ezb9.35230$kp.378082@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net>`
- **References:** `<akjogh$tdj$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <%aib9.18917$n73.1060431@news20.bellglobal.com> <R_nb9.162308$Yd.7151472@twister.austin.rr.com> <bTrb9.31573$kp.342673@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <CEyb9.139391$eK6.4143496@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
Paul,

below is a snippet of the thread I was replying to.....perhaps it started as
something else but this is how the thread started from my news reader....it
was posted by  Bill Klein

Starting a "new thread" for those who want to argue "might is right" versus
"right is might" versus "cultural relativism".
Let me preface this by my personal ethical "background".

I am of an age that I might have been subject to the US draft for Vietnam.
I applied for "conscientious objector" status with supporting documentation
from both my home rector and my bishop (Episcopal - not a common position -
but one supported for specific individuals in that denomination).  This
"claim" was denied by my draft board, but "luckily" I was declared 1F (not
as unfit as 4F - but close).  I had determined that I would go to jail
(probably NOT to Canada) if I were required to serve in a combatant role.
end of snip.......

now Paul, I don't see why the name or address of the poster matters but I
guess I should fear anyone like you and Comrade Klein because it sounds like
you would love to re-educate me, physically......tsk tsk peacenics...

but seriously - I too remember nuclear fears of the Soviets, lost many
nights sleep though now I worry about crazy Muslims and Arabs.   I also
remember fearing American Blacks when Martin Luther King was killed and
again when the first Rodney King verdict came in and the riots started

as for our "first strike" - it really is not a first strike......don't you
remember the Gulf war?, the attempt on Bush41, the UN sanctions that allow
them - still they have not been repealed so they are in effect, we can go in
by force if necessary. Also people have pointed out that Atta met with Iraqi
people in CZ prior to 9/11, Iraq has by UN, plus US accounts - a training
ground near Baghdad for terrorists to train on a real Boeing airplane. Not
to mention the attempts at shooting down our (UN sponsored, US owned)
airplanes. Then there are the oil well fires, the Vincennes, I believe that
Iraq hit with an Exocet in the 1980's ?

I for one do not want to keep worrying about more terrorist attacks on our
soil. We have a known bad guy, just like Chamberlain new about Hitler and
need to do something about it. There are far too many dangerous weapons over
there to just let him off. Before you liberals go into the old, but CS, they
never found any nuclear weapons in Iraq........ok, the UN did not but they
were not allowed free access everywhere, defectors have told us he has kept
working on nukes and then the chemical weapons. All sides agree, even the
inspectors who could not find nukes, that he had tons of chemical and
biological weapons and we were destroying them 24x7 up until the time the
inspectors were tossed out.

People (liberals, really) keep forgetting that when the inspectors left,
there job was not done in terms of destroying all disclosed/found weapons.
Forget about the nukes or absence of, he still had tons of weapons left that
we were never allowed to destroy.

That makes me think "duck and cover" but how do you cover from a gas attack
?
Like the 1940's Japanese, the enemy needs to fear a far more violent
response to any attack or counter.
After two atomic bombs the Japanese still had to take a few days to consider
surrender. I was not there nor was I yet born but from what I have read -
the surrender discussions in Japan were very close votes.

Don't get me wrong, it is not patriotic fervor driving my feelings, it is
FEAR. Americans have lots to boast about and lots to complain about but at
least we have the freedom to complain.

trying to tear each other apart. In fact, that is consistent with what you advocate, which again, probably means you are lying about
your age and what you do or do not remember.

troll trying to collect e-mail address or sate their need to prove their points.

you snipe from the shadows.

Lots of drama and no one gets hurt (that I have seen).

Lots of class in all of the "players" and fans too.

- **Message-ID:** `<3D710CAC.EBBEE6AB@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<akjogh$tdj$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <%aib9.18917$n73.1060431@news20.bellglobal.com> <R_nb9.162308$Yd.7151472@twister.austin.rr.com> <bTrb9.31573$kp.342673@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <RR3thKA1X2b9EwWj@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3D6F938A.9524A70A@shaw.ca> <UqscfAAaQ9b9EwFl@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <akolpr$uq3$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ZUEsOAZtNc9EwbU@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

Alistair Maclean wrote:

> In article <akolpr$uq3$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>, William M. Klein
> <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> writes
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well I'm glad you found curling entertaining. I didn't. Blame us Canucks - I
think we 'invented' it to show we could come up with something more dull than
baseball. The only 'exciting' thing I find about curling is the frenzy the
'assistants' get into sweeping the bloody ice !

I am no sports fan, and loathe being a couch potato watching endless hours of
sport on TV. When the Calgary Herald arrives, the first section I chuck on the
floor is the Sports Section.

Now baseball, as we played it in UK with PTA groups - 'Rounders' - chuck down
a few coats to mark the 'bases', a tennis ball and a bit of stick to hit the
damn thing with - impromptu, that can be fun. Now baseball as played in the
States, (and they have the audacity to refer to the 'World Series'), all I see
are great pauses in the game, while people incessantly chew gum or spit out
'baccie, and the pitcher performs like an orang-utan before he throws the damn
ball !

Now as you know only too well Alistair, cricket, not as played at Lords or the
international games, but on the village green, you can watch a bunch of
players prancing around in their whites while you are sipping a delightful
glass of beer sitting in a deck chair. To me that's a nice civilized approach
<G>.

Golf - well time wise that would conflict with my gardening. But I am
fascinated by the beautiful scenery that I see Tiger Woods and others playing
in. (No I don't watch it - my son switches it on when he comes over for a free
meal each Sunday !).

Let's see if we can come up with a list - add and change as you see fit folks
:-

ASCENDING Interest :

Curling
Baseball
Synchronized Swimming
Cricket
American/Canadian Football ( that's Rugby played wearing Ice Hockey equipment
- what a bunch of pansies <G>)
Rugby
Golf
Ice Hockey
Soccer

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

whether

in

colonies

than

you
> has happened time and time again.  I am personally too close to such cases

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> think that "your group is GOOD" and "their group is EVIL".  This applies

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Bill,

As for the jibe at the anonymous posting......would you punch me in the nose
if I put my name and address in?
Would you spam me, sign me up for porn sites?
Sounds kind of ominous to me.

As for the crusaders of the middle ages though, were they living in
Constitutional Republics (aka democracies) ?
No, things were pretty uncivilized back in those days. Things were (in my
humble opinion) pretty uncivilized until the 20th century when mass
communication brought technology (newspapers, radio, TV) that 'outed' abuses
and such.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - My ### is bigger than yours - but is it better?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-29T17:01:40-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<umt2qmhni0ov5c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<akjogh$tdj$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <aasb9.225289$983.481997@rwcrnsc53> <aklpsq$a7e$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <o_tb9.225693$983.481961@rwcrnsc53>`

```

Common_Sense <US_HAS_FREEDOM@YOU_dont.com> wrote in message
news:o_tb9.225693$983.481961@rwcrnsc53...
[snip]
>
> As for the crusaders of the middle ages though, were they living in
> Constitutional Republics (aka democracies) ?

A republic is not a democracy except for the one instance
where direct (non-representative) democracy is used.
In a republic, the supreme power, always, remains with
the people. In a representative democracy, the supreme
power is in the hands of the representatives.

A Constitutional Republic may have democratically elected
representatives; but that does not make it a Democracy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - My ### is bigger than yours - but is it better?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-08-30T15:19:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ako2ce$ff4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<akjogh$tdj$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <aasb9.225289$983.481997@rwcrnsc53> <aklpsq$a7e$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <o_tb9.225693$983.481961@rwcrnsc53> <umt2qmhni0ov5c@corp.supernews.com>`

```

On 29-Aug-2002, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

> A republic is not a democracy except for the one instance
> where direct (non-representative) democracy is used.
> In a republic, the supreme power, always, remains with
> the people. In a representative democracy, the supreme
> power is in the hands of the representatives.

Depends on which definitions you use:
http://www.m-w.com/cgi-bin/dictionary?book=Dictionary&va=republic
1 a (1) : a government having a chief of state who is not a monarch and who in
modern times is usually a president (2) : a political unit (as a nation) having
such a form of government b (1) : a government in which supreme power resides in
a body of citizens entitled to vote and is exercised by elected officers and
representatives responsible to them and governing according to law (2) : a
political unit (as a nation) having such a form of government c : a usually
specified republican government of a political unit <the French Fourth Republic>
2 : a body of persons freely engaged in a specified activity <the republic of
letters>
3 : a constituent political and territorial unit of the former nations of
Czechoslovakia, the U.S.S.R., or Yugoslavia

Notice the primary (and original) definition of the word is a government without
a monarch.

http://www.m-w.com/cgi-bin/dictionary?book=Dictionary&va=democracy
1 a : government by the people; especially : rule of the majority b : a
government in which the supreme power is vested in the people and exercised by
them directly or indirectly through a system of representation usually involving
periodically held free elections
2 : a political unit that has a democratic government
3 capitalized : the principles and policies of the Democratic party in the U.S.
4 : the common people especially when constituting the source of political
authority
5 : the absence of hereditary or arbitrary class distinctions or privileges

By these definitions, there can be considerable overlap.  Do you know of a
democracy that isn't a republic (using the primary definitions for each word)?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - My ### is bigger than yours - but is it better?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-08-30T14:28:15-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<umvdq36k7uo276@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<akjogh$tdj$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <aasb9.225289$983.481997@rwcrnsc53> <aklpsq$a7e$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <o_tb9.225693$983.481961@rwcrnsc53> <umt2qmhni0ov5c@corp.supernews.com> <ako2ce$ff4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:ako2ce$ff4$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 29-Aug-2002, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1 a (1) : a government having a chief of state who is not a monarch and
who in
> modern times is usually a president (2) : a political unit (as a nation)
having
> such a form of government b (1) : a government in which supreme power
resides in
> a body of citizens entitled to vote and is exercised by elected officers
and
> representatives responsible to them and governing according to law (2) : a
> political unit (as a nation) having such a form of government c : a
usually
> specified republican government of a political unit <the French Fourth
Republic>
> 2 : a body of persons freely engaged in a specified activity <the republic
of
> letters>
> 3 : a constituent political and territorial unit of the former nations of
> Czechoslovakia, the U.S.S.R., or Yugoslavia
>
> Notice the primary (and original) definition of the word is a government
without
> a monarch.

Whether the first definition, 1 a(1), is 'original' is questionable.
It may simply be the most common usage, today.

My Random House College Dictionary puts the definition
from 1 b(1) first and 1 a(1) as defintion 2. That was from
1975.


> http://www.m-w.com/cgi-bin/dictionary?book=Dictionary&va=democracy
> 1 a : government by the people; especially : rule of the majority b : a
> government in which the supreme power is vested in the people and
exercised by
> them directly or indirectly through a system of representation usually
involving
> periodically held free elections
> 2 : a political unit that has a democratic government
> 3 capitalized : the principles and policies of the Democratic party in the
U.S.
> 4 : the common people especially when constituting the source of political
> authority
> 5 : the absence of hereditary or arbitrary class distinctions or
privileges
>
> By these definitions, there can be considerable overlap.  Do you know of a
> democracy that isn't a republic (using the primary definitions for each
word)?

Yes, but I will use the 'primary' definitions from Random House.

For the U.S., city councils, county commissions, school boards,
etc., are representative democracies.

Also for the U.S., states are republics with democratically
elected legislators and executives.

The United States is, constitutionally, a Federal Republic
with democratically elected legislators and an indirectly
elected executive.

But notice the distinction between republic 1 b(1) and
democracy 1 b and how it relates to what I said, above.

republic -- 1 b (1) : a government in which supreme power
resides in a body of citizens entitled to vote and is exercised
by elected officers and representatives responsible to them
and governing according to law

democracy -- b : a government in which the supreme power
is vested in the people and exercised by them directly or
indirectly through a system of representation usually involving
periodically held free elections

> > In a republic, the supreme power, always, remains with
> > the people. In a representative democracy, the supreme
> > power is in the hands of the representatives.

In a republic, power resides in the people and is exercised
according to law. This means that irresponsible government
may be addressed in the courts.

In a representative democracy, the power 'of the people' is
exercised by representatives. This means that irresponsible
government must be addressed by removing the
representatives and by undoing their acts.

I see little evidence that most politicians or, for that matter,
voters understand the difference between a republic and
a democracy. When a politician runs for a U.S. State or
Federal office and claims that "If elected, I will < do this,
that, and the other >", they are stating that they do not know
what their duties are. If a politician states they will follow the
law (constitution) as set forth by the people, they understand
their duty; but have no chance at being elected.
```

<a id="t-ae73766eee"></a>
## Thread: Database versus COBOL indexed files — 14 this month / 26 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Database versus COBOL indexed files

- **From:** philhickling@compuserve.com (Phil Hickling)
- **Date:** 2002-08-29T06:23:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6a371be2.0208290523.13ca39d9@posting.google.com>`

```
What are the relative advantages and disadvantages of an sql database
system as compared with COBOL indexed files?

I have a client using a networked system for 100+ users, developed
using MicroFocus NetExpress, accessing a database of 30 indexed files,
3 Gb in total size, 1Gb largest file (plus 5 Gb archived data), about
10 million live records.  About 80% of users connect via the local NT4
LAN, the remainder via a WAN using Citrix WinFrame.  It all works OK,
but we face a possible 10 times expansion, and are concerned that
performance may disintegrate.

Would an sql database system such as Oracle, Sybase, etc. be better? 
Are there other options for improving performance?  Can COBOL systems
and indexed files cope with 1000+ users, and 30Gb databases?
```

#### ↳ Re: Database versus COBOL indexed files

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-29T10:15:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nfesmu88bk74mvkhm3va1vvd6f1mbm2b6i@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<6a371be2.0208290523.13ca39d9@posting.google.com>`

```
On 29 Aug 2002 06:23:25 -0700 philhickling@compuserve.com (Phil Hickling)
wrote:

:>What are the relative advantages and disadvantages of an sql database
:>system as compared with COBOL indexed files?

SQL access.

Lot more storage and DASD use. Lot more maintenance.

:>I have a client using a networked system for 100+ users, developed
:>using MicroFocus NetExpress, accessing a database of 30 indexed files,
:>3 Gb in total size, 1Gb largest file (plus 5 Gb archived data), about
:>10 million live records.  About 80% of users connect via the local NT4
:>LAN, the remainder via a WAN using Citrix WinFrame.  It all works OK,
:>but we face a possible 10 times expansion, and are concerned that
:>performance may disintegrate.

:>Would an sql database system such as Oracle, Sybase, etc. be better? 
:>Are there other options for improving performance?  Can COBOL systems
:>and indexed files cope with 1000+ users, and 30Gb databases?

Depends on the implementation of COBOL, coding skills of developers, type of
access to the data, etc.
```

#### ↳ Re: Database versus COBOL indexed files

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-08-29T12:39:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0208291139.47a4f2be@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6a371be2.0208290523.13ca39d9@posting.google.com>`

```
philhickling@compuserve.com (Phil Hickling) wrote 

> What are the relative advantages and disadvantages of an sql database
> system as compared with COBOL indexed files?

The major advantages that I see for SQL are that it can be accessed by
several different systems at one time independant of the computer
language and this includes direct client programs.

Indexed files are usually tied to the particular implementation of a
specific language.
 
> I have a client using a networked system for 100+ users, developed
> using MicroFocus NetExpress, accessing a database of 30 indexed files,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> and indexed files cope with 1000+ users, and 30Gb databases?

Could Windows cope with 1000 users and 30Gb database ?  Could the LAN
cope with the traffic ?  There are far more fundemental problems with
scaling performance than worying about whether you should should
rewrite the programs.

In fact I would suggest that you need to avoid going to an SQL server
because it alone could take some time to design and tune.  You would
most likely find that just going to SQL would initially give severe
degradation in performance until you worked out how to tune and
optimise it.

Does MF have FileShare server running on a really grunty multiCPU
server ?  If so that could help in keeping the LAN traffic down (by
localising the index lookups) and would also help consistency and
recovery.
```

#### ↳ Re: Database versus COBOL indexed files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-08-29T14:43:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akltha$nvm$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<6a371be2.0208290523.13ca39d9@posting.google.com>`

```
Some PROs and CONs of each.

Many COBOL implementations have "optimized" indexed file systems.  I believe
that NetExpress is one of those.  This MAY perform better than "generalized"
(even optimized) SQL systems.

Most SQL systems have BETTER "transaction processing" (checkpoint, rollback,
etc) features than "native COBOL".

If you know that your entire application will ALWAYS be written in COBOL,
then that MAY be the best fit for your "built-in" programmer knowledge base.
On the other hand, most SQL systems have built in "user inquiry" and "ad hoc
report" facilities.  Furthermore, it is easier to interface with OTHER
languages using SQL if you are running on a system where the compiler
vendor, not the operating system vendor provides the data access system.
(For example, on IBM mainframes, the indexed file system is "independent" of
COBOL while on Windows and Unix there are "some" independent systems such as
C-ISAM and Btrieve, but most compilers "default" to a proprietary system).

How many "search" fields do you think you will EVER need to use from your
data - and in what combinations?  Certainly COBOL "alternate indexes" work
in many applications - however, the more you have the more performance (and
data integrity) MAY suffer in certain cases.  SQL, on the other hand, is
DESIGNED for both optimized and occasional individual and multiple field
"searches".

I am certain that there are many, MANY, other things that might impact your
decision.  My "guess" is that for the long term, you would be better with a
"production quality" SQL than with a COBOL indexed file, but that will (of
course) add to the cost of your application - and its complexity if your
programmers are not used to SQL and COBOL applications.
```

#### ↳ Re: Database versus COBOL indexed files

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-08-29T22:34:18+00:00
